input:
- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to a one-count charge of being a felon in possession of a firearm under 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(1).
- On July 9, 2020, Defendant A pled guilty to the offense.
- The charged offense carries a maximum penalty of 10 years of imprisonment.
- The offense carries a maximum supervised release term of up to three years.
- A violation of supervised release could result in up to two years of imprisonment per violation, with no credit for time already spent on supervised release.
- The offense carries a maximum fine of $250,000.
- A $100 special assessment applies to the count of conviction.
- Defendant A agreed to pay the $100 special assessment on the day the guilty plea is accepted.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit two firearms and any associated ammunition pursuant to 18 U.S.C. § 924(d) and 28 U.S.C. § 2461(c).
- One firearm to be forfeited was a black Taurus Millennium G2 9mm handgun bearing serial number TK040325.
- One firearm to be forfeited was a black and silver Springfield .40 caliber handgun with an obliterated serial number.
- The government filed its sentencing memorandum on October 28, 2020.
- Sentencing was scheduled for November 4, 2020.
- The government summarized the offense facts by reference to the Presentence Report paragraphs 5–10.
- In the early morning hours of October 12, 2019, Hamden Police Department officers responded to a 911 report of shots fired near the Merritt Parkway.
- Officers conducted a traffic stop on a car seen driving at a high rate of speed away from the area of the gunshots.
- Defendant A was the sole passenger in the stopped vehicle.
- Officers instructed the vehicle’s occupants to place their hands outside the car windows while police approached.
- An officer identified that Defendant A was concealing at least one firearm underneath his legs.
- As officers attempted to remove Defendant A from the car, he ran from the vehicle.
- As Defendant A fled, two firearms fell from where he had been sitting.
- One firearm that fell was a black Taurus Millennium G2 9mm handgun bearing serial number TK040325.
- Another firearm that fell was a black and silver Springfield .40 caliber handgun with an obliterated serial number.
- Police later recovered .40 caliber casings near the Merritt Parkway exit ramp where the gunshots were first reported.
- Defendant A ignored officers’ verbal commands while fleeing.
- A pursuing officer deployed a taser on Defendant A, which had no effect.
- Defendant A was apprehended when a pursuing officer tackled him.
- Shortly after his arrest, Defendant A made statements indicating he took full responsibility for the contraband found in the vehicle.
- Restitution was not applicable in this case.
- The statutory exposure described in the Presentence Report included a maximum of 10 years’ imprisonment, up to three years of supervised release, a maximum fine of $250,000, and a $100 special assessment.
- On or about April 21, 2010, Defendant A was convicted in Connecticut of Robbery in the First Degree with a deadly weapon under Conn. Gen. Stat. § 53a-134.
- At the time of the October 12, 2019 arrest, Defendant A was wanted on a separate firearms charge out of New Britain, Connecticut.
- At the time of the October 12, 2019 arrest, Defendant A had an active warrant out of Waterbury, Connecticut, for failure to appear related to the 2009 robbery case.
- Defendant A had prior convictions including Carrying a Pistol without a Permit and Sale of Illegal Drugs.
- Defendant A was on probation for prior convictions when he committed the instant offense.
- Approximately four months after release from a transitional placement related to prior convictions, Defendant A was charged with Criminal Possession of a Firearm, a case noted as pending in the PSR.
- The government indicated it would seek to modify PSR paragraph 11 to clarify that Defendant A was never charged with the shooting referenced.
- The government stated that Defendant A accrued disciplinary tickets for fighting, disobedience, and interfering with security during a previous prison sentence and during current pretrial detention.
- Defendant A earned a GED during prior incarceration.
- Defendant A obtained Certified Nurse Aide and Hospice Aide qualifications.
- The PSR detailed that Defendant A has substance abuse issues.
- The district of conviction is the District of Connecticut.

- The case is in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia under case number 20-CR-0006 (KBJ).
- On February 7, 2020, Defendant A waived indictment and pleaded guilty to a single count of willful failure to collect, account for, and pay over employment taxes in violation of 26 U.S.C. § 7202.
- Defendant A agreed that the attached Statement of Offense fairly and accurately describes the offense conduct and signed it on December 23, 2019.
- The plea agreement was executed on December 23, 2019.
- The charge carried a statutory maximum penalty of 5 years’ imprisonment, up to a $250,000 fine (or higher under 18 U.S.C. § 3571(d)), and up to 3 years of supervised release.
- The plea agreement required a $100 special assessment.
- Defendant A agreed to pay restitution to the IRS of $4,903,820.93 and to the District of Columbia Office of Tax and Revenue of $6,490,515.16.
- The agreed restitution amounts include additional 2010 tax liabilities: about $442,405.97 in employment tax losses and $204,559.99 in sales tax losses.
- The government later calculated net restitution balances of $4,884,782.19 to the IRS and $6,390,182.69 to the D.C. Office of Tax and Revenue after crediting certain payments.
- The government’s net IRS restitution figure reflects $19,038.74 in payments credited by Defendant A.
- The government’s net D.C. restitution figure reflects $100,332.47 in payments credited by Defendant A.
- The sentencing date referenced in the record was scheduled for October 8, 2020.
- After pleading guilty on February 7, 2020, Defendant A was granted release pending sentencing and remained compliant with release conditions according to the defense submission.
- Defendant A founded, served as President, and operated several companies providing food services within government buildings in and outside the District of Columbia.
- Named entities included IL Creation of Maryland, Inc.; IL Creation of Washington DC, Inc.; JAC-USDA, Inc.; ITLC-LOC, Inc.; ILCC, Inc.; IL Creation The Market Place, Inc.; IL Green, Inc.; Market Place Parklawn, Inc.; and IL Creations, Inc.
- From 2011 to 2015, Defendant A willfully failed to account for and pay over $6,285,955.17 in District of Columbia sales taxes collected by the entities.
- The sales tax due and owing for IL Creations, Inc. was $634,631.47 for 2011–2015.
- The sales tax due and owing for IL Creation of Washington DC, Inc. was $129,318.50 for 2011–2015.
- The sales tax due and owing for ITLC-LOC, Inc. (one account) was $768,058.08 for 2011–2015.
- An additional ITLC-LOC, Inc. account had $567,817.21 in sales tax due for 2011–2015.
- The sales tax due and owing for JAC-USDA, Inc. (one account) was $586,855.72 for 2011–2015.
- An additional JAC-USDA, Inc. account had $831,851.11 in D.C. sales tax due for 2011–2015.
- The sales tax due and owing for ILCC, Inc. was $1,443,155.49 for 2011–2015.
- The sales tax due and owing for IL Creation The Market Place, Inc. was $772,397.20 for 2011–2015.
- Sales tax due and owing for IL Creation of Maryland, Inc. included $74,774.59 for one account and additional amounts across other accounts totaling hundreds of thousands of dollars for 2011–2015.
- From 2010 to 2015, the entities accrued a multi‑million‑dollar employment tax liability, with the core criminal conduct occurring from 2012 to 2015.
- As a responsible person, Defendant A had authority over the entities’ payroll tax obligations, including hiring and firing, approving payments, and making financial decisions.
- On March 30, 2011, Defendant A acknowledged to IRS personnel that he had the authority and responsibility to pay employment taxes when due.
- Defendant A was frequently apprised between 2011 and 2015 of payroll tax responsibilities and the accruing employment tax liability by employees and the IRS.
- Between 2011 and 2015, Defendant A caused the entities to spend substantial sums, including over a million dollars in personal salary and advances and donations to a church, while failing to pay over withheld payroll taxes.
- For 2011–2015, the total employment tax due and owing across the entities was $4,461,414.98.
- Within the $4,461,414.98 total, the trust fund (employee) portion due was $3,465,825.41 and the employer portion due was $1,675,011.41; $679,421.79 had been deposited or paid.
- As of 2015, employment tax delinquencies spanned eight entities and 57 quarters, with amounts including: IL Creation of Maryland ($813,047; 4 quarters), JAC‑USDA, Inc. ($1,368,254; 10 quarters), ITLC‑LOC, Inc. ($1,129,960; 14 quarters), ILCC, Inc. ($446,745; 6 quarters), IL Creation the Market Place Inc. ($324,529; 12 quarters), ILC Green, Inc. ($258,554; 5 quarters), Market Place Parklawn, Inc. ($84,414; 4 quarters), IL Creations, Inc. ($35,911; 2 quarters).
- In April 2013, the IRS approved an installment agreement for one entity to pay more than $1.5 million in back taxes, which was later defaulted.
- In 2013–2014, IRS Revenue Officers repeatedly contacted and advised about ongoing employment tax non‑compliance and defaults, including for the NASA cafeteria entity.
- The NASA cafeteria entity accrued over $240,000 in delinquent employment tax in 2015 alone.
- The D.C. Office of Tax and Revenue placed a lien related to unpaid sales taxes as early as 2011; at one point it was moved from the business to a personal lien to avoid endangering contracts.
- Defendant A late‑filed hundreds of D.C. sales tax returns in June 2015, and according to D.C. authorities had paid back only around $100,000 of the over $6 million by the time of sentencing.
- From 2011 to 2015, the businesses failed to timely file 333 D.C. sales tax returns across at least 14 locations.
- By location, D.C. sales taxes misappropriated included approximately: $831,851 at the State Department (JAC‑USDA); $768,058 at the Library of Congress (ITLC‑LOC); $726,436 at the Coast Guard (IL Creation the Market Place).
- Additional D.C. sales tax misappropriation by location included approximately: $634,631 at “An Uncommon Café” (IL Creations Inc.); $586,856 at USDA (JAC‑USDA); $567,817 at the American Red Cross (ITLC‑LOC).
- Further D.C. sales tax misappropriation by location included approximately: $278,584 at the Department of Energy (IL Creation of Maryland); $212,863 at the Department of Commerce (IL Creation of Maryland); $129,318 at “New World Café” (IL Creation of Washington DC).
- Other D.C. locations with misappropriated sales taxes included approximately: $81,708 at the Executive Office Building; $74,775 at Postal Square; $65,409 at the RFK Department of Justice Building; $43,092 at the New Executive Office Building; and $1,284,557 attributed to ILCC at one or more D.C. locations.
- The combined tax loss (employment and D.C. sales tax) tied to the conduct was $10,747,370.13.
- From 2016 onward, Defendant A began paying employment taxes as they became due each quarter according to the government’s memorandum.
- By the time of sentencing, according to the IRS, only $19,039 had been paid toward the $4.46 million employment tax component.
- Defendant A used business funds from 2012–2018 for personal expenditures including over $1.1 million in non‑payroll transfers to personal accounts and more than $448,000 in salary.
- Business funds were used to make approximately $1.2 million in mortgage payments, over $207,000 in college tuition and expenses, and over $100,000 on luxury cars.
- Business funds paid about $50,000 for cruises and tens of thousands for luxury retail purchases.
- Nearly $830,000 in payments to a church were made from business bank accounts during 2012–2018.
- For tax years 2013–2015, Defendant A reported no taxable income on individual returns, claimed business losses and deductions of over $1.1 million, paid no federal income taxes, and received a refund of over $30,000 in 2015.
- Defendant A owes over $370,000 in taxes, interest, and penalties for tax years 2016 and 2017 according to IRS transcripts cited in the record.
- Defendant A admitted the offense conduct and stipulated that the conduct was willful and knowing, not due to accident, mistake, or other innocent reason.
- The plea agreement states that the employment tax loss for 2012–2015 was $4,461,414.98 and that figure included both employer and employee portions.
- The plea agreement states D.C. sales tax losses caused from 2011–2015 were $6,285,955.17 for entities Defendant A owned and controlled.
- Restitution also covered additional 2010 employment tax quarters for IL Creation of Maryland, Inc.: 2010/06 $62,724.00; 2010/09 $264,445.18; 2010/12 $115,236.79.
- Defendant A acknowledged knowing about the entities’ employment tax liabilities as early as 2011 and was told by IRS revenue officers to pay the taxes in full.
- Accounting staff at the businesses tracked and reported outstanding employment tax liabilities to Defendant A, including summaries exceeding $2 million by early 2014.
- The businesses operated cafeterias and catering services for numerous federal departments and agencies in the Washington, D.C. area.
- The government asserted that at the same time taxes were not paid, Defendant A lived a luxurious lifestyle and paid little-to-no individual income taxes during certain years.
- The defense submission states Defendant A was debarred from federal contracts.
- The defense submission states the family home was being sold with net proceeds to be remitted to the IRS as part of restitution efforts.
- The defense submission states Defendant A has been compliant with employment tax obligations since early 2016.

- The case is in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Washington at Seattle.
- The presiding judge is the Honorable Robert S. Lasnik.
- The federal case number is CR20-00002RSL.
- The defendant waived indictment and agreed to proceed by Information.
- The defendant pleaded guilty on January 16, 2020.
- The plea agreement document was filed on January 16, 2020.
- The plea agreement reflects signatures dated approximately January 10, 2020.
- The defendant pleaded guilty to Count 1, Felon in Possession of a Firearm, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 922(g).
- The statutory maximum term of imprisonment for the § 922(g) count is 10 years.
- The statutory maximum fine for the count is up to $250,000.
- The maximum term of supervised release for the count is up to three years.
- A mandatory special assessment of $100 applies to the count of conviction.
- If probation is imposed, the probation period can be up to five years.
- The parties entered into the plea agreement pursuant to Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 11(c)(1)(B).
- On April 24, 2019, at approximately 8:39 a.m., a King County Sheriff’s Deputy was on the Muckleshoot Indian Reservation at the Muckleshoot Bingo Hall.
- Officers were attempting to locate Person J.M., who had an outstanding warrant, and found a car registered to J.M. in the Bingo Hall parking lot.
- Two officers approached a man at the Bingo Hall who resembled J.M.; the man was with a woman.
- The man refused to give his name, was detained, and later identified as Defendant A.
- Officers found a bullet in Defendant A’s pocket during the encounter.
- Officers obtained consent to search Defendant A’s car.
- A firearm with an extended magazine was located underneath the driver’s seat of the car.
- From the car, officers recovered a loaded Ruger SR40 .40 caliber pistol.
- From the car, officers recovered a loaded Taurus PT 945 .45 caliber pistol.
- From the car, officers recovered a Bryco Arms J22 .22 caliber pistol.
- Approximately 97 grams of heroin were located in the car.
- Officers searched a purse belonging to a woman who was with Defendant A.
- Two .32 caliber Autauga MK II pistols were recovered from the woman’s purse.
- Defendant A possessed the three firearms recovered from the car.
- Defendant A possessed the two firearms recovered from the woman’s purse.
- All of the firearms described were manufactured outside the State of Washington and had traveled in interstate commerce.
- At the time he possessed the firearms, Defendant A knew he had prior convictions punishable by imprisonment for a term exceeding one year.
- The prior convictions included Unlawful Possession of a Firearm in the First Degree.
- The prior convictions included Attempting to Elude a Pursuing Police Vehicle.
- The plea agreement lists the prior convictions as dated December 18, 2015.
- Defendant A was taken into state custody and sentenced for unrelated Department of Corrections violations after the incident.
- Defendant A’s state sentence was scheduled to end on September 24, 2021.
- Defendant A came into federal custody on October 25, 2019.
- The government reported that the two main responding officers were terminated for serious misconduct unrelated to this case and disclosed this to the defense.
- The plea agreement provides that the Court may order restitution to any victim of the offense, as required by law.
- The plea agreement advises that the consequences of pleading guilty may include forfeiture of certain property.
- The plea agreement states that any monetary penalty imposed is due and payable immediately and requires submission of a Financial Statement of Debtor form.
- The plea agreement includes an abandonment-of-contraband provision allowing administrative disposition or destruction of illegal contraband seized from Defendant A’s control.
- The plea agreement includes a non-prosecution clause for additional offenses known to the U.S. Attorney’s Office as of the plea, arising out of the conduct giving rise to the investigation.
- The plea agreement states that by pleading guilty the defendant waived the right to a jury trial and associated trial rights.
- The plea agreement warns that pleading guilty may have immigration consequences if the defendant is not a U.S. citizen.
- The government’s memorandum states that Defendant A was arrested in this matter on April 14, 2019.

- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Count 1 (transporting a visual depiction of a minor engaged in sexually explicit conduct) and Count 2 (possession of a visual depiction of a minor engaged in sexually explicit conduct) in an Information.
- In 2010, Defendant A previously pleaded guilty in federal court to receiving child pornography under 18 U.S.C. § 2252(a)(2).
- Defendant A served a five-year imprisonment sentence for the 2010 conviction.
- Defendant A was serving a five-year term of supervised release following the 2010 conviction.
- In April 2019, law enforcement received information that someone was transporting sexually explicit images of children to Google Photos linked to an email traced to Defendant A.
- On August 13, 2019, the Kentucky Attorney General's Office and the United States Probation Office searched Defendant A's home.
- During the August 13, 2019 search, a Google Pixel phone was found hidden in a couch at the residence.
- A search of the Google Pixel phone revealed hundreds of images and videos of children engaged in sexually explicit conduct.
- The material on the phone included depictions of the rape of prepubescent children.
- The National Center for Missing and Exploited Children identified over 600 images and 17 videos as depictions of previously identified minors.
- Defendant A admitted transporting sexually explicit images of minors from Garrard County, Kentucky to Google servers located outside Kentucky.
- Defendant A admitted that on August 13, 2019 he knowingly possessed additional images and videos of minors on his Google Pixel phone.
- The Google Pixel phone was manufactured outside Kentucky and had previously been transported in interstate commerce.
- Defendant A admitted he knew the images he transported and possessed depicted minors engaged in sexually explicit conduct.
- Defendant A admitted the conduct occurred on or around the dates alleged in the Information and in the Eastern District of Kentucky.
- Due to the prior qualifying conviction, Count 1 carries a statutory imprisonment range of 15 to 40 years.
- Due to the prior qualifying conviction, Count 2 carries a statutory imprisonment range of 10 to 20 years.
- Each count carries a potential fine of up to $250,000 and a supervised release term of at least 5 years up to life.
- A mandatory special assessment of $200 applies, to be paid at the time of entry of the plea.
- An additional $5,000 special assessment under 18 U.S.C. § 3014 applies to non-indigent defendants convicted of certain Chapter 110 offenses committed between May 29, 2015 and September 30, 2021.
- Restitution will be determined at the time of sentencing.
- Defendant A waived the right to appeal any sentence of 188 months or less and retained the right to appeal any sentence greater than 188 months.
- Defendant A will be required to register as a sex offender upon release from prison as a condition of supervised release.
- Defendant A must register with the sex offender registration agency in Kentucky and in any state where he resides, is employed, or is a student, and keep the registration current.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit all interest in property listed in the forfeiture allegation of the Information.
- Defendant A agreed to complete and sign a financial disclosure statement within 30 days of pleading guilty, disclosing all assets and recent transfers.
- Defendant A agreed to submit to an examination regarding financial disclosures, which may include a polygraph examination.
- Defendant A agreed not to encumber, transfer, or dispose of assets without written approval from the United States Attorney's Office.
- If incarcerated in connection with this case, Defendant A agreed to participate in the Bureau of Prisons Inmate Financial Responsibility Program.
- Monetary penalties imposed by the Court are due and payable immediately and subject to enforcement under 18 U.S.C. § 3613.
- If the Court imposes a payment schedule, it is a minimum schedule and not a limitation on enforcement methods.
- Defendant A waived any requirement for demand of payment on fines, restitution, or assessments and agreed unpaid obligations may be submitted for Treasury offset.
- Defendant A authorized the United States to obtain his credit reports at any time in connection with enforcement of monetary penalties.
- Defendant A authorized release of any posted appearance-bond funds to satisfy financial obligations in the judgment.
- If Defendant A violates the plea agreement, the United States may void the agreement and seek an indictment for any violations of federal law.
- The plea agreement states it contains the complete and only agreement between the parties, with no other promises made.
- The plea agreement does not bind United States Attorney's Offices in other districts or other prosecuting authorities.
- Defendant A affirmed understanding of the plea agreement and that entry into the agreement was voluntary.
- The Plea Agreement was filed on January 10, 2020 in the Eastern District of Kentucky, Central Division at Lexington.
- Defendant A admitted that the Google account containing child sexual abuse material was linked to his email address.
- A defense sentencing memorandum was filed on May 23, 2020.
- The defense memorandum stated that a statutory minimum under 18 U.S.C. § 2252(b)(1) applies to Count One due to a prior qualifying conviction under 18 U.S.C. § 2252(a)(2).
- The defense memorandum indicates Defendant A expressed remorse and acknowledged recidivism.
- The defense memorandum asserts that Defendant A has never had and does not intend to have sexual contact with a child.
- The defense memorandum states that Defendant A maintained long-term employment while on supervised release following his prior conviction.
- The defense memorandum states that Defendant A maintained a strong relationship with his mother despite strain from his offenses and prior conviction.
- The defense memorandum notes that Defendant A was previously incarcerated at FCI Elkton.
- The defense memorandum references a supervised release revocation case numbered 5:10-cr-00060-DCR-1 in connection with Defendant A's prior conviction.

- Defendant A was charged by Indictment with one count of Felon in Possession of a Firearm in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(1).
- Defendant A entered a guilty plea as charged on October 30, 2020.
- As of November 17, 2019, Defendant A had prior felony convictions for Unlawful Possession of a Firearm, Assault in the Third Degree (domestic violence), and Felony Harassment (domestic violence).
- Defendant A pled guilty to those three state offenses on October 5, 2011.
- On October 28, 2011, Defendant A was sentenced to 12 months and a day in prison on his state cases and served that sentence.
- Defendant A was informed in court in 2011 that his convictions prevented him from lawfully possessing a firearm and he signed a document acknowledging that fact.
- On November 17, 2019, a Seattle Police Department officer looked through the window of a car rented by Defendant A and saw a handgun in open view in the rear passenger-side map pocket.
- The vehicle at issue was a rental car in Defendant A’s name.
- Officer observation of the firearm in the car occurred in the Capitol Hill area of Seattle in the early morning hours of November 17, 2019.
- The same car was later found parked in a lot in an area known for high crime, gang, and drug activity.
- After approximately thirty minutes, Defendant A approached the parked car and used a key fob; officers contacted him as he opened the driver’s door.
- Defendant A identified himself to officers at the car on November 17, 2019.
- Officers determined on scene that Defendant A was a convicted felon and arrested him for unlawful possession of a firearm.
- In a search incident to arrest, officers recovered a Glock handgun with an extended magazine from Defendant A’s waistband.
- The Glock recovered from Defendant A’s person on November 17, 2019 was loaded with 25 rounds plus one in the chamber.
- Defendant A admitted that on November 17, 2019 he knowingly possessed a Glock 9mm handgun with an extended magazine.
- Defendant A admitted that the handgun he possessed had been shipped or transported in interstate or foreign commerce before his possession.
- At the time he possessed the handgun on November 17, 2019, Defendant A knew he had been convicted of a crime punishable by more than one year of imprisonment.
- A firearms examiner confirmed that the Glock pistol was a firearm under federal law and that it was manufactured in Austria and imported into the United States by a Georgia-based importer.
- Defendant A told officers on scene that the gun in the back seat belonged to his cousin.
- Officers impounded the car pending a search warrant after arresting Defendant A on November 17, 2019.
- An individual later approached the impounded car during the tow and tried to access the area where the handgun had been seen.
- Police obtained and executed a search warrant on the car associated with Defendant A.
- A second Glock handgun with an extended magazine was recovered from the rear of the car pursuant to the search warrant.
- The Glock found in the rear of the car was reported stolen.
- The Glock found in the rear of the car was loaded with 12 rounds including one in the chamber.
- Another loaded Glock magazine was found in the car’s glove box.
- A large knife was recovered from the car associated with Defendant A.
- Twenty-six Visa gift cards were recovered from the car associated with Defendant A.
- ATF examination confirmed that both Glock pistols were manufactured in Austria and imported into the United States by a Georgia-based importer.
- The firearm recovered from Defendant A’s person was test-fired and the casing was submitted to NIBIN, resulting in a presumptive investigative lead linking it to a September 23, 2019 drive-by shooting.
- Defendant A has multiple felony convictions prosecuted under a single state cause number referenced in the case record.
- In 2011, Defendant A was advised that his state crimes were punishable by more than one year and acknowledged he was ineligible to possess a firearm.
- On November 16, 2019, Defendant A posted a video to social media in which he admitted affiliation with the Deuce-8 gang.
- On October 3, 2019, Defendant A posted a photo to social media depicting a mask, Nike Air Force One shoes, and a handgun.
- Upon booking in this case, Defendant A told U.S. Marshals he was a Deuce-8 member.
- Defendant A has been shot three times, most recently in 2018, in incidents described as gang-related.
- In a December 9, 2019 jail call, Defendant A discussed plans to assault an unidentified person when released and referenced a possible informant.
- Defendant A was initially charged by Complaint and made his Initial Appearance on January 10, 2020, at which time he was detained.
- A grand jury returned a one-count Indictment for Felon in Possession of a Firearm on January 16, 2020.
- At the time of the November 17, 2019 offense, Defendant A was under supervision on prior state cases, according to the case record narrative.
- The government’s case narrative reports that after release on prior state cases, Defendant A violated supervision by failing to participate in domestic-violence treatment, absconding, using drugs, selling bunk, failing drug treatment, and changing addresses without permission.
- The government’s case narrative reports that Defendant A committed an obstruction offense while still on supervision prior to this case.
- Defendant A’s first prior gun offense occurred when he was approximately 19 and involved carrying a loaded concealed handgun at a bus stop after having been on school property earlier.
- In a separate prior incident, Defendant A assaulted Victim A by pulling Victim A to the floor by hair, kicking, dragging, holding by the throat, and placing a gun to Victim A’s head while threatening to kill.
- The handgun displayed in the prior domestic-violence incident was later recovered and was loaded.
- On November 17, 2019, Defendant A acknowledged the presence of a second person who attempted to get into the car around the time of his detention, according to his plea factual basis.
- According to the case narrative, the attempt by another individual to access the car occurred about 40 minutes after Defendant A’s arrest, during the tow process.
- Before parking, the car associated with Defendant A slowed as if to park and then sped away upon noticing an officer’s presence, according to the case narrative.

- Defendant will plead guilty to Count Two charging making false statements in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1001.
- Defendant admitted he committed the false-statement offense in Count Two.
- The maximum term of imprisonment for the offense is 5 years.
- The maximum term of supervised release is 3 years.
- The maximum fine is $250,000.
- A mandatory special assessment of $100 applies.
- Forfeiture is applicable to the extent charged in the indictment.
- Defendant agreed to a stipulated judicial order of removal under 8 U.S.C. §§ 1228(c)(5) and 1227.
- Defendant admitted he is a native and citizen of the People’s Republic of China and is removable under 8 U.S.C. § 1227.
- Defendant waived the notice and hearing provided by 8 U.S.C. § 1228(c)(2) regarding removability.
- Defendant waived rights to appeal, reopen, reconsider, or otherwise challenge the stipulated removal order.
- Defendant waived the right to a hearing before an immigration judge on the question of removability.
- Defendant waived the right to examine evidence, present evidence, and cross-examine witnesses in removal proceedings.
- Defendant waived eligibility for asylum as a form of relief from removal.
- Defendant waived eligibility for withholding of removal under 8 U.S.C. § 1231(b)(3).
- Defendant waived eligibility for protection under the Convention Against Torture, including withholding or deferral under 8 C.F.R. § 208.
- Defendant waived eligibility for cancellation of removal.
- Defendant waived eligibility for adjustment of status.
- Defendant waived eligibility for registry relief.
- Defendant waived de novo review of denial or revocation of temporary protected status.
- Defendant waived waivers under 8 U.S.C. §§ 1182(h) and 1182(i).
- Defendant waived relief via visa petitions and consular processing in connection with removal proceedings.
- Defendant waived eligibility for voluntary departure as a form of relief from removal.
- Defendant stated he has not been persecuted in the PRC and has no present fear of persecution on protected grounds.
- Defendant stated he has not been tortured in the PRC and has no present fear of torture there.
- Defendant requested that the Court issue an order for his removal to the PRC and agreed to accept a written order of removal as final.
- Defendant agreed to make the judicial order of removal a public document and waived related privacy rights, including under 8 C.F.R. § 208.6.
- Immigration and Customs Enforcement concurred with the request for a judicial order of removal.
- Upon completion of the criminal proceedings, including any incarceration and supervision, defendant shall be removed to the PRC.
- Defendant agreed to assist ICE in executing his removal, including procuring travel documents and meeting with representatives of the destination country.
- Defendant agreed to execute forms, applications, or waivers needed to execute or expedite his removal.
- Defendant acknowledged that failure to assist ICE in removal would breach the plea agreement and may subject him to penalties under 8 U.S.C. § 1253.
- Defendant acknowledged that the judicial order of removal renders him inadmissible to the United States for at least ten years.
- Defendant agreed not to enter, attempt to enter, or transit through the United States during the inadmissibility period without prior permission from the Department of Homeland Security.
- The plea agreement states that if the Court fails to enter the judicial order of removal, the plea agreement is null and void.
- Defendant waived and released any claims to vehicles, currency, or other personal property seized during the investigation and consented to forfeiture of those assets.
- The plea agreement does not affect any civil liability, including tax liability, arising from the conduct.
- Defendant acknowledged the plea agreement after it was read to him in his native language and signed it on 12/01/2020.
- Defendant stated he is satisfied with his legal representation and is entering the plea voluntarily because he is guilty.
- Defendant came to the United States on a student visa to conduct cancer research at a hospital-affiliated laboratory.
- Defendant removed material from his laboratory without his supervisors’ knowledge and placed it in his luggage for a flight to China.
- When questioned at the airport, defendant lied about what he had in his luggage.
- Subsequent analysis concluded the materials taken were DNA expression vectors, likely made by defendant.
- The DNA expression vectors involved had little financial value according to the analysis described.
- Expression vectors of the type described can be made inexpensively by someone with limited biomedical training.
- Defendant has no prior criminal history as represented in the sentencing materials.
- Defendant served approximately three months in jail prior to sentencing.
- Defendant’s false statement occurred when he answered “no” to whether he had biological materials in his suitcase at Logan Airport.
- Defendant was originally charged with a smuggling count predicated on alleged failure to package biological materials under hazardous materials regulations.
- A defense motion to dismiss the smuggling count argued the cited regulations did not cover non-hazardous biological materials lacking any listed hazardous substance.
- The defense asserted it was not alleged that the materials in defendant’s luggage contained any of the listed hazardous substances.
- Defendant was not convicted of the smuggling charge.
- Defendant was never charged with theft of laboratory materials.
- Defendant denies that his purpose in taking the materials was anything other than to continue his research.
- Defendant worked as a medical doctor and biomedical researcher before this case.
- Defendant spent over seven months on home confinement following his initial detention, per defense submission.
- Defendant is 31 years old, per defense submission.
- Defendant studied medicine at Sun Yat-Sen University and received his medical degree in 2017.
- Defendant came to a laboratory at a Boston academic medical center in August 2018 as part of his Ph.D. studies, with an appointment scheduled to end in August 2020.
- Before coming to the United States, defendant co-authored nine peer‑reviewed publications, including six as co‑first author, focused on basic cancer research.
- At the time of his arrest, defendant was listed as co‑first author on two papers from his lab submitted to a high‑impact journal for review.
- While a student, defendant won table tennis competitions, placing first in two and second in another.
- Defendant participated in a chorus that won first prize in a competition during his studies.
- Defendant took time off from school to help care for his father when his father had cancer, which motivated his interest in cancer research.
- Defendant’s spouse is also a doctor and works as a postdoctoral researcher at a U.S. federal research institute.
- Defendant was permitted to relocate to Maryland in October and has lived with his spouse since then, per defense submission.
- Defendant’s spouse’s appointment runs through September 2021, after which she plans to return to China, per defense submission.

- The case is in the Northern District of Illinois, Eastern Division, before Judge Elaine E. Bucklo under case number 1:20-cr-00035.
- An indictment charged Defendant A with 20 counts including four counts of mail fraud, two counts of aggravated identity theft, and fourteen counts of submitting a false claim.
- The indictment was filed on January 16, 2020.
- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Count One (mail fraud) and Count Five (aggravated identity theft).
- The plea agreement was signed on April 19, 2022 and filed on May 25, 2022.
- A sentencing hearing was scheduled for August 17, 2022 at 10:30 a.m.
- A Presentence Investigation Report was filed on July 12, 2022 as ECF No. 76.
- The government filed its sentencing memorandum on August 1, 2022.
- The defense filed a sentencing memorandum on July 29, 2022.
- Count One carries a maximum sentence of 20 years’ imprisonment and up to a $250,000 fine or twice the gross gain or loss, and up to three years of supervised release.
- Count Five carries a mandatory sentence of 2 years’ imprisonment that must run consecutive to any other sentence, with up to a $250,000 fine or twice the gross gain or loss, and up to one year of supervised release; probation is not permitted on this count.
- The total statutory maximum under the two counts of conviction is 22 years’ imprisonment, and the statutory minimum is 2 years’ imprisonment.
- A $100 special assessment applies to each count of conviction, totaling $200.
- Defendant A acknowledged that the Court must order restitution to the victims in an amount determined by the Court.
- Defendant A agreed to pay restitution totaling $221,923 to the U.S. Treasury.
- The government agreed to move to dismiss the remaining counts after sentence is imposed on the counts of conviction.
- From at least January 2013 through at least July 2017, Defendant A participated in a scheme to defraud the IRS using the mails in the Northern District of Illinois and elsewhere.
- Defendant A obtained personal identifying information of real individuals without authorization, including names, dates of birth, and identification documents such as foreign passports or driver’s licenses.
- Defendant A used the individuals’ PII to prepare and mail Forms W‑7 to apply for ITINs in their names.
- Defendant A purchased computer software and laser paper to create false Forms W‑2 reporting fictitious wages and withholding.
- Defendant A used tax preparation software to prepare false individual income tax returns.
- Defendant A prepared and filed false Forms 1040A and 1040EZ in his name and in the names of other individuals.
- For tax years 2011 to 2016, Defendant A filed false individual income tax returns using individuals’ PII without their knowledge or consent.
- The false tax returns claimed wage income and federal income tax withheld that Defendant A knew was not earned or withheld.
- Defendant A prepared and filed at least 77 false tax returns.
- The fraudulent returns claimed at least $328,582 in refunds.
- Of the $328,582 in claimed refunds, $221,923 was paid by the United States via Treasury checks and direct deposits into accounts accessible to Defendant A in the Northern District of Illinois.
- Defendant A directed that tax refunds be sent to U.S. Post Office Boxes and other mailing addresses he controlled.
- Defendant A cashed or caused others to cash the fraudulent Treasury checks at small grocery stores or currency exchanges in the Northern District of Illinois and elsewhere.
- Defendant A also directed that some fraudulent refunds be deposited into bank accounts he could access in the Northern District of Illinois.
- On January 22, 2015, Defendant A caused a false 2012 Form 1040EZ to be mailed to the IRS Center in Fresno, California as part of the scheme.
- The 2012 false return used the identity of Victim A and claimed a $2,519 refund.
- The false return claimed $20,300 in wages and $3,670 in federal tax withheld for Victim A, which Defendant A knew to be false.
- The refund from the false 2012 return was directed to a U.S. Post Office Box in Defendant A’s name.
- Fingerprints of Defendant A were found on the false 2012 Form 1040EZ and attached Form W‑2 submitted for Victim A.
- On or about that same date, Defendant A knowingly used Victim A’s name without consent to commit the mail fraud offense charged in Count One.
- The claimed refund for Victim A’s 2012 false return was mailed to a Post Office Box controlled by Defendant A.
- IRS forensic examiners determined Defendant A’s fingerprints were conclusively present on 23 false tax returns.
- Defendant A maintained control of at least ten U.S. Post Office Boxes, either opening them himself or directing others to open them, to receive false refund checks.
- Defendant A cashed the majority of the refund checks at a small family-owned grocery store in Mundelein, Illinois.
- Victim A is a lawful permanent resident residing in Charlotte, North Carolina and has never met Defendant A.
- Defendant A used Victim A’s PII to obtain an ITIN and filed a false 2012 return claiming a $2,519 refund, which was mailed to a P.O. Box he controlled.
- Victim B resides in Bremen, Indiana and is a Honduran citizen who traveled to the United States in 2012 with a valid U.S. visa.
- Defendant A used Victim B’s PII to obtain an ITIN and filed a false 2014 return claiming a $4,222 refund, which was mailed to a P.O. Box he controlled.
- The false tax returns were linked to Defendant A in part through fingerprint evidence.
- Defendant A’s criminal conduct included both intended loss of $328,582 and actual loss of $221,923.
- Defendant A acknowledged having zero criminal history points as of the plea agreement.
- Defendant A agreed to pay the $200 special assessment at the time of sentencing.
- Defendant A consented to disclosure of his future individual income tax returns to the Probation Office and the U.S. Attorney’s Office during any term of supervision for monitoring restitution and fines.
- Defendant A agreed to cooperate with collection of any unpaid fine and restitution, including by providing financial statements and records upon request.
- Defendant A was 31 years old at the time of the defense sentencing memorandum and was born in Juticalpa, Honduras.
- Defendant A moved to North Carolina at age 11 to live with his mother, stepfather, and half-sister.
- Defendant A reported experiencing abuse by his stepfather and left home at age 14 for a period.
- Defendant A moved to Chicago at age 19.
- Defendant A married Spouse A in 2016.
- Spouse A worked for the Transportation Security Administration at O’Hare International Airport and had a knee injury requiring surgery.
- Defendant A and Spouse A have a minor child (Child A) who was beginning kindergarten in fall 2022.
- Defendant A, Spouse A, and Child A resided together in Waukegan, Illinois at the time of the defense memorandum.
- Defendant A reported no gang affiliation and no history of firearm ownership.
- Defendant A reported good physical health and no diagnosed mental or emotional health problems.
- Defendant A reported no history of substance abuse and rarely consumes alcohol; a June 14, 2022 urinalysis was negative.
- Defendant A was working toward obtaining his GED at the College of Lake County.
- Defendant A worked full-time as a certified forklift operator at an employer in Antioch, Illinois, earning $17 per hour, and also worked weekends at a second job in Gurnee earning $25 per hour.
- Defendant A’s work history reflected consistent employment, often in more than one job.
- Defendant A is not a United States citizen and lacked lawful immigration status in the United States at the time of the memorandum.
- Defendant A reported concerns about returning to Honduras due to threats communicated to his relatives and reported to Honduran police.
- The government stated that the plea included an agreed intended loss of $328,582 and an actual loss of $221,923 for the scheme.
- Defendant A agreed that restitution in the amount of $221,923 is appropriate.
- The defense sentencing memorandum described that Defendant A had accepted responsibility for his conduct.

- Defendant A pleaded guilty to Counts 1, 11, 31, and 34 of the indictment.
- At sentencing, the government agreed to dismiss Counts 2–10, 12–30, 32–33, and 35–40 as to Defendant A.
- Conduct covered by the non-prosecution provision includes illegal prescriptions and kickbacks from 2014 to January 2020 and a mortgage-loan document submission known to the government at the time.
- Count 1 is a conspiracy charge under 18 U.S.C. § 371 related to controlled substances distribution conduct.
- Count 11 is a charge under 21 U.S.C. § 843(a)(3) for obtaining controlled substances by fraud or deception.
- Count 31 is a charge under 42 U.S.C. § 1320a-7b(b)(1)(B) for soliciting and receiving illegal kickbacks for referrals paid by federal health care programs.
- Count 34 is a health care fraud charge under 18 U.S.C. § 1347 involving a scheme to obtain money from health benefit programs by false representations.
- Defendant A is a doctor of chiropractic medicine licensed in Missouri and has never been licensed as a medical doctor in the United States.
- Since about April 2014, Defendant A owned, operated, or managed health care businesses including API, IPM, American Pain Clinic, Midwest Medical Marketing, LLC, and the Institute for Regenerative Medicine.
- Co-Defendant Physician A was a licensed medical doctor who owned or directed businesses including American Pain Institute (API) and IPM.
- Co-Defendant Physician B was a licensed medical doctor who owned or directed businesses including IPM, AMA Physician Group, and Central Diagnostic Laboratory (CDL).
- Co-Defendant C operated businesses including Doctors on the Go entities and had never been licensed as a medical doctor.
- The conspirators used their health care businesses to create prescriptions for controlled substances outside the scope of professional medical practice and without a legitimate medical purpose.
- Defendant A knew that the physicians had not examined the individuals whose names appeared on the controlled substance prescriptions and that there was no determination of medical need.
- Defendant A knew pharmacies would submit reimbursement claims to Medicare and Medicaid for medications obtained through the use of the illegal prescriptions.
- The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services administers the Medicare program, and Medicare Part B reimburses providers for covered outpatient services.
- Wisconsin Physicians Service Insurance Corporation (WPS) is the Medicare Part B Medicare Administrative Contractor for Eastern Missouri and processed reimbursement claims for the relevant entities.
- Medicare providers must execute an agreement obligating them to follow Medicare regulations and rules to receive reimbursement, and are assigned a provider number for billing.
- Compliance with the Anti-Kickback Statute is a condition of payment for both Medicare and Medicaid claims.
- The Anti-Kickback Statute criminalizes knowingly and willfully soliciting, offering, paying, or receiving remuneration to induce referrals for items or services paid by federal health care programs, and remunerations can include money, goods, or services.
- Between 2012 and 2016, Defendant A and co-defendants signed Medicare enrollment applications that included warnings about criminal penalties for fraud and false statements.
- In October 2013, Defendant A completed an individual Medicare provider enrollment application, and in March 2014, he completed an application as the "DC/Managing Owner" of API, agreeing to abide by Medicare laws and the anti-kickback statute and not to submit false claims.
- MO HealthNet administers the Missouri Medicaid program, which reimburses providers for covered services to low-income recipients.
- Between December 20, 2013 and May 9, 2016, Defendant A and certain co-defendants signed Missouri Medicaid provider agreements agreeing to comply with applicable rules and regulations in delivering services and submitting claims.
- Schedule II controlled substances referenced in the case include oxycodone, hydrocodone, methadone, morphine, hydromorphone, oxymorphone, fentanyl, and amphetamines.
- Federal regulation 21 C.F.R. 1306.04(a) requires controlled substance prescriptions to be issued for a legitimate medical purpose by a practitioner acting in the usual course of professional practice.
- Missouri rules prohibit doctors from signing blank prescriptions and require a physician-patient relationship and sufficient examination before prescribing.
- In February 2014, Defendant A and Co-Defendant Physician A opened API, a multilevel pain management clinic providing chiropractic and medical services.
- An August 2014 Medicaid application identified Defendant A as an 82% owner of API and Co-Defendant Physician A as an 18% owner, with the physician as medical director and Defendant A as day-to-day manager.
- On March 15, 2016, WPS notified Defendant A that his and API’s Medicare billing privileges were revoked due to a false statement on an earlier Medicare application.
- In April 2016, IPM was opened with Co-Defendant Physician B and Defendant A’s spouse identified as owners; in December 2017, Defendant A registered IPM in Missouri listing his mother as organizer and himself as "Chief of Staff."
- IPM operated out of several locations within St. Louis County at times, including Woodfield Lane, Old Tesson Ferry Road, and Grandview Plaza.
- Co-Defendant Physician B served as medical director of IPM from April 2016 to 2018, and Co-Defendant Physician A served as medical director at various times; they supervised IPM’s nurse practitioners.
- Defendant A knew that pre-signing controlled substance prescriptions without proper examination violated laws and regulations requiring physician-patient relationships and sufficient examinations.
- Defendant A knew that IPM staff, including nurse practitioners and chiropractors, were not authorized to prescribe controlled substances under state or federal law.
- At IPM, the physicians were typically present one day a week for about two hours to pre-sign stacks of controlled substance prescriptions without seeing, examining, or evaluating the patients on those dates.
- The physicians rarely reviewed patient charts before pre-signing prescriptions and did not determine a legitimate medical need for controlled substances before pre-signing.
- Controlled substance prescriptions were pre-signed for patients who repeatedly tested negative for the prescribed drugs but positive for non-prescribed or illegal controlled substances.
- Defendant A encouraged practitioners to prescribe controlled substances to patients despite drug screen results indicating diversion or illegal drug use.
- Defendant A used and signed physicians’ names on controlled substance prescriptions despite not being a licensed medical doctor and lacking authority to order such medications.
- Defendant A solicited and received money or other things of value from individuals in exchange for fraudulent controlled substance prescriptions for persons without a physician-patient relationship with the listed prescriber.
- Defendant A met about twice a month at IPM with Associate A and others, received lists of names to place on fraudulent prescriptions, and provided those prescriptions for pickup.
- Associates recruited or identified people to be listed as patients on fraudulent prescriptions, who were relatives or acquaintances and had no doctor-patient relationship with the prescriber.
- A physician was listed as the prescriber on many fraudulent prescriptions despite not having seen or examined the named individuals.
- Associate A typically paid Defendant A between $200 and $500 per controlled substance prescription and about $4,000 to $5,000 per pickup of fraudulent prescriptions.
- Fraudulent prescriptions were filled at local pharmacies including Walgreens and Shop 'n Save locations.
- Defendant A instructed clinic staff to tell pharmacies that the prescriptions were legitimate despite knowing they were fraudulent.
- On February 23, 2016, Defendant A formed Midwest Medical Marketing, LLC to refer urine specimens to laboratories including Central Diagnostic Laboratory (CDL).
- An agreement existed that CDL would pay Midwest Medical Marketing for each referred urine specimen it received.
- On April 30, 2017, CDL issued a $7,840 check to Midwest Medical Marketing with a memo indicating marketing fees for 224 specimens at $35 each, and Defendant A knew the payment for referrals was illegal.
- On July 17, 2017, CDL wrote a $1,200 check to Employee A of Co-Defendant Physician A, with one purpose being to induce referrals of specimens to CDL.
- Defendant A knew CDL would submit reimbursement claims to Medicare and Medicaid for tests performed on referred specimens and that those programs would not pay for services obtained by illegal kickbacks.
- The parties stipulated that CDL paid at least $150,795 to Midwest Medical Marketing as bribes for referred specimens.
- The parties stipulated that the anticipated benefit to CDL from referred specimens was at least $861,600 in federal health care program reimbursements.
- The parties stipulated that federal health care programs suffered a loss of $4,718,561 as a result of the conspiracy and fraud scheme.
- The stipulated loss amount is based on reimbursements paid to CDL for tests and reimbursements paid to pharmacies for fraudulent prescriptions.
- Between about 2014 and 2019, the conspirators possessed or caused to be possessed various controlled substances totaling 130,470 pills/units across schedules II–IV, with a combined converted weight listed as 12,561 kilograms of marijuana equivalent.
- For relevant conduct, Defendant A is accountable for 97,867 oxycodone pills prescribed for API and IPM patients and distributed outside professional practice and without a legitimate medical purpose between 2014 and 2019.
- The 97,867 oxycodone pills attributable to Defendant A are stated to be equivalent to 9,747.25 kilograms of marijuana for conversion purposes in the materials.
- The statutory maximum penalty for Count 1 (conspiracy under 18 U.S.C. § 371) is 5 years imprisonment, a fine up to $250,000, and up to 3 years of supervised release.
- The statutory maximum penalty for Count 11 (21 U.S.C. § 843(a)(3)) is 4 years imprisonment, a fine up to $250,000, and up to 3 years of supervised release.
- The statutory maximum penalty for Count 31 (42 U.S.C. § 1320a-7b(b)) is 10 years imprisonment, a fine up to $100,000, and up to 3 years of supervised release.
- The statutory maximum penalty for Count 34 (18 U.S.C. § 1347) is 10 years imprisonment, a fine up to $250,000, and up to 3 years of supervised release.
- A mandatory special assessment of $100 per count, totaling $400 for the four counts of conviction, applies and Defendant A agreed to pay it at sentencing.
- Defendant A agreed that restitution is mandatory and to provide full restitution to all victims of all charges in the indictment, with the stipulated loss amount to be included in restitution.
- Defendant A acknowledged understanding and waiving trial rights, including the right to plead not guilty, to a jury trial, to confront witnesses, and to remain silent, by entering the guilty plea.
- The plea agreement document reflects execution by counsel and the government in mid-January 2021.
- Defendant A used businesses including API and IPM to facilitate creating and distributing prescriptions outside professional practice for controlled substances.
- Pharmacies submitted reimbursement claims for medications obtained with the fraudulent prescriptions to government health care programs.
- Defendant A’s Medicare billing privileges with API were revoked due to a false statement on an earlier Medicare application.
- Defendant A managed the day-to-day operations of IPM, similar to his role at API.
- Defendant A and others agreed that CDL would pay Midwest Medical Marketing per referred urine specimen, and such payments were made by CDL to that entity.
- The parties stipulated that government health care programs including Medicare, Medicaid, and Tricare were among the payors harmed by the scheme.
- The fraudulent controlled substance prescriptions were sometimes presented to pharmacies along with assurances from clinic staff, at Defendant A’s direction, that the prescriptions were legitimate.
- Defendant A agreed to a restitution framework that includes reimbursements paid for laboratory tests and for controlled substance prescriptions obtained through fraud and kickbacks.
- The scheme involved the issuance of prescriptions without establishing physician-patient relationships or conducting sufficient examinations, contrary to professional standards.
- A portion of the scheme’s fraudulent reimbursements flowed through laboratory testing on referred urine specimens, which were tied to payments from government health care programs.
- Associate payments for prescriptions involved a per-prescription fee structure and periodic lump-sum transfers upon receipt of multiple fraudulent prescriptions.
- Prescription pre-signing took place on a scheduled basis (about one day per week for two hours) rather than in response to patient examinations on the days prescriptions were executed or delivered.
- Defendant A remained licensed as a chiropractor at the time of the 2025 sentencing memorandum filing, according to his counsel.
- According to the defense filing, Defendant A had diagnoses including high blood pressure, type 2 diabetes, chronic back muscle spasms (2019), arthropathy of spinal facet disorder, hypogonadism, and chronic tension-type headache disorder (2020).
- The defense filing states that Defendant A was prescribed medications including hydrocodone, alprazolam, cyclobenzaprine, lisinopril, and gabapentin during the relevant period.
- As of March 2026, defense counsel reported that Defendant A’s pretrial release had been revoked following a DWI arrest.
- As of the March 2026 filing, defense counsel reported that Defendant A was being held in a local county jail pending sentencing.
- The March 2026 defense filing asserts that federal agents recently conducted a raid at Defendant A’s business, during which an associate was briefly detained.

- The parties entered a plea agreement under Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 11(c)(1)(B).
- Defendant waived indictment and agreed to plead guilty to a Superseding Information.
- Counts 1–10 charge wire fraud in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1343.
- Counts 11–14 charge bank fraud in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1344.
- Counts 15–18 charge making false statements to a bank in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1014.
- Counts 19–23 charge filing false tax returns in violation of 26 U.S.C. § 7206(1).
- Defendant admitted committing the crimes specified in each count and stated he is guilty of each one.
- Each wire fraud count carries a maximum term of 20 years’ imprisonment.
- Each wire fraud count allows up to 3 years of supervised release.
- Each wire fraud count allows a fine up to $250,000 or twice the gross gain or loss, whichever is higher.
- Each wire fraud count requires a $100 special assessment and may include restitution and forfeiture.
- Each bank fraud count carries a maximum term of 30 years’ imprisonment.
- Each bank fraud count allows up to 5 years of supervised release.
- Each bank fraud count allows a fine up to $1,000,000 or twice the gross gain or loss, whichever is higher.
- Each bank fraud count requires a $100 special assessment and may include restitution and forfeiture.
- Each false statements to a bank count carries a maximum term of 30 years’ imprisonment.
- Each false statements to a bank count allows up to 5 years of supervised release.
- Each false statements to a bank count allows a fine up to $1,000,000 or twice the gross gain or loss, whichever is higher.
- Each false statements to a bank count requires a $100 special assessment and may include restitution.
- Each filing false tax return count carries a maximum term of 3 years’ imprisonment.
- Each filing false tax return count allows up to 1 year of supervised release.
- Each filing false tax return count allows a fine up to $250,000 or twice the gross gain or loss, whichever is higher.
- Each filing false tax return count requires a $100 special assessment and may include restitution.
- The government agreed not to charge obstruction of justice under 18 U.S.C. § 1503 arising from the stream of benefits from the Billerica business owner.
- The government agreed not to charge extortion under color of official right under 18 U.S.C. § 1951 arising from the stream of benefits from the Billerica business owner, including two payments.
- The non-prosecution commitment regarding obstruction and extortion was limited to facts known to the government as of the plea agreement date.
- Defendant understood that the Court would enter an order of forfeiture as part of the sentence.
- Forfeiture could include substitute assets and/or a money judgment equal to the value of proceeds or property involved in the offense.
- The parties agreed that the United States would not seek forfeiture of Defendant’s current residence in Lowell, Massachusetts.
- The United States reserved the right to pursue proceeds of any future sale of the residence to satisfy outstanding financial obligations including forfeiture and/or restitution.
- Defendant agreed to provide a sworn financial statement disclosing assets within 30 days after signing the plea agreement if requested by the government.
- Defendant signed the acknowledgment of the plea agreement on February 23, 2021.
- Defendant served as a state representative for approximately 16 years.
- Defendant served as Chair of the House Committee on Ethics.
- Defendant served as Chair of the Steering, Policy, and Scheduling Committee.
- By 2018, Defendant held a leadership position as a Second Division Chair on the House Speaker’s team.
- On September 7, 2016, Defendant met with the Office of Campaign and Political Finance regarding his per diem usage.
- OCPF calculated that to justify the collected per diems, Defendant would have had to drive over 40,000 miles in two years.
- Defendant collected approximately $8,000 in per diem payments in the two years preceding the OCPF meeting.
- From 2014 to 2016, the state legislature paid per diem rates of approximately $26 per day for legislators’ expenses.
- From 2017 forward, the state legislature paid a lump sum of $15,000 per legislator for miles, meals, lodging, and office expenses.
- Defendant used campaign funds to pay for private golf club dues.
- Defendant used campaign funds to pay for meals and trips to casinos.
- Defendant arranged campaign payments to purported consultants who cashed the checks and returned cash to him.
- On June 5, 2019, an FBI agent and an IRS agent met with Defendant at his home in Lowell.
- Agents possessed a screenshot from a May 2, 2018 video at Mohegan Sun casino showing payment to a “ten percenter” to collect slot machine winnings.
- Using a “ten percenter” caused the casino to issue a Form W-2G in the other person’s name instead of Defendant’s.
- Agents had evidence of multiple years of false federal tax returns by Defendant.
- Agents had evidence of bank fraud and theft from campaign funds.
- During the June 5, 2019 meeting, Defendant asked if he would be arrested that night and was told no.
- Defendant received a $10,000 check in December 2014 from a Billerica business owner for purported consulting.
- Defendant received a $17,000 check in February 2015 from the same Billerica business owner for purported consulting.
- On January 6, 2015, Defendant exchanged text messages with the Billerica business owner regarding legislative progress on a bill.
- Defendant advanced an amendment to House Bill H4553 that would create tax credits for developers in Boston’s Seaport area.
- A client of the Billerica business owner was a prospective developer who would benefit from the tax credit amendment.
- Defendant used the $27,000 in payments from the Billerica business owner to obtain a mortgage from a Lowell bank.
- From 2015 to 2018, Defendant continued to help move iterations of the tax credit bill through the state House.
- Defendant received free stays at an oceanfront vacation property associated with the Billerica business owner.
- The oceanfront property rented at approximately $7,500 per week.
- Defendant received thousands of dollars in political donations from the Billerica business owner and associates.
- In June 2019, Defendant received a target letter stating he was the target of a federal grand jury investigation.
- By June 2019, witnesses were being called to the grand jury and documents were being subpoenaed in the investigation.
- Believing his phone was tapped, Defendant used a mutual friend to relay to the Billerica business owner that authorities were looking into the checks.
- Defendant and the Billerica business owner created a backdated employment contract claiming the $27,000 was for real estate services.
- In late June 2019, Defendant and the Billerica business owner met in a mutual friend’s garage in Lowell and signed two copies of the backdated contract.
- The backdated contract initially stymied the grand jury investigation for a period of time.
- The presentence report indicated that the fraud loss exceeded $15,000.
- The presentence report indicated that the tax loss was more than $15,000 but not more than $40,000.

- Defendant A pled guilty to Counts One and Two of an Information charging aiding and abetting visa fraud in violation of 18 U.S.C. §§ 1546(a) and 2.
- Each count of conviction carries a statutory maximum of 10 years of imprisonment.
- Each count of conviction carries a fine of up to $250,000 or twice the gross gain or loss, whichever is greater.
- Each count of conviction carries up to three years of supervised release.
- A special assessment of $100 is mandatory for each felony count of conviction.
- Defendant A agreed to waive indictment and proceed by Information.
- Defendant A is a citizen of the United Kingdom and a lawful permanent resident of the United States.
- In 2014, the Orlando HSI Document and Benefit Fraud Task Force received information from USCIS FDNS regarding a suspected visa fraud facilitator.
- The referral indicated Defendant A was suspected of preparing fraudulent L‑1A and E‑2 visa petitions.
- Between 2013 and 2020, agents identified at least 115 E‑2 and L‑1A visa petitions associated with Defendant A and his companies.
- Defendant A’s companies included Decar Enterprises Inc. and Business Connexions Inc.
- Defendant A was the sole employee of Decar Enterprises Inc. and Business Connexions Inc.
- Since at least 2010, Defendant A offered services for preparing immigration benefits applications, including E‑2 and L‑1A visas.
- Defendant A input false and fraudulent information into USCIS immigration forms for clients.
- Defendant A requested client documents such as business tax returns, W‑2s, organizational charts, business letters, resumes, earnings statements, payroll statements, diplomas, and cover letters to support applications.
- Defendant A altered and manipulated documents received from clients and attached the resulting fraudulent documents to immigration applications.
- Defendant A did not list his name as preparer on the applications to distance himself from the fraudulent filings.
- Defendant A provided completed applications to clients and instructed them to mail the applications to USCIS processing centers in Vermont, California, and Texas.
- On or about November 24, 2015, Client A mailed a USCIS Form I‑129 petition for an L‑1 nonimmigrant worker from Florida to a USCIS service center in Vermont.
- Defendant A assisted Client A in completing the L‑1 application and creating the attached supporting documents.
- The L‑1 application for Client A contained false statements regarding the number of employees, gross annual income, and net annual income.
- Client A’s application packet included a fraudulent business letter, fraudulent W‑2 forms, a fake business organization chart, and other fraudulent documents.
- On or about March 6, 2019, Client B mailed a USCIS Form I‑140 petition from Florida to a USCIS service center in Texas.
- Defendant A assisted Client B in completing the application and creating the attached supporting documents.
- The application for Client B contained false statements regarding the number of employees, gross annual income, and net annual income.
- Client B’s application packet included a fraudulent business letter, fraudulent bank checking statements, fraudulent W‑2 forms, a false IRS Employer’s Quarterly Federal Tax Return, and other fraudulent documents.
- To initiate an E‑2 visa, a qualifying enterprise must file a USCIS Form I‑129 with a cover letter describing the enterprise and the applicant.
- Typical E‑2 supporting documents include business formation records, evidence of investment and its substantiality, payroll and tax records showing the enterprise is not marginal, and proof the business is real and operating.
- An L‑1A petition requires evidence of a qualifying relationship between foreign and U.S. employers and proof the role is executive or managerial.
- For a new‑office L‑1A, petitioners must show sufficient physical premises and financial ability to remunerate the employee and begin doing business.
- A new‑office L‑1A petition is initially approved for a period not to exceed one year.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit assets subject to forfeiture under 18 U.S.C. § 982(a)(6).
- Defendant A agreed to identify and locate forfeitable property, disclose all assets, and provide tax returns for the prior five years.
- Defendant A agreed to be interviewed regarding forfeitable assets and to undergo a polygraph on the issue of assets if deemed necessary by the United States.
- Defendant A agreed to the entry of a stipulated judicial order of removal under 8 U.S.C. §§ 1228(c)(5) and 1227.
- Defendant A admitted removability and requested that an order be issued for removal to the United Kingdom.
- Defendant A waived the right to notice and hearing under 8 U.S.C. § 1228(c)(2) and waived rights to appeal, reopen, reconsider, or otherwise challenge the stipulated removal order.
- Defendant A waived eligibility for relief from removal, including asylum, withholding, CAT protection, cancellation of removal, adjustment of status, registry, TPS review, waivers under 8 U.S.C. §§ 1182(h) or (i), visa petitions, consular processing, and voluntary departure.

- The Court may impose up to three years of supervised release for this offense.
- The offense carries a maximum fine of $250,000, subject to the alternative fine provision allowing up to twice the gross gain or twice the gross loss.

- The defendant pled guilty on November 1, 2021 to a portion of the Second Superseding Indictment pursuant to a Rule 11(c)(1)(C) agreement.
- The defendant agreed to plead guilty to Counts One and Two charging conspiracy to commit sex trafficking in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1594(c).
- The defendant agreed to plead guilty to so much of Count Four as charges sex trafficking of a minor, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1591(b)(2).
- The defendant agreed to plead guilty to Count Nine, transportation of a minor with intent to engage in criminal sexual activity, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 2423(a).
- The defendant agreed to plead guilty to Count Ten, obstruction of justice, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1503.
- The defendant agreed to plead guilty to Count Eleven, attempted witness tampering, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1512(b)(1).
- The United States agreed to dismiss Counts Three, Five, Six, Seven, and Eight charging sex trafficking by force, fraud, and coercion (18 U.S.C. § 1591(a)(1)) after sentencing.
- For Counts 1, 2, 4, and 9, the statutory maximum penalty includes life imprisonment.
- For Counts 4 and 9, there is a mandatory minimum penalty of 10 years’ incarceration.
- For Counts 1, 2, 4, and 9, the statutory maximum supervised release term is five years as stated in the plea letter.
- For Count 10 (obstruction of justice), the statutory maximum penalty includes ten years’ imprisonment and up to three years of supervised release.
- For Count 11 (attempted witness tampering), the statutory maximum penalty includes twenty years’ imprisonment and up to three years of supervised release.
- For Counts 1, 2, 4, and 9, the statutory maximum fine is $250,000 per count as stated in the plea letter.
- For Counts 10 and 11, the statutory maximum fine is $250,000 per count as stated in the plea letter.
- Each count of conviction carries a mandatory $100 special assessment, and an additional $5,000 assessment may apply under 18 U.S.C. § 3014 unless the defendant is indigent.
- Conviction in this case requires the defendant to register as a sex offender under SORNA and keep the registration current where he resides, works, and studies.
- Failure to comply with sex offender registration may violate supervised release and can result in new criminal charges under 18 U.S.C. § 2250.
- The defendant was arrested and detained on February 19, 2020 for the instant offenses and has remained in custody since his arrest.
- The plea agreement was signed by the defendant on October 25, 2021 and filed with the Court on October 26, 2021.
- The parties’ plea agreement was entered under Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 11(c)(1)(C), which binds the Court to the agreed disposition if accepted.
- Counts 1, 2, 4, and 9 carry a mandatory minimum supervised release term of 60 months under 18 U.S.C. § 3583(k).
- The defendant admitted he committed the crimes specified in the counts to which he is pleading and that he is in fact guilty of each one.
- The defendant consented to forfeiture of vehicles, currency, or other personal property seized during the investigation and prosecution and waived any claims to such assets.
- As early as 2004, the defendant began forcing victims to work for him as prostitutes.
- The defendant targeted particularly vulnerable and transient victims, including a drug addict, a domestic abuse survivor, a prior trafficking victim, and a minor.
- The defendant used psychological manipulation to control victims, including monitoring their whereabouts and communications to isolate them from support networks.
- The defendant physically beat a co-conspirator in front of victims, including Victim D and Victim B (a minor).
- Victim A estimated the defendant was physically violent with her on more than twenty occasions.
- The defendant threatened to find Victim A and kill her if she left.
- The defendant carried firearms and made his access to firearms known to victims.
- The defendant imposed rules on victims meeting customers, including taking money first, asking about a condom, and closing the door.
- Victims were not allowed to look at other men or use cell phones in the defendant’s presence.
- After being charged, the defendant attempted to obstruct justice and tamper with victims’ testimony.
- On recorded jail calls, the defendant directed co-conspirators to carry out a plan to contact victims to get them to alter or recant their testimony, which he referred to as “Plan B.”
- The defendant stated he could manipulate Victim D, saying he knew he could “finesse it.”
- A co-conspirator contacted Victim D and said the defendant wanted a letter stating they were “cool” and had “no beef, no drama.”
- The defendant exploited a minor who was 16 years old.
- The government informed victims of their right to seek restitution, and each declined to pursue restitution.
- The defendant’s criminal record spans over 25 years, including convictions for distribution of cocaine, grand theft auto, possession of firearms, and assault and battery.
- The defendant had minimal recent employment, including about one week at a casino and sporadic snow removal work, and reported being supported by family and friends during the trafficking period.
- The defendant made inconsistent statements about two women being his half-sisters, while the government’s investigation indicated one was a girlfriend and the other a love interest with whom he exchanged sexually explicit messages.
- The defendant reported a difficult upbringing, including an unstable home life with unmarried parents and abuse by his mother.
- The defendant reported being left in the care of different women while his father sold drugs, and that some of these women abused him physically and sexually between ages 6 and 16.
- The defendant reported that his mother died in the late 1990s due to complications from her own drug use.
- The defendant reported exposure to gangs, drugs, and violence in his neighborhood growing up.
- The defendant reported that during a prior period of incarceration he was sexually assaulted and forced to join a prison gang.
- The defendant reports current medical and mental health issues, including high blood pressure, diabetes, anxiety, depression, and PTSD.
- The defendant reports knee problems limiting exercise and that he contracted COVID-19 at least twice in custody with lingering effects.
- The defendant reports participation in facility drug treatment programs and receiving regular mental health care while detained.
- The defendant has two children and reports being close with them, including an 8-year-old daughter with whom contact has been limited during this case.
- The defendant received an earlier plea offer dated June 21, 2021 and rejected it.
- The defendant is responsible for conspiring to traffic Victims B, C, and E and for transporting a minor, as stated in the plea agreement.
