- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Count One and Count Two of the indictment.
- Count One charged Prohibited Person in Possession of a Firearm under 18 U.S.C. §§ 922(g)(1), 922(g)(9), and 924(a)(2).
- Count Two charged Possession of a Firearm in a School Zone under 18 U.S.C. §§ 922(q)(2)(A) and 924(a)(4).
- Defendant A pleaded guilty to one count of prohibited-person firearm possession and one count of possession of a firearm in a school zone.
- On the morning of December 11, 2019, Defendant A entered Harmon Elementary School in Las Vegas, Nevada.
- At that time, Defendant A knew and had reasonable cause to believe Harmon Elementary School was within a school zone.
- While inside a building at Harmon Elementary School that morning, Defendant A knowingly possessed a firearm.
- The firearm was a Ruger EC9s semiautomatic 9mm handgun.
- The handgun had been manufactured outside Nevada before being possessed in Las Vegas.
- On the afternoon of December 11, 2019, Defendant A returned to the grounds of Harmon Elementary School.
- That afternoon, while on the school grounds, Defendant A knowingly possessed the same Ruger EC9s 9mm handgun.
- As of December 11, 2019, Defendant A had a prior felony robbery conviction in Clark County, Nevada.
- At the time he possessed the handgun, Defendant A knew he had been convicted of a felony punishable by more than one year.
- As of December 11, 2019, Defendant A had a prior misdemeanor crime of domestic violence conviction in Los Angeles County, California.
- At the time he possessed the handgun, Defendant A knew he had been convicted of a misdemeanor crime of domestic violence.
- For Count One, the statutory maximum penalties are ten years of imprisonment, up to three years of supervised release, a fine up to $250,000, and a $100 special assessment.
- For Count Two, the statutory maximum penalties are five years of imprisonment, up to one year of supervised release, a fine up to $100,000, and a $100 special assessment.
- A sentence for a violation of 18 U.S.C. § 922(q)(2)(A) cannot run concurrently with any other term of imprisonment imposed.
- Defendant A admitted guilt to both offenses and that the admitted facts satisfy every element of the charged offenses.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit a Ruger EC9s 9mm handgun and any compatible ammunition.
- Defendant A agreed that the firearm and ammunition were involved in or used in the offense and are subject to forfeiture.
- The plea agreement was entered pursuant to Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 11(c)(1)(A) and (B).
- The prosecuting attorney signed the plea agreement on or about September 10, 2020.

- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Count One charging Conspiracy to Distribute a Controlled Substance—Methamphetamine in violation of 21 U.S.C. §§ 846 and 841(a)(1), (b)(1)(A)(viii).
- The plea agreement was entered pursuant to Fed. R. Crim. P. 11(c)(1)(A) and (B).
- The conspiracy timeframe ran from an unknown start date through on or about October 30, 2019.
- On October 23, 2019 and October 29, 2019, Defendant A sold methamphetamine to a DEA confidential source in Las Vegas, Nevada.
- DEA laboratory analysis determined the sold substance contained 2,122 grams of actual methamphetamine.
- The parties stipulated that 2,122 grams of actual methamphetamine was reasonably foreseeable to Defendant A.
- The plea agreement states the statutory maximum penalty is life imprisonment, 5 years of supervised release, a $10,000,000 fine, and a $100 special assessment, with a 10-year mandatory minimum absent safety-valve relief.
- At sentencing, the prosecution agreed to move to dismiss the remaining counts of the indictment against Defendant A.
- The parties agreed Defendant A did not use violence or credible threats of violence, did not possess a firearm or other dangerous weapon, and did not induce another participant to do so in connection with the charged offenses.
- The parties agreed the charged offenses did not result in death or serious bodily injury.
- The parties agreed Defendant A was not an organizer, leader, manager, or supervisor of others and was not engaged in a continuing criminal enterprise in the charged offenses.
- The parties made no agreement as to whether Defendant A qualifies for safety-valve relief from the statutory minimum sentence.
- The plea agreement was filed on June 2, 2021.
- The plea agreement bears signature dates in late April 2021.
- Defendant A was charged by indictment with one count of conspiracy to distribute methamphetamine and two counts of distribution of methamphetamine.
- On June 2, 2021, Defendant A pled guilty to one count of conspiracy to distribute methamphetamine.
- A Presentence Investigation Report was prepared on July 16, 2021.
- The PSR indicated Defendant A has zero criminal history points.
- Defendant A was born in El Paso, Texas and is the youngest of seven children.
- Defendant A’s only biological brother died in a car accident approximately 25 years before the sentencing memorandum.
- When Defendant A was eight, his mother left his father; he reunited with her in Las Vegas after an older sister arranged his travel there.
- Defendant A experienced financial hardship growing up, including periods when the family could not pay for both utilities and rent.
- Defendant A married in 2016 and divorced in 2019.
- Defendant A has one child who, at the time of the sentencing memorandum, was twelve years old and resided with Defendant A.
- The child’s other parent did not provide financial assistance, and Defendant A often assisted the other parent financially.
- Defendant A enjoys activities with Child A including hunting, camping, and fishing.
- Defendant A has been employed as a driver for a Las Vegas company since 2017 and joined a laborers’ union in 2018.
- An employer described Defendant A as someone who comes early, stays late, picks up extra shifts, and goes above and beyond.
- Defendant A was recently working six days a week, sometimes seven depending on project demands.
- Defense asserted that threats were made to Sister A and that if Co-Defendant A did not start selling methamphetamine, she would be harmed.
- Defense asserted that Co-Defendant A asked Defendant A to deliver a bag in return for cash.
- Defense asserted that Defendant A delivered the bag to protect Sister A and her family.
- Defense asserted Sister A regrets involving Defendant A in the drug transactions.
- In October 2019, Defendant A conspired with Co-Defendant A to distribute methamphetamine within the Las Vegas community.
- Defendant A distributed approximately five pounds of methamphetamine to a DEA confidential source during the conspiracy.
- A sentencing hearing for Defendant A was scheduled for March 23, 2022.
- The prosecution filed a motion for a status conference to address statements by Defendant A that appeared to negate the factual basis in the plea agreement (ECF No. 71).
- Defendant A admitted under oath that he conspired with his co-defendant to distribute approximately 2,122 grams (4.67 pounds) of actual methamphetamine in October 2019.
- On both sales, Defendant A brought the methamphetamine to the confidential source and completed the transactions.
- During the October 23, 2019 sale, Defendant A conducted the transaction alone.
- During the October 23, 2019 transaction, Defendant A stated he could acquire up to 50 pounds of methamphetamine.
- During the October 23, 2019 transaction, Defendant A stated he used flip phones and did not keep the same number for very long.
- On October 29, 2019, Defendant A transported four pounds of methamphetamine to the deal location.
- The prosecution asserted the drugs involved had a street value of $6,500.
- Defendant A had a pending case from November 2019 in which he was arrested for possessing approximately 55 grams of methamphetamine during a domestic disturbance call, which was not associated with the confidential source.
- At the June 2, 2021 plea hearing, Defendant A did not mention the alleged threats he later claimed as the reason for his conduct.
- Defendant A submitted a video-recorded mitigation statement (Exhibit A) in which he claimed he acted out of fear stemming from an alleged threat by the confidential source against Sister A.

- The defendant pleaded guilty to Counts One through Five of the Superseding Indictment for engaging in transactions or dealings in property or interests in property of designated foreign persons under the Kingpin Act.

- The plea agreement is pursuant to Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 11(c)(1)(B).

- The defendant agreed to pay a $100 special assessment per felony count prior to sentencing.
- The defendant admitted she is a United States person, specifically a natural-born U.S. citizen, at all relevant times.

- A two-count information was filed on February 5, 2020 charging Defendant A with bid rigging in violation of 15 U.S.C. § 1 and money laundering conspiracy in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1956(h).
- On March 5, 2020, Defendant A pleaded guilty to both counts pursuant to a written plea agreement.
- Defendant A agreed to waive indictment and plead guilty to a two-count information.
- At arraignment, the government agreed to stipulate to Defendant A’s release on personal recognizance pending sentencing.
- The relevant period for the charged conduct began at least as early as 2013 and continued through at least late 2017.
- During the relevant period, Defendant A was employed by a major flooring manufacturer (Manufacturer A) as an account executive responsible for promoting and selling commercial flooring products within an authorized pricing matrix.
- Manufacturer A’s account executives were incentivized to quote higher authorized prices because commissions were tied to the overall value of sales.
- Count One charged a conspiracy to suppress and eliminate competition by agreeing to rig bids and fix prices of commercial flooring services and products in violation of the Sherman Act.
- Company A, Company B, and Company E were Illinois corporations providing commercial flooring services and products during the relevant period.
- Defendant A initiated and orchestrated a bid-rotation conspiracy for projects at a public post-secondary institution in the district (Victim 12).
- Victim 12 was a community college located in Palos Hills, Illinois.
- Defendant A contacted co-conspirators at Company A, Company B, and Company E and invited them to participate in a bid rotation whereby the winning bidder would be rotated among the three companies.
- Defendant A offered the lowest product price to the designated winning installer and higher prices to the others, and directed the others to submit complementary (intentionally losing) bids.
- The bid-rotation arrangement initially relied on Manufacturer A’s products, with Defendant A providing an “edge” to the designated winner.
- The bid-rotation conspiracy did not rotate winners in a strict sequence, which helped disguise the scheme from Victim 12.
- Eventually, the installers continued the bid-rotation scheme on projects not involving Manufacturer A’s products.
- The bid-rotation conspiracy ended in 2017 after an FBI search of a participant’s offices and overt investigative steps.
- The conspirators rigged at least fifteen bids at Victim 12 totaling approximately $600,000 in project value.
- Defendant A caused a check for $1,962.40 from Company B to be deposited on January 8, 2014 into an MGAB13 account at Roselle Bank & Trust as kickback payments for two contracts.
- The bid-rigging conspiracy’s activities occurred within and affected interstate commerce.
- Count Two charged a conspiracy to conduct financial transactions involving kickback proceeds from specified unlawful activity (honest services wire fraud) designed to conceal the nature and source of the proceeds.
- Company G and Company H provided commercial flooring services and products in the United States during the relevant period.
- Defendant A solicited and accepted kickbacks in exchange for offering unlawfully low pricing to commercial flooring providers without disclosing this to his employer.
- Co-conspirators charged unlawfully fixed prices to end users for flooring products from Manufacturer A as set by Defendant A.
- A co-conspirator suggested that Defendant A create a shell company to receive kickbacks and conceal payments from law enforcement and the employer.
- On July 10, 2013, Defendant A caused MGAB13 Consulting, Inc. to be established in Illinois under the nominal ownership of a family member.
- MGAB13 bank accounts were opened in the family member’s name, and the family member was falsely indicated to be a consultant, although they had no industry experience and performed no consulting.
- The sole purpose of MGAB13 was to receive kickback payments intended for Defendant A and conceal their nature, location, source, ownership, and control.
- By soliciting and receiving kickbacks in exchange for discounts, Defendant A intended and caused monetary harm to the employer through lower revenue from low prices offered to co-conspirators.
- On August 9, 2013, a bank account for MGAB13 was established at Roselle Bank & Trust in Roselle, Illinois.
- Defendant A directed co-conspirators to make kickback checks payable to MGAB13, which were deposited and used to pay family credit card bills and other personal expenses.
- Defendant A completed annual ethics questionnaires acknowledging a duty of loyalty and prohibition on kickbacks, and falsely represented that neither he nor family members had received cash or cash equivalents from customers.
- During the conspiracy, at least twenty-four kickback payments totaling approximately $111,837.81 were laundered through MGAB13.

- The money-laundering conspiracy involved paying MGAB13 with checks from multiple flooring companies, including Mr. David’s Flooring International (identified elsewhere as Company A).
- Defendant A’s cooperation began in February 2019, before any other target of the investigation had been charged.
- Defendant A was interviewed by the government on four occasions during the investigation.
- Defendant A provided and quickly turned over paper records of kickback checks deposited into MGAB13’s bank account.
- Defendant A’s cooperation was corroborated by other witnesses and documentary evidence.
- Defendant A was prepared to testify against co-conspirators, though testimony was not required because co-conspirators pleaded guilty.
- Defendant A’s cooperation was particularly useful in the case against Mr. David’s Flooring International, which ultimately pleaded guilty in case number 21-cr-517 (N.D. Ill.).
- The maximum statutory penalty for Count One (Sherman Act bid rigging) is 10 years’ imprisonment.
- For Count One, the maximum fine is $1,000,000, or twice the gross pecuniary gain or loss, whichever is greater.
- For Count One, a term of supervised release of up to three years may follow any term of imprisonment.
- The maximum statutory penalty for Count Two (money laundering conspiracy under 18 U.S.C. § 1956(h)) is 20 years’ imprisonment.
- For Count Two, the maximum fine is $500,000, or twice the value of the property involved in the money laundering transactions, whichever is greater.
- For Count Two, a term of supervised release of up to three years may follow any term of imprisonment.
- During the relevant period, Defendant A’s role as account executive put him in a unique position to know all potential bidders and to determine winners by adjusting product quotes.
- The scheme’s bid rotation was mutually advantageous: installers avoided competition and could charge higher prices, and Defendant A could inflate product quotes leading to higher commissions.
- The money laundering transactions involved checks made payable to MGAB13 that were deposited into an MGAB13 account.
- The specified unlawful activity underlying the laundering scheme was honest services wire fraud under 18 U.S.C. §§ 1343 and 1346.
- The scheme to receive kickbacks in exchange for discounts began by 2013 and continued through at least late 2017.
- No violations of bond were reported during Defendant A’s pretrial release.
- Defendant A ceased drinking alcohol and using cocaine in 2018 and began attending Alcoholics Anonymous meetings.
- Defendant A has been employed since May 2020 after losing his long-term job when he pleaded guilty.

- The case was prosecuted in the United States District Court for the District of Columbia.
- The defendant pleaded guilty to one count of Delivering National Defense Information to Aid a Foreign Government, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 794(a).
- The statutory maximum penalty for a violation of 18 U.S.C. § 794(a) includes up to life imprisonment.
- The government agreed to move to dismiss the remaining counts of the indictment at sentencing in exchange for the guilty plea to 18 U.S.C. § 794(a).
- A $100 special assessment applies to the felony conviction.
- The defendant was required to be detained pending sentencing because the offense falls under 18 U.S.C. § 3142(f)(1).
- The defendant agreed to forfeit property used to facilitate the offense, including a Samsung smartphone and an Apple iPhone 7 Plus.
- The defendant was arrested on February 27, 2020, in Erbil, Iraq.
- The initial sealed complaint charged Unlawfully Retaining National Defense Information under 18 U.S.C. § 793(e).
- On March 3, 2020, a second complaint charged Delivering National Defense Information to Aid a Foreign Government under 18 U.S.C. § 794(a) and Conspiracy under 18 U.S.C. § 794(c).
- On March 5, 2020, a grand jury returned an indictment with three counts: conspiracy under 18 U.S.C. § 794(c), delivering national defense information under 18 U.S.C. § 794(a), and willful retention under 18 U.S.C. § 793(e).
- The defendant had been a contract linguist since the summer of 2006.
- Immediately prior to arrest, the defendant was assigned to a Special Operations Task Force facility in Iraq from mid-December 2019 until February 2020.
- During the assignment in Iraq, the defendant held a TS//SCI security clearance and was permitted access to national defense information up to the TS//SCI level.
- The defendant had to undergo a background investigation and sign non-disclosure and user agreements regarding classified national defense information.
- By training and agreement, the defendant understood that classified information could be shared only with authorized persons who had a need to know and must be protected from unauthorized disclosure.
- The defendant understood that unauthorized disclosure of SECRET information could be expected to cause serious damage to U.S. national security.
- The defendant had access to detailed files about clandestine human assets, which were maintained on electronic devices classified at the SECRET level.
- The defendant is a naturalized U.S. citizen who was born in Lebanon and maintained contact with family members in Lebanon.
- In 2017, a family member introduced the defendant via social media to a Lebanese national who expressed interest in marrying her.
- Between 2017 and February 2020, the defendant and the Lebanese national frequently communicated using the video-chat feature of a secure messaging application.
- The Lebanese national claimed to have contact with members of Lebanese Hizballah.
- Before January 2020, the Lebanese national had not asked the defendant to provide classified information.
- In late December 2019, the United States launched airstrikes against Kata'ib Hizballah in Iraq.
- Shortly after early January 2020 events, the Lebanese national asked the defendant to provide information about human assets who had helped the United States.
- The defendant understood that “them” referred to Lebanese Hizballah and an unnamed high-ranking military commander.
- The Lebanese national instructed the defendant to access the information herself or ask other linguists for it.
- The defendant decided to provide classified national defense information to the Lebanese national to preserve their relationship and anticipated marriage.
- Beginning in early January 2020, the defendant accessed Department of Defense systems containing information regarding human assets classified up to the SECRET level.
- She accessed information she knew she did not have a legitimate need to know.
- Initially, the defendant memorized and wrote notes on post-it notes, then prepared more detailed handwritten notes in her living quarters.
- She transmitted the handwritten notes to the Lebanese national via video-chat, which he captured by taking screenshots.
- The Lebanese national praised the defendant for the information and said his contacts were pleased.
- The defendant began creating her own composite intelligence reports by cutting and pasting from different classified files and taking cell phone photos of the compiled report without saving it on the classified system.
- She also used her cell phone to take pictures of incoming messages from human assets and transmitted those images.
- Transmitted content included the true names, personal identification data, background information, and photographs of human sources, and operations cables detailing information the sources provided.
- The defendant identified and transmitted information about priority U.S. targets, including warnings to U.S. targets.
- She transmitted information about tactics, techniques, and procedures used by human assets and understood it could be used to identify those assets.
- The defendant had reason to believe the information she provided was being passed to Lebanese Hizballah, a designated foreign terrorist organization.
- During the offense conduct, she passed information regarding at least eight clandestine human assets and at least ten U.S. targets, as well as multiple TTPs.
- She intended and had reason to believe the information would be used to the injury of the United States and to the advantage of Lebanese Hizballah.
- An original classification authority from CENTCOM confirmed that the transmitted information was properly classified at the SECRET level at the time of the disclosures.
- The defendant admitted at plea that she believed the information she provided was being passed to a designated foreign terrorist organization and that her access derived from her status as a U.S. government security clearance holder.
- Kata'ib Hizballah is an Iranian-backed force in Iraq that has been designated as a foreign terrorist organization since 2009.
- On January 8, 2020, Iran fired missiles at U.S. military bases in Iraq, including the base in Erbil where the defendant was based.
- The U.S. Secretary of State designated Lebanese Hizballah as a Foreign Terrorist Organization on October 8, 1997, and the designation was amended in 2017; it remains in effect.
- While working in Erbil, the defendant’s duties included translation services for U.S. personnel working with high-value human assets.
- The defendant created detailed reports that included photographs of human assets, identification cards, phone numbers, addresses, subsource identities, and technical data on how assets tracked and reported target locations.
- The defendant brought a cellular phone into a secure area to photograph classified information, contrary to rules prohibiting phones and photographing classified materials.
- An FBI search of the defendant’s living quarters in Erbil found a handwritten note concealed under a mattress containing the true names of three human assets and instructions to monitor their phones and warn a targeted individual.
- Pen register and trap and trace records show the defendant communicated with the Lebanese national until the evening before her arrest.
- On the day before her arrest, there were over 60 exchanges between the defendant and the Lebanese national, including texts, images, and voice calls.
- During post-arrest interviews, the defendant initially denied the Lebanese national’s affiliation with Lebanese Hizballah and claimed he was connected to a political party, and denied sending certain classified images.
- After indictment and before and after her guilty plea, the defendant met with the government, answered questions, and her responses were generally found credible; information she provided assisted force protection measures.
- The defendant’s security clearances progressed from interim SECRET in 2006 to full SECRET in 2009, interim TOP SECRET in 2010, full TOP SECRET in 2012, and TS//SCI in 2016.
- As a security clearance holder, the defendant received regular briefings and signed non-disclosure agreements addressing rules for handling and disseminating classified material.
- The defendant served extended tours in conflict zones including Baghdad and Erbil in Iraq and Kabul and Kandahar in Afghanistan, as well as Kuwait.
- The defendant understood that the Lebanese national sought the information on behalf of Lebanese Hizballah and that it would be used to the advantage of that organization.
- The defendant acknowledged at her plea that her access to the classified information she passed derived from her position of trust as a U.S. government security clearance holder.
- U.S. forces in Iraq faced an imminent threat of retaliatory attacks during the period when the defendant disclosed the information.
- The Department of State removed non-essential personnel from the U.S. Embassy in Baghdad and the Consulate in Erbil in spring 2019 due to escalating threats.
- The defendant’s disclosures included information used for defensive and offensive force protection measures in Iraq and elsewhere.

- The FBI in Los Angeles and the USAO for the Central District of California were conducting a federal investigation into public corruption throughout the City of Los Angeles related to suspected pay-to-play schemes.
- The defendant served as the Los Angeles City Councilmember for Council District 12 from 2011 until resigning on December 31, 2018.
- The defendant served as Council President Pro-Tempore and was on the Planning and Land Use Management Committee until resigning from the City Council in December 2018.
- The defendant served on various other City committees, including Chair of the Public Safety Committee, member of the Budget and Finance Committee, Personnel and Animal Welfare Committee, Executive Employee Relations Committee, ad-hoc Police Reform Committee, and Board of Referred Powers.
- The defendant was a reserve member of the Los Angeles Police Department.
- On June 1, 2017, the defendant traveled to Las Vegas with Businessperson A, City Staffer A, City Staffer B, Lobbyist A, and Developer A.
- Businessperson A provided hotel rooms at a resort and casino to the defendant and others using VIP “comps.”
- The defendant received transportation from the Las Vegas airport to the resort and casino.
- On June 1, 2017, the defendant accepted an envelope containing $10,000 in cash from Businessperson A in a casino bathroom.
- On June 1, 2017, Businessperson A gave the defendant approximately $1,000 in casino chips to gamble and the defendant later returned the chips after gambling.
- Businessperson A paid approximately $2,481 for dinner and drinks for the group on June 1, 2017 at a restaurant inside the resort and casino.
- The group took a limousine provided by the resort and casino to a nightclub at another hotel on June 1, 2017.
- At the nightclub, Businessperson A paid approximately $24,000 for bottle service and alcohol for the defendant and others.
- Developer A paid approximately $10,000 for additional bottle service and alcohol for the group at the nightclub.
- After returning to the resort in the early morning of June 2, 2017, Businessperson A arranged for two female escorts and paid for their services.
- Businessperson A instructed one escort to go to the defendant’s hotel room, and was unaware whether the defendant accepted those services.
- Between approximately June 10 and June 12, 2017, the defendant attended a golf tournament at a casino resort in Palm Springs.
- On or about June 12, 2017 at the Palm Springs event, the defendant accepted an envelope containing $5,000 in cash from Businessperson A in a casino bathroom.
- On June 19, 2017, the defendant arranged a lunch with Businessperson A and Developer B so Businessperson A could present his company and product to Developer B.
- After the June 19, 2017 lunch, Developer B emailed thanks and sought to set up a further meeting and presentation from Businessperson A.
- From about August 2017 through December 31, 2018, the defendant knowingly and willfully falsified, concealed, and covered up material facts in a matter within the jurisdiction of the executive branch (the USAO and FBI).
- The concealed material facts included that the defendant accepted items of value from Businessperson A (cash, hotel rooms, luxury outings, expensive meals, and offered escort services).
- The concealed material facts also included that the defendant attempted to coordinate his statements with Businessperson A and counseled Businessperson A on how to lie to and mislead the FBI and USAO.
- In or around August 2017, the defendant privately sent an encrypted Confide message to Businessperson A stating he wanted to reimburse portions of the June 2017 Las Vegas trip.
- After the FBI contacted the defendant on or about September 1, 2017 to request a voluntary interview, the defendant sent a check to Businessperson A to reimburse certain Las Vegas trip expenses.
- The reimbursement check sent after the FBI contact was dated August 4, 2017.
- On October 4, 2017, the defendant met Businessperson A for lunch in Downtown Los Angeles and discussed the June 2017 Las Vegas trip and the federal investigation.
- At the October 4, 2017 lunch, the defendant told Businessperson A that he had an upcoming FBI interview and agreed to call Businessperson A after the interview.
- On October 19, 2017, the defendant, with counsel present, was interviewed by the FBI and USAO about the investigation and the June 2017 Las Vegas trip.
- During the October 19, 2017 interview, the defendant falsely stated more than once that he had not informed anyone other than his lawyers and spouse about his upcoming FBI interview.
- On January 31, 2018, Businessperson A contacted the defendant via Confide referencing a February 6 event and that counsel had received an FBI call about the reimbursement check.
- On January 31, 2018 and February 1, 2018, the defendant and Businessperson A discussed the investigation via Confide, and the defendant provided a different phone number for further discussion.
- On February 1, 2018, Businessperson A left a voicemail on the new phone number the defendant had provided.
- Between February 2 and 5, 2018, the defendant and Businessperson A agreed via Confide to discuss the investigation at the defendant’s February 6, 2018 fundraiser.
- During a private conversation at the February 6, 2018 fundraiser, the defendant told Businessperson A to falsely state to the FBI that they had never talked about their FBI interviews.
- At the February 6, 2018 conversation, the defendant coached Businessperson A to answer FBI questions by saying "I don't know."
- At the February 6, 2018 conversation, the defendant told Businessperson A to falsely claim the defendant had repeatedly attempted to reimburse hotel and dinner costs from the Las Vegas trip.
- At the February 6, 2018 conversation, the defendant told Businessperson A not to mention that escort services were provided or offered during the Las Vegas trip.
- On February 7, 2018, before his interview, the defendant attempted to call Businessperson A via WhatsApp and then spoke with him, asking whether escorts had been disclosed to the FBI.
- On February 7, 2018, the defendant had a second interview with the FBI and USAO with counsel present about the investigation and the June 2017 trip.
- During the February 7, 2018 interview, the defendant falsely stated he was unaware that Businessperson A intended to attend the February 6 fundraiser.
- During the February 7, 2018 interview, the defendant falsely stated that he and Businessperson A did not discuss the FBI, the investigation, or the upcoming interview during the fundraiser.
- During the February 7, 2018 interview, the defendant falsely stated he had never been told the amount Businessperson A paid for bottle service at the nightclub.
- During the February 7, 2018 interview, the defendant falsely stated that Businessperson A did not provide benefits other than a hotel room, dinner, and beverage service during the June 2017 trip.
- During the February 7, 2018 interview, the defendant falsely stated he did not tell anyone, including Businessperson A, what to say to the FBI.
- During the February 7, 2018 interview, the defendant falsely stated that other than his lawyers and spouse, he had not informed anyone about his second interview.
- During the February 7, 2018 interview, the defendant falsely stated he had no plans to discuss his second interview with anyone after it occurred.
- From February 8 to February 12, 2018, the defendant and Businessperson A exchanged Confide messages and arranged to meet on February 12, 2018.
- On February 12, 2018, the defendant met Businessperson A in the defendant’s car in Downtown Los Angeles, turned up the car stereo loudly, and drove in circles while discussing the investigation and interviews.
- On February 12, 2018, the defendant told Businessperson A to say they never talked about their FBI interviews and to avoid mentioning such conversations.
- On February 12, 2018, the defendant told Businessperson A how to respond to FBI questions about reimbursement by saying he did not remember.
- On February 12, 2018, the defendant told Businessperson A to respond to questions about escorts by saying he did not remember.
- On February 12, 2018, the defendant told Businessperson A not to disclose their use of Confide.
- On February 12, 2018, the defendant told Businessperson A not to disclose their conversation at the February 6 fundraiser.
- On February 12, 2018, the defendant agreed to introduce Businessperson A to the defendant’s builder acquaintance and to tour Businessperson A’s showroom.
- On or about April 2, 2018, the defendant reported $1,202 in gifts/benefits on a 2017 Form 700 but did not report the $15,000 cash received from Businessperson A.
- On November 20, 2018, the defendant met with Businessperson A and discussed recent FBI actions related to the investigation.
- At the November 20, 2018 meeting, when Businessperson A referred to the Las Vegas cash, the defendant stated he was not going to say anything to the FBI about it.
- At the November 20, 2018 meeting, the defendant agreed that Businessperson A should omit that they talked about their FBI interviews and instead say they discussed the defendant’s new job.
- On December 31, 2018, the defendant had a third interview with the FBI and USAO with counsel present.
- During the December 31, 2018 interview, the defendant falsely said that beyond chips he paid back, dinners, and a hotel room, he did not recall other benefits.
- During the December 31, 2018 interview, the defendant falsely stated that Businessperson A never provided him cash payments.
- During the December 31, 2018 interview, the defendant falsely stated that he told Businessperson A to share everything with the FBI.
- During the December 31, 2018 interview, the defendant falsely stated he could not recall if he had used Confide, despite having Confide registered on his phone and having recently used it with Businessperson A and Lobbyist B.
- The defendant’s criminal conduct included directing Businessperson A not to disclose their use of Confide during his interactions with federal investigators.

- Defendant A transferred fraud proceeds into approximately eleven accounts in the names of her firm entity or its trade names.
- Defendant A transferred fraud proceeds into at least 68 other accounts she controlled, including accounts in her name, variants of her name, victims’ names, and joint accounts with victims.
- On June 22, 2018, Defendant A opened a personal cash management account at a brokerage firm and transferred approximately $285,000 into it between August 1 and August 17, 2018 from the business bank account.
- On September 11, 2018, Defendant A opened a personal checking account at a bank using a fraudulent identification document bearing another person’s photograph.
- Between September 11 and September 14, 2018, approximately $405,000 was transferred from the brokerage account to the newly opened bank account.
- Banks were often unable to return funds to original account holders because Defendant A rapidly transferred fraud proceeds to other accounts she controlled.
- Defendant A used counterfeit identification documents, including fake passports and driver’s licenses, to open accounts at multiple financial institutions.
- To open an account with a financial services company, Defendant A submitted a counterfeit U.S. passport photo page in an alternate name, with a photograph not of her and a non-valid passport number.
- Defendant A used a counterfeit temporary D.C. driver’s license in an alternate name to obtain a $30,000 loan from an online lender for her firm entity.
- As part of the loan application, Defendant A submitted purported signed tax returns in an alternate name using an actual person’s address and Social Security number.
- On January 18, 2019, the lender funded the $30,000 loan to a bank account in the law firm’s name and the firm later defaulted on the loan.
- Chase Bank is a financial institution within the meaning of 18 U.S.C. § 20, with deposits insured by the FDIC.
- Defendant A illegally accessed a victim’s Chase account and caused multiple unauthorized ACH transfers totaling $55,750 to her firm’s business account in early August 2018.
- Defendant A initiated or attempted to initiate at least $3,500,000 in fraudulent transactions from illegally accessed accounts.
- Defendant A ultimately obtained, directly or indirectly, between $1.3 million and $2.2 million from the fraud scheme.
- Approximately 202 individuals and 51 financial institutions were negatively affected by Defendant A’s conduct.
- The government asserted that Defendant A collaborated with known computer hackers in furtherance of her schemes.
- The government stated Defendant A used a purported foundation as part of the fraud activity.
- The government asserted that Defendant A’s criminal activity continued even after her arrest in October 2019.
- The government reported that Defendant A placed approximately 21 orders through a cryptocurrency platform via ACH, with seven chargebacks totaling $31,675 in loss to that platform.
- The government’s restitution table identified categories of losses, including to payment processors and a number of bank-related victim accounts, totaling $2,195,022.77.
- The seized assets credited toward forfeiture included funds from multiple financial accounts and a 2018 vehicle, collectively valued at $496,054.41.

- Defendant A registered and operated under trade names associated with her law firm entity.

- Defendant A sometimes used an alternate middle name variant in accounts she controlled.
- Defendant A opened accounts in victims’ names or jointly with victims’ names to facilitate transfers of fraud proceeds.
- In one example, after opening an account in a victim’s name and attempting to fund it with an unauthorized transfer, Defendant A wrote checks payable to her firm that could not clear due to insufficient funds when the incoming transfer was reversed.
- Defendant A made multiple transfers from the business bank account to a brokerage cash management account in mid-August 2018, including individual transfers ranging from $7,000 to $50,000.
- The government identified that certain bank losses in its restitution list were associated with accounts tied to multiple individual victims’ initials.
- Defendant A used her law license status to open accounts and lines of credit, according to government assertions referencing case materials.

- A superseding information charged the defendant with civil disorder in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3).
- The defendant agreed to plead guilty to interfering with a law enforcement officer during a civil disorder, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3).
- On May 30, 2020, the offense conduct occurred in Chicago, Illinois, during a civil disorder.
- Chicago Police Department officers responded to areas of Chicago after reports of property destruction and looting of businesses.
- The civil disorder took place in Chicago’s central business district known as the Loop.
- The civil disorder involved dozens of individuals causing damage to businesses including stores and hotels.
- The civil disorder caused businesses that purchase and sell goods moving in interstate commerce to close early.
- Responding CPD officers were in full uniform and in marked police vehicles.
- The defendant knowingly committed an act to obstruct, impede, and interfere with a law enforcement officer during the civil disorder.
- The civil disorder obstructed, delayed, and adversely affected commerce and the movement of articles and commodities in commerce.
- During the civil disorder, the defendant walked through the Loop wearing a Joker mask.
- The defendant joined with dozens of other individuals causing the civil disorder.
- With intent to obstruct and impede CPD officers, the defendant approached an unoccupied CPD vehicle parked on the 200 block of North State Street.
- The defendant used a lighter to ignite a piece of cloth that he placed in the CPD vehicle’s fuel filler area.
- The area around the fuel filler ignited and the fire spread to the interior of the CPD vehicle.
- Other individuals involved in the civil disorder exacerbated the fire with accelerants.
- The CPD vehicle was eventually destroyed by the fire.
- The CPD vehicle the defendant set on fire was for use by CPD officers engaged in official duties during the civil disorder.
- The defendant’s actions obstructed and interfered with CPD and the Chicago Fire Department by preventing use of the CPD vehicle and causing CFD to respond.
- The fire caused the total loss of the CPD vehicle.
- The cost to replace the destroyed CPD vehicle was $58,125.
- Restitution owed to the Chicago Police Department was acknowledged as $58,125, minus any credit for funds repaid prior to sentencing.
- The offense of conviction carries a maximum sentence of five years’ imprisonment.
- The offense carries a maximum fine of $250,000.
- The Court may impose a term of supervised release of up to three years.
- The Court must order restitution to the victims of the offense.
- Restitution was to be due immediately and paid on a schedule set by the Court.
- A $100 special assessment applies to the offense of conviction.
- The plea agreement was executed on February 2, 2022 and filed on February 3, 2022.
- On the date of the offense, thousands of people converged on downtown Chicago to protest the murder of George Floyd.
- The defendant was walking amongst a large group of people while wearing a replica Joker mask.
- The defendant came upon a CPD vehicle parked on the 200 block of North State Street.
- Another individual handed the defendant a piece of cloth before he set the fire.
- The defendant placed the cloth in the vehicle’s fuel filler and ignited it with a lighter.
- The fire spread to the interior of the CPD vehicle and ultimately destroyed the vehicle entirely.
- After starting the fire, the defendant posed for photos in front of the burning vehicle.
- The defendant recorded footage of the protests on a tablet and narrated some scenes.
- Before setting the fire, the defendant said, "beautiful day for a barbecue" while walking down Wacker Drive.
- Surveillance footage showed a crowd of people running away from the vehicle as the defendant lit the rag in the fuel filler area.
- No one was injured as a result of the fire set by the defendant.
- The defendant followed a crowd north as it confronted a line of CPD officers.
- After the vehicle was burning, the defendant made a recording in which he joked, "I wonder who started that."
- The defendant had a prior 2010 conviction for assault with intent to rob while unarmed in Ann Arbor, Michigan, and was sentenced to nine months’ confinement.
- The defendant had a 2013 conviction for driving without a license in Kankakee County and received 12 months’ supervision.
- The defendant had a 2015 conviction for theft by shoplifting in Atlanta, Georgia, and was sentenced to 30 days’ imprisonment.

- The defendant’s opioid addiction continued up until his arrest for this offense.
- The defendant had seven convictions and eleven arrests noted in his history.

- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Count One and Count Two of a Superseding Information.
- Count One charged conspiracy to defraud the United States and to pay and receive kickbacks, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 371.
- Count Two charged the receipt of kickbacks, in violation of 42 U.S.C. § 1320a-7b(b)(1).
- Each count carried a statutory maximum of five years’ imprisonment.
- Each count carried a maximum fine of $250,000 or twice the gross gain or loss, whichever is greater.
- Each count carried up to three years of supervised release.
- A $100 special assessment was due per felony count.
- Defendant A agreed to waive indictment and proceed by information.
- The parties agreed that Defendant A would be excluded from Medicare, Medicaid, and all federal health care programs as a result of the plea.
- Defendant A agreed to complete any necessary exclusion paperwork within 60 days of receiving the documents.
- Defendant A agreed to pay mandatory restitution to the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services in the amount of $53,699,934.
- The plea agreement is dated September 17, 2021.
- On October 1, 2021, a two-count Superseding Information was filed in the case.
- Defendant A pleaded guilty shortly after the two-count Superseding Information was filed.
- On May 27, 2020, a grand jury returned a 20-count Indictment against Defendant A and three others related to TRICARE compounding schemes.
- Co-Defendant A pleaded guilty on May 14, 2021, with sentencing pending at the time of the memo.
- Co-Defendant C pleaded guilty on October 12, 2021, with sentencing pending at the time of the memo.
- A Superseding Indictment against Co-Defendant B issued on October 26, 2021, and a December 12, 2022 trial resulted in a deadlocked jury with retrial pending.
- Florida Pharmacy Solutions, LLC (FPS) was a compounding pharmacy with principal places of business in Dade City and Zephyrhills, Florida.
- Co-Defendant A served in management roles at FPS, including President, Chief Executive Officer, and Director.
- Mediverse, LLC was owned and controlled by Defendant A and operated as a pharmacy consulting and marketing firm.
- Helix Management Solutions, LLC was owned and controlled by Defendant A and Co-Defendant A and performed billing and marketing services.
- TDSNOLEI, LLC was controlled by Co-Defendant B and provided sales and marketing services for FPS.
- TRICARE is a health care benefit program of the Department of Defense that provides coverage to service members, retirees, and beneficiaries.
- From about November 2012 through about September 2015, Defendant A participated in a scheme to defraud and to pay and receive illegal kickbacks related to compounded drugs billed to TRICARE.
- Participants engaged in test billing to identify highly reimbursed compounded drug formulations to maximize reimbursements from TRICARE.
- Doctors were asked to sign blanket letters authorizing FPS to alter compounded prescriptions without additional consultation to ensure reimbursement.
- FPS submitted claims for compounded drugs to TRICARE for beneficiaries in approximately 30 states and several foreign countries.
- Independent contractor sales and marketing representatives funded hunting trips and outdoor adventures for doctors to induce referrals for compounded drugs filled by FPS.
- On June 15, 2015, Defendant A received a $4.9 million payment as a kickback for referring TRICARE beneficiaries to FPS.
- FPS paid Defendant A approximately $11,718,474 in kickbacks for TRICARE referrals.
- TRICARE paid FPS approximately $22 million for compounded medications related to beneficiaries referred by Defendant A.
- From about January 2017 through August 2019, Defendant A and others referred Medicare beneficiaries for medically unnecessary cancer genetic (CGx) tests in exchange for kickbacks.
- Defendant A used Helix, Whitewater, and White Medical as consulting and marketing firms in the CGx testing scheme.
- Laboratories involved in the CGx scheme included LabSolutions, Med Health Services, Personalized Genetics, and a lab identified as Laboratory 1.
- Some kickback payments were concealed using sham contracts that falsely described hourly marketing services while payments were actually per-patient.
- A contract with LabSolutions provided for Defendant A to be paid 45% of monthly net revenue for tests referred by providers obtained through his efforts.
- Defendant A and co-conspirators operated call centers that ran telemarketing campaigns to recruit Medicare beneficiaries for CGx tests.
- Telemedicine companies were paid for doctors’ orders authorizing CGx tests in the scheme.
- Defendant A understood the CGx tests were marketed and used as preventive or screening tests rather than diagnostic tests.
- Defendant A understood the ordering doctors were not going to use the CGx test results in managing beneficiaries’ specific medical problems.
- Between approximately January 2017 and August 2019, Medicare paid LabSolutions about $19,699,934 for CGx testing tied to Defendant A’s referrals.
- Other laboratories (Personalized Genetics, Med Health Services, and Laboratory 1) received approximately $12 million from Medicare for CGx testing tied to Defendant A’s referrals.
- In sum, approximately $31 million in Medicare payments were made for CGx tests tied to Defendant A’s referrals as described in the plea agreement.
- The sentencing memo cites a total of approximately $31,699,934 in Medicare payments for CGx tests tied to the scheme.
- LabSolutions paid Defendant A or his companies approximately $15,061,317 related to CGx referrals.
- Personalized Genetics, Med Health Services, and Laboratory 1 paid Defendant A or his companies approximately $6,541,921 via a third-party company.
- Defendant A received approximately $33,321,712 in proceeds from FPS and the laboratories combined.
- TRICARE paid approximately $22,000,000 and Medicare paid approximately $31,699,934 for kickback-tainted claims, totaling about $53,699,934 in benefit conferred to providers involved in the schemes.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit at least $33,321,712 as proceeds obtained from the offenses of conviction.

- Defendant A was indicted for violations of 18 U.S.C. § 2119 and 18 U.S.C. § 2.

- The guilty plea was entered pursuant to Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 11(c)(1)(C).
- The carjacking count carries a statutory maximum of 15 years’ imprisonment.
- The carjacking count carries a statutory maximum fine of $250,000.
- The carjacking count carries up to three years of supervised release.

- Defendant A admitted the acts and essential elements of the count of conviction in connection with the guilty plea.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit all firearms and ammunition involved in the offense, including a 9mm M&P Shield pistol.
- Defendant A agreed to abandon any right to evidence and property seized during the investigation.

- On or about June 3, 2020 in Jefferson County, Kentucky, Defendant A, aided and abetted by others, took a black 2017 Subaru Forester from Victim A by force, violence, and intimidation.
- During the carjacking, a firearm was brandished or possessed.
- During the carjacking, Defendant A acted with the intent to cause death or serious bodily harm.
- The Subaru Forester had been transported, shipped, and received in interstate commerce before the carjacking.
- The vehicle was assembled in Gunma, Japan, indicating it traveled in interstate or foreign commerce to Kentucky.
- Two females and five males approached the victims requesting a ride shortly before the carjacking.
- Three males entered the vehicle, including Defendant A, before the carjacking.
- The group stopped at various locations, including to get gas, before the victims were ordered out.
- When Victim A told the men to get out, Defendant A pointed a small, black, semi-automatic pistol at Victim A’s head and threatened to shoot if the victims screamed.
- During the carjacking, one victim was physically restrained and her hands were pulled behind her back.
- The victims were left at the intersection of Strader and Wheeler Street in Louisville at approximately 12:23 a.m. on June 3, 2020.
- On June 4, 2020 at approximately 1:40 a.m., police pursued the stolen Subaru, which flipped into the median off Interstate 64 in Louisville.
- Five occupants, including Defendant A, were inside the Subaru during the crash and were taken to the hospital for injuries.
- Defendant A initially denied driving during the police pursuit but later admitted he was the driver of the stolen vehicle while fleeing police.
- A 9mm M&P pistol was recovered from the Subaru after the crash, and it functioned properly on testing.
- .40 caliber red tip hollow-point bullets were recovered from the vehicle.
- A photo and video on social media depicted Defendant A with what appears to be the same pistol, identifiable by a distinctive Punisher logo.
- Victims identified Defendant A as the person holding the gun during the carjacking.
- A juvenile participant told police he possessed the firearm used during the robbery, but did not specify the timing or whether he pointed it at the victims.
- Defense counsel asserted that an unindicted co-defendant possessed the weapon during the offense.
- Defense counsel stated that at the time of the police chase Defendant A was under the influence and reported having little memory of events.
- Defense counsel stated that Defendant A told officers he was on probation and not allowed to possess a gun, and that a passenger had a gun and later admitted possessing it.
- Defense counsel stated that Defendant A had an outstanding bench warrant for failure to appear the day before the police chase.
- Defense counsel stated that Defendant A knew the Subaru was stolen at the time of the police chase.
- During the June 4, 2020 pursuit and crash, the Subaru was totaled.
- Defendant A’s sentencing was scheduled for February 4, 2021.
- At the time of the defense sentencing memorandum, Defendant A was 21 years old.
- Defendant A obtained a GED in 2019.
- Defendant A expressed interest in education or training in the automotive industry while in custody.
- Defendant A had a prior Kentucky state case in which charges of murder and robbery were amended to facilitation offenses; he entered an Alford plea and received diversion.
- At the time of the carjacking and subsequent crash, Defendant A was on diversion from the state facilitation case.
- Defendant A has prior criminal history including resisting arrest and carrying a concealed deadly weapon, as described in the PSR.
- Defendant A had pending state charges including being a felon in possession of a handgun, failure to disperse, and possession of marijuana at the time of sentencing.
- Defendant A reported a history of mental health diagnoses and treatment, including treatment from March 2015 through August 2016, which he stopped in 2017.
- Defendant A reported daily marijuana use beginning in 2016.
- Defendant A reported weekly abuse of prescription opiates and ecstasy from 2018 until 2020.
- Defendant A reported daily Xanax use from 2018 up to the time of his arrest in this case.
- Defendant A was born to a teenage mother with substance addiction and experienced neonatal withdrawal symptoms, including seizures, per defense presentation.
- Child Protective Services removed Defendant A from his biological mother at approximately age two and he was raised by adoptive parents.
- Defendant A reported that a cousin who lent him a car to sleep in was killed in 2016.
- Defendant A’s adoptive father died of a heart attack in 2017, per defense submission.
- Defendant A’s full brother was murdered in 2018, per defense submission.
- Defendant A’s adoptive brother died of a drug overdose in March 2020, per defense submission.

- Law enforcement responded to a report of an explosion on January 1, 2016 in Richlands, Virginia involving Defendant A.
- A resident reported seeing Defendant A running from the explosion scene and throwing something behind a building during the 2016 incident.
- In the 2016 incident, Defendant A initially claimed he had fired a shotgun at reactive targets (Tannerite).
- Defendant A later admitted he detonated a reactive target using makeshift blasting caps ignited by a fuse during the 2016 incident.
- The 2016 blasting caps were metal tubes filled with gunpowder.
- Search warrants in the 2016 case found modified CO2 cartridges, fragments of galvanized pipe, a galvanized pipe endcap, and tubes of Tannerite at Defendant A's residence.
- Defendant A pled nolo contendere to two counts of manufacturing explosive devices in connection with the 2016 incident and served probation until September 2019.
- On June 2, 2020, Defendant A was taken to the hospital with amputated fingers and shrapnel wounds to his face and neck.
- Defendant A told the FBI that his injuries were caused by a lawn mower accident, which was false.
- Agents determined that Defendant A accidentally detonated homemade TATP in his bedroom.
- A search of Defendant A's bedroom revealed blood, flesh, shrapnel, and tattered blinds indicative of an explosion.
- Agents found a large quantity of TATP at Defendant A's residence in June 2020.
- Agents found a box of rusty nails and a pouch of BBs at the residence.
- Agents found extensive writings and drawings relating to explosives, including instructions for making TATP and HMTD.
- Instructions for making HMTD and TATP were found on the wall above Defendant A's bed.
- Agents found PVC pipes and fuzing materials at the residence.
- Agents recovered a modified pressure-cooker device at the center of the case.
- A shed near the residence contained a makeshift lab for synthesizing explosive materials, including a hotplate.
- Agents found a note in the shed referencing approaching cheerleaders and declaring an intent to “make a statement like Elliot Rodger.”
- Forensic analysis identified five partially constructed improvised explosive devices among the collected items.
- Device #1 was a pressure cooker sealed with hardware and loaded with TATP and BBs.
- X-ray imagery showed BBs, nuts, bolts, and washers associated with Device #1.
- Device #2 was a large PVC pipe with a priming hole drilled in the side.
- Defendant A had drawings depicting where a priming hole should be placed on a cylindrical device.
- Device #3 was a plastic container with HMTD residue and a partial electric-fuzing system using a bridge wire.
- Device #4 was another PVC pipe with a partial electric-fuzing system.
- Device #5 was a plastic jar wrapped in tape and foil that contained TATP and two paper pouches.
- One pouch in Device #5 contained a low-explosive mixture of potassium chlorate and a starch-like fuel.
- Remnants of devices at the residence included CPVC pipe with blast damage.
- Other materials identified by the examiner included ammonium nitrate, sulfur, nails, BBs, and fuzes.
- Defendant A’s drawings described fuzing circuits with remote initiation and various types of explosive devices, including pressure-cooker and pipe-bomb IEDs.
- Defendant A used Facebook Messenger to communicate about his explosives experimentation, which he referred to as “science.”
- Defendant A performed more than a dozen searches related to explosives and exchanged photos and videos of explosives via social media.
- In May 2019, Defendant A described an incident at a parade where a bomb-sniffing dog detected traces of explosives on him; he told the handler he had been working with fertilizer.
- After his arrest and confinement, Defendant A communicated with Person A through phone, video calls, email, and letters.
- During a September 2020 video call, Defendant A asked Person A to send drugs into the jail using hand-written letters.
- Person A admitted to making a methamphetamine-laced letter intended for Defendant A.
- On a September 14, 2020 call, Person A told Defendant A the letter contained approximately $60 worth of methamphetamine and advised using the top half first.
- Person A stated she mailed the drug-laced letter on September 10, 2020 and would mail another letter with more drugs the next morning.
- Defendant A was diagnosed in childhood with Autism Spectrum Disorder, Attention-Deficit Disorder, and Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder, with sporadic treatment.
- Following an earlier arrest when he was 19, Defendant A was prescribed Adderall and reported heavy use and hyper-focus.
- Defendant A substantially increased Adderall use in 2019 and later began using methamphetamine regularly after his probation ended.
- Defendant A’s interest in explosives began at about age 19 and initially involved modifying fireworks and using gunpowder.
- After his 2016 arrest, a crisis evaluation documented that Defendant A reported setting off pipe bombs in a field he said belonged to family and that he never meant to harm anyone.
- The crisis evaluation noted Defendant A described becoming obsessed with watching things destroyed and chose an out-of-the-way field to experiment.
- Defendant A stated that a pressure cooker found in his closet had been considered for filling with explosives during a period of drug use, with the idea to detonate it in the woods with a long fuse.
- Defendant A stated he removed the lid and explosive powder from the pressure cooker when he sobered up.
- Defendant A reported watching videos and documentaries about Elliot Rodger years earlier but stated he did not agree with him.
- Defendant A stated the June 2020 explosion occurred while making a device using a pill bottle filled with ammonium nitrate and a center tube filled with TATP as an igniter.
- Defendant A stated he used scissors to remove tape from the device, and friction detonated the TATP, causing the explosion in his hand.
- The explosion blew out a bedroom window and perforated the blinds.
- Defendant A suffered the loss of four fingers on one hand due to the explosion.
- After the explosion, Defendant A was transported to the hospital by his mother.
- Photographs in the defense submission depict injuries to Defendant A’s left and right hands following the incident.
- Defendant A reported that he sought out remote fields to detonate explosive experiments and that a companion sometimes watched and listened from a field on her family’s property.

- Defendant pleaded guilty to attempting to provide material support and resources to a designated foreign terrorist organization under 18 U.S.C. § 2339B(a)(1).
- Count One carries a statutory maximum term of imprisonment of 20 years and a fine of up to $250,000.
- A term of supervised release of any term of years or life is authorized for the offense of conviction.
- At sentencing, the remaining counts in the indictment (Counts Two and Three) were to be dismissed.
- Between March 29, 2020, and May 24, 2020, the defendant attempted to provide material support and resources to ISIS.
- ISIS was designated as a foreign terrorist organization at all relevant times, and the defendant knew ISIS was designated and engaged in terrorist activity and terrorism.
- The defendant is a United States citizen who spent most of his life abroad and was convicted in 2015 in Saudi Arabia for advocating for Jaysh al-Islam, serving approximately three years before removal to the United States.
- Upon the defendant’s arrival to the United States in or around December 2018, the FBI began investigating potential material support activity.
- In or around April 2020, the defendant began to plan an attack in support of ISIS and began to acquire multiple firearms.
- The defendant researched and scouted potential attack locations in the Tampa Bay area.
- Since at least May 2019, the defendant consumed ISIS propaganda, pledged allegiance (bay’ah) to ISIS, and spoke about avenging U.S. actions including imprisoning Muslims and military operations in the Middle East.
- The defendant rehearsed statements to be made during an attack in support of ISIS.
- Between late April and May 2020, the defendant was the subject of court-authorized surveillance and had multiple recorded interactions with an FBI undercover employee and a confidential human source.
- The defendant attempted to buy guns from an undercover employee, including a fully automatic rifle, but was arrested on unrelated state charges while negotiating the purchase.
- After meeting a confidential human source, the defendant confided his affiliation with ISIS, plans to support ISIS, and plans to send money to ISIS.
- The defendant recruited a confidential human source to help with plans in support of ISIS and with robberies.
- The defendant asked a confidential source to obtain a Glock pistol (serial EZT996US) and an unregistered silencer (serial OSP9M‑5489).
- On May 24, 2020, the defendant was arrested when he took possession of the Glock pistol and silencer.
- During the charged period, the defendant attempted to provide personnel (including himself) and services to ISIS.
- The government seized approximately $1,352.73 from a bank account as property involved in the offense for forfeiture.
- The defendant agreed to forfeit all assets acquired or maintained with the intent and for the purpose of supporting, planning, conducting, or concealing any federal crime of terrorism.
- A SilencerCo Osprey silencer (serial OSP9M‑5489) was administratively forfeited after no claim was filed.
- A Glock 17 pistol (serial EZT996US) with magazines was administratively forfeited after no claim was filed.
- A Walther UZI .22‑caliber rifle (serial W1002377) was administratively forfeited after no claim was filed.
- A SCCY CPX‑1 pistol (serial 492782) was administratively forfeited after no claim was filed.
- A Glock 9mm pistol with no serial number and an unidentified suppressor were administratively forfeited after no claim was filed.
- A small crossbow and multiple knives and a tactical stun gun were administratively forfeited after no claim was filed.
- Ammunition including 44 rounds of 9mm and 26 rounds of .22 caliber were administratively forfeited after no claim was filed.
- Two smartphones were administratively forfeited after no claim was filed.
- Cash amounts of $297.18 and $300.00 were administratively forfeited after no claim was filed.
- The defendant’s items listed for forfeiture were acquired and maintained with the intent to support, plan, conduct, or conceal a federal crime of terrorism, or were derived from, involved in, or used or intended to be used to commit such a crime.
- By late April 2020, the defendant had acquired firearms and tactical gear including a bulletproof vest, solvent filters, and a facemask.
- The defendant possessed a .22‑caliber Uzi submachine gun and a nine‑millimeter pistol before his federal arrest.
- Before arrest, the defendant indicated interest in obtaining a fully automatic AK‑47.
- On April 30, 2020, a recording shows the defendant rehearsing commands and a message of revenge referencing Guantanamo and Muslims in Iraq and Syria, while handling what appears to be an Uzi.
- On May 16, 2020, a recording captures the defendant saying, "Today we kill from you guys like you killed from us," and pledging allegiance to the then‑ISIS leader.
- The defendant expressed admiration for Omar Mateen and discussed plans to kill as many Americans as possible.
- The defendant stated he wanted to kill at least 50 nonbelievers (“kuffar”).
- The defendant discussed targeting people affiliated with the military or government, including kidnapping and killing a high‑ranking U.S. military officer.
- On or about May 1, 2020, the defendant was arrested on a state charge of carrying a concealed weapon at his place of employment.
- While in state custody on the concealed‑weapon charge, the defendant called his sister and instructed her to hide or dispose of his guns, referring to them as his “toys.”
- Shortly after the phone call, the FBI obtained a .22‑caliber Uzi from the defendant’s residence and a 9mm pistol from his car.
- Minutes before arrest, the defendant had affixed a silencer to a Glock 19 pistol.
- The defendant worked at a large home‑improvement retailer around the time of his state arrest in May 2020.
- Defense materials state that discussions with the confidential source included ideas about robbing banks and tire shops.
- Defense materials acknowledge that the informant sold the defendant a pistol with an unregistered silencer attached before the arrest.
- A forensic psychological evaluation diagnosed the defendant with schizophrenia (unspecified), autism spectrum disorder (Level I), and adjustment disorder with anxiety and depressed mood.
- The evaluation documented paranoid beliefs that law enforcement or foreign agents were trying to harm him, including fear of being followed and delusions involving family members colluding with authorities.
- On May 5, 2020, the defendant voluntarily presented to a psychiatric facility reporting anxiety, sleep trouble, and flashbacks; he said he bought a gun because he was afraid.
- A urine test around May 2020 returned positive for methamphetamine and amphetamines, but a second test on May 5, 2020 was negative.
- During the May 2020 inpatient stay, he was diagnosed with PTSD and started on Paxil and Vistaril; antipsychotic medication was added the next day.
- He was discharged on May 8, 2020, with Prolixin and Cogentin and did not appear for a scheduled outpatient appointment on May 11, 2020.
- An outpatient treatment plan dated May 20, 2020, noted symptoms including paranoia, flashbacks, auditory hallucinations, and a history of suicidal and homicidal intent.
- In a January 2019 FBI interview, the defendant reported spending three years in a Saudi prison, describing torture methods and coerced confessions.
- The defendant reported being released from Saudi custody and removed to the United States in December 2018.
- In the 2019 interview, the defendant stated he was "full of hate" toward the Saudi government and expressed a desire for it to be destroyed by the United States along with jihadist groups there.
- By October 2019, the defendant was living in Tampa, Florida.
- After returning to the United States, the defendant accessed online chat rooms to view ISIS media and learn about explosives, suicide belts, and solvent traps.
- The defendant told a confidential source he wanted to join ISIS and described the group as "real followers."
- The defendant pledged allegiance to ISIS leader Abu Ibrahim al‑Hashimi prior to his arrest, as captured in a recorded statement.
- The defendant sought to obtain extended magazines and silencers in addition to firearms during discussions with an undercover agent.
- The defendant used the internet to acquire firearm components and other gear relevant to his plans.

- On the same day, Defendant A and Co-Defendant A were observed leaving the apartment with the same duffel bag and placing it into Defendant A's vehicle.
- The duffel bag contained approximately 30 kilograms of powder cocaine.
- The duffel bag contained approximately 737 grams of cocaine base.
- The duffel bag contained two firearms.
- Defendant A admitted the duffel bag contained a Colt MK-IV series 80 semi-automatic pistol.
- Defendant A admitted the duffel bag contained a Kimber 9mm Solo Carry STS semi-automatic pistol.

- Defendant A admitted he possessed the firearms in furtherance of his drug trafficking activities.
- Defendant A admitted he acted knowingly and intentionally, understanding that his actions violated the law.

- Approximately $9,250 in cash was seized from Defendant A's person on or about March 25, 2020.
- Approximately $2,190 in cash was seized from Defendant A's residence on or about March 25, 2020.
- Defendant A agreed that the seized currency constituted proceeds of narcotics distribution and facilitated or was intended to facilitate the offense.

- Defendant A agreed to forfeit the two pistols and associated accessories and ammunition involved in the offense.

- The PSR attributed three criminal history points to Defendant A based on the 1999 federal drug-trafficking conviction.
- Defendant A obtained early termination from supervised release on the prior federal case less than five years before the March 2020 conduct.

- On July 4–5, 2020, the defendant was present outside the Mark O. Hatfield Courthouse in Portland, Oregon.
- At approximately 3:00 a.m. on July 5, 2020, the defendant shined a high-powered green laser.
- The laser beam struck the arm of a federal law enforcement officer identified as AV1.
- The laser beam struck the eyes of the officer (AV1).
- AV1 was a uniformed Federal Protective Services Officer.
- AV1 was engaged in official duties providing security at the Mark O. Hatfield Courthouse when the laser incident occurred.
- When officers approached, the defendant ran but was chased down and arrested.

- Officers recovered the laser device from a tactical vest worn by the defendant during a search incident to arrest.
- The laser was located in the defendant’s possession when apprehended.
- The laser device was marked as an SDLaser 303.
- The laser’s label warned that it emitted radiation and to avoid direct eye exposure.
- An FBI expert report concluded the device is a Class IIIb laser product.
- According to the FBI expert report, the laser was capable of causing serious retinal injury with exposure times as short as approximately 10 milliseconds.
- AV1 moved to avoid prolonged exposure and further strikes from the laser.
- AV1 avoided serious eye damage by taking evasive actions and limiting exposure to his eyes.
- The plea agreement states AV1 suffered no injuries.
- The government’s memorandum states AV1 suffered no serious injuries.
- AV1 maintained visual contact on the defendant and observed the laser being shined in the direction of other officers.

- Defendant A agreed with Co‑Conspirators A, B, and C to engage in an identity fraud scheme using victims' personal identifying information to obtain bank loans and other goods in victims’ names.
- Conspirators obtained personal identifying information of real persons, including names, Social Security numbers, dates of birth, and addresses, over the Internet and elsewhere.
- Conspirators shared victims’ personal identifying information using electronic wires, including text messages and internet messaging apps.
- Conspirators used fictitious identification documents and stolen personal identifying information to obtain fraudulent bank loans and make other fraudulent purchases.
- Conspirators used stolen personal identifying information of owners of legitimate car dealerships to open fraudulent bank accounts in those dealerships’ names that were controlled by the conspirators.
- The fraudulent bank accounts in the names of car dealerships were used to receive proceeds of the identity fraud conspiracy and bank fraud scheme.
- Conspirators electronically submitted false loan applications with false and falsified supporting documents to federally insured banks and credit unions.
- Conspirators applied for personal loans and car loans as part of the scheme.
- It was part of the conspiracy that conspirators transmitted interstate wire communications to execute the fraudulent scheme.
- It was part of the conspiracy to execute a scheme to defraud federally insured banks and credit unions by means of materially false and fraudulent pretenses, representations, and promises.
- On March 15, 2020, in Mecklenburg County, Western District of North Carolina, Defendant A submitted or caused to be submitted a fraudulent personal loan application for $8,500 in the name of Victim A to Sharonview Federal Credit Union.
- On March 15, 2020, in the Western District of North Carolina, Defendant A knowingly transferred, possessed, and used the name and Social Security number of Victim A, knowing it belonged to another actual person, during and in relation to financial institution fraud.
- The total loss, including actual and attempted loss known as of the plea date, was more than $550,000 but less than $1,500,000.
- The scheme involved more than ten victims.
- Upon acceptance of the plea, the United States would submit a Statement of Relevant Conduct to the Probation Office.
- Defendant A has been held in custody at the Mecklenburg County Jail since his federal arrest on July 24, 2020.
- From November 30, 2020 to January 14, 2021, Defendant A was housed in a pod in Respiratory Isolation due to COVID-19 at the Mecklenburg County Jail.
- During the Respiratory Isolation period, inmates stayed in their cells for 23.5 hours per day.
- During the Respiratory Isolation period, no visitation sessions were allowed, either in person or by video.
- During the isolation period, Defendant A could not meet with his attorneys in person or by video at the jail.
- Extension of isolation beyond ten days indicated that additional pod inmates had tested positive for COVID-19.
- The jail pod design consisted of small, non-airtight cells surrounding a large common area intended for communal living.
- The Mecklenburg County Jail experienced another large COVID-19 outbreak in August and September 2021.
