- The case is in the United States District Court for the Central District of California under case number CR 20-289(A)-MWF.
- A plea agreement was filed on August 5, 2021.
- Defendant agreed to waive indictment and plead guilty to a one-count information.
- The information charges possession of an unregistered destructive device, specifically an incendiary device, in violation of 26 U.S.C. § 5861(d).
- The charged conduct occurred on or about June 4, 2020.
- The offense took place in Los Angeles County within the Central District of California.
- On June 4, 2020, Defendant entered the Sake House by Hikari restaurant in Santa Monica, California without authorization while the business was closed.
- The restaurant was closed due to civil unrest taking place in the city at that time.
- While inside the restaurant, Defendant possessed and used an incendiary device to ignite a fire.
- The fire grew quickly and enveloped the entire restaurant space and spread to other areas of the building housing the restaurant.
- The Santa Monica Fire Department responded to the fire using several fire trucks and numerous firefighting personnel.
- Due to safety concerns associated with ongoing civil unrest, the fire department abandoned the scene prematurely.
- The fire department returned several times throughout the night to extinguish additional flare-ups.
- Defendant knew the incendiary device was a destructive device when he possessed and used it.
- The incendiary device was not registered to Defendant in the National Firearms Registration and Transfer Record at the time of the offense.
- The information specifies that the destructive device was as defined in 26 U.S.C. §§ 5845(a)(8) and (f).
- The statutory maximum penalties for the offense include up to 10 years’ imprisonment.
- The statutory maximum supervised release term for the offense is three years.
- The statutory maximum fine for the offense is $250,000.
- A mandatory special assessment of $100 applies to the count of conviction.
- Defendant agreed to pay full restitution to victims of the offense of conviction.
- At the time of the plea agreement, the parties believed the applicable restitution amount was approximately $500,000, subject to change before sentencing.
- The government agreed to move to dismiss the underlying indictment at the time of sentencing.
- Defendant agreed that the Court could consider dismissed charges when determining the sentence.
- Defendant agreed to appear for all court appearances and surrender as ordered for service of sentence.
- Defendant agreed to obey all conditions of any bond and any other ongoing court orders in the matter.
- Defendant agreed not to commit any crime while this agreement is in effect.
- Defendant agreed to be truthful at all times with the United States Probation and Pretrial Services Office and the Court.
- Defendant agreed to pay the special assessment at or before sentencing unless unable to pay.
- Defendant admitted guilt to the charged offense and agreed the factual basis was sufficient to support the plea.
- The plea agreement was signed by Defendant and defense counsel on June 17, 2021.
- The information alleges the device had not been registered in the National Firearms Registration and Transfer Record as required by law.

- The revised filing notes that an entity identified as LCFRE has a loss figure included in the Second Revised Presentence Report.
- The restaurant fire occurred during a period of civil unrest in the city.
- The information and plea agreement identify the device involved as an incendiary device, a type of destructive device.

- On June 22, 2020, a crowd of approximately 200 people gathered in Lafayette Park in Washington, D.C.
- The Equestrian Statue of Andrew Jackson is located at the center of Lafayette Park and is federal property.
- On June 22, 2020, the Andrew Jackson Statue was surrounded by an eight-foot-high chain-link fence due to recent protests.
- United States Park Police and Metropolitan Police Department officers monitored the crowd at Lafayette Park on June 22, 2020.
- An organized effort attempted to remove the Andrew Jackson Statue from its base and damage it, including the cannons at the base.
- Some individuals at the statue interlocked arms to block police from approaching the statue.
- Other individuals climbed onto the statue and affixed white ropes, chains, and a yellow strap to it.
- Multiple people pulled on ropes, chains, and a strap in an attempt to pull the statue down.
- The National Park Service advised that historic cannon carriages at the statue’s base were irreparably damaged.
- NPS reported some parts of the statue were bent and other parts were damaged by blunt objects and chemicals.
- NPS estimated the replacement cost for the historic cannon carriages at $76,000.
- NPS estimated the cost to repair a bent portion of the statue at $2,000.
- Law enforcement reviewed online open source videos and MPD body-worn camera footage to identify individuals involved in damaging the statue.
- USPP and FBI disseminated lookout bulletins as part of the effort to identify participants.
- At the time of the Statement of Facts, officers had positively identified four individuals who participated in the attempt to remove the statue.
- Defendant B was observed on videos rocking two wooden wheels of a cannon at the base of the statue until they crumbled.
- Defendant B was seen helping individuals climb onto the statue during the incident.
- Defendant B was seen breaking off and destroying four wooden wheels from cannons at the base of the statue and overturning a cannon with another individual.
- Defendant B was seen pulling on ropes attached to the statue attempting to pull it off its base.
- Defendant B handed a hammer to another individual on the statue during the incident.
- Defendant B was seen pushing officers as they cleared individuals out of the park.
- An NBC news video included a clip where the subject later identified as Defendant B spoke to a reporter and was labeled as a demonstrator.
- Defendant C was captured affixing a white rope to the statue and later pulling on a yellow strap attached to the horse’s neck of the statue.
- A USPP lookout poster contained four pictures taken at the Jackson Statue on June 22, 2020, of the subject later identified as Defendant C.
- A USPP officer reported that Defendant C assaulted him by repeatedly punching his chest and torso and spitting at him while officers were clearing the area near the statue.
- The officer recognized Defendant C as his assailant later in the evening based on distinctive tattoos on his arms, chest, and back.
- A specific tattoo on Defendant C’s right shoulder (a demonic face with horns) matched a 2016 booking photo from Arlington County.
- Defendant D was observed picking up a long wooden board and attempting to pry the statue figure off its base.
- Defendant D was also observed pulling on a yellow strap attempting to pull the statue down.
- USPP disseminated numerous lookout bulletins to every sworn officer on June 25, 2020, to identify individuals involved in the statue destruction.
- Defendant D had been arrested on June 22, 2020, at approximately 8:30 p.m. in the area of H St., NW and Lafayette Square.
- Defendant D was transported to a hospital after claiming injury during apprehension, and two officers maintained watch over Defendant D for approximately three hours.
- Two officers later recognized Defendant D from a photograph based on a distinctive right-arm sleeve tattoo including a flower design.
- Defendant A was identified in open source videos as a person wearing a maroon button-down shirt, gray pants, a backpack, and a brown face mask during the incident.
- Video shows Defendant A holding a black pole with a strap tied to it, with the other end tied to the statue.
- In one video, at 0:02, Defendant A appears to be yelling, "get down off the statue."
- Another video shows Defendant A on the statue grounds pulling a rope tied to the statue.
- MPD body-worn camera footage captured digital images of Defendant A without a facemask holding a bottle of water.
- Law enforcement developed a potential identity of Defendant A as a student enrolled at The George Washington University in Washington, D.C.
- On June 26, 2020, a witness shown two images of Defendant A initially stated 75% certainty and then, upon seeing a second photo with better lighting, stated 100% certainty that it was Defendant A.
- A second witness, identified via Defendant A’s affiliation with a collegiate fraternal organization, said the person in the photograph looked like Defendant A after being asked about the organization.
- Two neighbors near Defendant A’s residence did not recognize the individual in a body-worn camera photograph but said that a group of students from a local university lived at the residence.
- The affiant asserted probable cause to issue arrest warrants for four suspects for violating 18 U.S.C. § 1361 (Destruction of Property of the United States) causing damage in excess of $1,000.
- The Statement of Facts was attested by telephone under Fed. R. Crim. P. 4.1 on June 26, 2020, and a U.S. Magistrate Judge approved it.
- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to one count of Restricted Building or Grounds, in violation of 18 U.S.C. §§ 1752(a)(2) and 1752(b)(2), charged in Count 1 of an Information.
- The White House complex, including the White House and its grounds, is a restricted area in Washington, D.C.
- Lafayette Park is located immediately across Pennsylvania Avenue NW and directly north of the White House and the White House complex.
- The Equestrian Statue of Andrew Jackson and historic cannons are displayed at the center of Lafayette Park, which is federal property.
- The statue is always surrounded by a low metal fence to prevent public access.
- Defendant A knew Lafayette Park, including the fenced-in area around the statue, is in close proximity to the White House and the White House complex, which are restricted areas.
- On June 22, 2020, the crowd dismantled large chain-link fences that surrounded the statue in order to access it.
- During the event, approximately $34,000 of damage was done to the statue and surrounding historic cannons and cannon carriages.
- The demonstration and ensuing damage required dozens of officers from USPP and MPD to respond to secure the park and property.
- Defendant A acknowledged the truth of the Statement of Offense after reviewing it with counsel and accepted it without reservation.
- The statutory maximum imprisonment for a violation of 18 U.S.C. §§ 1752(a)(2) and 1752(b)(2) is one year.
- The statutory maximum fine for the offense is $100,000.
- A term of supervised release of not more than one year may be imposed for the offense.
- A special assessment of $100 applies to the offense.
- A criminal complaint was filed against Defendant A on June 26, 2020.
- A federal grand jury returned a two-count indictment on July 16, 2020.
- On September 3, 2021, Defendant A pled guilty to a superseding Information charging one count of Restricted Building or Grounds under 18 U.S.C. §§ 1752(a)(2) and 1752(b)(2).
- The conduct at issue occurred on or about June 22, 2020, in Lafayette Park in Washington, D.C.
- The government described the aggregate damage from the breach as approximately $34,000 to the statue.
- The government filed surveillance video of the incident under seal as an exhibit, with Defendant A visible beginning at timestamp 4:38.
- The White House complex is a restricted area and Lafayette Park is directly north of the complex, on federal property, as reiterated by the government.
- The crowd dismantled chain-link fencing around the statue to access it before vandalism occurred, according to the government’s narrative.
- Dozens of USPP and MPD officers were required to secure the park and protect property during and after the breach on June 22, 2020.
- The superseding Information and plea in this case were docketed under Case No. 20-CR-117 (DLF).
- Defendant A acknowledged that the crowd’s actions caused approximately $34,000 of damage to the statue and surrounding cannons and carriages.
- The offense to which Defendant A pled guilty occurred in, or within proximity to, restricted building or grounds near the White House complex.
- As of sentencing, the government represented that this was Defendant A’s first criminal conviction.
- The government stated that Defendant A agreed to pay $2,600 in restitution to the U.S. Department of the Treasury pursuant to the plea agreement.
- A supplemental filing clarified the government’s sentencing recommendation by removing a supervised release component initially suggested in error.
- The case caption includes multiple co-defendants in addition to Defendant A.
- The elements of the offense of Restricted Building or Grounds include disorderly or disruptive conduct with intent to impede or disrupt government functions in or near restricted grounds, and that the conduct in fact impeded or disrupted such functions.
- Defendant B was identified through body-worn camera footage and open source videos, and matched to a driver’s license image.
- Defendant C was tentatively identified through body-worn camera images showing distinct tattoos and confirmed by comparison to a prior booking photo.
- Defendant D was identified after officers who had monitored him for hours during a hospital detail recognized his facial features and right-arm sleeve tattoo.
- Defendant A was identified with assistance from witnesses familiar with a collegiate fraternal organization and from neighbors who associated the residence with students.
- The government’s sentencing narrative reiterated that the activity occurred mere yards from the White House and the White House complex.

- Defendant A was charged in a one-count indictment with forcibly assaulting a Deputy United States Marshal with a deadly and dangerous weapon while the deputy was engaged in official duties, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1) and (b).
- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Count One of the indictment charging assault of a federal officer with use of a deadly and dangerous weapon, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1) and (b).
- The offense conduct occurred on or about July 11, 2020 and involved a forcible assault on a Deputy United States Marshal.
- The statutory maximum penalty for the offense is twenty years of imprisonment.
- The statutory maximum fine for the offense is $250,000.
- The offense carries a maximum of three years of supervised release.
- A $100 special assessment applies to the count of conviction.
- Defendant A agreed to pay the $100 special assessment by the time of entry of the guilty plea or explain to the Court why this could not be done.
- The plea agreement was made under Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 11(c)(1)(B).
- The prosecution agreed not to bring additional charges in the District of Oregon arising out of the investigation known at the time of the plea agreement.
- Defendant A agreed to abandon a 4‑pound blacksmith's hammer as property.
- Defendant A signed the plea agreement on August 8, 2021.
- Defendant A attempted to forcibly break into a closed federal building by striking a barricaded door with a four-pound hammer in the early morning hours of July 11, 2020.
- After piercing the door, a team of Deputy U.S. Marshals attempted to exit to prevent trespass and further damage.
- Defendant A attacked the lead Deputy U.S. Marshal and repeatedly struck the deputy with the four‑pound hammer.
- Deputies subdued and arrested Defendant A after the assault at the courthouse door.
- The incident occurred at the Mark O. Hatfield United States Courthouse in downtown Portland, Oregon.
- During summer 2020, the courthouse had sustained damage, including destruction of large glass windows and commercial-grade fireworks mortars launched into the lobby and upper floors.
- Plywood barricades were erected around the entryways to the courthouse and the plywood door on the south side (Main Street) was reinforced with protective bars when closed.
- On July 10–11, 2020, a Deputy U.S. Marshals team was tasked with protecting the courthouse and initially remained inside on alert to respond to attacks.
- At about 1:00 a.m. on July 11, 2020, Defendant A approached the south-side plywood door and beat on it with his fists but could not penetrate the barricade.
- The U.S. Marshals team assembled inside opposite the door and was instructed not to exit unless the building was physically breached.
- An unknown associate provided Defendant A with a four‑pound blacksmith hammer during the incident.
- Defendant A used the four‑pound hammer to strike the same plywood door in an attempt to break in.
- Defendant A observed through a small crack in the barricade that a U.S. Marshals team was on the other side of the door and only reinforced plywood separated them.
- During the pounding, Defendant A yelled at the team, including statements that he could see them through the cracks and that they would have to kill him.
- Defendant A eventually broke open a hole in the plywood door, creating a physical breach.
- At approximately 1:15 a.m., the Marshals team was instructed to deploy by exiting the courthouse to prevent trespass and further damage.
- Defendant A is approximately 6'2" tall and weighed about 320 pounds at the time, and leaned against the door to prevent the team from exiting.
- As the door opened, Defendant A held the hammer over his head for about ten seconds, waiting to strike as the Marshals exited, while yelling that they would have to kill him.
- The first deputy through the door was a local deputy and the head of the team.
- Defendant A immediately swung the hammer at the deputy’s head and upper body, striking the deputy in the shoulder and back.
- Defendant A broke away, backed up, and delivered a full overhead swing with the hammer, striking the deputy in the back of the head and upper back.
- The Marshals subdued Defendant A by taking him to the ground and restraining him with plastic zip ties before bringing him into the courthouse where he was arrested.
- Onlookers recorded video of the assault and posted it to Twitter, and the FBI later slowed a video of the attack to 20% of its actual speed.
- The victim deputy wore protective gear including body armor and a helmet during the attack.
- Defendant A has no known criminal history and records indicate this was his first arrest.
- Defendant A was detained pending trial as a danger to the community and a risk of flight, and this detention order was affirmed by the Ninth Circuit.
- Defendant A has remained in custody since his arrest in July 2020.
- Defendant A is originally from Texas and resided with his father in California until age nineteen.
- In May 2019, Defendant A moved to Bend, Oregon for a few months, returned to Texas until May 2020, then returned briefly to Bend before relocating to Portland.
- At the time of his arrest, Defendant A and Spouse A lived nomadically in a bus and had been parking the bus on a street in North Portland.
- Multiple firearms were found in Defendant A’s bus, including two handguns and a shotgun.
- Defendant A was unemployed at the time and experienced anger episodes associated with ADHD.
- Shortly before his arrest, Defendant A quit two prior jobs after working at each for only a few weeks.
- Other than Spouse A, Defendant A had no family ties in Oregon at the time of his arrest.
- Defendant A acknowledged daily marijuana use prior to his arrest and reported experimenting with LSD and hallucinogenic mushrooms.
- Spouse A reported that Defendant A drinks alcohol to excess when he does consume alcohol.
- A defense-retained forensic evaluator interviewed Defendant A twice and diagnosed Major Depressive Disorder, ADHD, and severe substance abuse disorders related to alcohol, cannabis, and hallucinogens.
- The defense evaluator recommended mental health and residential drug treatment due to the severity of polysubstance use and inability to maintain sobriety in the community.
- The defense evaluator’s diagnosis appeared to rely largely on statements Defendant A made during their second interview.
- Shortly after arrest, Defendant A told a pretrial services officer that he rarely consumes alcohol and denied any history of alcohol abuse.
- In the pretrial interview, Defendant A reported daily cannabinoid use during the four months prior to his arrest.
- Spouse A stated that Defendant A had been a daily cannabis user since high school.
- Defendant A reported experimenting with LSD and mushrooms at age 22 with no further use since that time, according to pretrial reporting.
- The defense evaluator reported that Defendant A minimized his alcohol and hallucinogen use during their first meeting but acknowledged heavier use during the second meeting.
- The government reported no evidence that Defendant A consumed alcohol in the hours leading up to his arrest during the assault incident.
- Defendant A reported that he had ingested marijuana sometime prior to his arrest on July 11, 2020.
- Defendant A reported never having experienced serious withdrawal symptoms from drugs or alcohol.
- Defendant A was described as polite and cooperative after his arrest.
- During the summer protests in Portland, agitators had baited law enforcement to exit the courthouse and officers were often attacked with fireworks, lasers, bottles, and other objects when outside.
- Rioters had previously breached and set fires inside the Multnomah County Detention Center located directly across the street from the courthouse.

- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to count seven (bank fraud) and count ten (transactional money laundering).
- Count seven charged bank fraud in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1344(2).
- Count ten charged transactional money laundering in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1957.
- The statutory maximum for the bank fraud count is 30 years' imprisonment.
- The statutory maximum for the § 1957 money laundering count is 10 years' imprisonment.
- The combined maximum imprisonment exposure for both counts is 50 years.
- Defendant A agreed that full restitution would be required and that the parties then believed the amount was approximately $282,566.89.
- On April 7, 2020, Defendant A submitted a PPP loan application for Aviron Group claiming 33 employees and $236,250 in quarterly payroll expenses.
- On April 9, 2020, Defendant A submitted a PPP loan application for Aviron Releasing claiming 33 employees and $224,418 in quarterly payroll expenses.
- On April 24, 2020, Defendant A submitted a PPP loan application for Aviron Licensing claiming 33 employees and $224,418 in quarterly payroll expenses.
- Bank A approved the three PPP loan applications and on May 1, 2020 credited the companies’ operating accounts with $1,712,715 in PPP proceeds.
- On May 1, 2020, $561,375.52 was transferred from the Aviron Licensing account to the Aviron Group account.
- On May 1, 2020, $561,062.35 was transferred from the Aviron Releasing account to the Aviron Group account.
- On May 4, 2020, $966,000 of PPP proceeds were transferred from the Aviron Group account to Defendant A’s personal checking account at Bank A.
- At least $409,103 of PPP proceeds were used for Defendant A’s personal transfers and expenditures.
- Defendant A transferred $185,000 of PPP proceeds to accounts held by Spouse A.
- Defendant A transferred $98,000 of PPP proceeds to another personal account in Defendant A’s name.
- More than $85,000 of PPP proceeds were used to pay down credit cards held by Defendant A and Spouse A.
- PPP proceeds were spent on utilities, mortgage expenses, and personal attorney fees.
- None of the PPP proceeds were used to pay employees of the Aviron companies.
- Federal agents seized $308,058.11 of PPP proceeds from the Aviron Group operating account.
- Defendant A returned $1,122,090 of PPP funds to Bank A.
- The resulting loss from the PPP scheme to the SBA, which guaranteed the loans, was $282,566.89.
- Bank A was a financial institution insured by the FDIC.
- The PPP loan applications were submitted to Bank A and to the SBA as the administrator of the program.
- By early 2020, Aviron Group, Aviron Releasing, and Aviron Licensing had no employees and were no longer operational.
- By early 2020, Aviron Pictures had fewer than 10 employees.
- In December 2019, Defendant A was terminated from a managerial position at Aviron Pictures after BlackRock discovered funds had been stolen from its investments.
- On May 1, 2020, Defendant A engaged in monetary transactions through a financial institution involving more than $10,000 in property derived from bank fraud.
- Defendant A operated Aviron Pictures and related entities Aviron Group, Aviron Releasing, and Aviron Licensing.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit $308,058.11 seized from a JP Morgan Chase account ending in 3760 as criminal proceeds.
- The USAO agreed to move to dismiss the remaining counts of the indictment at sentencing.
- The USAO agreed to recommend the sentence run concurrently with the sentence imposed in United States v. Defendant A, CR 20-320-PAE (S.D.N.Y.).
- Each count of conviction carries a mandatory special assessment of $100.
- The PPP loan applications were processed through JPMorgan Chase (JPMC).
- On May 13, 2020, Defendant A instructed JPMC to return the PPP loans for Aviron Licensing and Aviron Releasing.
- In the May 13, 2020 email, Defendant A stated an intention to use the remaining funds to retain/rehire Aviron employees and seek loan forgiveness after eight weeks.
- On May 14, 2020, Defendant A and former Aviron personnel scheduled a Zoom meeting to discuss relaunching a production and distribution company.
- Witness A (a former Aviron Pictures executive) was terminated around January 2020 as part of layoffs.
- Witness A stated they were only employed by Aviron Pictures, not by Aviron Group, Aviron Releasing, or Aviron Licensing.
- Around March 2020, Defendant A told Witness A he might start a new company and could get PPP loans to fund it.
- Witness A stated Defendant A had lost control of Aviron Pictures by December 2019 and was not in control of it in April and May 2020.

- At the SDNY sentencing, the parties acknowledged the government would recommend a concurrent sentence in the California case.
- The SDNY court stated Defendant A forged the signature of a BlackRock portfolio manager to release UCC liens and then sold those assets.

- Cairn Capital provided approximately $20 million in lending to Aviron related to the film Serenity.
- A $3 million final advance from Cairn was conditioned on performance metrics, and Defendant A provided fabricated reporting to induce release of that advance.

- Defendant A stated in the PPP applications that the loan proceeds would be spent on business operational expenses.
- Defendant A acknowledged acting with specific intent to defraud Bank A and the SBA in connection with the PPP applications.
- The PPP loan proceeds were credited to the operating accounts of Aviron Group, Aviron Releasing, and Aviron Licensing on May 1, 2020.

- Defendant A agreed to waive indictment and plead guilty to a Superseding Information charging conspiracy to distribute and possess with intent to distribute 500 grams or more of cocaine and cocaine base (Count One).
- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to being a felon in possession of a firearm (Count Two) in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(1).
- Defendant A expressly admitted that he committed the charged offenses knowingly and intentionally.
- On February 2, 2021, Defendant A pled guilty pursuant to a Rule 11(c)(1)(C) plea agreement.
- Count One carries a mandatory minimum term of 5 years and a maximum of 40 years of imprisonment.
- Count One carries a supervised release term of at least 4 years up to life.
- Count One carries a maximum fine of $5,000,000 and a $100 special assessment.
- Count Two carries a maximum term of 10 years of imprisonment.
- Count Two carries up to 3 years of supervised release, a maximum fine of $250,000, and a $100 special assessment.
- The plea agreement was entered pursuant to Fed. R. Crim. P. 11(c)(1)(C).
- The case arose from a joint Drug Enforcement Administration and Boston Police Department investigation into a drug trafficking organization led by Person B.
- Investigators identified Defendant A as a member of the Favre Street Gang and an associate of the Wainwright Street Gang.
- Investigators classified Defendant A as a wholesale supplier of cocaine to Person B, Person C, and others.
- On June 13, 2020, intercepted communications indicated Defendant A supplied his own customers with cocaine or cocaine base and conducted deals throughout the day.
- In October 2019 and February 2020, Defendant A was supplied with cocaine by Person D.
- Defendant A prepared cocaine base for his own customers and for Person B, who had trouble preparing cocaine base himself.
- In August 2019, Defendant A asked Person B for feedback on the batch he had prepared, referring to it as "spaghetti," and Person B responded positively.
- In October 2019, Person B asked Defendant A to come over and prepare cocaine base, saying "come through and make your magic."
- On June 13, 2020, Defendant A told a customer he would let him know as soon as he put the product together.
- On June 13, 2020, Defendant A’s phone registered location data at a hotel in Canton where investigators later recovered paraphernalia from his room.
- Defendant A supplied Person C; Person C told Person B he would ask "Bags," referring to Defendant A.
- Defendant A has a tattoo reading "Money Bags" on his left wrist.
- In April 2020, Defendant A’s phone pinged in California during a call where Person C said Defendant A was out west trying to obtain two kilograms of cocaine.

- Defendant agreed to plead guilty to Count One (wire fraud) and Count Six (securities fraud).
- Wire fraud under 18 U.S.C. § 1343 carries a statutory maximum of 20 years of imprisonment.
- Securities fraud under 15 U.S.C. §§ 78j(b) and 78ff carries a statutory maximum of 20 years of imprisonment.
- For the wire fraud count, the maximum fine is the greater of $250,000 or twice the gross gain or loss.
- For the securities fraud count, the maximum fine is the greater of $5,000,000 or twice the gross gain or loss.
- Each count carries a maximum supervised release term of three years.
- Defendant admitted the Statement of Facts and agreed it establishes guilt beyond a reasonable doubt.
- Restitution is mandatory and the defendant agrees restitution is at least $18,500,000.
- Defendant agrees to forfeit fraud-related assets and to a forfeiture money judgment of $3,700,000.
- The parties stipulated that actual proceeds the defendant obtained from the offense were $3,700,000 and are not available, and substitute asset provisions under 21 U.S.C. § 853(p)(1) apply.
- The United States agreed to move to dismiss the remaining counts at the conclusion of sentencing.
- Defendant agreed that victims of conduct in the charging instrument, Statement of Facts, or related conduct are entitled to restitution as determined by the Court.
- Defendant agreed to identify assets and provide financial information within 14 days of a request by the government or Probation Office.
- Defendant agreed to participate in the Bureau of Prisons’ Inmate Financial Responsibility Program if incarcerated.
- Trustify, Inc. was incorporated in Delaware in 2015.
- Trustify’s original principal place of business was in Washington, D.C., and the company moved its offices to Arlington, Virginia, in 2016.
- Defendant resided in Alexandria, Virginia beginning in November 2015.
- Trustify’s primary business was connecting individual and corporate clients with private investigators.
- Trustify raised money by selling shares of privately held stock to investors through private placements under Regulation D, Rule 506(b).
- From at least February 2015 through at least February 2019, the defendant engaged in a scheme to defraud investors using interstate wires.
- During the same period, the defendant used manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase and sale of securities, including untrue statements and omissions.
- The defendant misrepresented Trustify’s growth, size, and performance, and the intended use of investor funds.
- The defendant provided investors with financial statements that falsely inflated Trustify’s performance, including monthly and annual revenues.
- In investor communications, the defendant falsely inflated Trustify’s revenue growth.
- The defendant made false statements about the number of private investigators and employees to exaggerate Trustify’s size.
- The defendant concealed the large amounts of money he received from Trustify and misrepresented his compensation structure.
- The defendant diverted investor funds for personal benefit, including purchasing an expensive home in Alexandria, private jet travel, and furnishing a seaside vacation home.
- Between March 2015 and December 2015, over $500,000 was transferred from Trustify to GoLean DC, LLC, an entity controlled by the defendant.
- Over the course of the scheme, the defendant paid himself several million dollars via transfers from Trustify and personal credit card charges paid with Trustify funds.
- Company C was an investment firm in New York, and Individual 3 worked there and communicated with the defendant about a potential investment in Trustify.
- On March 1, 2017, the defendant emailed Individual 3 from Arlington, Virginia, attaching financial statements overstating certain 2016 monthly revenues by more than 300%.
- On May 22, 2017, after receiving false statements, Company C invested approximately $4.75 million in Trustify.
- On May 30, 2018, the defendant provided a written presentation overstating Trustify’s 2017 monthly revenues by more than 300%.
- On June 14, 2018, the defendant sent a fraudulent email that appeared to come from Individual 4 at Company D, falsely stating a $7.5 million wire was being sent to lead a Series B round.
- The June 14, 2018 message was sent from an account the defendant created to impersonate Individual 4, and Company D was not investing.
- Later on June 14, 2018, Company C invested an additional approximately $2 million in Trustify.
- On or about October 14, 2018, the defendant sent a third-quarter investor update falsely claiming an agreement with Client A to help with a 700,000-candidate security clearance backlog for a federal agency.
- Trustify had not entered into any agreement with Client A as claimed in the October 2018 investor update.
- The defendant also made material misstatements to investors by phone and during in-person meetings.
- Over the course of the scheme, more than 90 individuals and entities invested approximately $18.5 million in Trustify, which was lost due to the fraud and the failure of the business.
- The defendant personally derived at least $3,700,000 in proceeds from the fraud.
- The defendant acknowledged that his actions were knowing and deliberate, not the result of mistake or accident.
- The defendant stipulated that the Statement of Facts is true and accurate.
- The defendant agreed to timely notify authorities of his intention to plead guilty, assisting the government in avoiding trial preparation.
- A sentencing hearing was scheduled for March 26, 2021 in this case.
- According to the sentencing materials, the presentence report noted that the defendant complied with all conditions of pretrial release and timely notified the government of his intention to plead guilty.
- The plea agreement was filed December 3, 2020, and bears the case number 1:20-cr-167 in the Eastern District of Virginia, Alexandria Division.
- The Statement of Facts was filed on December 3, 2020 in the same case.

- The case is in the Eastern District of Wisconsin.
- The defendant was charged in a single-count indictment with violating 18 U.S.C. § 39A.
- The indictment alleged that on or about June 7, 2020, the defendant knowingly aimed a laser pointer at an aircraft and its flight path in the special aircraft jurisdiction of the United States.
- The defendant agreed to plead guilty to the single count in the indictment.
- The defendant acknowledged that the facts in Attachment A are true and establish guilt beyond a reasonable doubt.
- The offense carries a maximum penalty of five years’ imprisonment.
- The offense carries a maximum fine of $250,000.
- The offense carries a mandatory special assessment of $100.
- The offense carries a maximum supervised release term of up to three years.
- The government would have to prove that the defendant knowingly aimed the beam of a laser pointer at an aircraft or its flight path to sustain the charge.
- The government would have to prove that at the time of the act, the aircraft was in the special aircraft jurisdiction of the United States.
- The parties agreed to waive the standard presentence report disclosure timing in Rule 32 in favor of a schedule set by the court.
- The defendant agreed to pay the $100 special assessment prior to or at sentencing.
- The defendant agreed to pay restitution as ordered by the court and to cooperate in efforts to collect any restitution obligation.
- On the night of June 3 to early June 4, 2020, an FBI aircraft operated over Milwaukee within the special aircraft jurisdiction of the United States.
- During the June 3–4, 2020 flight, law enforcement observed a green laser targeting their aircraft from the ground.
- Law enforcement used a forward-looking infrared (FLIR) camera to capture the origin point of the laser during the June 3–4, 2020 incident.
- The FLIR footage showed the laser originating from one of three individuals on the lower porch of a Milwaukee residence.
- On June 6, 2020, officers compared FLIR footage to the residence at the intersection and confirmed a match.
- On the evening of June 7, 2020, two vehicles registered to the defendant were observed parked near the identified residence.
- On the evening of June 7, 2020, an FBI aircraft again observed a green laser targeting it over Milwaukee.
- During the June 7 incident, FLIR footage showed the laser emitting from one of two individuals on the lower porch of the same residence.
- Ground officers observed an unidentified female and a male matching the defendant’s description pointing a handheld object toward the sky on June 7, 2020.
- Based on the investigation, law enforcement obtained an arrest warrant for the defendant on June 8, 2020.
- On June 8, 2020, agents arrested the defendant and searched his residence.
- During the June 8, 2020 search, agents recovered the defendant’s cell phone and a green-light laser pointer.
- Agents interviewed the defendant and his spouse on June 8, 2020.
- The defendant’s spouse stated that the defendant had previously purchased multiple green laser pointers.
- The defendant’s spouse stated that the defendant used laser pointers to aim at objects in the sky.
- The defendant’s spouse stated that the defendant pointed a laser pointer at objects in the sky on June 7, 2020.
- The defendant’s spouse described that the defendant used a telescope to observe aircraft and then point his laser at them.
- After waiving Miranda rights, the defendant admitted firing his laser pointer into the sky on the evening of June 7, 2020, at some sort of aircraft.
- The defendant initially claimed he believed he was aiming at UFOs and did not understand he was targeting an aircraft.
- The defendant eventually acknowledged he understood the objects he targeted on June 7 were traditional aircraft.
- Law enforcement personnel reported that pointing a laser at an aircraft is dangerous.
- Law enforcement personnel reported that the laser incidents described detracted from their law enforcement mission at those times.
- The defense memorandum states the defendant shined a laser pointer at aircraft flying over Milwaukee in summer 2020.
- The defense memorandum indicates the defendant pleaded guilty in this matter.
- The defendant was born in Massachusetts and is ethnically Puerto Rican.
- The defendant moved to the Milwaukee area as a toddler.
- The defendant grew up on Milwaukee’s south side and later in Racine after his parents separated.
- At age 16, the defendant sustained misdemeanor convictions from fights and served a period of probation.
- The defendant left high school, enrolled in a GED program, and began working to help his mother.
- The defendant has experience as a welder and metal fabricator, including contract work through a placement firm.
- The defendant and spouse have two children who are teenagers.
- The defendant and family lived for many years in a small apartment above a church.
- During the June 2020 period, protests in Milwaukee centered around a police district station, with late-night activity and aerial surveillance by law enforcement.
- The defense memorandum states the defendant knowingly pointed his laser at an FBI surveillance aircraft while standing on his porch late at night.
- The defense memorandum states the defendant knew the law prohibited shining lasers at aircraft and that his actions were illegal and dangerous.
- According to the defense memorandum, the defendant struck an FBI surveillance plane with a laser on at least two occasions.
- The defense memorandum reports that each laser incident caused the aircraft crew to divert from their assigned task of monitoring protests.
- The defense memorandum states that during the second incident, an FBI agent on the ground saw the defendant wielding the laser.
- The day after the second incident, the FBI executed a warrant at the defendant’s home and arrested him; he confessed thereafter.
- The defense memorandum reports that the defendant’s spouse was handcuffed and questioned during the search.
- According to the defense memorandum, the defendant’s arrest received widespread media coverage locally and nationally.
- No members of the aircraft’s flight crew were injured in the incidents, according to the defense memorandum.
- The defense memorandum states the FBI did not find evidence that the defendant was affiliated with any protest group or intended to target law enforcement.
- Defense counsel submitted an apology letter from the defendant to the FBI prior to sentencing.
- The defense memorandum reports that the FAA imposed an $8,000 civil penalty on the defendant for the laser incidents.
- According to the defense memorandum, the FAA civil penalty will be collected through wage garnishment.
- The defense memorandum notes the defendant experienced employment difficulties after his arrest and one of the family’s cars was repossessed.
- An employer letter in the PSR described the defendant as very reliable with good attendance and a great attitude, per the defense memorandum.
- The defense memorandum states the defendant and family were amateur stargazers and used a telescope, which relates to his possession of a laser pointer.

- The case is in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia under Criminal Case No. 20-cr-165 (JEB).
- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to one count of making or using a false writing or document in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1001(a)(3).
- On August 19, 2020, the guilty plea was filed in court.
- Defendant A signed the Statement of Offense on August 14, 2020.
- Defendant A signed the Plea Agreement acceptance on August 14, 2020.
- The statutory maximum penalty for a violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1001(a)(3) is up to five years of imprisonment.
- The offense carries a maximum fine of $250,000.
- The offense carries a maximum supervised release term of up to three years.
- The offense requires a $100 special assessment.
- Defendant A is an attorney admitted to practice law in November 2007.
- From July 12, 2015 to September 21, 2019, Defendant A served as an FBI Assistant General Counsel in the National Security and Cyber Law Branch.
- Defendant A’s office was located in the J. Edgar Hoover Building in Washington, D.C.
- As part of his duties, Defendant A assisted FBI agents and NSD in preparing FISA applications.
- On July 31, 2016, the FBI opened the Crossfire Hurricane investigation into possible coordination between individuals associated with the 2016 presidential campaign and the Russian government.
- By August 16, 2016, the FBI had opened individual cases on four U.S. persons under Crossfire Hurricane, including “Individual #1.”
- Defendant A was assigned to provide legal support to FBI personnel working on Crossfire Hurricane and served as a liaison to another U.S. government agency.
- Four FISA applications targeting Individual #1 were approved: October 21, 2016; January 12, 2017; April 7, 2017; and June 29, 2017.
- The fourth FISA authorization targeting Individual #1 expired on September 22, 2017.
- Each FISA application alleged probable cause that Individual #1 was a knowing agent of a foreign power, specifically Russia.
- On August 17, 2016, the OGA provided a memorandum indicating Individual #1 had been approved as an “operational contact” for the OGA from 2008 to 2013 and had provided information about contacts with certain Russian intelligence officers.
- The first three FISA applications did not include Individual #1’s history or status with the OGA.
- Before submitting the fourth FISA application, an FBI Supervisory Special Agent (SSA) asked Defendant A to inquire with the OGA whether Individual #1 had ever been a “source.”
- On June 15, 2017, Defendant A emailed an OGA Liaison asking whether Individual #1 was a source in any capacity and what the OGA’s digraph designation meant.
- The OGA Liaison replied on June 15, 2017, listing OGA documents (including the August 17 Memorandum) and stating the OGA encrypts U.S. persons when they provide reporting and that the liaison’s recollection was that Individual #1 was or is [digraph].
- Defendant A responded that the definition of the digraph answered the team’s questions.
- Defendant A told DOJ OIG he did not recall reviewing the documents referenced by the OGA Liaison and did not have access to them in OGC space, although they were available elsewhere in the Hoover Building upon request.
- On June 19, 2017, the SSA asked Defendant A via instant message for an update on the OGA source request regarding Individual #1.
- In these messages, Defendant A told the SSA that Individual #1 was a “subsource” and “was never a source,” and that the OGA had explicitly confirmed Individual #1 was never a source.
- When the SSA asked if there was written confirmation, Defendant A said he had it and would forward the OGA email.
- Immediately after the instant messages on June 19, 2017, Defendant A forwarded the OGA Liaison’s June 15 email to the SSA with the added words “and not a ‘source.’”
- The alteration made it appear the OGA Liaison had written that Individual #1 was not a source for the OGA.
- The SSA relied on the altered email and signed the fourth FISA application on June 29, 2017; that application did not include Individual #1’s OGA history or status.
- Defendant A admitted that the original June 15, 2017 OGA email did not contain the words “not a source” and that he knowingly and willfully inserted that materially false statement.
- The altered email pertained to matters within the jurisdiction of both the executive and judicial branches of the U.S. Government.
- The OGA uses a specific two-letter “digraph” designation to describe a U.S. person approved for operational contact.
- Defendant A acknowledged in his initial email to the OGA that whether Individual #1 was a source would need to be disclosed in the next FISA renewal.
- On June 15, 2017, after receiving the OGA response, Defendant A forwarded the OGA Liaison’s email to the case agent and a covering supervisor, omitting his initial inquiry email from the chain.
- On June 16, 2017, Defendant A spoke by phone with the DOJ Office of Intelligence attorney and forwarded the OGA Liaison’s email; the attorney replied, “thanks I think we are good and no need to carry it any further.”
- In the June 19, 2017 instant messages, Defendant A told the SSA that the OGA had confirmed explicitly that Individual #1 was never a source and that he would forward written confirmation.
- The altered email sent to the SSA on June 19, 2017 inserted the phrase “and not a ‘source’” into the OGA Liaison’s message.
- The DOJ OIG later discovered the alteration; when confronted, Defendant A initially said he was not certain how the alteration occurred and then acknowledged making the change.
- The OGA Liaison told the DOJ OIG that, using FBI terminology, Individual #1 had been a source for the OGA and that it was incorrect to describe Individual #1 as a subsource.
- The DOJ OIG and participants in the application process indicated that Individual #1’s prior relationship with the OGA should have been disclosed to the FISC because it bore on probable cause.
- According to the SSA, the words “not a source” in the altered email were the most important part for resolving the issue; without them he would have sought further clarification.
- After the OIG’s findings, DOJ notified the FISC of the alteration and other misstatements and omissions identified in the four FISA applications targeting Individual #1.
- DOJ later acknowledged that for the final two FISA applications targeting Individual #1 there was insufficient predication to establish probable cause that Individual #1 was acting as an agent of a foreign power.
- The FISC ordered the government to identify all prior matters before the FISC in which Defendant A participated and verify that submissions in those matters were complete and truthful.
- The Statement of Offense describes the elements the government would have to prove for a violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1001(a)(3), including materiality and that the writing pertained to a matter within the jurisdiction of the executive or judicial branch.
- In April and May 2017, Individual #1 publicly stated that he or she had assisted the U.S. government in the past.
- Defendant A understood that if Individual #1 had been a source for the OGA, that information needed to be provided to the court and would drastically change handling of the FISA application.
- Defendant A agreed in the Plea Agreement to be debriefed by the FBI regarding its review of FISA matters and any information he possessed that should be brought to the FISC.
- Defendant A’s role included communicating with the OGA to raise questions or concerns for the Crossfire Hurricane team.
- The OGA Liaison’s June 15, 2017 email offered to provide a formal definition for use in a FISA if needed.
- According to the government’s summary, when interviewed by OIG, the OGA Liaison said there was no basis for Defendant A to conclude, based on their communications and the August 17 Memorandum, that Individual #1 never had a direct relationship with the OGA.
- The SSA stated that if the OGA had said Individual #1 was not a source then the team would be fine; otherwise he would have sought clarification.
- The government’s memorandum states that the DOJ OIG review identified numerous misstatements and omissions in the four FISA applications on Individual #1.
- The June 29, 2017 fourth FISA application did not disclose Individual #1’s prior relationship with the OGA.
- The Plea Agreement states the government would not further prosecute Defendant A for the specific conduct set forth in the Statement of Offense.
- Defendant A waived indictment and agreed to plead to a criminal information.
- Defendant A acknowledged in the Statement of Offense that the proffered facts provided a sufficient legal basis for his guilty plea.
- Defendant A affirmed he read and accepted the Statement of Offense and did so voluntarily.

- The case is in the Eastern District of Wisconsin under docket number 20-CR-120.
- A two-count indictment charged distribution of child pornography in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 2252A(a)(2)(A).
- The indictment was filed on July 21, 2020.
- Defendant A was arrested at his home in Lake Havasu City, Arizona, on July 28, 2020.
- Defendant A made an initial appearance in the District of Arizona on July 30, 2020.
- Defendant A was released on a personal recognizance bond with Adam Walsh Act conditions, including electronic monitoring, on August 14, 2020.
- On August 25, 2020, Defendant A appeared via Zoom from Arizona and was arraigned in the Eastern District of Wisconsin; bond conditions continued.
- Defendant A’s bond conditions were later modified to allow him to move from Arizona to Arkansas to live with his mother.
- A bond condition requiring weekly contact with counsel was removed by modification.
- Defendant A has been compliant with his bond terms while on pretrial release.
- Defendant A pled guilty to Count One on December 16, 2020, pursuant to a written plea agreement.
- The government agreed to move to dismiss the remaining count at sentencing.
- Sentencing was scheduled for March 22, 2021.
- The plea agreement was signed by Defendant A on December 1, 2020.
- Defense counsel signed the plea agreement on December 4, 2020.
- The Assistant United States Attorney signed the plea agreement on December 6, 2020.
- Count One alleges that on or about February 10, 2020, Defendant A distributed child pornography in the Eastern District of Wisconsin and elsewhere using a means and facility of interstate and foreign commerce, including by computer.
- The distributed child pornography included a video depicting a nude infant female with an adult male inserting his penis into the infant’s anus.
- Beginning in January 2020, an FBI Online Covert Employee had access to multiple messenger chat groups focused on child pornography sharing.
- Participants in the chat groups shared images and videos containing child pornography with the OCE in the Eastern District of Wisconsin.
- A user account with the handle used by Defendant A was a member of the chat groups.
- On February 10, 2020, the account associated with Defendant A sent the charged video to members of a chat group including the OCE.
- The chat group host reported the account used by Defendant A was created on December 20, 2018.
- The chat group host reported the account had been accessed multiple times by a specific IP address.
- An IP lookup resolved the IP address to Suddenlink Communications, an internet service provider.
- The ISP reported the IP address was assigned at the relevant time to an account at a residence in Lake Havasu City, Arizona.
- County property records showed Defendant A co-owned the Lake Havasu City residence with another person at the time of the distribution.
- On June 10, 2020, search warrants issued in the District of Arizona were executed at the Lake Havasu City residence and on Defendant A’s person.
- On June 10, 2020, after being advised of his rights, Defendant A admitted the account username used in the chat groups was his.
- Defendant A admitted saving images of persons under 18 to his phone and stated he may have saved videos as well.
- Defendant A admitted he had distributed child pornography before, replying “technically, yes.”
- Defendant A stated he obtained child pornography from messenger chat groups.
- Defendant A confirmed he used an email address to register the chat account and acknowledged it was a fake email address.
- Law enforcement access to the chat groups included personnel in Winnebago County, Wisconsin.
- Defendant A’s distribution activity occurred in February and March 2020.
- On June 10, 2020, investigators served search warrants on Defendant A and his residence.
- Defendant A confessed to participating in the targeted internet chat group and to sending and receiving child pornography.
- A forensic search of Defendant A’s computer media found five videos containing child pornography.
- A forensic search of Defendant A’s computer media found 33 images containing child pornography.
- The offense conduct involved online viewing and sharing rather than production of images.
- The record, as presented in the memo, does not describe hands-on contact offenses by Defendant A.
- The offense carries a mandatory minimum term of 5 years’ imprisonment.
- The offense carries a maximum term of 20 years’ imprisonment and a maximum fine of $250,000.
- Each count carries a mandatory special assessment of $100.
- The offense carries a term of supervised release of at least five years and up to life.
- If the court finds Defendant A non-indigent, an additional $5,000 special assessment per count applies under 18 U.S.C. § 3014 to qualifying offenses committed after May 29, 2015.
- Restitution is mandatory for the offense and shall be imposed regardless of Defendant A’s financial resources.
- No restitution had been requested according to the sentencing memorandum’s citation to the PSR.
- Defendant A agreed that properties listed in the indictment were used to facilitate the offense and agreed to their forfeiture and entry of a preliminary order of forfeiture.
- The parties agreed to waive the Fed. R. Crim. P. 32 time limits for presentence report disclosure in favor of a schedule set by the court.
- Defendant A was 35 years old at the time of the sentencing memorandum.
- Defendant A is the only child of his parents’ relationship.
- Defendant A grew up in California and lived there until 2015.
- Defendant A moved to Arizona around 2015 and lived there for approximately five years until this offense.
- During pretrial release, Defendant A moved to his mother’s home in Arkansas and has lived there since November 2020.
- Defendant A’s parents divorced when he was approximately five years old.
- He was raised primarily by his mother and stepfather.
- Defendant A’s contact with his biological father ended when he was 16 and he has had no contact since.
- Defendant A has never been married and has no children.
- Defendant A has a long-term relationship with a girlfriend of approximately 12 years.
- Defendant A’s girlfriend submitted a letter of support.
- Defendant A’s mother submitted a letter of support.
- Defendant A reported situational depression related to his arrest and prosecution.
- Defendant A has not sought mental health treatment in the past and was not engaged in therapy at the time of sentencing.
- Defendant A sporadically used marijuana when younger but reported no use since 2009.
- Defendant A reported no use of other controlled substances.
- Defendant A had a DUI in California in 2008 and no subsequent law enforcement contacts prior to this case.
- Defendant A graduated from high school in Santa Clarita, California, in 2003.
- Defendant A has no further formal education beyond high school.
- Defendant A learned on-the-job mechanical repair skills for boats and cars.
- Defendant A maintained full-time employment from 2004 up to his arrest in this case.

- Defendant A was charged in a single-count indictment with conspiracy to obstruct justice under 18 U.S.C. § 371.
- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to the 18 U.S.C. § 371 charge as alleged in the indictment.
- The offense of conviction carries a maximum term of imprisonment of 5 years.
- The offense of conviction carries a maximum fine of $250,000 or twice the gross gain or loss, whichever is greater.
- The offense of conviction carries a maximum supervised release term of 3 years.
- A $100 mandatory special assessment applies upon conviction.
- The offense may be subject to an order of restitution under 18 U.S.C. §§ 3663A and 3664.
- At the time of the obstructive conduct, Defendant A was under supervision of the U.S. Probation Department pending a federal sentencing.
- The obstructive conduct occurred from some time prior to May 31, 2020 to on or about June 2, 2020.
- The conduct took place at or near Grandview and Beaver in Raleigh County and in Charleston, Kanawha County, West Virginia, within the Southern District of West Virginia.
- Defendant A and Spouse A conspired to fake Defendant A's disappearance to avoid her sentencing.
- Defendant A and Spouse A intentionally made false statements to officers with the National Park Service and to the U.S. Probation Department during the scheme.
- The scheme sought to obstruct the due administration of justice in Defendant A's pending case, United States v. [Defendant A], Criminal No. 2:19-CR-00235, in the Southern District of West Virginia.
- Defendant A, Spouse A, and Minor A agreed to stage Defendant A's disappearance and/or death to avoid a federal sentencing scheduled for June 12, 2020.
- The plan included Defendant A going into permanent hiding with assistance from Spouse A.
- On or about May 31, 2020 at Grandview, Spouse A and Minor A prompted an emergency call to Raleigh County 911 claiming Defendant A had fallen from an overlook and was missing.
- Spouse A and Minor A personally provided statements to the Raleigh County 911 Center that Defendant A had fallen from an overlook within Grandview.
- Defendant A knew the false reports triggered state, federal, and local search and rescue efforts to locate her.
- Spouse A and Minor A gave further false statements to federal officials, including National Park Service and U.S. Probation, that Defendant A had fallen and was missing.
- These false statements included details about how Defendant A purportedly fell from the overlook.
- Spouse A and Minor A gave similar false statements to the West Virginia State Police, the Raleigh County Sheriff's Office, and the West Virginia Division of Natural Resources.
- On or about June 1, 2020, Spouse A posted on a personal social media account about an "accident" involving Defendant A at Grandview Park and expressed hope she would be found, to mislead the public and authorities.
- A shoe and a cellphone belonging to Defendant A were thrown from the Grandview overlook to mislead search and rescue teams.
- From May 31 through June 2, 2020, Defendant A concealed herself at her home in Beaver, West Virginia.
- On at least two occasions during the search, Defendant A hid in a closet while law enforcement, including U.S. Probation, were in the home coordinating efforts to locate her.
- On or about June 2, 2020, the West Virginia State Police located Defendant A hiding in a closet in her home.
- Grandview Park is a scenic overlook that is part of the New River Gorge National River, a unit of the U.S. National Park Service.
- The National Park Service is an agency of the federal government within the executive branch.
- The United States Probation Department is an agency of the federal government within the judicial branch.
- The plea agreement was executed on October 7, 2020.
- Sentencing in this obstruction case was scheduled for January 7, 2021 at 10:30 AM.
- On June 30, 2020, in case 2:19-cr-00235, the Court sentenced Defendant A to 42 months of imprisonment for a healthcare fraud conviction.
- In the healthcare fraud case 2:19-00235, the sentence included three years of supervised release and restitution of $289,055.07 to the Veterans Health Administration Office of Community Care.
- As of the obstruction sentencing date, Defendant A's projected release date on the prior sentence was May 26, 2023.
- A presentence report figure of $27,293.94 was referenced as the asserted amount of search expenditures, based on an investigating officer’s assertion, with documentation not provided to the defense at that time.
- The government stated the PSR reported investigative costs of $27,253.94 provided by the National Park Service.
- The government also stated the National Park Service summarized expenses totaling $27,523.94 in the PSR.
- According to the government, the initial search for Defendant A lasted approximately two and one-half days (May 31 and June 1) and she was found on June 2, 2020.
- Investigators became suspicious when they found no body, impact crater, or signs of a fall at the base of an approximately 80-foot cliff.
- On May 31, 2020, the U.S. Attorney’s Office informed law enforcement that the missing person was due in federal court for a criminal sentencing.
- The government stated that there were no known expenses to the Court or the Court’s Probation Office resulting from the incident.
- The National Park Service created records identifying all volunteer agencies who worked to locate Defendant A and used an estimate of $10 per hour for volunteer time as a record-keeping device.
- No volunteers were paid by the National Park Service for search efforts in this incident.
- The West Virginia State Police and the Army National Guard provided helicopter air support during the search.
- The National Guard estimated the cost of the Lakota helicopter at $2,628 per hour, for a total of four hours ($10,512).
- The West Virginia State Police helicopter cost was estimated at $1,700 per hour for three hours ($5,100).
- As of the government’s filing, neither the West Virginia State Police nor the National Guard had submitted a bill to the National Park Service for helicopter use.
- The National Park Service maintained a record of all its personnel who worked on the search; these employees are salaried federal employees.
- Overtime for National Park Service personnel was paid by the agency’s Emergency Services Branch pursuant to 54 U.S.C. § 103101.
- The government identified $2,285.44 as the total overtime paid to National Park Service employees who worked on the search and rescue operation (excluding the criminal investigation).
- The government identified the combined estimated helicopter costs at $15,612.
- The government indicated that, if restitution were legally permissible, a combined total of $17,897.44 (helicopter estimates plus NPS overtime) would be the sum it would limit its request to.
- The National Park Service compiled a daily list of costs associated with the search for Defendant A.
- In the healthcare fraud case, the Court briefly continued sentencing to allow the parties to respond to a revised presentence report.
- Defendant A’s defense filed a sentencing memorandum on December 30, 2020 in this obstruction case.
- The United States filed its response on January 11, 2021 addressing Defendant A’s objections and restitution in this obstruction case.

- Defendant A agreed to waive indictment and plead guilty to one count of conspiracy to commit mail fraud and honest services mail fraud under 18 U.S.C. § 1349
- Defendant A admitted factual guilt to the conspiracy count in the plea agreement
- Defendant A agreed to waive venue, any applicable statute of limitations, and legal or procedural defects in the Information
- The plea agreement letter is dated October 21, 2019, and Defendant A signed the acknowledgment that date
- The government agreed that no further criminal charges would be brought against Defendant A for the conduct set forth in the Information
- Defendant A faces a statutory maximum of 20 years’ imprisonment for the offense, with up to three years of supervised release, a fine up to $250,000 or twice gain/loss, a $100 special assessment, restitution, and forfeiture
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit assets seized during the investigation and prosecution of the case
- Formal charges in the case were filed on August 21, 2020, after the plea agreement was signed
- Defendant A paid Organizer A $40,000 to produce a desired ACT standardized test score for Child A
- The $40,000 payment was disguised as a business consulting fee from Defendant A’s insurance company to The Edge College & Career Network, LLC (“The Key”)
- Child A’s extended-time testing accommodation was used as a pretext to move the ACT exam to a site controlled by Organizer A
- On April 7, 2016, Organizer A told Defendant A that Child A’s “double time” accommodation allowed taking the ACT over multiple days
- On April 8, 2016, Organizer A indicated the ACT exam would be administered in mid-June if the helpers could accommodate
- On April 11, 2016, Organizer A emailed Middleman A to ask about taking the June ACT in Houston for a student with 100% extended time
- Organizer A instructed Defendant A to tell the school they were not prepared so the exam could be moved to June
- On May 4, 2016, Spouse A falsely told Child A’s high school they would not be in Los Angeles and had found a school in Cincinnati that would accommodate her
- On May 6, 2016, Organizer A asked ACT, Inc. to send Child A’s materials to Test Administrator A at a Houston public school, using her double-time accommodation to request a location change
- In June 2016, Bookkeeper A sent Defendant A a $75,000 invoice from the Key Worldwide Foundation for a purported “Private Contribution,” offering to switch to a consulting invoice if preferred
- On June 18, 2016, Defendant A took Child A to the Houston public school where Test Administrator A worked, and Child A took the ACT that day
- Test Administrator A allowed Test Corrector A to purport to proctor the exam and secretly correct answers, resulting in a score in the 95th percentile
- Test Administrator A mailed the falsified ACT exam to ACT, Inc., with a false report that the exam had been administered over three days
- Two days after the exam, the Key Worldwide Foundation paid $25,000 to Middleman A with the understanding part would go to Test Administrator A, who received between $5,000 and $10,000 in cash
- The Key Worldwide Foundation issued two payments of $5,000 each to Test Corrector A for the cheating
- In July 2016, Bookkeeper A sent Defendant A two invoices on behalf of The Key for “Business Consulting & Oversight Review” for $40,000 (2016) and $35,000 (2017)
- In August 2016, Defendant A caused his company to mail a $40,000 check to The Key even though no consulting work had been performed
- Organizer A accepted the reduced $40,000 amount as payment in full because Defendant A introduced other clients to him
- In November 2016, Child A’s fraudulent ACT score was submitted with applications to multiple colleges, including Boston College
- In October 2018, Organizer A told Defendant A during a recorded call that the IRS was auditing and referenced the test in Houston, and Defendant A agreed to the cover story that his payment was to help underserved kids
- The $40,000 payment was disguised as a consulting fee to The Key rather than a donation to the Key Worldwide Foundation
- Defendant A did not directly pay the test administrator or test corrector and did not know the detailed mechanics; Organizer A facilitated the payments and arrangements
- Child A had no knowledge of the plan to cheat on the ACT
- Organizer A arranged for Defendant A and Child A to travel to a testing center in Houston, Texas, where the ACT exam would be rescored after completion
- Defendant A held leadership roles as founder of a private equity firm and chairman of an insurance agency during the relevant period
- Prior to this case, The Hauser Group employed approximately 100 employees and had annual gross sales of $20 million
- Hauser Private Equity was founded in 2008, closed its first $100 million fund in 2013, and has offices in Cincinnati, Los Angeles, and Washington, D.C.
- Defendant A was approximately 60 years old at the time of sentencing
- Defendant A had no prior criminal history before this offense
- A reckless driving charge against Defendant A was dismissed, and he admitted to a speeding infraction
- Brown & Brown agreed to pay $187 million to purchase Defendant A’s insurance company, with a closing scheduled for late August 2020
- After the government announced the plea agreement on August 21, 2020, Brown & Brown pulled out of the sale due to publicity
- According to a letter cited in the defense memorandum, three days after the plea agreement was unsealed Brown & Brown walked away from the deal citing negative publicity
- The cancellation of the sale caused significant financial impact, noted as in excess of $150,000,000, affecting Defendant A and company employees
- Defendant A took an indefinite, voluntary unpaid leave of absence from the company, which was described as a hardship on other employees
- Defendant A was forced to give up his insurance license following his guilty plea
- Family members of Defendant A experienced collateral consequences, including one being questioned by an employer, another receiving anonymous complaints, and Child A facing negative media attention and harassment
- Defendant A engaged with Cincinnati State Technical and Community College to explore structured community service activities
- Defendant A served on the board of the Springer School and Center, a school for students with learning disabilities, for nine years and sponsored construction of a new library
- Defendant A served on the board of Marymount High School for six years, including four years as chair, recruited a new board, and led a significant expansion while improving finances
- Defendant A launched a Habitat for Humanity initiative through his insurance company for management and staff to donate time
- Defendant A has served as a lector for nearly 20 years and has been a significant fundraiser for his church
- Defendant A agreed to donate 10% of the gross profits of one of his businesses for approximately ten years, donating more than $5 million to the March of Dimes
- Defendant A co-created and funded a free pharmacy program for impoverished and homeless individuals with the Society of St. Vincent de Paul
- Defendant A served on the board of the Cincinnati Museum Center for about ten years, including as finance chair, and helped stabilize its finances
- Defendant A has taught and tutored at Envision (formerly Home for the Mentally Retarded) for a number of years
- Defendant A served more than ten years on the board of the Griffith Insurance Education Foundation
- Defendant A launched a volunteer organ donor initiative through his business with the Life Center Organ Donor Network
- Organizer A used Child A’s extended-time accommodation because it allowed students to select the exam location for ACT testing
- Defendant A’s payment for the cheating was negotiated down from a higher amount in return for his referral of other clients to Organizer A
- After the cheating, Defendant A agreed on a recorded call to provide a false explanation that his payment was a donation to help underserved children
- Organizer A’s bookkeeper initially proposed invoicing the payment as a $75,000 charitable contribution through the Key Worldwide Foundation
- Defendant A instead paid $40,000 via a for-profit consulting invoice from The Key rather than as a charitable donation
