- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Count 1 of an Information charging assault on a federal officer in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 111(a).
- On August 24, 2020, in the District of Oregon, Defendant A forcibly assaulted a federal employee.
- The federal employee was engaged in official duties when the assault occurred.
- The maximum imprisonment for the offense in Count 1 is not more than one year.
- The maximum fine for the offense in Count 1 is up to $100,000.
- The offense in Count 1 carries up to a one-year term of supervised release.
- The plea letter states a special assessment of $100.25 for Count 1.
- Defendant A agreed to pay the special assessment by the time of the guilty plea or explain why it could not be paid then.
- At sentencing, the government agreed to move to dismiss any remaining counts against Defendant A.
- The government agreed not to bring additional District of Oregon charges arising from this investigation that were known at the time of the agreement.
- The plea agreement was made under Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 11(c)(1)(B).
- The plea agreement was signed on October 28, 2020.
- Defendant A made an initial appearance on August 24, 2020.
- On October 28, 2020, Defendant A pleaded guilty to a single-count Information charging assault on a federal officer.
- A sentencing hearing was scheduled for January 25, 2021 at 10:00 a.m.
- The incident occurred in the early morning hours of August 24, 2020.
- Defendant A was on supervision at the time of the offense conduct.
- The location of the incident was near the Mark O. Hatfield United States Courthouse in Portland, Oregon.
- Victim A was a government contractor on the way to work at the courthouse.
- As Victim A passed near the group, Defendant A started following with a black paintball gun and shouted obscenities.
- As Victim A crossed into federal property toward the courthouse, Defendant A followed closely behind.
- Defendant A stated that although it was a paintball gun now, it was "going to be an AR."
- Victim A believed the "AR" comment referred to an assault rifle.
- Victim A became concerned for his safety and quickened his pace while checking behind him.
- After entering the courthouse, Victim A immediately reported the incident to Deputy U.S. Marshals.
- The victim is referred to as Adult Victim 1 (AV1) in the record.
- The incident occurred following ongoing nightly protests in downtown Portland.
- The case is in the United States District Court for the District of Oregon.
- The case is docketed as Case No. 3:20-cr-00392-MO.
- Defense materials describe the conviction as a misdemeanor assault on a federal officer under 18 U.S.C. § 111(a).
- Defense materials indicate Defendant A would appear for sentencing on January 25, 2021.
- Defense materials state Defendant A is approximately 20 years old.
- Defense materials state that earlier that day Defendant A was at a Starbucks across the street from the courthouse with friends.
- Defense materials state surveillance video shows Defendant A began walking north past the courthouse to meet a friend who was walking south.
- Defense materials state Defendant A had a paintball gun that he found a few days earlier after a Proud Boys rally at the waterfront.
- Defense materials state the victim passed by giving Defendant A a wide berth while walking to work.
- Defense materials state both Defendant A and the victim crossed the street toward the courthouse at the same time.
- Defense materials state the victim headed toward the courthouse entrance while Defendant A headed to meet a friend on that side of the road.
- Defense materials assert it would have appeared to the victim that Defendant A was following him.
- Defense materials state there is no audio on the surveillance video of the incident.
- Defense materials state Defendant A does not recall his exact words during the encounter.
- Defense materials state Defendant A acknowledges his statements were threatening.
- Defense materials state Defendant A takes responsibility for the statements and regrets them.
- Defense materials state Defendant A engaged with mental health services after release.
- Defense materials state Defendant A obtained full-time employment after release.
- Defense materials state Defendant A moved back in with his spouse and child after release.
- Defense materials state Defendant A and spouse were expecting a second child around the time of sentencing.
- Defense materials state Defendant A was saving money to move to a residence near extended family support.
- Defense materials state Defendant A had not been involved in further protest activity since his arrest.
- Defense materials state the incident date as October 24, 2020.

- Defendant agreed to waive indictment and plead guilty to Counts 1–3 of the Information
- Count 1 charges Conspiracy to Commit Bank Fraud in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1349
- Count 2 charges Aggravated Identity Theft in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1028A
- Count 3 charges Conspiracy to Manufacture or Possess with Intent to Manufacture, Distribute, or Dispense Marijuana in violation of 21 U.S.C. §§ 841(a)(1), 841(b)(1)(C), and 846
- The maximum sentence for Count 1 is 30 years of imprisonment
- Count 1 carries a maximum fine of $1,000,000 or twice the gross pecuniary gains or losses
- Count 1 carries a maximum of five years of supervised release
- Count 2 requires a mandatory two-year term of imprisonment to be served consecutive to any other term of imprisonment
- Count 2 carries a maximum fine of $250,000 and a one-year term of supervised release
- Count 3 carries a maximum sentence of 20 years of imprisonment
- Count 3 carries a maximum fine of $1,000,000 and at least three years of supervised release
- Defendant admitted the elements of Counts 1 through 3 and agreed that the government could prove specified facts beyond a reasonable doubt
- The investigation uncovered a credit card 'bust-out' fraud involving the defendant and other co-conspirators
- In the bust-out scheme, perpetrators obtained credit cards to make purchases and run up balances with no intent to pay
- Bust-out schemes included making initial payments to make cards appear legitimate and increase credit limits
- After purchases, payments were made from co-conspirator bank accounts to reduce balances, enabling additional purchases
- Co-conspirators later reported to banks that the payments made to the cards were not authorized, causing banks to reimburse and deposit funds back into co-conspirator accounts
- The scheme resulted in losses to financial institutions often substantially greater than the credit limit
- Fraud proceeds were used to establish and operate a marijuana grow and a state-licensed marijuana retail business in Corvallis, Oregon known as Corvallis Cannabis Club (CCC)
- Defendant was an owner of Corvallis Cannabis Club (CCC)
- Defendant and co-conspirators fraudulently obtained credit card accounts at federally insured institutions
- Defendant and co-conspirators opened credit card accounts using stolen and fictitious identities, including the identity referenced in Count 2
- Fraudulently obtained credit cards were used for personal purchases including expensive jewelry, clothing, gambling, and vacations
- Fraud proceeds were used to establish and operate large marijuana grows and to cover operating expenses of CCC
- Defendant acted as a manager and supervisor of both the credit card bust-out conspiracy and the marijuana conspiracy
- Defendant obtained numerous credit cards using others' personal identifying information and worked with co-conspirators to create fake or altered identification, including fake Oregon ID cards
- Defendant recruited CCC workers to engage in the fraud in exchange for monetary kickbacks
- Defendant assisted by calling banks and reporting that payments made to credit cards were fraudulent
- Defendant used for her benefit and for CCC fraudulent credit cards opened by co-conspirators
- The investigation uncovered loss to financial institutions from the conspiracy totaling over $1,500,000
- Investigators uncovered two active marijuana grows, both unlicensed by the State of Oregon
- One marijuana grow was located in a warehouse one block away from CCC
- Defendant and co-defendants established the warehouse grow and paid rent, utilities, and supplies using credit cards from the fraud conspiracy
- On June 12, 2018, agents seized over 1,200 marijuana plants from the warehouse grow
- Marijuana grown at the warehouse was shipped out of state to California, Illinois, and Florida
- Marijuana from the warehouse grow was sold at CCC
- Defendant and co-conspirators purchased cheap marijuana from state-licensed distributors and entered it into Oregon's Cannabis Tracking System
- They replaced the tracked cheap product with marijuana grown at illegal sites, raised the price, and sold to consumers for profit
- They then sold the state-licensed and tracked marijuana illegally to others, including out-of-state purchasers
- A second grow was established in Philomath, Oregon in 2018, also unlicensed
- Funds from the credit card fraud were used to establish and operate the Philomath grow
- On June 12, 2018, agents seized over 200 marijuana plants from the Philomath grow
- The investigation began with a Synchrony Bank report of a loss over $170,000 from fraudulent use of eight credit cards issued to [Person A]
- The eight Synchrony credit card accounts were opened using an address of a building leased by co-conspirators and used as an unlicensed marijuana grow
- All the Synchrony accounts were applied for online using an IP address that resolved to Corvallis Cannabis Club
- [Person A] was not in the United States when the credit cards were applied for or issued in that name
- Charges on [Person A]'s cards included Amazon purchases shipped to [Defendant] and a co-conspirator, including a $9,000 Hublot watch, personal items, and airfare
- Charges also included rent and utilities at the marijuana grow and rent at CCC
- Co-conspirator bank accounts were used to pay down credit card balances to allow more purchases
- Defendant or co-conspirators called banks and reported the payments as fraudulent, resulting in losses for the credit card companies
- Investigators uncovered over a dozen fraudulent Synchrony credit cards resulting in losses of over $420,000
- Defendant and co-conspirators opened credit cards at numerous institutions including Discover, Capital One, Citibank, U.S. Bank, Bank of America, Wells Fargo, Chase, Comenity, and Oregon State Credit Union
- In total, defendant and co-conspirators opened well over 100 credit cards as part of the conspiracy
- The credit card conspiracy resulted in losses to financial institutions in excess of $2.5 million
- On June 12, 2018, agents seized over 1,200 marijuana plants from the same warehouse where fraudulent credit cards were sent
- Agents also seized over 200 marijuana plants from a separate grow in Philomath operated by the conspirators
- Some of the marijuana grown at the warehouse was sold at Corvallis Cannabis Club
- Marijuana at the grow sites was largely sold to bulk out-of-state purchasers
- Defendant and co-conspirators used the proceeds of the fraud to establish and operate at least two marijuana grows and CCC
- Defendant and co-conspirators replaced cheap, state-licensed product with marijuana grown at illegal sites to increase profit at CCC
- Defendant was charged in a three-count information with the bank fraud conspiracy, aggravated identity theft, and marijuana conspiracy
- On October 13, 2020, the defendant pleaded guilty to Count 1, Count 2, and Count 3
- The defendant attempted to cooperate with the government but was unable to provide substantial assistance
- The defendant pleaded guilty at an early stage of the proceedings
- In 2009, the defendant was charged and convicted of making unauthorized charges on a credit card at a high-end retailer
- In the 2009 matter, investigators determined the defendant ordered goods with stolen credit card information and had the items delivered to herself
- In 2010, the defendant was charged and convicted of opening a credit card in the name of a deceased individual
- In the 2010 matter, the defendant purchased thousands of dollars of merchandise, later returned it for store credit, leaving an outstanding balance of over $17,000
- During the 2010 investigation, officers found the fraudulent credit card in the defendant's vehicle and vehicle registration in the stolen identity's name; the car dealership verified the vehicle had been purchased fraudulently and it was returned
- The government stated that the conspiracy used numerous stolen and synthetic identities
- The government stated that the conspiracy began as early as 2015 in the Corvallis, Oregon area
- The Corvallis Cannabis Club business was owned and operated by the defendant and co-conspirators
- The 24-month term for the aggravated identity theft count must run consecutive to the sentences on Counts One and Three by statute

- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Count 1 (conspiracy to commit bank fraud) and Count 7 (failure to appear in court as required).
- The plea agreement is pursuant to Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 11(c)(1)(B).
- The parties stipulated that the intended loss amount for Count 1 is $543,959.
- For Count 1, the statutory maximum is 30 years of imprisonment, up to 5 years of supervised release, a fine of $1,000,000, and a $100 special assessment.
- For Count 7, the statutory maximum is 10 years of imprisonment, up to 3 years of supervised release, a fine of $250,000, and a $100 special assessment.
- If imposed consecutively, the combined statutory maximums for Counts 1 and 7 total 40 years of imprisonment, a $1,250,000 fine, and up to 5 years of supervised release, with a mandatory special assessment of $200.
- The government agreed it would move at sentencing to dismiss Counts 2 through 6 of the indictment.
- Defendant A agreed that time between filing of the plea agreement and the change-of-plea date is excludable under the Speedy Trial Act.
- Defendant A agreed to promptly submit a completed Department of Justice financial statement to the U.S. Attorney’s Office.
- Defendant A authorized the U.S. Attorney’s Office to obtain a credit report to evaluate ability to satisfy any financial obligation imposed by the Court.
- The plea agreement requires payment of the mandatory special assessments upon returning the signed agreement to the U.S. Attorney’s Office.
- The plea agreement was signed on May 6, 2021.
- The plea agreement is limited to the District of Rhode Island and does not bind other authorities.
- The government’s obligations under the plea agreement are contingent on Defendant A not engaging in conduct constituting obstruction of justice and not committing new offenses.
- Sentencing was scheduled for September 30, 2021.
- The offense conduct involved attempts to defraud the Paycheck Protection Program (PPP).
- On April 6, 2020, Defendant A submitted a PPP loan application to BankNewport for Oakland Beach Restaurant Group LLC, d/b/a Top of the Bay.
- The Top of the Bay loan application amount was $185,750.
- The Top of the Bay application represented that the business had 26 employees and an average monthly payroll of $53,000.
- In reality, Defendant A did not own the Top of the Bay restaurant and there were no employees and no payroll for that entity.
- Fraudulent tax return documents were submitted to BankNewport in support of the Top of the Bay application, and those documents were created by Co‑Conspirator A.
- On April 6, 2020, Defendant A submitted a PPP loan application to BankNewport for Apponaug Restaurant Group LLC, d/b/a Remington House.
- The Remington House loan application amount was $144,050.
- The Remington House application represented 18 employees and an average monthly payroll of $46,000.
- The Remington House restaurant had been closed since November 2018 and Apponaug Restaurant Group LLC had no employees when the application was submitted.
- Fraudulent tax return documents were submitted to BankNewport for the Remington House application, and those documents were created by Co‑Conspirator A.
- On April 6, 2020, Defendant A submitted a PPP loan application to BankNewport for New Flat Penny LLC, d/b/a On The Trax, a restaurant in Berlin, Massachusetts.
- The On The Trax loan application amount was $108,777.50.
- The On The Trax application represented 22 employees and an average monthly payroll of $36,000.
- The On The Trax restaurant was closed permanently on about March 10, 2020, after its liquor license was revoked, and New Flat Penny LLC had no employees when the application was submitted.
- On April 6, 2020, Co‑Conspirator A submitted a PPP loan application to BankNewport in his own name on behalf of Dock Wireless, an unincorporated entity.
- The Dock Wireless loan application amount was $105,381.50.
- The Dock Wireless application represented 7 employees and an average monthly payroll of $42,152.60.
- In reality, Dock Wireless had no employees and no wages were ever paid by Dock Wireless.
- In total, four fraudulent PPP loan applications were submitted, totaling $543,959.
- A concerned citizen alerted law enforcement to the fraudulent nature of the applications, which ultimately led to their denial by BankNewport.
- After arrest, Defendant A removed his electronic monitoring device and fled.
- On May 26, 2020, Defendant A staged a suicide by leaving suicide notes with family members and associates and in his car, which he left unlocked and parked by the Atlantic Ocean.
- Law enforcement deployed a search and rescue boat to search the nearby ocean for Defendant A’s body, without success.
- During approximately the next three months, Defendant A traveled to various states using false identities and stolen license plates to avoid apprehension.
- During flight, Defendant A changed his phone number on at least five occasions.
- Defendant A was apprehended by the U.S. Marshals Service in Alpharetta, Georgia, on July 23, 2020.
- At the time of apprehension, Defendant A possessed multiple false identification documents used during the period of flight.
- Defendant A has a 2009 federal conviction in the District of New Hampshire for wire fraud involving submission of $280,000 in false invoices from fictitious companies related to a baseball park construction project.
- For the 2009 wire fraud conviction, Defendant A was sentenced to 30 months of imprisonment.
- After completing the 2009 sentence, Defendant A violated supervised release and received an additional 3 months’ imprisonment for Violation of a Trespass Order.
- In 2015, Defendant A sustained a second federal conviction in the District of New Hampshire for wire fraud arising from a mortgage and bank fraud scheme occurring in 2003–2005.
- For the 2015 federal wire fraud conviction, Defendant A was sentenced to 27 months of imprisonment.
- In 2015, Defendant A was convicted in Rhode Island state court of Willful Trespass connected to stalking behavior toward a former intimate partner.
- The Willful Trespass conduct included entering Victim A’s home uninvited and changing the locks in her home, according to police information referenced in the PSR.
- Victim A obtained a restraining order against Defendant A in connection with the 2015 conduct.
- Victim A reported that Defendant A sent harassing texts, emails, and voicemails, accessed her private messages and bank account, and made homicidal threats.
- Defendant A reported to the Probation Office that he was a victim of trauma and that he suffers from mental health disorders.
- Defendant A told Probation that he committed the PPP offense because he was in a bad relationship at the time and was experiencing trauma (PSR ¶ 78).
- Defendant A told Probation that Co‑Conspirator A told him to remove his electronic monitoring equipment and drive south, and he claimed he was blacking out until he was found in Georgia (PSR ¶ 80).
- A search and rescue operation initially considered the possibility that Defendant A had died following the staged suicide scene, before later investigation determined he had fled.
- BankNewport was the lender to which the PPP loan applications were submitted.
- The four PPP applications represented payrolls and employee counts that the businesses did not in fact have at the time of application.
- The On The Trax restaurant’s liquor license had been revoked prior to the PPP application, leading to its permanent closure.
- The PPP loan applications were pending when the concerned citizen alerted law enforcement.
- Defendant A used stolen license plates during his period of flight to evade capture.
- The U.S. Marshals Service conducted efforts to locate and apprehend Defendant A during his flight.

- The defendant was charged by Information with six counts of cyberstalking in violation of 18 U.S.C. §§ 2261A(2)(B) and 2261(b).
- The defendant agreed to plead guilty to Counts One through Six of the Information.
- Each count of conviction carries a maximum penalty of five years of imprisonment.
- Each count carries a maximum fine of $250,000.
- Each count carries a term of supervised release of up to three years.
- A special assessment of $100 per felony count applies.
- The defendant waived indictment and agreed to proceed by Information.
- The defendant waived venue challenges for Counts Three and Six, which were committed in the Eastern District of New York, agreeing to resolve all charges in the Middle District of Florida.
- The plea agreement identifies the offense elements as use of an electronic service to engage in a course of conduct toward another person, intent to harass or intimidate, and causing or reasonably expecting to cause substantial emotional distress.
- The defendant agreed to make full restitution to Victims 1 through 6.
- The defendant agreed not to oppose bifurcation of the sentencing hearing if victims' losses were not ascertainable prior to sentencing.
- The plea agreement includes a cooperation provision in which the defendant agrees to provide truthful information and testify as needed.
- Between February 2013 and August 2019, the defendant posted sexually explicit and nude images of victims online and included victims' personal information.
- The defendant solicited individuals online to contact victims in an attempt to extort additional sexually explicit images.
- On one occasion the defendant solicited the rape of a victim who was a minor.
- The defendant directly messaged some victims via text message, Facebook, and other electronic communication services.
- The defendant created multiple fictitious social media and email accounts and used a website that allowed users to post anonymously.
- The FBI obtained a significant amount of the defendant's internet posts during its investigation.
- The defendant used the 4Chan website to post anonymously.
- Victim 1 dated the defendant beginning in fall 2010; the relationship ended in summer 2012.
- During the relationship, Victim 1 sent the defendant intimate photos of herself.
- In or around February 2013, strangers contacted Victim 1 saying nude photos of her were posted online and that they obtained her number from the posting site.
- For approximately six years, the defendant repeatedly posted sexually explicit photos of Victim 1 online alongside her phone number, address, and social media accounts.
- During the harassment period, Victim 1 resided in Celebration, Florida, and received phone calls from strangers.
- A collage of nude photos of Victim 1 was sent to her father or her father's coworker.
- Victim 1 changed her phone number and deleted her social media due to harassing messages and calls.
- Victim 1 suffered substantial emotional distress, including mental health challenges while battling a physical illness.
- Victim 2 is related to the defendant and was 14 years old when the defendant began posting about her in August 2016.
- Victim 2 lived in Daytona, Florida during the relevant period.
- For approximately two years, the defendant repeatedly posted photos of Victim 2 and attempted to solicit individuals to sexually abuse her or induce her to produce child pornography.
- On August 10, 2018, the defendant posted an offer seeking nude images of Victim 2, directing people to contact him on a messaging app.
- On August 13, 2018, the defendant solicited explicit messages to be sent to Victim 2 and asked people to contact him via Instagram and a messaging app.
- On August 20, 2018, the defendant posted that he wanted to watch Victim 2 get raped and said he would pay someone to do it.
- Victim 2's parents increased home security by installing additional lighting and cameras and changing door locks in response to the defendant's posts.
- Victim 2's parents requested increased police patrols and notified her school about the posts; school security closely monitored her for safety.
- The defendant and Victim 3 were childhood friends; harassment of Victim 3 began around October 2016 when she resided in Suffolk County, New York.
- Victim 3 received a text with a nude photo she had taken at age 15; the sender asked if it was her and said she needed to "cooperate."
- Over the next few months, Victim 3 received messages and calls approximately every two days from various numbers, some suggesting surveillance of her movements.
- Victim 3's photos began being posted online during the harassment period.
- Victim 3 confronted the defendant via Facebook Messenger; he admitted to being the individual contacting her, and she provided screenshots to law enforcement.
- Victim 3 was terrified, believed someone was following her, had escorts to her car at night, her personal relationship suffered, and she lost her job due to the harassment.
- Beginning around 2013 and continuing for at least six years, the defendant repeatedly posted sexually explicit photos of Victims 4 and 5 online.
- Victim 4 resided in Melbourne, Florida; in November 2016 she learned from friends and family that nude photos of her had been posted online and she began receiving harassing messages on Facebook and Instagram.
- Individuals threatened Victim 4 with further distribution of her photos unless she sent more; she also began receiving unsolicited explicit images from others.
- In 2017, the defendant conspired with Victim 4's ex-partner to obtain additional sexually explicit photos of Victim 4 and posted them online.
- Some postings about Victim 4 included her name, address, phone number, and social media accounts.
- The defendant solicited people online to demand that Victim 4 provide additional sexually explicit photographs.
- An anonymous caller told Victim 4's former supervisor about the images; photos were also sent to some of Victim 4's clients.
- Victim 4 experienced difficulty sleeping, nightmares, fear of an intruder, trust issues, and sought counseling due to the harassment.
- From at least February 2018 through at least December 2018, the defendant repeatedly posted sexually explicit photos of Victim 5 online.
- Victim 5 resided in Sarasota, Florida during the relevant period.
- Victim 5 feared job loss due to the defendant's actions and attended couples therapy with a partner to address harm caused by the defendant.
- The defendant and Victim 6 were in a relationship beginning around 2015; during the relationship she provided him with sexually explicit photos.
- Victim 6 ended the relationship around March 2019; beginning in April 2019 the defendant repeatedly posted sexually explicit photos of her online.
- Victim 6 resided in New York during the relevant period.
- In May 2019, Victim 5 informed Victim 6 that the defendant had posted sexually explicit photos of Victim 6 online.
- Victim 6 reviewed and identified the images as ones she had sent the defendant during their relationship.
- When confronted by Victim 6 and asked to stop, the defendant responded, "ok it's done."
- Victim 6 felt violated and tried to avoid thinking about the defendant's actions because it upset her.
- The defendant continued harassing Victim 1 even after she confronted him in 2014, stating he was "in a sharing mood" and saying there was "nothing better than knowing all her info is exposed."
- The defendant acknowledged learning that Victim 1 had suicidal ideations but continued his conduct and said he only regretted not seeing her reaction or blackmailing for more images.
- Victim 3 and a family member of the defendant also confronted him about his posts; he continued the harassment through August 2019.
- At the time he posted about Victim 2, the defendant recognized that someone could follow through on his solicitation to rape her.
- The FBI identified the defendant as the perpetrator of the online conduct.
- On December 17, 2020, the Information charging six counts was filed in the Middle District of Florida.
- On January 14, 2021, the defendant pleaded guilty to the Information.
- Sentencing was scheduled for August 12, 2021 at 10:00 AM.
- On August 6, 2021, a Final Presentence Investigation Report was issued, and defense counsel submitted legal objections.
- The defendant asserts he has regularly attended therapy for several years and has been placed on suicide watch multiple times in his life.
- The defendant grew up in New York and lost his mother to cancer when he was about 11 or 12 years old.
- According to the record, the defendant has no prior criminal history.
- The defendant is college educated and studied Aerospace Engineering.
- The defendant removed 734 photographs he had posted from an online archive site as part of remedial efforts.
- The defendant created multiple fictitious social media and email accounts to conceal his identity during the offense conduct.
- The defendant used interactive platforms including text messaging, Facebook, and anonymous online forums to harass and solicit actions against victims.
- The defendant used instant messaging and social media applications to solicit explicit images related to Victim 2.
- Victims experienced substantial emotional distress attributable to the defendant's course of conduct.
- The government described the defendant as having used perceived online anonymity to enlist others to harass victims, providing personal information and instructions.
- The defendant continued stalking and harassment over a period of years despite being confronted by multiple victims and relatives.
- The defendant acknowledged posting about Victim 1 because he was “in a sharing mood.”
- The court adjudicated the defendant guilty on January 28, 2021.
- The defendant created posts about multiple victims over a span of at least seven years.

- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to an 11-count First Superseding Information charging conspiracy, transporting/receiving firearms across state lines, making false statements in firearm purchases, and possession of unregistered firearms.
- Count One charged conspiracy to violate 18 U.S.C. §§ 922(a)(3) and 922(a)(6).
- Counts Two and Eight charged transporting and receiving firearms across state lines in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 922(a)(3).
- Counts Three through Seven charged making false statements during firearm purchases in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 922(a)(6).
- Counts Nine through Eleven charged possession of unregistered firearms (short-barreled rifles) in violation of 26 U.S.C. § 5861(d).
- The statutory maximum for the conspiracy count (18 U.S.C. § 371) is 5 years’ imprisonment, 3 years of supervised release, a $250,000 fine (or twice gross gain or loss), and a $100 special assessment.
- The statutory maximum for each 18 U.S.C. § 922(a)(3) count is 10 years’ imprisonment, 3 years of supervised release, a $250,000 fine (or twice gross gain or loss), and a $100 special assessment.
- The statutory maximum for each 18 U.S.C. § 922(a)(6) count is 10 years’ imprisonment, 3 years of supervised release, a $250,000 fine (or twice gross gain or loss), and a $100 special assessment.
- The statutory maximum for each 26 U.S.C. § 5861(d) count is 10 years’ imprisonment, 3 years of supervised release, a $10,000 fine, and a $100 special assessment.
- The total maximum sentence exposure across all counts is 105 years’ imprisonment and $1,100 in mandatory special assessments.
- The government will seek forfeiture of firearms involved in the offenses under 18 U.S.C. § 924(d)(1) and 28 U.S.C. § 2461(c).
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit 12 specified firearms including multiple Glock pistols, a Walther P-99, Mossberg rifles/shotgun, a Browning BL22, a Winchester XPR, two Colt M4 carbines, and a Stag Arms STAG-15.
- Tillamook Sporting Goods and Sportsman's Warehouse were licensed firearms dealers located in Oregon.
- ATF Form 4473 requires the purchaser to certify they are the actual buyer of the firearm.
- Defendant A and Co-Conspirator 1 were not federally licensed to import, manufacture, or deal in firearms.
- Between at least July 23, 2014, and August 20, 2018, Defendant A conspired with Co-Conspirator 1 to acquire firearms in Oregon and transport them to California using false buyer statements.
- During the conspiracy, Defendant A provided cash to Co-Conspirator 1 to buy firearms in Oregon for delivery to California.
- On July 23, 2014, Defendant A obtained a Walther P-99 9mm semiautomatic handgun via the conspiracy.
- On December 26, 2017, Defendant A obtained a Glock 26 9mm handgun via the conspiracy.
- On December 26, 2017, Defendant A obtained a Glock 43 9mm handgun via the conspiracy.
- On August 20, 2018, Defendant A obtained a Glock 19X 9mm handgun via the conspiracy.
- On January 4, 2018, Defendant A received the Glock 26 and Glock 43 in California after they were purchased in Oregon for him.
- Defendant A kept the Glock 26, Glock 43, and Glock 19X at residences in California and never registered them in California.
- Between March 18 and 29, 2020, Defendant A falsely stated on Forms 4473 that he was a Washington resident when purchasing firearms from licensed dealers.
- The false residency statements were material because Oregon firearms dealers were not legally permitted to sell firearms to California residents.
- On March 18, 2020, Defendant A acquired a Mossberg Model 464 rifle in Oregon.
- On March 22, 2020, Defendant A acquired a Bergara Model B-14 rifle in Oregon.
- On March 22, 2020, Defendant A acquired a Winchester XPR rifle in Oregon.
- On March 29, 2020, Defendant A acquired a Mossberg Model 500 shotgun in Oregon.
- On March 29, 2020, Defendant A acquired a Browning Model BL22 rifle in Oregon.
- On March 30, 2020, Defendant A transported the five firearms acquired in March 2020 to his residence in Lodi, California.
- Defendant A kept the five firearms acquired in March 2020 at residences in California and did not register them in California.
- On May 31, 2020, 50–100 individuals assembled at Fair Oaks Avenue and Colorado Boulevard in Pasadena, California to protest police treatment of minorities and support the Black Lives Matter movement.
- Shortly before 5:00 PM on May 31, 2020, Defendant A drove a modified Dodge pickup truck with a train horn, raised suspension, metal bumpers, three large flags, and a “WAR RIG” vanity plate to the demonstration to counterprotest.
- During the Pasadena incident, Defendant A had a Glock 26 in a closed fanny pack beneath the middle seat of the truck.
- As Defendant A approached the intersection, he saw other cars stopping or turning around but accelerated toward the demonstrators.
- A protestor threw an object at the truck; Defendant A’s passenger said “horn ’em,” and Defendant A sounded the train horn, paused, then continued into the intersection and turned left.
- As he passed through, Defendant A caused the truck to emit a large amount of exhaust (“coal rolling”).
- After the Pasadena incident, officers found in the truck a loaded Glock 26 with a 17-round magazine and a second loaded 15-round magazine, a trigger guard, and a flashlight.

- The case is in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Kentucky, Central Division.
- The charging instrument is an Information.
- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Count 1.
- Count 1 charges a violation of 21 U.S.C. § 331(t) for unlawful wholesale distribution of prescription drugs.
- Company A is a compounding pharmacy located in Nicholasville, Kentucky, in the Eastern District of Kentucky.
- Company A was subject to Kentucky Revised Statutes Chapter 315 and the Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act.
- The Kentucky Board of Pharmacy is located in Frankfort, Franklin County, in the Eastern District of Kentucky.
- The Kentucky Board of Pharmacy oversees implementation of KRS Chapter 315 and regulates pharmacists, pharmacies, and wholesale drug distributors.
- Following an on-site inspection by the Kentucky Board of Pharmacy, Company A received its Kentucky pharmacy license in November 2015.
- The FDCA prohibits wholesale distribution of prescription drugs without a license from the designated state authority.
- For interstate wholesale distribution, a license is also required from the designated authority of the destination state.
- The Kentucky Board of Pharmacy is the designated authority that inspects and licenses wholesale distributors of prescription drugs shipped to and from Kentucky.
- Company A did not apply for or receive a permit from the Kentucky Board of Pharmacy to act as a wholesale distributor of prescription drugs.
- Doctor 1 is a licensed physician who operates an anti-aging and wellness clinic in the Greater Los Angeles, California area.
- Doctor 1’s practice focuses on optimized muscular health, optimized physical fitness, and sex drive, among others.
- Doctor 1 ordered Methylcobalamin 10mg/ml 10mL from Company A beginning on October 23, 2018.
- Doctor 1 continued ordering Methylcobalamin from Company A through May 14, 2020.
- Methylcobalamin 10mg/ml 10mL is a prescription form of vitamin B12.
- Doctor 1 made bulk orders without issuing individualized prescriptions.
- Doctor 1 did not provide accurate patient names with the bulk orders.
- Company A filled the bulk orders totaling 112 vials.
- The vials shipped to Doctor 1 did not have patient-specific names on them.
- Company A sold bulk, wholesale distributions of Methylcobalamin 10mg/ml 10mL to Doctor 1 that moved in interstate commerce.
- The volume of Doctor 1’s orders was far more than a de minimis amount for in-office use.
- During the wholesale distributions to Doctor 1, Defendant A was the owner and chief executive officer of Company A.
- During that period, Defendant A knew Company A had never applied for permission from the Kentucky Board of Pharmacy to act as a wholesale distributor of prescription drugs.
- Defendant A knowingly caused Company A to fill and ship bulk, wholesale distributions of Methylcobalamin to Doctor 1.
- Authorities from the FDA and the Kentucky Board of Pharmacy inspected Company A between August 20 and October 24, 2018.
- During those inspections, Defendant A took steps to hide records of Company A’s wholesale distributions of Methylcobalamin and other records.
- The statutory maximum term of imprisonment for the charge is not more than 10 years.
- The statutory maximum fine for the charge is not more than $250,000.
- The maximum term of supervised release for the charge is not more than 3 years.
- A mandatory special assessment of $100 applies to the count of conviction.
- The plea agreement was filed on October 29, 2020.
- The plea agreement bears signature dates in September 2020.
- The sentencing memorandum was filed on February 19, 2021.
- Both Defendant A and Company A, LLC are listed as defendants in the case caption of the sentencing memorandum.
- Defendant A is described as having no prior criminal history in the sentencing memorandum.
- Company A has paid approximately $1.7 million in forfeiture related to the conduct.
- Defendant A has sold Company A.
- Defendant A founded Company A in December 2013 in Nicholasville.
- Company A provided jobs to nearly 100 workers in roles including pharmacists, chemists, laboratory technicians, managers, and customer service representatives.
- Defendant A admits he caused his company to sell drugs in a wholesale fashion without a license.
- Company A sold certain quantities of new drugs that the FDA had not approved for distribution in the United States.

- Defendant A pled guilty to Count 4 charging violation of 18 U.S.C. § 2251(a).
- At sentencing, the United States would move to dismiss Counts 1 through 3 of the Superseding Indictment charging receipt, distribution, and possession of child pornography.
- Defendant A admitted the Forfeiture Allegation in the Superseding Indictment.
- On September 25, 2018, NCMEC received a CyberTipLine report from Tumblr about a user distributing child pornography.
- Tumblr provided a representative sample of 46 images and two videos to NCMEC and shut down the blog.
- The Tumblr profile had a specific URL and was associated with an email account, with a last login on September 3, 2018.
- A Google subpoena return for the associated email provided account information including creation date and recovery email.
- IP addresses from Tumblr resolved to Charter Communications, whose records returned subscriber information matching Defendant A and a Berea, Kentucky address.
- A Verizon Wireless subpoena for a related phone number returned account information associated with Defendant A and the Berea, Kentucky address.
- On January 6, 2020, FBI agents executed a federal search warrant at Defendant A’s residence in Berea, Kentucky and seized numerous electronic devices.
- A preliminary forensic examination of the seized devices located images of child pornography.
- A forensic examiner located an image on Defendant A’s phone depicting a minor female being vaginally penetrated by an adult male.
- Non-pornographic images on Defendant A’s devices showed background items that matched items in his residence, linking the abuse image to the residence.
- On May 28, 2020, a Child Advocacy Center conducted an interview of the minor victim.
- During the CAC interview, the minor victim believed the images were of her due to matching physical and background items.
- The minor victim’s body has a distinct mole near the belly button matching the mole visible in the abuse image.
- On June 9, 2020, a certified trauma pediatrician documented the minor victim’s mole in the same location as depicted in the image.
- More than one image on Defendant A’s phone depicted vaginal penetration of a minor with the same mole and background characteristics.
- A forensic examination of Defendant A’s electronic media revealed other still images and videos of child pornography beyond the images of the identified victim.
- The minor victim was in Defendant A’s custody, care, or supervisory control at the time of the offense conduct.
- Defendant A knew the visual depictions were produced using materials that were mailed, shipped, or transported in or affecting interstate or foreign commerce, including a cellular telephone.
- The statutory imprisonment range for the § 2251(a) offense is 15 to 30 years.
- The offense carries a maximum fine of $250,000.
- The offense carries a supervised release term of at least 5 years and up to life.
- A mandatory $100 special assessment applies to the count of conviction.
- An additional mandatory $5,000 special assessment applies to non-indigent defendants for qualifying Chapter 110 offenses committed after May 29, 2015 through September 30, 2021.
- The offense conduct occurred between on or about March 1, 2018 and September 30, 2019.
- FBI investigation following the CyberTip led to a federal search warrant for Defendant A’s premises and the present prosecution.
- The Tumblr report indicated the user’s profile was associated with a specific email address.
- Defendant A’s electronic devices contained hundreds of child pornography images.
- The United States requested an additional ninety days after sentencing to finalize restitution to assess the minor victim’s counseling needs.
- The minor victim has been receiving mental health counseling, and the counselor could not yet determine future counseling needs.
- The minor victim requested to address the Court at sentencing.
- The minor victim’s mother requested to address the Court at sentencing.
- The parties agreed the November 1, 2018 Sentencing Guidelines Manual would determine the advisory range.
- The plea agreement defines relevant conduct to include all information in paragraph 3 of the plea and discovery provided.
- Restitution would be determined at sentencing, and Defendant A agreed to pay restitution to victims of the offense of conviction and to victims whose images were in the dismissed counts.
- Defendant A agreed not to seek a mitigating role reduction.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit all interest in property listed in the forfeiture allegation and acknowledged a nexus between the property and the offense or that the property contains contraband images.
- Defendant A agreed to make full and complete financial disclosure within 30 days of pleading guilty, including assets held by third parties.
- If incarcerated, Defendant A agreed to participate in the Bureau of Prisons Inmate Financial Responsibility Program.
- Defendant A will be required to register as a sex offender upon release as a condition of supervised release.
- Defendant A will be subject to federal and state sex offender registration requirements, including keeping registration current and periodic in-person verification.
- Upon release from confinement, notice will be provided to certain law enforcement agencies under 18 U.S.C. § 4042(c).
- Any monetary penalties imposed will be due and payable immediately and subject to enforcement by the United States, including Treasury offset.
- Funds posted as security for an appearance bond may be applied to satisfy financial obligations in the judgment.
- If Defendant A violates the plea agreement, the United States may void the agreement and pursue additional charges.
- The plea agreement does not bind other federal, state, or local prosecuting authorities.
- The plea agreement was filed on September 1, 2020 in the Eastern District of Kentucky, Lexington.
- The sentencing memorandum was filed on November 30, 2020.
- The minor victim and the adult male depicted in one or more images were ultimately determined to be the minor victim and Defendant A, respectively.

- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to conspiracy to commit embezzlement from a program receiving federal funds (18 U.S.C. § 371) and embezzlement from a program receiving federal funds (18 U.S.C. § 666).
- Defendant A admitted that she committed the offenses charged in Counts One and Two and is in fact guilty of each one.
- The plea agreement required the change of plea to occur no later than June 18, 2021.
- Count One carries a statutory maximum of 5 years of imprisonment and Count Two carries a statutory maximum of 10 years of imprisonment.
- Each count carries up to three years of supervised release.
- Each count carries a potential fine of up to $250,000.
- Each count requires a $100 mandatory special assessment.
- The plea agreement specified restitution of $36,028.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit assets seized during the investigation and prosecution and waived claims to such property.
- Defendant A signed the plea agreement acknowledgment on June 3, 2021.
- Defendant A began her career as a Boston Police officer in 1985.
- Defendant A worked as a patrol officer in multiple districts before serving in the DARE Program (1991–1992) and teaching at the police academy (1992–1995).
- Defendant A worked as a clerk in internal affairs from 1995 to 2004 and later worked at the Evidence Warehouse starting in 2006.
- Defendant A worked in the Evidence Control Unit until retiring in January 2020.
- Evidence Control Unit officers worked a regular shift from 7:30 a.m. to 4:00 p.m., Mondays through Fridays.
- The Evidence Control Unit typically included a Lieutenant or Captain, two Sergeants, and five or more officers.
- Evidence Control Unit duties included controlling access to the warehouse, storing and cataloging evidence, retrieving evidence for court, and transporting evidence when necessary.
- Around 2011, the Evidence Control Unit began a 'purge overtime' program due to running out of storage space.
- Purge overtime shifts ran from 4 p.m. to 8 p.m., most often Monday through Thursday.
- Purge overtime was paid on an hour-for-hour basis in 15-minute increments.
- Purge overtime required manual work and could not be performed remotely.
- Overtime hours were recorded on overtime slips that required the employee's name, purpose, start and end times, 'Actual Hours Worked,' and signatures of the employee, supervisor, and commander, with one slip required per overtime shift.
- On October 4, 2018, an overtime slip for Defendant A reflected a 4:00–8:00 p.m. purge shift and 'Actual Hours Worked: 04:00,' but alarm records showed departure at 5:16 p.m.
- The Evidence Warehouse was secured by perimeter and internal motion alarms, and alarm system data logged arming/disarming activity; to exit without triggering motion sensors one would need to disarm the alarm.
- From at least January 2015 through February 2019, Defendant A and others conspired to be paid for hours not worked by submitting false purge overtime slips claiming full shifts when they had not worked the full time.
- Supervisors in the Evidence Control Unit routinely endorsed purge overtime slips for themselves and subordinates despite knowing the full shifts had not been worked.
- The total amount paid for unworked purge overtime across the scheme during the charged period was approximately $386,766.
- Defendant A personally received approximately $36,028 for overtime hours she did not work during the conspiracy period.
- For purge shifts that Defendant A personally attended, the loss to the police department was approximately $258,389.
- The government viewed Defendant A as responsible for the full $386,766 scheme loss under relevant conduct principles during her tenure.
- Probation concluded that only the loss incurred on days when Defendant A worked purge shifts was reasonably foreseeable to her, calculated at approximately $258,389.
- Four charged officers tried before the Court in a related case were acquitted.
- Defendant A participated in trial preparation in early 2023 and testified before the Court in April 2023 about routinely leaving early from purge and kiosk overtime shifts and lying about it.
- Defendant A testified that officers began staying until 8 p.m. after learning of a possible investigation in May 2016.
- In March 2024, Defendant A testified in the related Evans case for the defense and stated she had no intent to steal when submitting overtime slips.
- In that same Evans testimony, Defendant A indicated concerns were raised to her about losing her family if convicted, and she acknowledged on cross-examination that she pled guilty freely and voluntarily.
- The government agreed that restitution for Defendant A should be limited to the amount she personally obtained, $36,028.
- The defense sentencing memorandum asserted that the improper overtime paid directly to Defendant A was $36,254.
- The defense sentencing memorandum stated that the Evans case resulted in a guilty verdict around March 14, 2024.
- The government stated that Defendant A had personally been paid for more unworked overtime than anyone besides one supervisor among the involved officers.
- Government filings reported testimony that Defendant A explained to new arrivals how the purge overtime scheme worked.
- The defense sentencing memorandum asserted that overtime abuse within the Evidence Control Unit was widespread and accepted by supervisory personnel.
- The defense sentencing memorandum asserted that, under the collective bargaining agreement, certain overtime shifts paid four hours even if fewer hours were worked.
- Defendant A testified that she often completed purge overtime work during her regular shift to leave earlier.
- The government’s filings referenced both 'purge' and 'kiosk' overtime shifts in describing the conduct.
- The defense sentencing memorandum stated that Defendant A participated in multiple proffer sessions and testified before a grand jury.

- The case is in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Florida, Orlando Division.
- The case number is 6:20-cr-80-Orl-37DCI.
- Count One of the Indictment charges conspiracy to defraud the United States with respect to claims in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 286.
- The defendant agreed to plead guilty to Count One.
- Count One carries a maximum term of imprisonment of 10 years.
- Count One carries a maximum fine of $250,000 or twice the gross gain or loss, whichever is greater.
- Count One carries a maximum term of supervised release of 3 years.
- A special assessment of $100 per felony count applies.
- The defendant agreed to make full restitution to the U.S. Department of the Treasury, Internal Revenue Service.
- The conspiracy operated by at least January 2012 through at least June 2016.
- The conspiracy occurred in the Middle District of Florida and elsewhere.
- The defendant conspired with Co-conspirator A (a sister of the defendant) and others.
- The conspiracy aim was to defraud the IRS by submitting false tax returns to obtain fraudulent tax refunds through a tax preparation business.
- The defendant knew the unlawful purpose of the plan and voluntarily joined it.
- Co-conspirator A owned a tax preparation business established in 2010 as a sole member LLC.
- The defendant managed the day-to-day operations of the tax preparation business at Co-conspirator A’s direction.
- The business initially operated from an office in Winter Garden, Florida.
- By 2011, the business expanded to additional offices in Altamonte Springs and Orlando, Florida.
- The business briefly operated an office in Tampa, Florida.
- Co-conspirator A and the defendant hired individuals to prepare tax returns at each office location.
- Co-conspirator A instructed the defendant to hire individuals with no tax preparation experience who spoke Spanish.
- They obtained Electronic Filing Identification Numbers (EFINs) from the IRS for each office, authorizing electronic filing from those locations.
- Co-conspirator A marketed the tax preparation business to the Latino community.
- Many clients of the business were undocumented and nonresident aliens.
- Co-conspirator A traveled to agricultural farms in Georgia and Florida to prepare returns for foreign workers that were filed through the business.
- Co-conspirator A and the defendant prepared and submitted ITIN applications to the IRS for clients.
- They submitted false utility and other residency documents to the IRS to obtain ITINs for clients.
- An ITIN is a unique nine-digit number enabling undocumented and nonresident aliens to file tax returns and obtain refunds.
- Co-conspirator A charged about $50 to prepare an ITIN application.
- Co-conspirator A charged approximately $300 to prepare a tax return.
- The business advertised “100% Refund Guarantee” on its Facebook page and office signage.
- Instruction sheets were placed at office computers listing items that could be added to returns to increase refunds, such as dependents.
- Beginning in 2012, employees were instructed to file fraudulent returns for previous clients without their consent.
- Preparers were instructed to submit false returns when the IRS filing season opened.
- False W-2s, identification documents, and dependent information were provided to employees to generate refunds.
- Employees were directed to use the names and ITINs of previous clients without their consent on returns.
- If a client returned, preparers were instructed to falsely tell them that a return had already been filed due to a computer glitch.
- Tax refunds were processed through a third-party processor.
- The defendant was responsible for printing tax refund checks after the processor notification.
- The processor deducted the preparation fee from the refund and deposited refunds into the business’s account.
- Fraudulently obtained refund checks were placed in lock boxes.
- Unclaimed refund checks were cashed at check-cashing businesses in Ocala and other areas in central Florida by co-conspirators.
- On January 27, 2014, a false 2013 tax return was filed using Victim A’s name and ITIN without consent.
- Victim A had previously used the business for 2011 and 2012 tax returns but went to a different preparer for 2013.
- The IRS rejected Victim A’s 2013 return because a return had already been filed using Victim A’s personal information.
- Victim A confirmed the W-2 and wage information on the false return were false and did not receive the refund.
- On February 7, 2014, a false joint 2013 return was filed using the personal information of Victim B and Victim C without their consent.
- The defendant was listed as the preparer on the false joint return filed on February 7, 2014.
- The false joint return on February 7, 2014 was filed from the Winter Garden office.
- Victim B and Victim C had their 2011 and 2012 returns prepared at the business but did not use the business for 2013 because their 2012 return was flagged as false by the IRS.
- Victim B and Victim C confirmed the W-2 and wage information on the 2013 return were false and they did not receive the refund.
- On January 28, 2014, Co-conspirator A prepared and filed a false 2013 return using Victim D’s personal information; Victim D resided in Georgia.
- The January 28, 2014 false return for Victim D was filed from the Winter Garden office.
- Victim D had a 2012 return previously prepared by Co-conspirator A when she traveled to Georgia to prepare returns.
- Victim D confirmed the wage and dependent information and the W-2 attached to the 2013 return were false.
- Victim D notified state police, who then notified the IRS.
- On April 22, 2014, the IRS suspended Co-conspirator A and the business from the IRS e-file program for filing fraudulent tax returns and revoked the business’s EFINs.
- In May 2014, the business name was changed to Universal Financial, Inc.
- The Winter Garden office was listed as the principal address for Universal Financial, Inc. with the State of Florida.
- Between June and July 2014, the defendant obtained EFINs for the office locations using the name Universal Financial, Inc.
- On those EFIN applications, the defendant listed herself as the principal and primary contact.
- After the name change, false tax returns continued to be e-filed using Universal Financial's EFINs.
- In October 2015, the IRS suspended the defendant and Universal Financial from participating in the IRS e-file program and revoked Universal Financial’s EFINs due to fraud.
- On October 26, 2015, a shell corporation named Resurrection Tax, Inc. was incorporated using a nominee.
- Resurrection Tax, Inc. listed the Winter Garden office address as its principal address with the State of Florida.
- On November 5, 2015, an EFIN was obtained for the Winter Garden office in the name Resurrection Tax, Inc., listing the nominee as principal and primary contact.
- After obtaining the Resurrection Tax EFIN, false tax returns continued to be filed.
- On October 26, 2015, a shell corporation named Living Faith Tax, Inc. was incorporated using a nominee.
- Living Faith Tax, Inc. listed the South Orange Blossom Trail office address as its principal address with the State of Florida.
- On November 3, 2015, an EFIN was obtained in the name Living Faith Tax, Inc. for the South Orange Blossom Trail office, listing the nominee as principal and primary contact.
- On October 27, 2015, a shell corporation named Living Hope Tax, Inc. was incorporated using a nominee.
- Living Hope Tax, Inc. listed the Altamonte Springs office address as its principal address with the State of Florida.
- On November 6, 2015, an EFIN was obtained in the name Living Hope Tax, Inc. for the Altamonte Springs office, listing the nominee as principal and primary contact.
- During 2013 and 2014, Co-conspirator A built luxury homes for herself and for the defendant.
- Co-conspirator A paid more than $1 million in cash for her own home.
- Co-conspirator A paid more than $800,000 for the defendant’s home.
- Co-conspirator A previously paid the defendant about $250,000 in commissions.
- Approximately 7,234 false tax returns claiming refunds were submitted or caused to be submitted through the business and its nominees.
- The fraud loss stipulated in the plea was more than $9.5 million and less than $25 million.
- The scheme ran for more than three years and involved filing more than 7,000 fraudulent returns.
- The fraudulent returns caused the Treasury Department to pay more than $24 million in fraudulent refunds.
- The Treasury Department paid fraudulent refunds totaling $24,940,495.
- The defendant managed day-to-day operations of the business and also prepared and filed tax returns.
- The defendant printed tax refund checks and filed applications with the IRS.
- The business operated at five locations.
- The business marketed its services primarily to the Latino community, and many clients only spoke Spanish.
- Employees were instructed to submit false utility and residency documents to obtain ITINs for clients so the IRS would accept and process their returns.
- Employees were instructed to falsify deductions, exemptions, and credits on client tax returns to generate refunds.
- Employees were instructed to file fraudulent returns using the personal information of previous clients without consent.
- Employees prepared and filed fraudulent returns using foreign identification documents and ITINs of individuals whom they had never met.
- Co-conspirator A provided identity and return information to employees in folders and boxes and instructed them to manipulate numbers to ensure refunds.
- Unindicted co-conspirators illegally cashed unclaimed refund checks at check-cashing businesses in Ocala, Florida and other areas in Central Florida.
- After the April 2014 suspension, the defendant changed the business name to Universal Financial and applied to participate in the IRS’s e-file program using that name.
- On the EFIN application for Universal Financial, the defendant listed herself as the principal and primary contact and did not disclose Co-conspirator A’s involvement.
- After the October 2015 suspension, the defendants used nominees to incorporate shell entities Resurrection Tax, Inc., Living Faith Tax, Inc., and Living Hope Tax, Inc.
- The nominees included Co-conspirator A’s daughter and her boyfriend.
- The defendant applied for and obtained EFINs for the business’s locations in the names of the shell entities and listed the nominees as principals and primary contacts.
- The defendants continued to operate the business and e-file fraudulent tax returns using the shell entities’ EFINs.
- Approximately 7,234 fraudulent tax returns were submitted or caused to be submitted through the business.
- During the conspiracy, Co-conspirator A paid the defendant salary and commissions of at least $250,000.
- At least $800,000 of fraudulent proceeds were used to pay for the defendant’s custom-built house.
- The IRS assigns a unique identifier or document locator number on each tax return tied to a particular taxpayer.
- The defendants used names, Social Security numbers or ITINs of previous clients to produce fraudulent returns to obtain refunds.
