- Victim A sent Defendant A nude pictures of herself, and Defendant A sent Victim A pictures of himself masturbating.
- Law enforcement determined Defendant A resided in Pennsylvania during the investigation.
- On October 12, 2018, a parent reported to the FBI that two 12-year-old minors had been in contact on Snapchat with an adult using the screen name [redacted].
- Parents discovered the Snapchat communications while reviewing their children's devices, including a cellular phone and an Apple iPod.
- On October 19, 2018, the FBI and St. Louis County Police interviewed both minors and their parents at the St. Louis County Police Department.
- Investigators determined that Victim A sent numerous nude videos and images of herself to the Snapchat user, who also sent videos and photographs of his genitals to her.
- Victim A began Snapchat messages with the user in September 2018, after a voice chat where he said he wanted to have sex with her in a hotel and she declined.
- The adult groomed Victim A by asking for photos of her feet and other areas of her body, progressing from clothed to bra and underwear.
- On September 30, 2018, Victim A sent partly nude photos including bare breasts and images of her genital area, and then videos of her masturbating at the adult's direction.
- After receiving the videos, the adult sent Victim A a video depicting himself masturbating.
- During the September 30, 2018 chat, the adult stated he lived in the New York area.
- Snapchat message exchanges between Victim A and the adult continued intermittently until October 12, 2018.
- A review of the Snapchat messages revealed consistently sexually explicit conversations, but most images sent on Snapchat were not recovered by law enforcement.
- The adult stated in messages that he was 20 years old and acknowledged he could get in trouble by sending and receiving sexually explicit content with Victim A.
- Victim A identified herself as 13 years old in one message, though she was actually 12 at the time.
- The adult directed Victim A to make at least one video saying a statement referencing her 'thirteen-year-old feet.'
- On October 4, 2018, the adult asked Victim A to take 'feet and [genitals] pics' in the mirror and to send videos as well.
- On October 5, 2018, the adult instructed Victim A to 'finger yourself' and to redo a video without covering her face and to 'bend over and touch yourself.'
- Investigators linked Defendant A to the Snapchat account used in the communications.
- The email address associated with the Snapchat account was identified as belonging to Defendant A through a subpoena to the email provider.
- A phone number tied to the associated email account was subscribed to by Defendant A per a subpoena to the carrier.
- IP address logs for Snapchat logins were traced to an address in Allentown, Pennsylvania, linked to Defendant A.
- Snapchat returned some images of investigative interest from the search of the account used by the adult.
- On February 28, 2019, Victim A identified Defendant A in a photo lineup as the person using the Snapchat account.
- Victim A identified herself in three images returned from Snapchat, including a nude image focusing on her buttocks and feet, a bathroom image with exposed breasts, and an image showing the bottom of her foot.
- On April 24, 2019, law enforcement executed a search warrant at Defendant A's home in Pennsylvania and seized twenty devices.
- During the search, after initial questioning, Defendant A requested an attorney and questioning ceased.
- An All-in-One Mac computer contained three suspected files of child pornography not involving Victim A.
- Several devices, including an Apple iPhone, Apple iPod Touch, LG cell phone, and Motorola smartphone, were initially locked and inaccessible to law enforcement.
- In summer 2021, the Regional Computer Center accessed Defendant A's Motorola smartphone and found approximately 55 files of child pornography.
- A search warrant for Victim A's Instagram account yielded images and videos similar to what Defendant A requested in chats, but most Snapchat images created at his request were not located.
- Defendant A received images and videos of child pornography over the internet from Victim A via social media applications including Snapchat.
- The images from Victim A depicted a minor engaging in sexually explicit conduct or the lascivious exhibition of genitals.
- Defendant A knew Victim A was a minor when he received the images of child pornography from her.
- The internet is described as a means and facility of interstate and foreign commerce used to transmit data streams for graphic files in this case context.
- On September 9, 2020, Defendant A was taken into federal custody by the United States Marshals Service in Allentown, Pennsylvania.
- Defendant A was jailed without bond during the pendency of the case.
- A sentencing hearing was set for April 14, 2022, at 2:00 p.m.
- During the chats, Defendant A requested from Victim A images and movies of her genitals and of her engaging in sexual acts.
- Victim A identified Defendant A in a photographic lineup on February 28, 2019, as the person with whom she was communicating on Snapchat.
- Victim A identified herself in images returned from Snapchat, including one with her nude on her bed focusing on her buttocks and feet and another with exposed breasts in a bathroom.
- According to the defense memorandum, the government views Defendant A as solely culpable for the offense conduct described.
- Defendant A has no prior criminal history as represented in the defense memorandum.
- Defendant A is described as having lived in Pennsylvania his entire life prior to arrest.
- The defense memorandum reports that Defendant A experienced bullying for being overweight when younger.
- The defense memorandum states that as a youth, Defendant A’s parents purchased him a computer he could use in the privacy of his room and he began viewing pornography.
- The defense memorandum reports that a friend introduced Defendant A to the website 'chatroulette,' where he began engaging in online interactions.
- The defense memorandum states that early in his teenage years, Defendant A developed a foot fetish and asked peers for pictures of their feet online.
- According to the defense memorandum, Defendant A’s internet use for sexual gratification became an addiction, reaching up to five hours per day.
- The defense memorandum states that his requests for feet pictures broadened to include other males and minors over time.
- The defense memorandum reports that Defendant A has experienced periods of depression and at one time was prescribed Zoloft, though he was not taking medication at the time of the memo.
- At the time of the defense memorandum, Defendant A was 26 years old.
- The defense memorandum states that prior to arrest, Defendant A was attending school to begin a career in information technology and had begun working for an IT firm.
- The defense memorandum states Defendant A intends to participate in treatment and support resources while incarcerated and after release.
- Victim A’s parents initially notified local law enforcement (Des Peres Police Department) about inappropriate Snapchat communications on October 14, 2018, leading to an FBI referral.
- Investigators associated the Snapchat account with an email address and phone number identified as belonging to Defendant A.
- The plea agreement describes that the majority of sexually explicit images Victim A created at Defendant A’s request via Snapchat were never recovered.

- The case is in the Central District of California under case number 5:20-00171-PA.
- The defendant pleaded guilty on January 19, 2021, pursuant to a plea agreement.
- The plea agreement required the defendant to plead guilty to Count One charging attempted arson of a building used in interstate or foreign commerce, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 844(i).
- The offense occurred in the early morning of May 31, 2020.
- The incident took place at the building housing the East Valley Republican Women Federated in La Quinta, California, within the Central District of California.
- The EVRWF sold merchandise that had been shipped in interstate or foreign commerce.
- The EVRWF building was used in interstate or foreign commerce and in an activity affecting interstate commerce.
- The defendant constructed a homemade Molotov cocktail by taping together three glass bottles.
- The bottles used in the device were filled with an ignitable fluid.
- A rag was placed into the mouth of one of the bottles as part of the device.
- The defendant broke the front windows of the EVRWF building.
- The defendant lit the rag on fire before throwing the device.
- The defendant repeatedly threw the homemade Molotov cocktail into the EVRWF building.
- The incident caused at least $5,426.64 in property damage.
- At all relevant times, the defendant intended to damage and destroy the EVRWF building using fire or an explosive and understood the nature and consequences of his actions.
- For a violation of 18 U.S.C. § 844(i), the statutory maximum term of imprisonment is 20 years.
- For a violation of 18 U.S.C. § 844(i), the statutory maximum term of supervised release is three years.
- For a violation of 18 U.S.C. § 844(i), the statutory maximum fine is $250,000 or twice the gross gain or gross loss, whichever is greatest.
- For a violation of 18 U.S.C. § 844(i), a mandatory special assessment of $100 applies.
- The offense of conviction carries a statutory mandatory minimum sentence of five years' imprisonment.
- The parties believed the applicable restitution amount was approximately $5,426.64, subject to change before sentencing.
- The plea agreement required the defendant to make restitution at or before the time of sentencing.
- At sentencing, the government agreed to move to dismiss the remaining count of the indictment.
- The plea agreement document was filed on March 4, 2021.
- The Presentence Report was disclosed on April 19, 2021.
- On May 31, 2020, a fire was set at the EVRWF headquarters.
- The EVRWF is located in a shopping center.
- The EVRWF purchases and ships goods throughout the United States.
- The EVRWF windows prominently display political signs, banners, and other items.
- Law enforcement reviewed video footage from the EVRWF and a nearby business during the investigation.
- The footage shows a man later identified as the defendant running to the front of the EVRWF with a metal bat.
- The man shattered some of the EVRWF’s windows with the bat.
- He retrieved a set of three clear bottles that had been bound together from his backpack.
- He unscrewed the cap to one of the bottles and inserted an orange rag.
- He used a green lighter from his right front pocket to light the rag.
- He threw the set of bottles through one of the broken windows.
- The bottles appeared to hit a metal pull-down grate behind the broken glass.
- He picked up the backpack and bat and ran away after the first attempt.
- He returned to the EVRWF about seven minutes later.
- Upon returning, he smashed more windows.
- He reached into the EVRWF to retrieve the bottles before trying again.
- About thirty seconds later, he threw the bottles into the EVRWF again and a fire immediately ignited.
- After the fire ignited, he ran back to his car and drove away.
- Law enforcement determined the cause of the fire was arson.
- Law enforcement determined the method of ignition was a firebomb or Molotov cocktail.
- Law enforcement determined the device had an explosive or incendiary charge of more than one-quarter ounce.
- Law enforcement determined the device met the definition of a destructive device under 26 U.S.C. § 5845(f)(1)(A) and (D).
- Law enforcement executed search warrants on the defendant’s residence and car.
- A dash camera was recovered during the searches.
- The dash camera contained a recording dated June 28, 2020.
- In the recording, the defendant described lighting the fire at the EVRWF and discussed the incident in detail.
- In the recording, he described his planning for the offense.
- In the recording, he described attempts to recruit others to help him with the offense.
- In the recording, he described the steps he took to start the fire.
- In the recording, he said that after setting the fire he shaved his beard to change his appearance.
- In the recording, he said he altered his car to make it look different from the night of the fire.
- In the recording, he stated, "I know what I did, I wasn't drunk, I have no excuses."
- In the recording, he said he did it "to prove it to myself ... I've been saying I'm gonna do this ... then I did it."
- A sentencing hearing was set for May 24, 2021, at 8:30 a.m.
- The defendant served as a scout for people who smuggled undocumented individuals into California.
- On two occasions ten days apart, the defendant was arrested for attempting to drive undocumented people through a highway checkpoint.
- The defendant stated he has regularly used drugs and alcohol since childhood.
- The defendant has never participated in drug treatment and expressed interest in receiving treatment.
- The defendant has a high school degree.
- The defendant aspires to work in heating, ventilation, and air conditioning or welding.

- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Count 1, conspiracy to commit a violation of the Travel Act, under 18 U.S.C. § 371.
- The conspiracy’s object was to use interstate or foreign commerce facilities or wires to promote or facilitate commercial bribery in violation of California Penal Code § 641.3 or New York Penal Law § 180.3.
- The statutory maximum sentence for Count 1 is 5 years of imprisonment.
- Count 1 carries a potential fine of up to $250,000.
- Count 1 carries up to three years of supervised release.
- Count 1 carries a mandatory special assessment of $100.
- The conspiracy began no later than July 2017.
- During the conspiracy, Defendant A resided in California.
- Defendant A conferred bribery payments to employees of Amazon.com, Inc. and its subsidiaries to obtain confidential information and other benefits on the Amazon Marketplace.
- The bribery payments were made with intent to influence employees’ conduct without Amazon’s knowledge or consent.
- Defendant A previously worked as an Amazon Seller Support Associate in Hyderabad, India until around December 2015.
- After leaving Amazon and immigrating to the United States, Defendant A used knowledge and connections within Amazon to provide consulting services to third-party sellers, relying on bribe-funded access to protected networks and confidential information.
- Defendant A also used the alias “Roger” in connection with the conduct.
- Defendant A engaged multiple former Amazon colleagues, including individuals identified by initials, to accept bribes in exchange for misusing employee privileges.
- Compromised Amazon insiders agreed to accept money corruptly and without employer knowledge in return for using their positions to benefit outside parties.
- From around January 2017 until around August 2018, Defendant A at times served as an intermediary between corrupted Amazon employees and outside consultants and their clients.
- Defendant A forwarded third-party sellers’ work requests to Amazon insiders who performed the work in exchange for bribe payments.
- Internal Amazon annotations were referred to in code as “fruit.”
- On about April 25–26, 2018, annotation requests were relayed and the annotation history for requested seller accounts was provided after a payment arrangement labeled as “friends and family” was suggested.
- On or about October 2, 2017, Defendant A sent approximately $754 (INR 48,495) to an Amazon insider in Hyderabad, India using an online money transfer service.
- On or about October 30, 2017, Defendant A sent another $1,500 (INR 96,375) to the same insider via online money transfer.
- On or about March 28, 2018, Defendant A sent $1,000 (INR 64,270) to another corrupt Amazon insider.
- Defendant A helped arrange bribes funneled through an associate in Texas, characterizing a payment as tuition-related to conceal its nature.
- Defendant A connected corrupted Amazon employees directly with persons paying for improper assistance with seller accounts, enabling direct work and payments.
- Over nearly one year, one corrupted Amazon insider received ongoing payments totaling more than $20,000 for misusing access to provide internal information and unfair advantages to a consultant’s client.
- On about January 19, 2018, participants discussed and agreed on $4,000 per approval to obtain approvals to sell two restricted products.
- Defendant A’s conduct included use of interstate or foreign wires affecting the Western District of Washington.
- For sentencing purposes, Defendant A agreed that total bribes paid to Amazon employees by him or those introduced through him during his active involvement were approximately $100,000.
- In exchange for the plea, the prosecution agreed to move to dismiss the remaining counts of the indictment at sentencing.
- The Court may order restitution for losses caused by Defendant A’s criminal conduct, not limited to the offense of conviction.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit to the United States any property constituting or derived from proceeds obtained, including a sum of money to be determined at sentencing.
- On September 16, 2020, a grand jury returned a nine-count indictment including conspiracy, conspiracy to commit wire fraud, and wire fraud counts.
- Defendant A was arrested on September 18, 2020, in the Central District of California and made a first appearance the same day.
- On October 25, 2020, Defendant A was released on bond in the Central District of California, including a $100,000 surety bond.
- On October 15, 2020, Defendant A had initial appearance, arraignment, and detention hearing in the Western District of Washington, and was placed on bond with computer limitations and location monitoring.
- On September 28, 2021, the appearance bond was amended to remove location monitoring.
- Pretrial Services records indicate Defendant A complied with all court-ordered conditions of release.
- The plea agreement was entered pursuant to Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 11(c)(1)(B).
- Defendant A admitted using coded terminology such as “fruit” to refer to internal Amazon annotations.
- Defendant A suggested that annotation-related payments be sent as “friends and family” through a bank to conceal the nature of the transactions.
- Defendant A used an online money transfer service headquartered in the Western District of Washington to send bribe payments abroad.
- Participants used end-to-end encrypted messaging and alias email accounts to conceal communications.
- Co-conspirators bribed Amazon insiders to obtain confidential internal documents describing Amazon’s review-positioning formula and other proprietary information.
- Co-conspirators bribed insiders to obtain competitor business information such as business reports, pending suspension lists, and inventory lists.
- Internal annotations documented Amazon’s reasons for suspensions or reinstatements and were used by the conspirators to craft appeals.
- Insiders sometimes drafted Plans of Action for suspended sellers, and conspirators submitted appeals containing materially false information to obtain reinstatements.
- A prolific third-party seller paid $200,000 for reinstatement; during about seven months between suspensions, that seller had revenue exceeding $10 million.
- An insider told a client that the remedial steps described in a Plan of Action need not actually be implemented, revealing the appeals were crafted to ‘make the POA work.’
- Insiders reinstated suspended products on request from co-conspirators in mid-2018.
- Co-conspirators used false intellectual property complaints and negative-feedback campaigns to suspend a competitor, including instructing to submit more trademark complaints and to place orders to leave counterfeit feedback.
- Co-conspirators removed negative customer feedback for favored sellers upon request.
- Co-conspirators defaced a third-party seller’s product listings by altering listing content and images.
- Co-conspirators increased clients’ hazardous materials inventory allowances through bribery, allowing more units to be stored and sold.
- Co-conspirators obtained brand ‘ungating’ by stealing and doctoring invoices submitted by other sellers to appear as authentic supplier invoices for their own clients.
- Co-conspirators filed fraudulent reimbursement requests under Fulfillment by Amazon and had insiders approve them, causing losses to Amazon.
- For two sellers, fraudulent FBA reimbursements approved through insiders totaled about $15,297 and $19,475, respectively.
- Defendant A directed an insider to fulfill an order to acquire 1,000 customer email addresses, with a quoted charge of $0.60 per email.
- Defendant A imposed late fees on a client’s outstanding payment and relayed that late fees were charged because the client had not paid.
- Defendant A discussed pricing for services with insiders, including per-annotation rates, invoice pricing, and listing alterations.
- Defendant A arranged to transfer $4,000 to an insider through intermediaries in the United States and India.
- On August 19, 2018, Defendant A and an insider discussed an outstanding amount owed of 1270 and a separate payment of 1500 that had not yet been received.
- Defendant A discussed optimizing four product listings (ASINs) with a proposed payment of $1,000 per ASIN contingent on ranking outcomes.
- Participants used the email subject line “Re: Fruit (2,3,4)” in communications concerning internal annotations, reflecting the coded terminology.
- In late August and early September 2018, Amazon identified and terminated several corrupt employees in India following a complaint about leaked internal information.
- Shortly after Amazon’s internal purge, Defendant A deleted WhatsApp and email communications.
- Defendant A thereafter minimized future communications and collaboration with consultants following the 2018 terminations.
- Defendant A acknowledged that the Court may consider additional facts contained in the Presentence Report and facts presented by the parties at sentencing.
- Defendant A was contacted via a professional networking platform by a co-defendant around 2017, which led to his involvement.
- Defendant A realized in 2018 that his conduct was illegal when insiders in India were fired, and he then extricated himself and blocked involved contacts.
- Defendant A used aliases and took steps to avoid detection, reflecting awareness that the conduct was wrongful.
- Participants discussed risk and being under internal investigation at Amazon during August 2018.
- Defendant A assisted in obtaining internal seller business reports filtered by time period to gauge competitor sales and units sold.
- Defendant A coordinated with insiders to obtain 3P seller account annotations and reports in exchange for specified payments.
- The scheme included a practice of routing payments through third parties and labeling transfers to disguise their nature.
- Defendant A worked with insiders to adjust Amazon product listings (e.g., title, image, bullet points) for a per-change fee.
- The conspirators used coded email subjects and nicknames to discuss annotation requests and payments.
- Defendant A’s involvement included facilitating assistance for accounts he helped operate on the Amazon Marketplace.
- Defendant A admitted that additional facts beyond those in the plea’s factual statement could be presented at sentencing.
- Defendant A agreed to financial disclosure and cooperation obligations to facilitate restitution and forfeiture, including executing a financial disclosure statement and providing supporting documents.
- Defendant A’s plea agreement includes a waiver provision for appeal and collateral attack under specified conditions.
- Defendant A’s role included coordinating between U.S.-based consultants and India-based Amazon employees for bribe-funded tasks such as annotations and account actions.
- Participants discussed future pricing and ongoing service arrangements for annotations and other insider-provided services, anticipating repeated transactions.
- The scheme’s tactics included manipulating search rankings, reviews, seller permissions, and reimbursements to confer unfair advantages to certain sellers.

- The case is in the United States District Court for the District of Alaska.
- The docket number includes 3:20-cr-00081-JMK-MMS.
- The defendant agreed to plead guilty to Count 1: Threatening Interstate Communication, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 875(c).
- The defendant agreed to plead guilty to Count 2: Obstruction of Free Exercise of Religion, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 247(a)(2), (d)(5).
- The plea agreement is governed by Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 11(c)(1)(C).
- On or about November 1, 2019, the defendant was in Anchorage, Alaska.
- On or about November 1, 2019, the defendant called a synagogue in Los Angeles, California, using a cellular phone.
- The defendant left a voicemail threatening violence against synagogue congregants.
- The voicemail used a derogatory slur for Jewish people.
- The defendant intended the voicemail as a threat and knew it would be viewed as a threat.
- The defendant intended to obstruct congregants of the synagogue from enjoying the free exercise of their religious beliefs.
- Count 1 carries a maximum term of imprisonment of not more than 5 years.
- Count 1 carries a maximum supervised release term of up to 3 years.
- Count 1 carries a maximum fine of up to $250,000.
- Count 1 requires a mandatory special assessment of $100.
- Count 2 carries a maximum term of imprisonment of not more than 1 year.
- For Count 2, probation of up to 5 years is an authorized alternative to imprisonment.
- Count 2 carries a maximum fine of up to $100,000.
- Count 2 requires a mandatory special assessment of $25.
- Count 1 is identified as a felony offense in the plea agreement.
- The defendant agreed that the Court will order restitution to the victim or victims, to be determined at sentencing.
- The parties anticipated restitution would be not less than $19,000 and could be higher.
- The defendant agreed to pay the entire special assessment on the day of sentencing.
- The United States agreed not to prosecute the defendant further for other offenses related to the events underlying the indictment, contingent on the plea.
- The defendant signed the plea agreement in late March 2021.
- The government executed the plea agreement on April 1, 2021.
- On November 1, 2019, the defendant was under the influence of alcohol when the call was made.
- The defendant viewed a YouTube video depicting a confrontation between members of a synagogue in California and a YouTube personality.
- The defendant was a fan of the YouTube personality shown in the video.
- Motivated by anger at the video, the defendant sought to retaliate on behalf of the YouTube personality.
- The defendant searched the internet to find contact information for the synagogue before calling.
- The defendant called the synagogue’s publicly accessible phone number and left a voicemail when the call was answered by a voicemail program.
- When confronted by police, the defendant initially denied leaving the voicemail but later admitted to making the call.
- The defendant denied any intent to carry out the threats in the voicemail.
- The government found no evidence that the defendant had any prior relationship with the synagogue or its congregants.
- There was no indication that the defendant generally harbors ill will toward the victims specifically or Jewish people in general.
- There was no evidence that the defendant intended to or planned to carry out any violence.
- The defendant had no prior criminal charges or convictions according to the sentencing memorandum.
- The defendant has a history of mental health issues requiring treatment.
- The defendant is approximately 50 years old at the time of the sentencing memorandum.
- The defendant prefers female pronouns while continuing to use the given name as shown in the case caption.
- The victim impact statement for sentencing was provided by the Anti-Defamation League Pacific Northwest at the request of the synagogue’s congregation president.
- The synagogue congregation’s president served as the primary point of contact with the U.S. Attorney’s Office regarding victim rights.

- Defendant A pled guilty to one count of Criminal Infringement of a Copyright under 17 U.S.C. § 506(a)(1)(B) and 18 U.S.C. § 2319(c)(1).
- A one-count Information charging Criminal Infringement of a Copyright was filed on September 22, 2020.
- Defendant A pled guilty to the Information on December 3, 2020.
- The statutory maximum term of imprisonment for the offense is 3 years.
- The offense carries a maximum fine of $250,000 or twice the pecuniary gain or loss.
- The offense carries a maximum supervised release term of up to 3 years.
- A $100 special assessment applies to the felony conviction.
- Between October 26, 2017 and January 2018, law enforcement reviewed IP addresses sharing suspected child sexual abuse material on a P2P network that resolved to D.C. addresses.
- An identified IP address (IP Address A) had been sharing content via a P2P network and was believed to be physically located in the District of Columbia.
- On October 26, 2017, an undercover user connected to IP Address A using a P2P client and partially or completely downloaded multiple files, including images and videos.
- On November 3, 2017, an undercover user connected to IP Address A using a P2P client and partially or completely downloaded multiple files, including images and videos.
- On December 9, 2017, an undercover user connected to IP Address A using a P2P client and partially or completely downloaded multiple files, including images and videos.
- Queries to ARIN indicated the subject IP address was associated with Verizon.
- On or about November 1, 2017 and January 17, 2018, administrative subpoenas to Verizon showed the subject IP was assigned to Defendant A at a Washington, D.C. residence.
- A search warrant was executed at Defendant A's residence on February 12, 2018.
- When officers entered, Defendant A’s computer was open, unlocked, connected to the internet, and actively downloading numerous files via a P2P service.
- File names observed during the search included titles of television shows and movies such as Detective Conan and A Plastic Ocean.
- Several laptops and hard drives were seized from the residence and forensically examined.
- Forensic artifacts showed Defendant A used P2P services and the internet to download copyrighted TV shows and movies and made them available to others via P2P.
- A Netgear ReadyNAS device used by Defendant A is a storage system designed to share files over the internet and allows user connections with a username and password.
- On an external storage device, folders labeled 'torrents files', 'Torrents complete', and 'torrents temps' had last modified dates of February 12, 2018.
- There were 585 files in the 'torrents' folder on the external storage device.
- The 'Torrents complete' folder contained copyrighted content including episodes of 'Altered Carbon' and films such as 'Coco' and 'Phantom Thread'.
- One external storage device contained over 1,000 commercially produced motion pictures.
- The same device contained eight television documentaries.
- The same device contained 187 television shows.
- A Drobo external storage device had a 'Movies' folder last accessed on February 11, 2018.
- The 'Movies' folder on the Drobo device contained over 9,000 copyrighted and commercially produced movies.
- Another Drobo device had a 'TV Shows' folder last accessed on February 4, 2018 containing over 1,200 copyrighted television shows.
- A Lenovo laptop’s registry indicated recent access to a 'Downloads' folder.
- A WD Passport external hard drive contained user folders labeled with multiple television series, many of which contained full seasons.
- Defendant A admitted he also lawfully purchased some new and used videos and digitized them by burning them onto his computer hard drive.
- Defendant A admitted he improperly downloaded over 2,500 videos, including copyrighted television shows and motion pictures, using P2P services and the internet.
- More than 10 of the improperly downloaded videos were obtained between October 26, 2017 and February 12, 2018.
- The value of videos downloaded between October 26, 2017 and February 12, 2018 was at least $2,500.
- Defendant A knew that by downloading the videos he was reproducing and distributing them by making them available to others via P2P and the internet.
- Using a median price of $7.50 per video, the conduct between October 26, 2017 and February 12, 2018 represents an approximate loss of $18,750.
- The sentencing memorandum described the loss amount as nearly $19,000.
- During the investigation, law enforcement connected directly to Defendant A’s IP address and downloaded files that included images and videos depicting the sexual abuse of children.
- Some file names observed during the February 12, 2018 search were indicative of child pornography.
- The sentencing memorandum included a photograph of Defendant A’s computer screen displaying files with titles indicative of child sexual abuse material.
- The case was scheduled for sentencing on February 23, 2021.
- Defendant A signed the Statement of Offense on September 8, 2020, acknowledging it was true and accurate.
- Defense counsel acknowledged the Statement of Offense on September 22, 2020.
- Defendant A executed the Plea Agreement on September 21, 2020.
- The government stated in the Plea Agreement that, based on information then available, Defendant A had no criminal convictions.
- The sentencing memorandum asserted that Defendant A had prior arrests for theft-related offenses involving fraudulent checks.
- The sentencing memorandum asserted that one prior conviction involved writing fraudulent checks to a department store totaling over $23,000.
- Defendant A consented to conduct plea and sentencing proceedings by video teleconference and/or teleconference due to the COVID-19 pandemic.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit digital storage devices seized in the investigation, including multiple hard drives and external storage devices, as property used in violation of federal law.
- Defendant A agreed to relinquish all claim, title, and interest in the seized digital storage devices and consented to their forfeiture and potential destruction.
- The initial investigation method involved identifying torrent infohash values associated with shared files on a P2P network.
- An undercover agent directly connected to Defendant A’s IP address to initiate downloads using a P2P client software program.
- The sentencing memorandum stated Defendant A had a normal, healthy childhood, a GED, some college education, and a technical institute certification in computer engineering.
- The sentencing memorandum asserted that Defendant A relied on specialized computer knowledge in his livelihood and used it to illegally download copyrighted materials.

- A federal grand jury indicted Defendant and Co-Defendant A on September 29, 2020 for conspiracy to distribute and possess with intent to distribute fentanyl resulting in death and distribution of fentanyl resulting in death.
- On August 29, 2024, Defendant executed a Rule 11(c)(1)(C) plea agreement.
- Defendant agreed to plead guilty to so much of Count One as charged conspiracy to distribute and possess with intent to distribute fentanyl and so much of Count Two as charged distribution of fentanyl.
- The United States agreed to dismiss the death-resulting allegations in Counts One and Two at sentencing.
- On August 29, 2024, Defendant entered a guilty plea to the conspiracy and distribution counts without the death-resulting allegations.
- The Court deferred acceptance of the Rule 11(c)(1)(C) plea agreement pending preparation of a Presentence Report.
- Defendant acknowledged in the plea agreement that the statutory maximum penalty includes up to 20 years of imprisonment.
- Defendant acknowledged in the plea agreement that supervised release of at least 3 years and up to life applies.
- Defendant acknowledged in the plea agreement a possible fine up to $1,000,000.
- The DEA investigation determined Defendant was trafficking fentanyl out of his home in Wareham, Massachusetts over approximately four months in 2019.
- A confidential source (CS) made four controlled purchases of fentanyl at the direction of the DEA in February and March 2019.
- Before and after each controlled purchase, the CS was searched with negative results and was provided official advance funds by law enforcement.
- The CS was provided an audio recorder for each controlled purchase.
- On February 6, 2019, Defendant told the CS he was waiting for a runner to deliver product during the controlled buy.
- On February 6, 2019, law enforcement observed a vehicle with a Rhode Island license plate arrive and depart the area of Defendant’s residence during the buy.
- The DEA laboratory confirmed that the February 6, 2019 purchase was 9 grams of a mixture and substance containing fentanyl.
- On February 21, 2019, the CS was provided $750 in official funds for a controlled purchase of approximately 10 grams of fentanyl.

- In May 2020, the defendant was President of Premier Healthcare Management.

- The defendant and affiliated entities maintained bank accounts at First Financial Bank, which has a principal office in Cincinnati, Ohio.

- To execute the scheme, 203 checks were written and negotiated.
- The total value of the checks used in the scheme exceeded $118 million.

- On May 15, 2020, approximately 61 checks totaling about $26 million moved from an S&T Bank account to First Financial Bank Accounts 3, 4, and 5.

- On May 18, 2020, approximately 54 checks totaling about $33 million moved from an S&T Bank account to First Financial Bank Accounts 3, 4, and 5.

- A grand jury returned an indictment charging Defendant A and four others with a bank fraud scheme between April 2020 and July 2020 and related money laundering offenses.
- The indictment alleged six counts of bank fraud under 18 U.S.C. § 1344 and eight counts of money laundering under 18 U.S.C. § 1957.
- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Count Two of the indictment, a bank fraud charge.
- Defendant A admitted that the facts in the plea factual basis are true and establish guilt beyond a reasonable doubt.
- The offense of conviction carries a statutory maximum term of 30 years’ imprisonment and a maximum fine of $1,000,000.
- The offense carries a mandatory special assessment of $100 and a maximum of five years of supervised release.
- Restitution is mandatory for the offense and Defendant A agreed to pay restitution as ordered by the Court.
- The Paycheck Protection Program (PPP) authorized forgivable small-business loans for specified expenses and required that the business be in operation on February 15, 2020.
- Bank 1 (Financial Institution 1) was an SBA-approved lender headquartered in Green Bay, Wisconsin, and its deposits were insured by the FDIC.
- PPP loans were issued by private approved lenders using their own funds, 100% guaranteed by the SBA.
- From April 2020 through July 2020, participants submitted and caused to be submitted fraudulent PPP loan applications to Financial Institution 1.
- Fraudulent applications falsely claimed the applicant businesses were operational as of February 15, 2020 and falsely represented employee counts and payroll.
- The fraudulent applications included falsified IRS Forms 941 for each quarter of 2019 and the first quarter of 2020.
- None of the businesses involved filed Forms 941 in any quarter of 2019 or in the first quarter of 2020, indicating absence of employees.
- Fraudulent applications claimed funds would be used for payroll, lease/mortgage, interest, and utilities, but funds were used for personal and non-business expenses.
- Participants opened or directed others to open new business bank accounts at Financial Institution 1 to receive PPP funds.
- At least approximately $960,000 in PPP loan proceeds was obtained through the scheme, with an additional approximately $442,500 attempted.
- Defendant A caused the submission of eight fraudulent PPP loan applications seeking approximately $1,250,000 in total.
- In April 2020, Defendant A submitted two PPP applications for businesses he controlled or was associated with, seeking approximately $237,500, and those loans were funded.
- Defendant A assisted Co-Defendant A in creating a fraudulent PPP loan application for CFA Auto Transport LLC for approximately $242,500, including false certifications and fabricated Forms 941.
- Bank 1 approved the CFA loan and wired approximately $242,500 to CFA’s business account.
- After funding, Co-Defendant A provided Defendant A with three cashier’s checks totaling approximately $65,000 from the CFA loan proceeds.
- Defendant A created a fraudulent PPP application for Rebels Paris LLC for approximately $155,000, including false employee and payroll claims and fabricated Forms 941.
- Bank 1 approved the Rebels loan and wired approximately $155,000 to the Rebels business account.
- Approximately $45,000 from the Rebels loan proceeds was distributed to Defendant A and his entities via cashier’s checks.
- After Bank 1 froze the Rebels account on or about June 7, 2020, Defendant A created a fake lease between his company and Rebels to conceal the true purpose of a payment.
- Defendant A and others submitted a fraudulent PPP application for New Beginnings Family Services LLC for approximately $155,000 claiming 28 employees and $92,000 average monthly payroll; the application was denied.
- Defendant A created a fraudulent PPP application for Premier Logistic Solutions LLC for approximately $212,500, which Bank 1 did not approve.
- Defendant A created a fraudulent PPP application for Davis Development Group Inc. for approximately $177,500, including falsified Forms 941 and false operational and employee claims.
- Bank 1 approved the Davis Development loan and wired approximately $177,500 to that company’s business account.
- After the Davis loan funded, the owner obtained a cashier’s check for approximately $75,000 payable to Defendant A’s company T&T Holdings, which Defendant A picked up.
- Defendant A later created a fake joint venture agreement between his company and Davis Development to conceal the purpose of the $75,000 payment.
- Defendant A created a fraudulent PPP application for Glory Transportation Services LLC for approximately $155,000 with false operational and employee claims and fabricated Forms 941.
- Bank 1 approved the Glory Transportation loan and wired approximately $155,000 to that company’s business account.
- After Glory’s loan funded, the owner obtained a cashier’s check for approximately $30,000 payable to Defendant A from the loan proceeds.
- Defendant A instructed the Glory owner to claim the $30,000 was for equipment or vans if asked, though the payment was for preparing fraudulent paperwork.
- Defendant A and Person D submitted a fraudulent PPP application for Charisma Care Family Services LLC for approximately $107,500 with false employee and payroll data and fabricated Forms 941.
- Bank 1 approved the Charisma Care loan and wired approximately $107,500 to that company’s business account.
- From Charisma Care’s proceeds, a cashier’s check for approximately $40,000 was issued payable to one of Defendant A’s entities.
- Defendant A created a fraudulent PPP application for Comfort Care Transit LLC for approximately $230,000 with false operational and employee claims and fabricated Forms 941.
- Bank 1 approved the Comfort Care loan and wired approximately $230,000 to that company’s business account.
- From the Comfort Care proceeds, cashier’s checks totaling approximately $115,000 were issued at Defendant A’s direction to him and his entities.
- Defendant A told participants he required a fee from each fraudulent loan, sometimes up to 50% of the proceeds.
- Defendant A recruited individuals with existing companies, created the loan applications and false tax documents, and had them sign the paperwork.
- Defendant A directed co-schemers to split payments among his various companies and provided cover stories for the payments.
- On June 8, 2020, a representative of Bank 1 contacted Defendant A about payments he received from PPP loans.
- On June 8, 2020, Defendant A falsely told the bank that the $65,000 from the CFA loan was payment for equipment and a trailer.
- On June 8, 2020, Defendant A falsely told the bank that the $45,000 from the Rebels loan was advance rent.
- On June 8, 2020, Defendant A falsely told the bank that the $75,000 from the Davis Development loan was for an investment in a nursing home.
- On June 8, 2020, Defendant A falsely told the bank that the $115,000 from the Comfort Care loan was for purchase of a truck and other vehicles.
- After being contacted by Bank 1, Defendant A created fake documents to support his false stories: two vehicle bills of sale, a commercial lease, and a joint venture agreement.
- Defendant A provided copies of the fake documents to Bank 1 and to co-schemers to present to the bank.
- Defendant A participated in a call with co-schemers to coordinate a consistent false story to tell the bank after inquiries began.
- Defendant A directed others, including a participant associated with Comfort Care, to delete emails and text messages related to the schemes.
- Defendant A sent approximately $400 to the owner of Davis Development to pay a fee to reinstate the company with the state before applying for the PPP loan.
- CFA Auto Transport LLC’s PPP application falsely claimed average monthly payroll of $97,000 and 38 employees.
- Comfort Care Transit LLC’s PPP application falsely claimed average monthly payroll of $92,000 and 23 employees.
- Davis Development Group Inc.’s PPP application falsely claimed average monthly payroll of $71,000 and 20 employees.
- New Beginnings Family Services LLC’s PPP application falsely claimed average monthly payroll of $92,000 and 28 employees.
- Premier Logistic Solutions LLC’s PPP application falsely claimed average monthly payroll of $85,000 and 23 employees.
- Rebels Paris LLC’s PPP application falsely claimed average monthly payroll of $62,000 and 17 employees.
- Glory Transportation Services LLC’s PPP application falsely claimed average monthly payroll of $62,000 and 14 employees.
- CFA Auto Transport LLC was delinquent with the state as of July 1, 2019 and was reinstated on or about May 5, 2020 before its PPP application.
- Davis Development Group Inc. had been administratively dissolved as of August 10, 2018 and was reinstated on or about May 2, 2020 before its PPP application.
- New Beginnings Family Services LLC had been administratively dissolved as of April 13, 2020 and was reinstated on or about May 1, 2020 before its PPP application.
- Comfort Care Transit LLC had been administratively dissolved on June 13, 2017 and was restored to active status on or about May 12, 2020 before its PPP application.
- Premier Logistic Solutions LLC had been administratively dissolved as of June 14, 2019 and was reinstated on or about May 14, 2020 before its PPP application.
- Participants opened new business accounts at Financial Institution 1 for several entities in May 2020 to receive PPP funds.
- After PPP funds were deposited, participants transferred or directed the transfer of proceeds among themselves and to entities controlled by participants.
- When Financial Institution 1 froze or inquired about loan applications, participants took steps to misrepresent, conceal, and hide the scheme.
- The indictment’s money laundering counts describe cashier’s checks moving PPP proceeds, including approximately $30,000 from Glory Transportation to Defendant A on May 6, 2020.
- The indictment’s money laundering counts include approximately $50,000 from Glory Transportation deposited into a personal credit union account on May 8, 2020.
- The indictment’s money laundering counts include approximately $20,000 from Rebels deposited into an account held by Defendant A’s company on May 18, 2020.
- The indictment’s money laundering counts include approximately $20,000 from Rebels deposited into a business account associated with Co-Defendant A on May 18, 2020.
- The indictment’s money laundering counts include approximately $40,000 from Comfort Care deposited into an account held by Defendant A on May 20, 2020.
- The indictment’s money laundering counts include approximately $75,000 from Davis Development deposited into an account held by Defendant A’s company on May 21, 2020.
- The indictment’s money laundering counts include approximately $25,000 from CFA deposited into an account held by Defendant A’s company on May 22, 2020.
- The indictment’s money laundering counts include approximately $25,000 from CFA deposited into a business account associated with Co-Defendant A on May 22, 2020.
- As of February 15, 2020, none of the companies for which Defendant A prepared applications had any employees or independent contractors.
- Defendant A acknowledged the PPP forms warned that false statements could result in criminal penalties and that proceeds must be used for specified expenses.
- Defendant A admitted he acted knowingly and intentionally in the conduct described in the factual basis.
- Defendant A’s scheme included five funded PPP loans he facilitated totaling approximately $960,000, as later cited in restitution discussions.
- Defendant A’s recruitment included telling Co-Defendant C to consider paying Co-Defendant A a share because Co-Defendant A introduced him to the idea of applying.
- The indictment specified individual act-executions for six fraudulent PPP applications with approximate amounts sought: $177,500 (Davis Development), $242,500 (CFA), $155,000 (Glory Transportation), $155,000 (Rebels), $230,000 (Comfort Care), $212,500 (Premier Logistic).
- Defendant A’s plea agreement was executed in writing and filed in January 2021, with signatures dated January 22, 2021.
- Defendant A entered a guilty plea to Count Two before sentencing, as noted in the sentencing memorandum’s procedural background.
- Defendant A acknowledged that by pleading guilty he would be adjudicated guilty and lose certain civil rights, including firearm possession.
- The indictment alleged that multiple entities’ PPP applications falsely claimed operations and employees despite administrative dissolution prior to early 2020.

- On June 24, 2020 at approximately 12:15 a.m., surveillance footage showed a large group of protestors at the corner of Doty and Carroll streets in Madison.
- In the surveillance video, the defendant was seen crossing the street toward the City County Building holding a plastic bottle with a wick.
- Approximately 25 minutes later, dispatch employees inside the City County Building heard glass breaking near their workspace and observed smoke in the building.
- Fire alarms in the City County Building were activated and employees began evacuating.
- Because of the fire, employees were unable to answer calls to the 911 Call Center for a period of time.
- Photographs from outside the City County Building showed the defendant and others throwing projectiles through the building’s windows.
- After the windows were broken, the defendant threw a lit roll of paper towels into the City County Building.
- Shortly after the lit roll of paper towels was thrown inside, a fire was visible inside the building.
- At the time of the attempted arson, approximately 229 people were inside the City County Building, including about ten deputies and about 182 adult and juvenile inmates at the jail.
- Responding officers found a clear plastic bottle with a burned wick in its neck outside the City County Building.
- Investigators found two additional clear plastic bottles with burned necks inside the City County Building at the scene of the fire.
- All three plastic bottles recovered in relation to the fire were sent to the ATF laboratory for latent prints and DNA testing.
- Later that day, ATF agents examined the arson scene before cleanup contractors entered the secured area.
- Agents observed projectiles used to break windows, including several rocks on the floor, a metal bar on a desk in the cubicle where the fire started, and a small hammer on the floor near that cubicle.
- On June 30, 2020, the defendant was arrested by Madison police and his iPhone was seized.
- A search of the defendant’s iPhone revealed geographic data placing him in the vicinity of the City County Building at the time of the arson.
- On October 1, 2020, an ATF laboratory report indicated the defendant’s DNA was found on a hammer recovered from the scene.
- The ATF laboratory report indicated Person A (described as the defendant’s girlfriend) had DNA on the wick of a plastic bottle found outside the building.
- Photographs from outside the City County Building show Person A (the defendant’s girlfriend) in close proximity to him during the incident.
- The total cost of damage to the City County Building from the fire, including cleanup, was $105,171.29.
- The sentencing hearing was set for June 2, 2021.
- The government filed a sentencing memorandum on May 28, 2021 in Case No. 20-cr-00132-jdp.
- The City County Building is a government building that housed a jail and county personnel at the time of the incident.
- The case number is 20-cr-00132-jdp in the Western District of Wisconsin.
- On the previous day, a small group of protestors caused disturbances in downtown Madison, including tearing down statues near the Capitol and assaulting a state senator.
- The defendant was released from prison on March 17, 2020.
- At the time of the offense, the defendant was on state supervision for six different convictions in Dane County from 2012–2016.
- The defendant was approximately 26 years old at the time referenced in the sentencing memorandum.
- The defendant’s criminal history includes felony convictions for receiving stolen property, theft, battery (domestic abuse), felony bail jumping, and contact after domestic abuse.
- One prior conviction involved the defendant violently attacking Person A (his girlfriend).
- According to the sentencing memorandum, the offense involved destruction of city cameras, the use of projectiles, and plastic bottles filled with accelerant.

- The plea agreement includes a provision resolving other possible federal criminal violations in the district, limited to conduct related to the indictment and known to the United States as of the plea date.

- The defendant possessed and used specialized violin expertise in the context of his relationship with MV-1.

- Defendant A admitted being a part owner and manager operating Royal Metals Group LLC in the Southern District of Iowa.
- Royal Metals Group advertised itself as a precious metals dealer offering clients opportunities to buy and sell precious metals such as gold and silver.
- Royal Metals Group offered clients the option to sell their precious metals to the company for an agreed price.
- The fraud scheme operated from at least May 2014 through at least February 2018.
- Defendant A represented to clients and financial advisors that Royal Metals Group would buy and sell precious metals for them.
- Instead of delivering metals or paying for metals received, Royal Metals Group failed to purchase and deliver promised metals and failed to pay clients for metals it received.
- Defendant A accepted payments from clients for precious metals but failed to purchase the metals on clients’ behalf or only partially filled orders without notifying clients.
- Defendant A accepted precious metals from clients but failed to pay them, or paid only partially.
- Funds and property obtained from clients were used to pay business expenses, personal expenses, and to pay other clients.
- To perpetuate and conceal the scheme, Defendant A provided false delivery tracking numbers and issued checks that were unsigned, had incorrect amounts, or could not be cashed due to stop payment orders or insufficient funds.
- The scheme used both mail and interstate wires in furtherance of the fraud.
- On or about August 3, 2017, a package sent by a commercial interstate carrier contained a single one-ounce gold coin to Victim A, even though the full order had not been fulfilled, to lull the victim into believing full delivery would occur.
- Defendant A agreed that venue is proper in the United States District Court for the Southern District of Iowa.
- The parties stipulated in the plea agreement to recommend a loss amount of more than $1.5 million for sentencing purposes.
- Defendant A agreed that the Court should impose restitution for all relevant conduct, with amounts detailed in a sealed attachment to the plea agreement.
- The government stated Defendant A defrauded at least 71 clients of Royal Metals Group.
- The government stated the fraud involved nearly $3 million over approximately four years.
- The government described Defendant A providing victims with false mail tracking numbers, bad checks, unsigned checks, and false money transfer numbers.
- The government indicated it compiled a list of victims with names, addresses, and restitution amounts to be submitted under seal at sentencing.
- Victim B lost $34,000, which created significant hardship when preparing to move to an assisted living facility.
- Victim C reported investing $260,326.20, representing an entire retirement fund, and not receiving the coins purchased.
- Victim D reported a loss of $43,000 and described specific unmet needs resulting from the loss.
- Victim E reported a loss of $18,000, representing 10% of a 401(k), and noted living on a fixed income with health issues.
- Victim F, living on a fixed income, intended the precious metals purchased to be a future safety net for grandchildren.
- The government stated Defendant A continued to accept money and metals from customers while using funds to pay other customers and personal expenses.
- The government reported that Defendant A made repeated false representations to clients and financial advisors to perpetuate and conceal the scheme.
- The government characterized the victims’ experiences as financially and emotionally devastating.
- The government indicated Defendant A’s name appeared on Royal Metals Group bank accounts and that she was a signatory on the accounts.
- The government stated Defendant A’s name appeared on emails and other correspondence with victims and their financial advisors.
- The defense stated Royal Metals Group was established in 2009 by Person A and Person B, who were partners in an insurance agency.
- The defense stated that upon formation, a 50% ownership share was placed in Defendant A’s name and the other 50% in Person B’s name.
- The defense stated a third owner, Person C, was later added so that Persons B and C held the controlling interest on paper.
- The defense stated that RMG used a network of insurance agents and investment advisors who received commissions for placing client orders through RMG.
- The defense stated Defendant A did not provide investment advice and that most customers were represented by licensed agents or advisors who placed orders with RMG.
- The defense stated that RMG’s gross receipts rose to about $25 million by 2012 with a profit of about $779,000 and a profit margin of 3.1%.
- The defense stated that gold and silver prices plunged in 2013 and RMG’s gross income dropped to $20 million in 2013, $9.4 million in 2014, and $11.4 million in 2015.
- The defense stated that to keep the business operating, high-interest loans from payday lenders were obtained.
- The defense stated Defendant A obtained a personal home equity loan and an additional loan to pay customers.
- The defense acknowledged Defendant A lied to customers, investment advisors, and insurance agents about unfilled orders and continued accepting payments when RMG lacked capital or cash flow to fulfill orders.
- The defense stated that portions of RMG’s 2009–2016 tax returns were prepared by a CPA and that the 2014 and 2015 years were audited by the IRS.

- Defendant was employed by Cal Poly Pomona Foundation, Inc. from approximately 2000 to February 2020.
- Defendant served as an accounting specialist for the Kellogg West Conference Center and Hotel, a facility owned and used by the Cal Poly Pomona Foundation for conventions and events.
- As an accounting specialist, defendant’s responsibilities included receiving invoices from Kellogg West vendors.
- Defendant’s responsibilities included verifying invoices and authorizing payment of those invoices.
- Defendant ensured that invoices were entered into the Cal Poly Pomona Foundation processing system that generated payments for those invoices.
- From July 2010 through February 2020, defendant knowingly devised and executed a scheme to defraud Cal Poly Pomona Foundation.
- Defendant entered or caused to be entered her mother (Person A) into the Cal Poly Pomona Foundation processing system as a Kellogg West vendor.
- Defendant generated invoices falsely stating that Person A had performed services for Kellogg West when no such services were performed.
- Defendant authorized payment on the fraudulent invoices and entered them into the Cal Poly Pomona Foundation processing system.
- Defendant delivered hard copies of the fraudulent invoices intended for payment to the Cal Poly Pomona Foundation Accounting Department.
- In reliance on the fraudulent invoices, the Cal Poly Pomona Foundation Accounting Department generated checks drawn from a CPPF Wells Fargo account and payable to Person A.
- Defendant picked up the fraudulent checks from the Cal Poly Pomona Foundation Accounting Department or had them mailed to her at Kellogg West.
- Defendant had the fraudulent checks mailed to Person A’s address in Rowland Heights, California, where she would pick them up.
- Defendant forged Person A’s signature to endorse the fraudulent checks.
- Defendant deposited the endorsed checks into a joint JPMorgan Chase Bank account ending in 9900 held by defendant and Person A.
- Defendant used funds from the joint Chase account for her mortgage payments.
- Defendant used funds from the joint Chase account for credit card payments.
- Defendant used funds from the joint Chase account for cash ATM withdrawals and personal purchases.
- Approximately $925,486.99 in checks generated by the scheme were deposited into the joint Chase account ending in 9900.
- On or about January 15, 2016, a check for $2,874.55 payable to Person A and drawn from the CPPF Wells Fargo account was mailed.
- On or about January 26, 2017, a check for $2,897.03 payable to Person A and drawn from the CPPF Wells Fargo account was mailed.
- On or about January 16, 2018, a check for $2,997.81 payable to Person A and drawn from the CPPF Wells Fargo account was mailed.
- On or about January 11, 2019, a check for $4,998.55 payable to Person A and drawn from the CPPF Wells Fargo account was mailed.
- On or about January 22, 2020, a check for $4,998.99 payable to Person A and drawn from the CPPF Wells Fargo account was mailed.

- For Counts 1 and 4, a fine of up to $250,000 may be imposed.
