- Defendant A pleaded guilty on December 7, 2023 to securities fraud, wire fraud, and obstruction of agency proceedings in the superseding indictment.
- Count One charged securities fraud under 15 U.S.C. §§ 78j and 78ff and 17 C.F.R. § 240.10b-5, with a statutory maximum of 20 years’ imprisonment.
- Count Two charged wire fraud under 18 U.S.C. § 1343, with a statutory maximum of 30 years’ imprisonment.
- Count Three charged obstruction of an agency proceeding under 18 U.S.C. § 1505, with a statutory maximum of 5 years’ imprisonment.
- The government voluntarily dismissed Count Four on November 10, 2023.
- Between March and April 2020, Defendant A issued multiple press releases about developing a blood-based COVID-19 test.
- The press releases were publicly distributed via the internet and viewable by investors and potential investors.
- Defendant A admitted he willfully and intentionally made materially misleading statements in the press releases about the progress and status of the COVID-19 blood test with intent to defraud.
- On April 23, 2020, the SEC suspended trading of DECN stock due to questions about the accuracy of press releases regarding the COVID-19 blood test.
- At the December 7, 2023 plea hearing, Defendant A admitted he influenced an investor to draft a letter accusing the SEC of misconduct and recruited shareholders to sign it using an alias.
- Defendant A served as Chief Executive Officer of Decision Diagnostics Corp. during the relevant period.
- Throughout March and April 2020, twelve false and misleading press releases were issued about a 15-second COVID-19 blood test that did not exist, with examples on dates including March 3, 4, 11, 16, 17, 18, 20, 23, 25, April 3, 7, and 23, 2020.
- During the period of the false press releases, DECN’s stock price increased by over 1,500 percent.
- In a March 20, 2020 private email, Defendant A wrote the current design could identify a virus but probably could not distinguish common types of flu.
- On March 20, 2020, a public press release claimed the GenViro! Rapid Kit would provide COVID-19 test results for $6.95 in under a minute from a finger-prick sample.
- In a March 21, 2020 private email, Defendant A wrote the method would not be specific enough to tell which flu was detected and described it as a screening method.
- In a separate private conversation, Defendant A described what he was doing as a "penny pump."
- After the SEC suspended trading on April 23, 2020, Defendant A sought to influence the SEC to end its investigation through a campaign that included using an alias to orchestrate a shareholder letter.
- On May 7, 2020, Defendant A submitted a sworn, verified petition to the SEC asserting the press releases were true and not misleading, and posted it publicly on OTCMarkets.com.
- On June 17, 2020, Defendant A submitted a brief with a 20-page sworn affidavit to the SEC accusing an SEC staff attorney and others of lying and conspiring with cybercriminals; it was publicly available on the SEC’s website.
- On July 15, 2020, Defendant A submitted another sworn affidavit to the SEC again accusing staff of crimes; it was publicly available.
- Using the alias "Matthew Steinmann," Defendant A worked with an investor to draft a 38-page shareholder letter and obtained signatures from over 50 others.
- On June 2, 2020, a shareholder letter was sent to senior SEC officials; it accused the SEC of colluding with cybercriminals and claimed the SEC’s actions were "damaging to humanity."
- The June 2, 2020 shareholder letter was posted on OTCMarkets.com for investors to see.
- Additional shareholder letters were sent on July 28, 2020 (with over 120 signatories) and August 11, 2020 (with approximately 80 signatories), which Defendant A also signed using the alias.
- Between March and October 2020, Defendant A posted over 1,100 messages on an investors’ message board using the alias "plutoniumimplosion," including accusations against SEC staff and threats against informants.
- On July 10, 2020, Defendant A issued a press release falsely claiming DECN had a working COVID-19 blood test producing results in 10.5 seconds.
- On October 9, 2020, Defendant A provided false and misleading sworn testimony to the SEC about his participation on stock message boards.
- On October 27, 2020, Defendant A lied to FBI agents, denying he posted on stock message boards and denying involvement in the shareholder letters.
- A grand jury indicted Defendant A on December 15, 2020 for securities fraud; a superseding indictment on May 11, 2021 added wire fraud and obstruction of agency proceedings.
- On or around March 8, 2023, the government moved to revoke bond, alleging violations including contacting victims, witness tampering, and fraudulent financing agreements.
- On December 21, 2020, while on pretrial release, Defendant A communicated with a victim to organize an attack campaign against a nonprofit competition related to COVID-19 testing.
- In February 2021, Defendant A contacted two victims to discourage cooperation and influence their views of the case.
- From at least June through October 2021, Defendant A discussed his criminal case with victims in a small group chat and asked them to support him on internet message boards.
- Throughout 2021 until at least April 2022, Defendant A communicated with numerous victims on Facebook about his case and tried to influence their opinions.
- From late 2021 through early 2022, Defendant A entered into six agreements to sell approximately $463,120 of DECN’s future receipts for $330,000 in cash advances, then defaulted on each.
- Defendant A failed to disclose the financing agreements and resulting default judgments in a financial affidavit he signed under penalty of perjury.
- In January 2023, Defendant A attempted to secure a $6 million loan using a 20% equity stake in a subsidiary as collateral, despite lacking a business purpose and ability to repay.
- On April 25, 2023, after a hearing, the Court revoked Defendant A’s bond and ordered detention, finding probable cause of witness tampering and obstruction and that he posed a danger to the community.
- The Court noted concerns that the defendant’s financial conduct in the loan agreements was strongly suggestive of fleecing counterparties and showed real victims.
- The initial Presentence Investigation Report was filed March 11, 2024 and the final PSR with addendum was filed April 4, 2024.
- Defendant A admitted at the plea hearing that he made misleading statements to generate favorable publicity and attract a larger corporate partner.
- The government asserted an aggregate loss figure of approximately $60.8 million, with a fallback position of at least $27.8 million.
- Victim A and Victim B jointly reported approximately $139,000 in DECN stock losses and obtained a home equity line of credit to cover losses and living expenses.
- Victim C reported an $89,415.24 loss in DECN stock and had to postpone retirement.
- Victim D reported losing over $40,000 and stated the family used children’s college funds and stopped contributing to their own retirement to cover expenses.
- Victim E reported a loss of approximately $2,800 and used savings and cut expenses to recover financially.
- Victim F and spouse reported losing approximately $66,102, representing half of retirement savings from 18 years in the military, and delayed retirement plans to save for a home.
- Defendant A used the alias "Matthew Steinmann" linked to the email address "madefortunes@yahoo.com" and the message board handle "plutoniumimplosion."
- The government alleged that at the start of 2020 DECN was in a perilous financial situation and that Defendant A had misused company funds to pay a webcam model.
- The SEC’s investigation and trading suspension threatened Defendant A’s ability to raise money for DECN.
- On July 10, 2020 through October 20, 2020, Defendant A posted around 150 additional iHub messages using the alias in furtherance of the scheme.
- On October 9, 2020, when asked by the SEC about posting on stock message boards, Defendant A misled investigators regarding his membership and later refused to answer further questions.
- The government asserted that Defendant A only stopped committing similar misconduct when his bond was revoked and he was detained.
- The government stated that it presented expert testimony by Professor Joshua Mitts regarding loss at an evidentiary hearing and incorporated those materials by reference.
- The parties engaged in plea negotiations; the government’s most recent offer on December 5, 2023 proposed that Defendant A plead guilty to Counts One and Three.
- Defendant A requested an evidentiary hearing to resolve disputed guideline factors, including loss amount, prior to sentencing.
- The defense asserted that by at least July 20, 2020, OTCMarkets displayed Caveat Emptor warnings on DECN’s page with skull-and-crossbones imagery and warnings to investors.
- The defense asserted that investors placing trades after the SEC suspension would receive call-in warnings that the security was speculative and could result in total loss.
- The defense asserted that on May 20, 2020 an SEC declaration publicly stated DECN disseminated false and misleading information in press releases.
- The defense asserted that in July 2020 DECN announced a switch from a blood test design to a saliva-based COVID-19 test and continued saliva test development into 2021.
- The defense memorandum states Defendant A is 70 years old and has no prior criminal record.
- The defense described DECN as selling glucose test strips using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and working with a Korean biotechnology company and a U.S.-based scientist.
- The defense stated Defendant A hired FDA-experienced counsel and sought Emergency Use Authorization for a COVID-19 test between March and June 2020.
- The defense asserted that after the SEC suspension and related disclosures, speculation and chart-driven trading in the penny stock market influenced DECN’s price movements.
- The defense asserted that an evidentiary hearing on March 20, 2024 addressed loss amount and that the government presented expert testimony.
- Defendant A’s December 7, 2023 plea admissions included that he used an alias to recruit shareholders and authorize the submission of a letter to the SEC to influence the investigation’s outcome.
- The government claimed Defendant A repeatedly lied in sworn SEC submissions in May, June, and July 2020, and later to the FBI in October 2020.
- The government alleged Defendant A targeted an SEC staff attorney with accusations of perjury and evidence fabrication in public filings and message board posts.
- The defense memorandum states the SEC ultimately upheld the trading suspension against DECN.
- The defense memorandum states Defendant A has accrued a spotless disciplinary record while detained, citing the PSR.
- Defendant A’s plea statement summarized the government’s last plea offer and included an outline of disputed issues to be resolved at sentencing, including loss amount.
- The government’s sentencing memorandum stated the final PSR had been filed as ECF 191.
- The government’s memorandum asserted that after indictment, Defendant A continued similar misconduct including attempting to defraud new parties via future receipts and a proposed $6 million loan.
- The defense memorandum describes Defendant A’s long career in medical devices, including work at major firms and navigating FDA processes before founding and leading DECN.
- The defense memorandum asserts that Defendant A believed impedance spectroscopy could be used to develop a COVID-19 test and consulted with a Korean partner about its theoretical feasibility.
- On or around March 13, 2020, the Court issued an arrest warrant based on violations of pretrial release conditions, and on or around March 18, 2020, Defendant A was taken into custody (as stated in the government’s memo, though dates appear inconsistent with other record chronology).

- Defendant A pled guilty on July 6, 2021 to Count Two charging a violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1959(a)(3), assault resulting in serious bodily injury in aid of racketeering.
- The events described in the indictment occurred in the Eastern District of Texas and elsewhere.
- On October 7, 2020, a federal grand jury returned an indictment charging Defendant A with one count of assault resulting in serious bodily injury in aid of racketeering.
- The charged offense carries a statutory maximum penalty of twenty years’ imprisonment under 18 U.S.C. § 1959(a)(3).
- Defendant A signed a factual basis and stipulation in support of the guilty plea.
- Defendant A requested alterations to the factual basis at the plea hearing, which were made by the government.
- The Aryan Circle (AC) is a criminal organization whose members and associates engaged in controlled substances distribution, firearms trafficking, and acts of violence including murder, kidnapping, assault, robbery, and witness intimidation.
- The Aryan Circle operated in the Eastern District of Texas and in states including Alabama, Arkansas, Indiana, Kentucky, Louisiana, Mississippi, Missouri, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Texas, and Virginia.
- The Aryan Circle is a violent, race‑based, whites‑only prison‑based gang with hundreds of members operating inside and outside of penal institutions.
- The Aryan Circle was established around 1985 within the Texas Department of Criminal Justice.
- The Aryan Circle’s structure and influence expanded to rural and suburban areas in Texas, Louisiana, Arkansas, Missouri, and Indiana in recent years.
- The Aryan Circle grew in stature within TDCJ in the 1990s through violent conflicts with other gangs including the Mexican Mafia and the Aryan Brotherhood of Texas.
- The Aryan Circle has a militaristic structure and members refer to the gang as the Family.
- An Upper Board of five members oversaw the Aryan Circle and had ultimate authority in gang matters.
- The Upper Board included a president, vice president, administrative chairman, and two other members.
- The Aryan Circle had five branches: Texas prisons, federal prisons, out‑of‑state prisons, international members, and the free world (non‑incarcerated members), each with its own Middle Board.
- The Aryan Circle had a motorcycle biker branch as a subgroup.
- Out‑of‑state prisons were divided into regions and the Center Rings included all Middle Board members who issued rules and regulations.
- In the free world, Texas was divided into districts led by district captains who reported to majors.
- The Aryan Circle maintained separate prison and free world chains of command.
- The Aryan Circle had a Task Force of members handpicked by the Upper Board to enforce rules and perform specific tasks including violent acts.
- Aryan Circle leaders could issue direct orders (D.O.s) such as violations, smash on sight orders, and Green Lights, with punishments for noncompliance ranging up to death.
- Aryan Circle members used a handshake tied to the motto: Silence is Golden; Silence is Deadly; Silence is Mandatory; My Honor is called Loyalty.
- Aryan Circle colors are blue and gray; members wore clothing with those colors and Nazi‑style symbols.
- Aryan Circle members used Nazi‑style symbols including the Iron Cross, eternal flame, 13, swastika, and SS lightning bolts in tattoos.
- The most coveted Aryan Circle tattoo was the AC patch, often earned by committing a blood‑in mission such as aggravated assault or murder.
- The AC patch design included a diamond with a swastika tilted like a diamond, SS bolts, and the letters AC; an older version was a circle with SS bolts.
- Aryan Circle lexicon included 113%, 1388, CFFC, and DFFD.
- Once released, Aryan Circle members were required to report to outside leaders and continue furthering gang goals through criminal activity.
- Aryan Circle membership was for life with no retirement.
- Aryan Circle members were required to attend monthly church meetings where criminal activity was discussed, proceeds were collected, and disciplinary beatings were administered.
- Prospective members had to be sponsored, serve a prospect period, study AC rules, and be voted on, with any single member able to black‑ball admission.
- Incarcerated Aryan Circle members were expected to commit acts of violence to earn the right to wear the AC patch; the rule was more loosely applied in the free world.
- The Aryan Circle admitted women as full members and had female associates who facilitated communications and smuggled contraband for imprisoned members.
- The AC enterprise was an association‑in‑fact that engaged in and affected interstate commerce and functioned as a continuing unit for common purposes.
- Enterprise purposes included enriching members through drug and firearms trafficking and preserving power and territory through threats and acts of violence.
- Enterprise members kept victims in fear and provided financial support to members charged with or incarcerated for gang‑related activities.
- Leaders sanctioned acts; members committed and conspired to commit acts of violence; and members engaged in drug and weapons trafficking under the enterprise’s protection.
- Free world members acquired and used firearms for protection, attacks, and armed combat; imprisoned members acquired and used shanks.
- Members used gang‑related symbols and gestures, communicated in code to avoid detection, preferred in‑person communication, and forbade cooperation with law enforcement with paperwork checked for signs of cooperation.
- The enterprise’s pattern of racketeering included multiple acts involving murder under various state laws, kidnapping in Missouri, and controlled substances trafficking offenses under 21 U.S.C. §§ 841, 846, and 843.
- Defendant A had been an Aryan Circle member since at least 2002.
- Defendant A admitted gang affiliation to law enforcement in 2008 and 2012.
- Defendant A had tattoos indicating gang affiliation with the Aryan Circle.
- Defendant A attended and participated in church meetings and paid gang dues during his membership.
- Defendant A knew that Aryan Circle members engaged in racketeering activity, including acts involving murder and drug trafficking.
- Before October 2, 2016, Aryan Circle members learned that Victim A wanted to switch gang affiliation from the Aryan Circle to the Banditos.
- Leader A ordered Aryan Circle members to attack Victim A to remove him from the gang for violating AC rules.
- A gang meeting was held at Co‑defendant A’s home in the Tyler, Texas area where logistics of the beating were planned and ground rules for the assault were set.
- On October 2, 2016, Defendant A and other Aryan Circle members met at a park near Tyler, Texas to discipline Victim A.
- Members of the Banditos drove Victim A to the park site.
- Before arriving at the site, Victim A contacted Aryan Circle members begging them not to carry out the beating.
- Upon arrival at the park, Leader A announced that Aryan Circle members had brought firearms.
- Defendant A and Co‑defendant B struck Victim A multiple times using hands and feet and kicked Victim A in the head while on the ground.
- Defendant A continued striking Victim A while Victim A was balled up on the ground, including blows to the face.
- Victim A did not fight back during the assault.
- Members of the Banditos stopped the beating.
- After the beating ended, Leader A told Victim A that the Aryan Circle could have beaten him worse and that they kill people.
- Victim A received hospital treatment for injuries including fractures to the left orbital floor and left medial ethmoid bones, a nasal septum fracture, and a fractured tooth that required removal.
- Victim A suffered migraine headaches and blurred vision for years following the assault.
- Defendant A committed the beating because it was expected of him as an Aryan Circle member and to maintain his position within the gang.
- Defendant A would not have been involved in the attack but for his position within the gang and desire to remain in good standing.
- Defendant A personally assaulted the victim using hands and feet and kicked the victim in the head while the victim was on the ground.
- A co‑defendant stated in a Mirandized interview on January 15, 2019 that before the assault Defendant A displayed a firearm and laid it on top of a vehicle for all to see.
- A witness testified to a grand jury on May 15, 2019 that after arriving at the park, Defendant A came out with a pistol and was instructed by Leader A to put it away.
- On November 7, 2017, the same witness told agents that at the time of the assault both Defendant A and Leader A had pistols.
- On March 4, 2020, Victim A testified to a grand jury that immediately following the assault, Leader A put a gun to Victim A’s head and said the only reason they let the victim live was because the Banditos were there.
- Victim A further testified that the victim was kicked in the face with a steel‑toed boot.
- Another co‑defendant stated in an August 23, 2017 Mirandized interview that a few of the guys at the scene had weapons, specifying two individuals had pistols.
- On October 14, 2020, a co‑defendant stated that Leader A told the Banditos the AC members had brought guns to the park but did not show a gun.
- On October 14, 2020, the same co‑defendant stated that another AC member had a pistol and tried to give it to a co‑defendant, who declined.
- On October 14, 2020, the co‑defendant also stated that the referenced AC member always had a gun in his pocket.
- One witness told agents earlier that the victim was not kicked in the face and that Co‑defendant B did not wear steel‑toed boots.
- Defendant A’s criminal history dates back to 1998, according to the presentence report referenced in the sentencing memorandum.
- Defendant A was convicted in 2010 of being a felon in possession of a firearm after selling two firearms to undercover agents, per the PSR reference.
- Defendant A has a conviction for assault causing bodily injury, per the PSR reference in the sentencing memorandum.
- Defendant A has other convictions including drug offenses, theft, evading arrest, and forgery, per the PSR reference.
- Defendant A had pending charges for manufacture or delivery of a controlled substance and engaging in organized criminal activity, with an arrest in 2019, per the PSR reference.
- Defendant A had numerous instances of noncompliance during a prior federal supervised release term for a felon in possession conviction, per the PSR reference.
- Defendant A’s probation was revoked on a 2000 conviction for violating conditions of probation, per the PSR reference.
- In 2001, Defendant A led police on a high‑speed chase for approximately eight miles, disobeyed an officer’s commands, struck the officer, and was subdued with pepper spray, per the PSR reference.
- Defendant A’s probation was again revoked in 2006 due to violations including failure to report, failure to pay fees, and failure to submit to a substance abuse evaluation, per the PSR reference.
- Defendant A was on supervised release at the time of the October 2016 offense, per the PSR reference in the sentencing memorandum.
- Law enforcement was unable to locate and arrest Defendant A for nearly two months in this case despite efforts, per the PSR reference in the sentencing memorandum.
- According to the factual basis, Defendant A personally carried out the assault along with one other Aryan Circle member.
- The assault planning meeting discussed the logistics of the attack and set ground rules for how the assault would take place, with Defendant A present.
- After the assault, Leader A threatened Victim A by saying the Aryan Circle kills people and that the victim could have been beaten worse.
- The victim suffered ongoing effects of the assault including migraine headaches and blurred vision for years.
- Defendant A continued participating in the Aryan Circle despite knowing its involvement in racketeering activity.

- The case is in the Eastern District of Missouri with docket number 4:20-CR-00798-HEA.
- Defendant A pleaded guilty to Count I charging Possession of Child Pornography under 18 U.S.C. § 2252A(a)(5)(B).
- The guilty plea was entered pursuant to Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 11(c)(1)(A).
- The government agreed to bring no further federal prosecution in the district relative to Defendant A’s receipt and possession of child pornography between approximately June 1, 2020 and July 27, 2020, of which it was aware at the time of the plea.
- To establish the offense, Defendant A admitted knowing possession of material containing images of child pornography depicting minors engaged in sexually explicit conduct, with images contained on or transported through interstate commerce materials.
- On May 5, 2020, as part of a federal child exploitation investigation of Person A, the FBI seized a McLaren 5G cell phone from Person A.
- A forensic examination of the seized McLaren 5G cell phone revealed child pornography.
- Law enforcement discovered that Person A was using the Telegram application to communicate about trading child pornography.
- Between April 12, 2020 and May 5, 2020, Person A and Defendant A exchanged child pornography with each other using a Telegram account associated with Defendant A.

- Defendant A waived indictment by a grand jury and agreed to proceed by information.
- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to a single-count information charging attempted importation of MDMA in violation of 21 U.S.C. §§ 963 and 952(a).
- On December 22, 2020, Defendant A pleaded guilty to the single-count information.
- The statutory maximum imprisonment for the offense is 20 years.
- The statutory maximum term of supervised release for the offense is three years.
- The statutory maximum fine is $1,000,000 or twice the gross gain or gross loss, whichever is greater.
- A mandatory special assessment of $100 applies to the offense.
- The offense conduct occurred beginning on an unknown date and continued until on or about October 2, 2019.
- The offense occurred in Los Angeles County, within the Central District of California, and elsewhere.
- Defendant A attempted to import approximately 970 grams of liquid MDMA.
- Defendant A intended to refine and process the liquid MDMA into usable powder form.
- Defendant A used the Wickr application to contact a supplier identified as "vangrails."
- Defendant A purchased the liquid MDMA using cryptocurrency.
- The cryptocurrency payment was made from an online virtual currency wallet.
- Defendant A understood the liquid MDMA would be shipped from the Netherlands or another location outside the United States to his home in Los Angeles County.
- On or about October 2, 2019, Defendant A possessed approximately 175 tablets of MDMA.
- On or about October 2, 2019, Defendant A possessed approximately 18 grams of MDMA in powder form.
- On or about October 2, 2019, Defendant A possessed approximately 105 grams of cocaine in powder form.
- Cocaine is a Schedule II controlled substance.
- On or about October 2, 2019, Defendant A possessed approximately 27 grams of ketamine.
- Ketamine is a Schedule III controlled substance.
- Defendant A intended to distribute some or all of the MDMA, cocaine, and ketamine to others.
- MDMA (ecstasy) is a Schedule I controlled substance.
- The parties agreed that the liquid MDMA would have been substantially reduced in weight during processing into a usable powder form.
- U.S. customs agents intercepted the liquid MDMA shipment.
- Agents executed a controlled delivery operation on or about October 2, 2019.
- Following the controlled delivery, Defendant A was arrested.
- After arrest, Defendant A made a full confession.
- Defendant A consented under Section 15002(b) of the CARES Act to proceed with his change of plea hearing by videoconference or telephone if videoconference was not reasonably available.
- Defendant A consented under Section 15002(b) of the CARES Act to proceed with his sentencing hearing by videoconference or telephone if videoconference was not reasonably available.
- Defendant A consented under 18 U.S.C. § 3148 and Section 15002(b) of the CARES Act to proceed with any hearing regarding alleged violations of pretrial release by videoconference or telephone.
- Defendant A requested consideration for participation in the Court's CASA program.
- The parties agreed to request that preparation of the presentence report be delayed and any previously set sentencing date be vacated, with a status conference approximately 30 days after the guilty plea, in connection with CASA consideration.
- If accepted into CASA, the parties would execute an amended plea agreement and request transfer to a CASA judicial officer for the amended plea and participation.
- If Defendant A was not accepted into CASA or the Court did not allow participation, the guilty plea would remain in place and the parties would request preparation of the presentence report and setting of a sentencing date.
- Defendant A agreed to pay the applicable special assessment at or before sentencing unless he demonstrated inability to pay.
- Defendant A agreed to be truthful at all times with the United States Probation and Pretrial Services Office and the Court.
- The parties agreed that Defendant A did not use violence or credible threats of violence or possess a firearm or other dangerous weapon in connection with the offense.
- The parties agreed that the offense did not result in death or serious bodily injury to any person.
- The parties agreed that Defendant A was not an organizer, leader, manager, or supervisor and was not engaged in a continuing criminal enterprise.
- The parties agreed that Defendant A truthfully proffered to the government concerning the offense conduct.

- COVID‑19 related disruptions caused delays in obtaining and confirming laboratory results for substances seized from Defendant A.
- Defendant A made a complete proffer and attempted cooperation with the government following his arrest.
- Defendant A has no prior criminal history as noted at sentencing.
- Defendant A generally remained gainfully employed after the offense conduct.
- Defendant A complied with all terms of supervision prior to sentencing.
- Defendant A has a documented narcotics addiction.

- The maximum penalties for Count Two include up to 10 years of imprisonment, a $250,000 fine, up to 3 years of supervised release, and a $100 special assessment.

- For Count Two, Defendant A admitted he knowingly possessed a destructive device that was a Molotov Cocktail and knew its characteristics.

- Defendant A used the Molotov Cocktail in connection with another felony offense, identified as Delaware second-degree arson under 11 Del. C. § 802.

- The case is in the Eastern District of Wisconsin under case number 2:21-cr-00005.
- The defendant agreed to plead guilty to Count One (Conspiracy to Distribute Controlled Substances) and Count Fourteen (False Statements).
- The indictment charged the defendant in fourteen counts of a fifteen-count indictment alleging violations of 21 U.S.C. §§ 841(a)(1), 841(b)(1)(C), 846, 856(a), and 18 U.S.C. §§ 2 and 1001.
- Count One alleges a conspiracy between at least January 2018 and December 2020 to distribute controlled substances outside professional practice and without legitimate medical purpose.
- The controlled substances at issue in Count One included Buprenorphine, Ritalin, Xanax, and Adderall.
- Count Fourteen alleges that on or about December 3, 2020, the defendant made materially false statements to DEA Diversion Investigators.
- The false statements included that the Saturday prescribing business was not a for‑profit enterprise.
- The false statements also included that most Saturday prescription recipients were patients seen at a counseling center.
- Those statements were false because the individuals receiving Saturday prescriptions were not patients of the identified counseling center.
- Those statements were also false because the defendant charged between $200 and $300 in cash for Saturday prescribing, depositing the cash into personal bank accounts.
- Count One carries a maximum penalty of 20 years’ imprisonment and a $1,000,000 fine, with a term of supervised release of at least 3 years.
- Count Fourteen carries a maximum penalty of 5 years’ imprisonment and a fine of the greater of $250,000, twice the gross pecuniary gain, or twice the gross pecuniary loss, with up to 3 years of supervised release.
- Each count carries a mandatory $100 special assessment.
- The government agreed to move to dismiss the remaining counts at sentencing as they pertain to the defendant.
- The parties agreed to waive the Rule 32 time limits for presentence report disclosure in favor of a court‑set schedule.
- The defendant acknowledged guilt and that the facts in the attached factual basis establish guilt beyond a reasonable doubt.
- The defendant agreed that offense proceeds totaled at least $285,000 and to entry of a $285,000 money judgment.
- The defendant agreed that properties listed in the indictment constituted proceeds of or were used to facilitate the offenses and to their forfeiture.
- The defendant agreed that he has an interest in each property listed for forfeiture.
- The government reserved the right to proceed against assets not identified in the plea agreement for forfeiture.
- The defendant agreed to pay the $200 in special assessments prior to or at sentencing.
- From at least January 2018 to December 2020, the defendant and Co‑Conspirator A operated a prescribing business at Co‑Conspirator A’s residence in Fond du Lac.
- The business operated primarily on Saturdays and involved prescribing controlled substances for cash.
- The defendant and Co‑Conspirator A knowingly agreed to prescribe controlled substances outside the course of professional practice in exchange for cash payments.
- They utilized at least three other individuals, including family members, to assist in the business.
- Co‑Conspirator A’s roles included scheduling purported patients, writing prescriptions, and collecting payments.
- Co‑Conspirator A asked patients what controlled substances they wanted or had taken to decide what prescriptions to write.
- Co‑Conspirator A assessed insurance requirements and wrote prescriptions for the defendant to sign.
- On some occasions, the defendant saw patients in the basement of the residence.
- When he saw patients, the defendant did not perform physical examinations or tests.
- The defendant did not provide mental health or counseling services in the operation.
- The defendant did not assess patients’ need for particular prescriptions before issuing them.
- Sometimes prescriptions were issued to individuals whom the defendant did not see for appointments.
- The operation did not perform urine drug screens to monitor medication compliance.
- Patient files maintained by the operation contained sparse information and no justification for prescribed medications.
- The defendant and Co‑Conspirator A often prescribed dangerous combinations such as benzodiazepines with buprenorphine.
- On four separate occasions, including October 17, 2020, the defendant prescribed controlled substances to an undercover agent and a confidential source without medical justification.
- On October 17, 2020, the defendant provided the confidential source’s Suboxone and methylphenidate prescriptions to the undercover agent despite the source’s absence.
- On October 17, 2020, the defendant increased the confidential source’s methylphenidate from 40 to 60 tablets of 20 mg without a request and without assessment due to the source’s absence.
- The defendant prescribed buprenorphine to both the undercover agent and the confidential source without diagnosing a substance abuse disorder.
- The defendant prescribed Adderall to the undercover agent without diagnosing any need for the amphetamine prescription.
- The methylphenidate prescription for the confidential source was based on an insurance coverage determination that Adderall would not be covered while methylphenidate would be.
- The defendant and Co‑Conspirator A knowingly and intentionally dispensed and distributed controlled substances through this operation.
- The defendant profited over $285,000 in cash from the prescribing operation.
- On December 3, 2020, the defendant told DEA investigators that the Saturday prescribing with Co‑Conspirator A was not a for‑profit business enterprise.
- The statement to DEA that the operation was not for‑profit was false because the defendant had made hundreds of thousands of dollars.
- The false statement to DEA was material because investigators would have behaved differently if they believed it.
- The defendant made the false statement to DEA knowingly and willfully.
- From 2017 onward, the defendant understood that the Saturday prescribing practices and lying to DEA about them were wrong.
- The defense sentencing memorandum states there were no objections to the Presentence Report.
- The sentencing hearing was scheduled for Monday, March 4 at 9:30 a.m.
- At the time of sentencing, the defense represented that the defendant was almost 78 years old.
- The defense represented that a physician reported mild cognitive decline and another diagnosed Major Neurocognitive Disorder.
- The defense represented that the defendant’s medical conditions include alcoholism.
- The defense represented that the defendant likely has early, mild Alzheimer’s dementia.
- The defense represented that brain scans showed brain atrophy.
- The defense represented that the defendant has mobility problems and an inability to walk more than six feet.
- The defense represented that the defendant has Type 2 diabetes.
- The defense represented that the defendant has heart failure and hypertension.
- The defense represented that the defendant experiences incontinence.
- The defense represented that the defendant’s DEA registration was taken away prior to trial.
- The defense represented that the defendant relinquished his medical license.
- The defense represented that since being indicted, the defendant has been taken to hospitals on more than one occasion for falls and other health conditions.
- The defense represented that the defendant had practiced medicine for forty years or more prior to these offenses.
- The plea agreement states the defendant agreed to provide a financial statement to the Financial Litigation Unit at least 30 days before sentencing and to cooperate in identifying and liquidating assets upon request.
- The plea agreement states the defendant will participate in the Bureau of Prisons’ Inmate Financial Responsibility Program if incarcerated.
- The plea agreement indicates the defendant understood that a restitution order may be entered by the court.

- On January 6, 2021, the U.S. Capitol was secured 24 hours a day by U.S. Capitol Police, and only authorized individuals with appropriate identification were allowed inside.
- On January 6, 2021, the exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to members of the public.
- A joint session of Congress to certify the 2020 Electoral College vote convened at approximately 1:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, with the Vice President presiding.
- By approximately 1:30 p.m. on January 6, 2021, the House and Senate adjourned to separate chambers to resolve an objection.
- At approximately 2:00 p.m., individuals in the crowd forced their way over barricades and past U.S. Capitol Police, advancing to the Capitol’s exterior.
- Shortly after 2:00 p.m., individuals forced entry into the U.S. Capitol, including by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement officers.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m. on January 6, 2021, members of the House and Senate were evacuated, and Congressional proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- Vice President Pence remained in the U.S. Capitol from the time he was evacuated from the Senate Chamber until proceedings resumed around 8:00 p.m.
- The January 6, 2021 riot resulted in substantial damage to the U.S. Capitol, requiring expenditure of more than $1.4 million for repairs.
- The defendant pleaded guilty to one count of assaulting, resisting, or impeding a federal officer in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1).
- The offense of conviction carries a statutory maximum of 8 years of imprisonment, a fine up to $250,000, and up to 3 years of supervised release.
- The defendant agreed to pay a $100 special assessment for the felony conviction.
- The defendant agreed to pay $2,000 in restitution to the Architect of the Capitol.
- As of May 17, 2021, the total damage to the U.S. Capitol from the January 6 riot was approximately $1,495,326.55.
- The government agreed to move to dismiss the remaining counts of the indictment at the time of sentencing as part of the plea agreement.
- The defendant signed the Statement of Offense acknowledging it was true and accurate.
- On January 6, 2021, around 4:00 p.m., the defendant stood just inside the Senate Wing entrance at the front of a crowd confronting a line of police officers.
- Police officers from the U.S. Capitol Police and the Metropolitan Police Department were attempting to keep the crowd at bay near the Senate Wing entrance.
- The defendant punched one officer, then punched a second officer near the Senate Wing entrance.
- As officers attempted to detain him near the Senate Wing entrance, the defendant punched the first officer again.
- When the defendant punched the two officers, both officers were employees of the U.S. Capitol Police engaged in the performance of their official duties.
- On January 6, 2021, at approximately 2:30 p.m., an officer inside the Capitol’s northwest hallway attempted to form a barrier to stop more individuals from entering the building.
- During that encounter in the northwest hallway, an individual later identified as the defendant attempted to push past officers and then punched an officer repeatedly with a closed fist.
- The officer reported being struck in the helmet and chest during the hallway assault.
- The individual struggled while being detained in the hallway but was brought under control by officers.
- The individual was transported to U.S. Capitol Police headquarters for processing after the hallway incident.
- While in custody and before being advised of Miranda rights, the individual spontaneously apologized for striking the officer and then apologized directly to the affiant when told the officer he struck was the affiant.
- A magistrate judge attested to the complaint by telephone on January 7, 2021.
- The initial complaint affidavit asserted probable cause that the defendant violated 18 U.S.C. § 111(a) by assaulting a federal law enforcement officer.
- The initial complaint affidavit asserted probable cause that the defendant violated 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a) related to restricted building or grounds.
- The initial complaint affidavit asserted probable cause that the defendant violated 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D), (F), and (G) relating to conduct in the Capitol and its buildings.
- On January 11, 2021, a federal grand jury returned an indictment charging the defendant with civil disorder, two counts of assaulting certain officers, restricted area offenses, and Capitol building offenses.
- On April 9, 2021, a superseding indictment added a count of engaging in physical violence in a restricted building or grounds under 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(4).
- On October 26, 2021, the defendant pleaded guilty to assaulting certain officers under 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1).
- The defendant agreed in his plea to dismiss the remaining counts at sentencing.
- The defendant acknowledged in the plea agreement that he had no criminal convictions based on information then available to the parties.
- The defendant and his attorney signed the plea agreement on September 16, 2021.
- The defendant made his way to the Upper West Terrace and the Senate Wing entrance after earlier violence had occurred at that location.
- A combined line of U.S. Capitol Police and Metropolitan Police Department officers had formed at the Senate Wing entrance to prevent further entry.
- While at the front of the crowd at the Senate Wing entrance, the defendant chanted at officers to 'join us!'
- When some in the crowd called for others to back up, the defendant shouted, 'If you back up, you'll never get back in!'
- The defendant struck two officers in the head during the altercation at the Senate Wing entrance.
- After punching the officers, the defendant attempted to retreat into the crowd but was apprehended and taken to the ground.
- Officers escorted the defendant to another part of the Capitol building where he was arrested on scene.
- The defendant later participated in a debrief with the U.S. Attorney’s Office and U.S. Capitol Police before his plea.
- During his debrief, the defendant expressed remorse, saying he felt embarrassed and that his conduct was stupid, and stated he would apologize to the officers.
- The two victim officers did not submit formal victim impact statements for sentencing.
- The government reported that the victim officers did not suffer bodily injury as a result of the defendant’s assault.
- The defendant is a military veteran who served in the U.S. Marine Corps and the Army National Guard.
- The defendant is on disability due to a combat-related injury sustained during service in Iraq in 2008.
- The defendant has certain medical conditions, including neurological conditions, associated with his combat injury.
- The defendant lacks scorable criminal history according to the presentence information discussed in the sentencing materials.
- The defendant traveled from Seattle to Philadelphia and then to Washington, D.C. for the events of January 6, 2021.
- The defendant reported he came to Washington, D.C. to show support for a former President and to be part of a large rally.
- Before reaching the Capitol, the defendant and a friend stopped for lunch and then rejoined the crowd in front of the building.
- At the time the crowd entered, individuals did not submit to security screenings or weapons checks by U.S. Capitol Police.
- Proceedings in Congress could not resume until every unauthorized occupant left the Capitol and the building was secured.
- The defendant chanted phrases such as 'stop the steal' and 'shame' while at the front of the crowd at the Senate Wing entrance.
- After his guilty plea, the government stated it would seek restitution consistent with the plea agreement’s $2,000 amount.
- The indictment and superseding indictment encompassed both restricted area violations (18 U.S.C. § 1752) and Capitol building conduct violations (40 U.S.C. § 5104).

- On December 31, 2020, at approximately 10:01 p.m., Defendant A was observed walking on Falls Terrace toward Alabama Avenue in Southeast Washington, D.C.
- When officers attempted to approach, Defendant A immediately ran from the officers.
- While fleeing, Defendant A’s left arm swung freely while his right arm and shoulder moved as if reaching into his waistband.
- Officers briefly lost sight of Defendant A during the foot chase.
- Officers approaching from another direction observed Defendant A make a large tossing motion and reported the object may have landed on a nearby roof.
- Immediately after the tossing motion, Defendant A fell to the ground.
- At Defendant A’s feet, officers found a cardboard box of ammunition and three loose rounds.
- A search of Defendant A revealed additional ammunition in his right front pocket.
- DC Fire and EMS assisted at the scene to search for a weapon in the area of the tossing motion.
- With a ladder truck, an officer accessed the roof of 4400 E Street SE and found a firearm.
- The recovered firearm was a Glock 17 with serial number BFEF180.
- When recovered, the firearm had one 9mm round in the chamber and twelve 9mm rounds in an extended high-capacity magazine.
- There are no firearm or ammunition manufacturers in the District of Columbia.
- The firearm and ammunition would have traveled in interstate commerce to reach the District of Columbia.
- A criminal history check through NCIC showed Defendant A had a prior felony conviction for Attempted Robbery in case number 2013 CF3 20897.
- Defendant A was sentenced to 12 months’ incarceration for the Attempted Robbery conviction in case number 2013 CF3 20897.
- Defendant A had an additional conviction for Robbery and Unlawful Possession of a Firearm in case number 2016 CF3 2996.
- For case number 2016 CF3 2996, Defendant A was sentenced to 36 months for Unlawful Possession of a Firearm and a consecutive 12 months for Robbery.
- At the time of his arrest, Defendant A was aware he had prior convictions for offenses punishable by more than one year.
- A complaint with arrest warrant was initiated and assigned case number 1:21-mj-00003 on January 1, 2021.
- The statement of facts was attested by the applicant by telephone under Fed. R. Crim. P. 4.1 on January 1, 2021 and approved by a U.S. Magistrate Judge.
- Defendant A later pleaded guilty to one count of unlawful possession of a firearm and ammunition by a person convicted of a crime punishable by imprisonment for a term exceeding one year, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(1).
- The statutory maximum term of imprisonment for the § 922(g)(1) count was 10 years.
- The offense carried a statutory maximum fine of up to $250,000 and up to 3 years of supervised release.
- Defendant A’s sentencing was scheduled for March 4, 2022 in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia.
- Defendant A had been in federal custody for over 15 months since his arrest on December 31, 2020.
- A motion to suppress was filed and denied prior to the guilty plea.
- The Presentence Investigation Report was completed on January 28, 2022.
- Defendant A submitted objections to the PSR’s guideline-related assertions on February 10, 2022.
- Defendant A specifically challenged the assertion that the handgun had been reported stolen.
- The PSR indicated the handgun was reported stolen from East Point, Georgia based on its serial number.
- Defense counsel stated they had not been provided independent proof that the handgun was stolen.
- Defense counsel reported that a search of Hotgunz.com for serial number BFEF180 indicated the firearm was not reported stolen.
- Defense counsel asserted that official confirmation of a firearm’s stolen status can only be obtained by law enforcement.
- In describing the incident, the memorandum states the recovered Glock had one 9mm round in the chamber and 12 rounds in a 31‑round magazine.
- The memorandum notes Defendant A was not observed aiming or firing the weapon during the incident.
- At the time of sentencing, Defendant A was approximately 25 years old.
- Defendant A has a high school diploma from a public charter high school.
- Defendant A lived with both parents until about age 11, when his father moved out.
- Defendant A reported that he continues to reside with his mother and plans to return to living with her after release.
- Defendant A reported no medical or mental health issues and stated he had not contracted COVID‑19 as of sentencing.
- Defendant A reported regular marijuana use since about age 14 or 15.
- Prior to his arrest, Defendant A was unemployed and lacked specialized training or skills.
- Defendant A had no assets or active bank accounts as reported in the PSR.
- The PSR concluded Defendant A was financially unable to pay a fine.
- A letter from Defendant A’s aunt was submitted describing him positively and discussing his accountability and community ties.
- Defendant A was incarcerated at the D.C. Jail during the COVID‑19 pandemic and experienced significant lockdown conditions.
- An inspection by the U.S. Marshals Service found the D.C. Jail conditions did not meet minimum standards of confinement.
- After a short period at Hazelton, Defendant A was held at the Northern Neck Regional Jail, away from his family.
- The sentencing memorandum restates that prior to December 31, 2020, Defendant A had been convicted of a felony.
- The sentencing memorandum reiterates the incident facts, including the foot chase, tossing motion, recovery of ammunition next to Defendant A, and discovery of the Glock on a nearby roof.

- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Count 24 charging a conspiracy to commit money laundering in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1956(h).
- The object of the money-laundering conspiracy was to engage in monetary transactions over $10,000 in criminally derived property under 18 U.S.C. § 1957(a).
- The specified unlawful activity underlying the money-laundering conspiracy was health care fraud and conspiracy to commit health care fraud under 18 U.S.C. §§ 1347 and 1349.
- Defendant A acknowledged reading the Indictment and having the charges fully explained by counsel.
- After sentencing, the government agreed to dismiss Counts 25, 26, 27, 29, 30, 31, and 32 as to Defendant A.
- The statutory maximum term of imprisonment for Count 24 is up to 10 years.
- The offense carries a potential supervised release term of up to three years.
- The offense carries a potential fine up to the greater of $250,000 or twice the pecuniary gain or loss under 18 U.S.C. § 3571(d).
- A $100 special assessment is to be imposed at sentencing for Count 24.
- As a consequence of the conviction, Defendant A will be excluded as a provider from Medicare, Medicaid, and all federal health care programs and must execute related documents within 60 days of receipt.
- Defendant A was advised that the conviction may have immigration consequences, including potential removal or denaturalization, and chose to plead guilty regardless of those consequences.
- Defendant A agreed to cooperate by providing full, complete, and truthful information to DOJ, the USAO, and federal regulatory officials overseeing Medicare, and to testify if called in criminal, civil, or administrative proceedings.
- The plea agreement requires Defendant A not to minimize his own involvement or fabricate or exaggerate the involvement of others; untruthful statements would constitute a material breach.
- Defendant A must provide a full, complete, and accurate personal financial statement to Probation and the USAO.
- Defendant A admitted he knowingly and willfully participated in the conspiracy to commit money laundering charged in Count 24.
- Defendant A admitted he agreed and intended to launder health care fraud proceeds with ultimate proceeds valued at more than $3,500,000 but less than $9,500,000.
- Defendant A agreed that certain funds seized from a bank account held in his name are subject to criminal forfeiture as property involved in the offense.
- Defendant A agreed that funds seized from a bank account held by Waterstone Capital Management, LLC are subject to criminal forfeiture related to the offense.
- Defendant A agreed that funds seized from bank accounts held by Ness Group Foundation, Inc. are subject to criminal forfeiture related to the offense.
- Defendant A agreed to entry of a forfeiture money judgment in the amount of $5,792,828 as property involved in the offense or traceable to such property.
- Defendant A waived any hearing to determine the nexus between the identified property and the offense and to determine the forfeiture money judgment amount.
- Defendant A agreed not to oppose forfeiture of the identified property and not to act in opposition to entry or satisfaction of the forfeiture money judgment.
- Defendant A waived constitutional, legal, and equitable defenses to forfeiture, including double-jeopardy and Eighth Amendment excessive-fine defenses, and any right to appeal a forfeiture order.
- Forfeiture will not be treated as satisfaction of any assessment, fine, restitution, or other penalty that may be imposed.
- Defendant A agreed to assist in forfeiture proceedings, including identifying property subject to forfeiture and transferring such property to the United States upon request.
- Within 10 calendar days of signing the plea agreement, Defendant A must submit a completed financial disclosure statement identifying all assets directly or indirectly controlled, including those held by third parties and outside the United States, and authorizes the USAO to obtain a credit report.
- Defendant A agreed not to sell, hide, waste, encumber, destroy, or otherwise devalue any asset without prior government approval until the forfeiture money judgment and restitution judgment are paid in full, and to identify any asset transfers over $25,000 since the indictment or awareness of the investigation.
- Defendant A agreed to liquidate assets or take other steps to enable immediate or expedited payment of the forfeiture money judgment and restitution judgment.
- Defendant A must notify the Clerk and the USAO within 30 days of any change of name, residence, or mailing address, and of any material change in economic circumstances affecting ability to pay forfeiture.
- Restitution is mandatory, and Defendant A stipulated to $2,701,090 in restitution, jointly and severally with co-conspirators.
- Defendant A waived most rights to appeal the sentence under 18 U.S.C. § 3742, including forfeiture and restitution orders, except if the sentence exceeds the statutory maximum or results from an upward departure or variance.
- Defendant A also waived rights to argue on appeal that the statute of conviction is unconstitutional or that the admitted conduct does not fall within the statute’s scope; if the government appeals, the appeal waiver is void.
- The plea agreement binds the DOJ Fraud Section and the USAO for the Southern District of Florida and does not release Defendant A from claims under Title 26 of the U.S. Code.
- In the event of a breach of the plea agreement, Defendant A cannot withdraw the guilty plea, the government may take any position on sentencing, and the defendant waives protections that otherwise limit use of his statements.

- On January 15, 2021, a plea agreement was filed in which Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Count 1 of the Superseding Indictment charging 18 U.S.C. § 2252(a)(1).

- On August 22, 2017, the Kentucky State Police Electronic Crimes Branch received a CyberTip from NCMEC regarding uploads to a Google account associated with Defendant A.
- Eight images were uploaded to the referenced Google account on July 30, 2017.
- The CyberTip included identifying information for the files, including an IP address from which the images were uploaded.
- A detective reviewed the CyberTip images and determined they contained child pornography.
- The upload IP address was used by a wireless internet service and could not identify an individual user account on its own.
- Login IP addresses for the Google account were obtained and subscriber information for those addresses was subpoenaed.
- Subscriber information for the login IP addresses returned to an account subscribed by Defendant A’s spouse at Defendant A’s residence.
- On December 12, 2017, a state judge issued a search warrant for Defendant A’s residence.
- Law enforcement executed the search warrant on December 12, 2017 and seized multiple electronic devices belonging to Defendant A.
- An on-scene preview during the search identified at least twenty files containing child sexual exploitation images.
- A forensic examination of Defendant A’s smartphone revealed 23 images and 2 videos of child pornography.
- The images on the smartphone included depictions of children under 12 engaged in sexually explicit conduct.
- The images on the smartphone included depictions portraying sadistic or masochistic conduct.
- Defendant A admitted he knew the images he received, possessed, and transported depicted minors engaged in sexually explicit conduct.
- Defendant A admitted he used the internet to access the images.
- Defendant A admitted he transported child pornography in interstate commerce by uploading images via his computer on July 30, 2017.

- For Count 1, the term of supervised release is at least 5 years and up to life.
- A mandatory $100 special assessment applies to Count 1.

- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to a single-count Information charging a violation of 21 U.S.C. §§ 842(a)(5) and (c)(2)(B) for knowingly failing to file suspicious order reports.
- The government agreed to move at sentencing to dismiss the earlier Indictment in case number 6:19-CR-14-REW.
- The statutory maximum imprisonment for the offense is one year.
- The statutory maximum fine for the offense is $100,000.
- The statutory maximum term of supervised release is one year.
- A mandatory $25 special assessment applies to the offense.
- The United States did not seek restitution in the case.
- Defendant A waived any statute of limitations defense for the conduct charged in the Information and admitted in the plea agreement.
- Defendant A worked for McKesson Corporation, starting in inventory management at the Washington Court House, Ohio distribution center.
- McKesson is a DEA-registered distributor and among the largest distributors of controlled substances.
- In 2008, McKesson paid $13.25 million in a DOJ settlement resolving allegations it failed to report suspicious orders of controlled substances.
- Following the 2008 settlement, McKesson implemented a Controlled Substance Monitoring Program (CSMP).
- Defendant A was promoted to Director of Regulatory Affairs for McKesson’s North Central region, covering 15 states including Kentucky.
- As a DRA, Defendant A identified suspicious orders, approved new customers for controlled substances, and set monthly volume limits (thresholds).
- McKesson’s CSMP instructed personnel to refrain from using the word “suspicious” in communications because doing so required stopping controlled substance sales and notifying DEA.
- Defendant A understood McKesson’s policy as reporting suspicious orders only after deeming a customer suspicious and terminating controlled substance sales.
- On July 27, 2011, Defendant A met with DEA agents after an inspection; DEA advised that orders of unusual size, pattern deviation, or unusual frequency should be reported as suspicious.
- In June 2011, the owner of Community Drug and Medi Center Drug in Manchester, Kentucky switched to McKesson and was approved by Defendant A to receive controlled substances.
- Community Drug estimated dispensing 5,000 oxycodone dosage units per month on its pharmacy questionnaire.
- In July 2011, McKesson shipped 21,900 oxycodone dosage units to Community Drug.
- In August 2011, McKesson shipped 36,100 oxycodone dosage units to Community Drug.
- In September 2011, McKesson shipped 47,500 oxycodone dosage units to Community Drug.
- In October 2011, McKesson shipped 48,200 oxycodone dosage units to Community Drug.
- In November 2011, McKesson shipped 37,300 oxycodone dosage units to Community Drug.
- From December 2011 through April 2012, Community Drug received between 25,500 and 31,100 oxycodone dosage units per month from McKesson.
- Defendant A raised Community Drug’s oxycodone order thresholds multiple times to allow increased sales.
- In September 2011, Community Drug ordered 27,900 oxycodone 30 mg dosage units, more than 58% of its total oxycodone orders that month.
- Defendant A knew oxycodone 30 mg had high illicit market demand and that a disproportionate 30 mg mix was a suspicious ordering pattern.
- After McKesson implemented a subset threshold for oxycodone 30 mg in November 2011, Defendant A set Community Drug’s 30 mg subset threshold at a high ratio to its overall oxycodone threshold.
- On November 3, 2011, Community Drug provided dispensing data showing 73,062 oxycodone 30 mg dosage units dispensed in 478 prescriptions from August 1 to October 31, 2011, averaging 152.8 doses per prescription, and Defendant A approved a threshold increase the same day.
- On May 1, 2012, Defendant A wrote DEA that McKesson had ceased selling controlled substances to Community Drug.
- On May 21, 2012, Defendant A wrote DEA that McKesson had conducted additional review and resumed sales to Community Drug on May 18, 2012.
- The May 21, 2012 letter did not disclose Community Drug’s high volume of out-of-state prescriptions or that no legitimate justification was found for its suspicious ordering.
- On May 17, 2012, Defendant A told the pharmacy owner that resumption of sales was conditioned on ceasing fills from outside the immediate vicinity, from three named pain clinics, from any out-of-state doctors, and from three named doctors.
- Subsequent dispensing data showed Community Drug continued dispensing for prescriptions from outside the immediate vicinity, including out-of-state doctors and the named clinics and doctor, and Defendant A did not report this ordering to DEA as suspicious.
- In August 2012, Defendant A approved a doubling of Community Drug’s oxycodone and hydrocodone thresholds.
- McKesson continued to ship to Community Drug after a DEA subpoena regarding the pharmacy, after media reports of a DEA search warrant, and after a DEA agent interviewed Defendant A about the pharmacy’s ordering.
- Defendant A knew Community Drug placed oxycodone orders from July 2011 to April 2012 that were of unusual size and deviated substantially from a normal pattern.
- Defendant A knew, at least through deliberate ignorance, that Community Drug continued ordering after May 2012 to fill prescriptions it had agreed not to fill.
- Defendant A knew he was required to report these orders as suspicious but did not do so.
- Defendant A pled guilty to the misdemeanor offense based on conduct in 2011–2012 connected with Community Drug Pharmacy in Manchester, Kentucky.
- As a DRA, Defendant A administered McKesson’s CSMP for retail pharmacy customers across approximately 15 states, including in the Eastern District of Kentucky.
- Defendant A monitored thousands of pharmacies, including 3000 to 4000 independent retail pharmacies and certain national chain accounts.
- Defendant A’s compensation was not tied to the sale of controlled substances to pharmacies under his purview.
- In July 2011, Community Drug’s initial oxycodone threshold was set at 8,000 dosage units per month and then increased to 13,000 and 23,000 during that first month to accommodate ordering.
- Defendant A later acknowledged that Community Drug’s increase in oxycodone ordering from about 22,000 units in July 2011 to 47,500 units in October 2011 was suspicious based on the size of the increase.
- On April 20, 2012, Defendant A visited Community Drug and noted red flags, including uncertainty whether certain pain clinics were cash-only and that some prescriptions were written by out-of-state prescribers.
- After the April 2012 visit, Defendant A contacted the Kentucky Board of Pharmacy, which recommended not selling more than default threshold amounts to Community Drug.
- On May 1, 2012, Defendant A notified DEA that McKesson had terminated Community Drug’s ability to order controlled substances, and on May 21, 2012, he notified DEA of reinstatement with thresholds set back to default levels.
- Following reinstatement in May 2012, Community Drug’s oxycodone threshold was limited to 8,000 dosage units per month until later increases.
- On July 23, 2012, a sales representative requested threshold increases for Community Drug; Defendant A stated any increases would require not servicing pain clinics or out-of-state doctors and keeping the oxycodone 30 mg proportion at or below 25%.
- On July 25, 2012, an internal review flagged some out-of-state prescriptions at Community Drug; Defendant A did not escalate to a Level 3 review at that time.
- In late July 2012, Defendant A met Community Drug’s owners in person to discuss compliance expectations, including not filling prescriptions from out-of-state doctors.
- On August 2, 2012, Community Drug informed Defendant A they had added another questionable prescriber to their internal do-not-fill list.
- On August 23, 2012, Defendant A increased Community Drug’s oxycodone and hydrocodone thresholds from 8,000 to 16,000 dosage units per month.
- On September 9, 2012, DEA interviewed Defendant A about prior issues relating to Community Drug.
- DEA executed a search warrant at Community Drug on September 11, 2012.
- On September 19, 2012, Defendant A reduced Community Drug’s oxycodone threshold to approximately 6,700 dosage units per month.
- On September 25, 2012, McKesson’s distribution center received a subpoena for records related to Community Drug, which included a nondisclosure mandate.
- Following the subpoena, Defendant A reduced Community Drug’s hydrocodone threshold to the default amount of 8,000 dosage units per month.
- Around October 18–23, 2012, Defendant A concluded Community Drug was engaged in illegal activities, made the pharmacy ineligible to purchase controlled substances, and notified DEA of the termination on October 23, 2012.
- Defendant A admitted he should have reported a suspicious order in connection with Community Drug before May 2012 and that his failure to do so formed the basis of his misdemeanor plea.
- McKesson reported its Schedule II and Schedule III narcotics distributions, including to Community Drug, to DEA monthly through ARCOS.
- Defendant A left the Director of Regulatory Affairs position in early 2014 and retired from McKesson in 2016.
- Defendant A has no prior criminal history according to the sentencing memorandum.
- Defendant A reported significant cardiac medical conditions and stress-related concerns in the sentencing materials.
- Defendant A stated that after reinstating Community Drug in May 2012, he initially limited thresholds and monitored the pharmacy more closely before later increases.
- According to a DEA agent’s affidavit cited in the sentencing memorandum, the pharmacy owner taught an employee how to hide prescriptions from the distributor’s systems.
- The sentencing memorandum asserts that DEA interviewed Defendant A on September 9, 2012 and did not recommend McKesson terminate Community Drug at that time.
- The sentencing memorandum states that a wiretap was initiated on January 25, 2012 that captured incriminating conversations involving Community Drug.
- The sentencing memorandum indicates McKesson twice terminated and reinstated (May 2012) and later finally terminated (October 2012) Community Drug’s ability to order controlled substances and notified DEA after each action.
- The sentencing memorandum asserts Defendant A was not charged regarding Family Discount Pharmacy in West Virginia, but related conduct was discussed in the presentence report.
- The sentencing memorandum asserts Family Discount submitted a questionnaire around January 26, 2010, disclosing 155,000 hydrocodone dose units dispensed per month.
- The sentencing memorandum relays that a 2009 visit by another distributor’s investigator found Family Discount did not present a significant diversion risk and that a hydrocodone overage was filled without a suspicious order report being apparent.
- In 2012, Family Discount transitioned to McKesson, and its questionnaire stated another distributor had restricted hydrocodone purchases due to a policy cap.
- On September 6, 2012, Defendant A sought vetting of Family Discount by the West Virginia Board of Pharmacy and contacted manufacturers for input before onboarding.
- The sentencing memorandum reports that a West Virginia Board of Pharmacy investigator described Family Discount as a “reliable high volume account.”
- On November 1, 2013, Defendant A substantially lowered Family Discount’s thresholds as part of broader threshold reductions for independent pharmacies.
- On December 16, 2013, Family Discount requested a hydrocodone threshold increase, citing a patient care need, and Defendant A approved this increase on December 23, 2013.
- Defendant A resigned from the DRA position on March 17, 2014 due to health and stress concerns related to the role.
- After his DRA tenure and before retirement, Defendant A conducted warehouse audits for regulatory compliance from 2014 to 2016.
- The sentencing memorandum asserts DEA served an Administrative Inspection Warrant on Family Discount on November 4, 2013 and took no action to suspend or revoke its registration.
- The sentencing memorandum asserts that after McKesson terminated Family Discount in April 2014, the pharmacy obtained controlled substances from another distributor in similar amounts.

- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to the sole count charging attempted online enticement under 18 U.S.C. § 2422(b).
- Beginning in late 2018, Defendant A corresponded with Victim A via Facebook Messenger.
- Victim A was 15 years old when the Facebook Messenger correspondence began in late 2018.
- Defendant A knew Victim A from a previous relationship with Victim A's mother and was aware of Victim A's age.
- Around the times alleged in the indictment, the conversations became sexual in nature.
- On 10/05/2019, Victim A's school counselor contacted Kentucky State Police after Victim A reported feeling unsafe due to unwanted sexual messages from an adult male.
- A Kentucky State Police detective obtained signed consent from Victim A and Victim A's mother to take control of Victim A's Facebook account and Messenger.
- The detective conducted online chats with Defendant A for over a month, with Defendant A believing he was communicating with Victim A.
- Defendant A sent messages expressing a desire to engage in sexual conduct with Victim A, including oral and vaginal sex.
- Defendant A sent multiple images of his genitalia during the online conversations.
- On 10/06/2019, Defendant A deposited $200 into Victim A's personal bank account.
- The messages were sent via Facebook and a computer, both means and facilities of interstate commerce.
- Defendant A discussed traveling to Kentucky to meet Victim A for sexual purposes.
- On 11/23/2019, Defendant A traveled from North Carolina to Morehead, Kentucky, intending to meet Victim A for sexual activity.
- At the time of the intended meeting on 11/23/2019, Victim A was 16 years old and Defendant A was 46 years old.
- Victim A resided in Rowan County within the Eastern District of Kentucky during the events.
- The plea agreement states that the sexual activity described would violate Kentucky laws prohibiting third-degree sodomy and third-degree rape.
- The statutory imprisonment range for the offense is a mandatory minimum of 10 years up to life imprisonment.
- The statutory maximum fine for the offense is $250,000.
- The term of supervised release for the offense is at least 5 years up to life.
- A $100 special assessment applies to the conviction, to be paid by sentencing.
- An additional $5,000 assessment may apply under 18 U.S.C. § 3014 if Defendant A is non-indigent and the offense occurred between May 29, 2015 and September 30, 2021.
- Defendant A will be required to register as a sex offender upon release as a condition of supervised release and under applicable federal and state law.
- Defendant A must register with the sex offender registration agency in Kentucky and in any jurisdiction where he resides, is employed, or is a student, and keep the registration current.
- Defendant A must notify the registration agency of changes and periodically verify registration information in person, and may face penalties for noncompliance.
- Upon release, notice will be provided to certain law enforcement agencies pursuant to 18 U.S.C. § 4042(c).
- Defendant A agrees to forfeit property listed in the indictment’s forfeiture allegation and to execute necessary documents.
- Defendant A agrees that the United States can prove the required nexus for forfeiture and waives timing provisions of Rule 32.2.
- Within 30 days of pleading guilty, Defendant A agrees to complete and sign a financial disclosure statement listing all assets and any transfers within the prior three years.
- Defendant A agrees to submit to an examination related to financial disclosure, which may be under oath and may include a polygraph.
- Defendant A agrees not to encumber, transfer, or dispose of assets without approval from the United States Attorney’s Office.
- If incarcerated, Defendant A agrees to participate in the Bureau of Prisons Inmate Financial Responsibility Program.
- Monetary penalties imposed by the Court will be due immediately and subject to immediate enforcement by the United States.
- Any court-imposed payment schedule is a minimum and not a limitation on enforcement methods.
- Defendant A waives any requirement for demand of payment, and unpaid obligations may be submitted for Treasury offset.
- Defendant A authorizes the United States to obtain his credit reports at any time.

- If Defendant A violates the plea agreement, the United States may void the agreement and seek additional charges.
