- The Court ordered NASA Federal Credit Union to remit $27,432 to the Clerk of Court to be applied to restitution in this case.
- At the time of the offense, the defendant resided in Indian Trail, North Carolina.
- The scheme occurred from about March 2020 through about July 2020.
- The conduct occurred in the Western District of North Carolina and elsewhere.
- The offense involved a benefit connected to a presidentially declared major emergency as defined by the Stafford Act.
- The defendant intentionally participated in a scheme to defraud and to obtain money and property by false pretenses.
- The defendant applied for fraudulent unemployment insurance benefits in North Carolina, New Jersey, Connecticut, Florida, Minnesota, New York, Massachusetts, Maine, Washington, and Illinois.
- The unemployment insurance claims included false representations such as non-existent employment and false reasons for termination.
- The defendant falsely claimed to have been laid off from a large retailer in North Carolina due to COVID when in truth he was laid off for misconduct.
- The defendant claimed prior employment with multiple large retailers where he had never worked.
- The defendant falsely claimed he was laid off from some companies because he had been diagnosed with COVID-19 or due to lack of work because of COVID-19.
- On some occasions, the defendant used a social security number and a mailing address that did not belong to him in unemployment claims.
- The defendant directed payments of fraudulent unemployment benefits to bank accounts or Green Dot cards under his control.
- On or about March 26, 2020, the defendant submitted a fraudulent unemployment claim to North Carolina falsely claiming layoff due to a COVID-19 diagnosis and directed payments to a credit union account.
- On or about March 28, 2020, the defendant submitted a fraudulent unemployment claim to New York falsely claiming prior employment with a large retailer and directed payments to a credit union and a bank account.
- On or about March 29, 2020, the defendant submitted a fraudulent unemployment claim to Illinois falsely claiming prior employment with a retailer and layoff due to COVID-19 and directed payments to a credit union account.
- On or about April 4, 2020, the defendant submitted a fraudulent unemployment claim to Massachusetts falsely claiming prior employment with a large retailer and directed payments to a credit union account.
- On or about April 5, 2020, the defendant submitted a fraudulent unemployment claim to New Jersey falsely claiming prior employment with a large retailer and claiming layoff due to COVID-19 and directed payments to a credit union and another bank account.
- On or about April 7, 2020, the defendant submitted a fraudulent unemployment claim to Minnesota falsely claiming prior employment with a home improvement retailer and directed payments to a credit union account.
- On or about April 30, 2020, the defendant submitted a fraudulent unemployment claim to Washington falsely claiming prior employment with Costco and directed payments to a credit union and a bank account.
- On or about May 10, 2020, the defendant submitted a fraudulent unemployment claim to Connecticut falsely claiming prior employment with Best Buy and layoff due to a COVID-19 diagnosis and directed payments to a credit union and a bank account.
- The defendant acted willfully and with intent to defraud.
- From March 2020 through July 2020, the defendant used false representations about employment and reasons for termination in unemployment filings across multiple states.
- The CARES Act expanded states' ability to provide unemployment insurance for many workers impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic.
- The CARES Act provided an additional $600 weekly payment known as Federal Pandemic Unemployment Compensation to certain eligible individuals receiving other benefits.
- In addition to unemployment insurance fraud, the defendant applied to the Small Business Administration for four Economic Injury Disaster Loans using fictitious entities.
- The fictitious entities used in the SBA EIDL applications included entities named Ransome Investments, Ransome Financial, Rosa Milk and Dairy, and Ransome Pizza.
- The SBA EIDL applications contained false financial information to purportedly qualify for the loans.
- The SBA disbursed one of the EIDLs by depositing $9,000 into the defendant's bank account.
- The SBA later determined the EIDL application was fraudulent and recovered the $9,000.
- The defendant used another individual's social security number to apply for a car loan while using his own name in the application.
- The defendant has not defaulted on the car loan obtained using another individual's social security number.
- The intended loss across the schemes is more than $163,000.
- After receipt of fraudulent unemployment benefits, many states uncovered the fraud and were able to recoup some of the deposited funds.
- The unemployment insurance claims were submitted over the Internet.

- The defendant was charged by information with two violations of 18 U.S.C. § 1365(a)(1).
- The defendant waived prosecution by indictment in open court.
- The defendant agreed to plead guilty to two counts of attempted consumer product tampering.
- Count One alleged attempted tampering with Moderna COVID-19 vaccine on or about December 24, 2020 in the Eastern District of Wisconsin.
- Count Two alleged attempted tampering with Moderna COVID-19 vaccine on or about December 25, 2020 in the Eastern District of Wisconsin.
- Each count of conviction carries a statutory maximum of 10 years’ imprisonment and a $250,000 fine.
- Each count carries a mandatory $100 special assessment and up to three years of supervised release.
- The Court may order restitution for these offenses under 18 U.S.C. § 3663A(c)(1)(A)(iv).
- The defendant agreed that restitution is mandatory for the offenses of conviction and to cooperate in its collection.
- The defendant agreed to pay a total special assessment of $200 at or before sentencing.
- As part of financial disclosure obligations, the defendant agreed to provide a sworn financial statement to the Financial Litigation Unit at least 30 days before sentencing and upon request thereafter.
- The defendant has been licensed as a pharmacist in Wisconsin since August 22, 1997.
- In late 2020, the defendant worked as a third-shift pharmacist at a medical center in Grafton, Wisconsin, typically from approximately 11:00 PM to 7:00 AM.
- The defendant’s job duties included ensuring that drugs and vaccines at the facility’s pharmacy were stored and maintained properly.
- The defendant held and communicated various conspiracy beliefs to coworkers for at least two years prior to the offense period.
- The defendant was skeptical of vaccines generally and skeptical of the Moderna COVID-19 vaccine specifically, and communicated those views to coworkers.
- As a pharmacist, the defendant knew public health authorities viewed COVID-19 as a serious global health threat and that the Moderna vaccine was intended to mitigate that threat.
- The defendant knew that improper vaccine storage could pose a risk of bodily harm or death due to reduced potency, breakdown into dangerous components, or contamination.
- The defendant knew that Moderna’s COVID-19 vaccine should not be left out of refrigeration for more than twelve hours before administration.
- On two successive shifts beginning the evenings of December 24 and 25, 2020, the defendant removed the same box of Moderna COVID-19 vaccine from refrigeration for multiple hours intending to render it inert or ineffective.
- On the morning of December 26, 2020, 57 individuals at the Grafton facility received doses of the vaccine that had previously been removed from refrigeration by the defendant.
- The vaccine the defendant removed from refrigeration had previously been transported in interstate commerce.
- As of the plea agreement attachment, efforts were ongoing to determine whether the affected vaccine doses were inert or harmful.
- The Moderna COVID-19 vaccine received Emergency Use Authorization from FDA on December 18, 2020.
- The Moderna COVID-19 vaccine is intended for intramuscular injection for active immunization to prevent COVID-19 in individuals 18 years and older.
- Vials of the Moderna COVID-19 vaccine are stored frozen between -25° to -15°C and in the original carton to protect from light.
- Vials should not be stored on dry ice or below -40°C and should not be refrozen once thawed.
- Vials can be refrigerated between 2° to 8°C for up to 30 days prior to first use.
- Unpunctured vials may be stored between 8° to 25°C for up to 12 hours.
- The investigation into the defendant’s conduct involved FDA-OCI, the FBI, and the Grafton Police Department.
- Investigative steps included employee interviews, review of electronic records related to pharmacy access and operations, analysis of the defendant’s communications, collection of surveillance footage, and an interview of the defendant.
- The defendant concealed his misconduct initially, and for weeks victims did not know if they had been inoculated, poisoned, or given a placebo-equivalent.
- The defendant pled guilty to two counts of attempted consumer product tampering, and sentencing was set for June 8, 2021.
- The box of vaccine the defendant removed from refrigeration contained over 500 doses.
- A pharmacy technician working in the early morning hours of December 26 discovered the vaccine outside the refrigerator and alerted supervisors.
- When first confronted, the defendant falsely claimed the vaccine had been left out accidentally and did not initially acknowledge the prior removal.
- After the defendant’s arrest by state law enforcement, multiple firearms were recovered from him.
- As of the time of the sentencing memorandum, it appeared that the vaccine received by the fifty-seven people remained effective.
- Victims reported anxiety, anger, lasting worry and stress, and sleep difficulties after learning of the defendant’s conduct.
- A victim medical doctor reported fear, anxiety, dread, and significant anguish lasting many weeks.
- Another victim wrote that the defendant violated their autonomy by making a medical decision for them and took away the security of knowing they were protected.
- The employer facility reported multiple levels of harm, including reputational damage, employee welfare issues, physical security concerns, and loss of patient confidence.
- The defendant’s actions were lauded online by individuals expressing anti-vaccine views, according to exhibits cited in the memorandum.
- Despite being a medical professional, the defendant espoused beliefs such as that the authorities were out to get him, Judgment Day was imminent, the Earth was flat, and that vaccines were of the Devil and microchipped.
- On multiple occasions, the defendant facilitated co-workers receiving saline in place of an annual flu vaccine by generating labels and providing saline-filled syringes to accomplices.
- The defendant personally avoided receiving the annual flu vaccine using a similar plan to substitute saline.
- The defendant brought firearms to the medical facility in violation of employer rules and showed a witness a handgun around November 2020, saying it was for use in case the military came to take him.
- In divorce proceedings filed December 30, 2020, the defendant’s ex-spouse reported that the defendant stored bulk food and guns in rental units and left emergency supplies due to fears of government cyberattacks and power grid failure.
- At the time of the memorandum, the defendant was subject to a no-contact order with his children.
- Public health authorities declared COVID-19 a pandemic in March 2020 and the United States declared a public health emergency.
- As of January 17, 2021, CDC reported over 23 million COVID-19 cases and over 390,000 associated deaths in the United States.
- The defendant’s third-shift schedule often left him as the only pharmacist on duty for hours at a time.
- The defendant’s misconduct involved removing vaccine from refrigeration on December 24 and 25, 2020 and leaving it out for multiple hours each night with intent to render it inert.
- The 57 impacted doses were administered largely to healthcare workers at the facility.

- On January 21, 2021, a two-count indictment was filed alleging distribution of fentanyl on November 16, 2019.
- On June 14, 2024, the defendant pled guilty to two counts of distribution of fentanyl.
- The guilty plea was entered pursuant to Rule 11(c)(1)(A).
- The plea was to lesser-included offenses in both counts for distribution of fentanyl under 21 U.S.C. § 841(a)(1).
- By pleading guilty, the defendant admitted he intentionally distributed fentanyl, a Schedule II controlled substance.
- The defendant admitted he knew he possessed a controlled substance, specifically fentanyl, at the time of distribution.
- On November 16, 2019, the defendant texted Victim A to see if she was interested in obtaining drugs from him.
- On November 16, 2019, the defendant and Victim A met in the Whitmoor Country Club parking lot in the Eastern District of Missouri.
- Surveillance footage from the Whitmoor Country Club depicted the defendant and Victim A meeting on November 16, 2019.
- At the Whitmoor Country Club parking lot, the defendant intentionally and knowingly distributed fentanyl to Victim A.
- After leaving Whitmoor Country Club, Victim A parked in a Dobb's parking lot and did not leave her vehicle.
- Victim A was found unconscious in her vehicle about an hour and fifteen minutes after leaving the Whitmoor Country Club on November 16, 2019.
- A bystander administered Narcan to Victim A, but it was ultimately unsuccessful.
- Victim A died on November 16, 2019, and the medical examiner determined the immediate cause of death was fentanyl intoxication.
- Later on November 16, 2019, the defendant intentionally and knowingly distributed fentanyl to Victim B.
- Victim B would testify that she wanted to buy oxycodone from the defendant and that he represented the drugs as oxycodone.
- Although not admitting misrepresentation, the defendant agreed not to contest at sentencing that he represented the fentanyl as oxycodone to Victim B.
- The defendant admitted the drugs he distributed to Victim B on November 16, 2019 were fentanyl.
- After receiving the fentanyl, Victim B ingested it and later believed she was overdosing and went to a hospital the same day.
- Victim B was evaluated, observed for approximately three hours, treated with intravenous fluids and anti-nausea medication, and released the same day.
- The defendant frequently displayed drugs on social media between September 2019 and April 2020.
- On November 16, 2019, the defendant appeared to be posting pictures of drugs on social media.
- On November 20, 2019, the defendant posted a picture of drug capsules on top of a can of Narcan on social media.
- At other times, the defendant posted photos of cash and capsules on social media.
- The defendant admitted that death resulted from Victim A’s use of the fentanyl he distributed on November 16, 2019.
- The defendant admitted that serious bodily injury resulted from Victim B’s use of the fentanyl he distributed on November 16, 2019.
- For each count of conviction, the statutory maximum penalty is up to 20 years of imprisonment and a fine of up to $1,000,000.
- A term of supervised release of at least three years and up to life applies to each count of conviction.
- The defendant agreed to forfeit all his interest in items seized by law enforcement during the investigation and any property used to facilitate the offense or constituting proceeds.
- The defendant admitted that currency, weapons, property, and assets seized constitute proceeds of his illegal activity, were commingled with illegal proceeds, or were used to facilitate the illegal activity.
- On November 16, 2019, the defendant and Victim A arranged to meet at Whitmoor Country Club in St. Charles, Missouri to conduct a drug transaction.
- At the country club, the defendant provided Victim A with a capsule, and they both snorted it there.
- About an hour and fifteen minutes after the Whitmoor Country Club encounter, Victim A was found unconscious in her vehicle at a Dobb’s Tire and Auto parking lot.
- Emergency personnel attempted life-saving measures on Victim A, but she did not survive; her cause of death was fentanyl intoxication.
- Victim B had previously purchased opioids from the defendant on at least one prior occasion before November 16, 2019.
- On November 15, 2019, Victim B provided the defendant with $20 cash for him to purchase an opioid on her behalf.
- On November 16, 2019, Victim B met the defendant at his residence to retrieve the opioid.
- When handing Victim B the opioid on November 16, 2019, the defendant told her to “be careful.”
- Victim B ingested the opioid at a friend’s house and then experienced severe symptoms, including inability to stand and vomiting.
- Victim B was taken to SSM Health St. Joseph West Hospital in Lake St. Louis, Missouri, where she received treatment and was released the same day.
- The plea agreement records that the events of November 16, 2019 occurred within the Eastern District of Missouri.
- On August 26, 2024, the probation office filed a presentence investigation report in this case.
- The defendant first used drugs in 2016 at age 16, according to the sentencing memorandum.
- By 2019, the defendant had a severe addiction to opioids, specifically fentanyl and benzodiazepines, per the memorandum.
- The memorandum states the defendant began dealing to close friends or acquaintances to support his addiction.
- The defendant and Victim A had a prior relationship dating back to school, including spending time together and using marijuana after school by fall 2016, per the memorandum.
- In 2013, the defendant was diagnosed with depression, according to the memorandum.
- During high school, the defendant began using Adderall obtained from a classmate in fall 2016 and started daily marijuana use after school, per the memorandum.
- The memorandum states the defendant regularly used Xanax and experimented with opioids, benzodiazepines, and psychedelics by late high school.
- The defendant’s grades declined significantly by senior year; he graduated with an unweighted GPA of 2.85 and a class rank of 276 out of 423, per the memorandum.
- In August 2018, the defendant was assaulted by a group, later diagnosed with a closed head injury and postconcussional syndrome at urgent care, according to the memorandum.
- Following the 2018 assault, the defendant reportedly isolated at home for about nine months and dropped out of community college, per the memorandum.
- In June 2019, the defendant experienced a suspected overdose after taking a pill he thought was Xanax that contained fentanyl, according to the memorandum.
- By age 20, the defendant had at least ten suspected overdoses on fentanyl, per the memorandum.
- In January 2020, the defendant was arrested in St. Charles County for possession of fentanyl, according to the memorandum.
- On April 29, 2020, the defendant entered inpatient addiction treatment for 20 days at a facility referred to as Harris House, per the memorandum.
- Beginning June 1, 2020, the defendant worked as a sourcing analyst and later full-time until his post-plea incarceration in June 2024, according to the memorandum.
- On January 12, 2021, the defendant began a 20-month county drug court treatment program, per the memorandum.
- During the drug court program, the defendant paid approximately $1,800 in costs, passed 185 drug tests, attended 114 group and 14 individual therapy sessions, and completed 45 hours of community service, according to the memorandum.
- The defendant graduated from the county drug court program on August 24, 2022, according to the memorandum.
- Following graduation, a state possession charge was dismissed, per the memorandum.
- On January 26, 2024, the defendant began bi-weekly mental health counseling and was diagnosed with anxiety and PTSD; he was prescribed related medications, according to the memorandum.
- Since the June 14, 2024 change-of-plea hearing, the defendant has been incarcerated at the Franklin County Adult Detention Facility in Union, Missouri, per the memorandum.
- While incarcerated post-plea, the defendant completed 702 courses totaling 10,002 credits through a program called Pathway to Achieve, according to the memorandum.
- The plea agreement states the United States Attorney’s Office for the Eastern District of Missouri agreed not to bring further federal prosecution in the district for known conduct arising out of the indictment events.

- Defendant A agreed to waive indictment and plead guilty to a one-count Information charging distribution of child pornography under 18 U.S.C. § 2252(a)(2), (b)(1).
- One element of the offense was that Defendant A knowingly distributed a visual depiction of sexually explicit conduct involving a minor.
- Another element of the offense was that the visual depiction was transported in interstate or foreign commerce, including by computer.
- Another element of the offense was that Defendant A knew the performer was a minor and that the depiction showed a minor engaged in sexually explicit conduct.
- For the distribution count, the statutory imprisonment range was a mandatory minimum of 5 years and a maximum of 20 years.
- For the distribution count, the supervised release term carried a minimum of 5 years and a maximum of life.
- The maximum fine for the offense was $250,000.
- A $100 special assessment applied, and a $5,000 assessment under 18 U.S.C. § 3014 could apply.
- Restitution was mandatory in this case under 18 U.S.C. § 2259.
- Defendant A was advised that he will be required to register as a sex offender under SORNA as a consequence of this conviction.
- Defendant A consented to conduct the Rule 11 guilty plea hearing remotely under the CARES Act due to pandemic-related concerns.
- Defendant A agreed to the forfeiture of an Apple iPhone 6s used in the offense.
- Defendant A signed the plea agreement on or about January 26, 2021.
- Defendant A was a Maryland resident during the time period relevant to the investigation.
- During the relevant period, Defendant A held positions with the Maryland state government including Deputy Legislative Officer and Chief Operating Officer with the Maryland Department of Commerce.
- Between April 2019 and September 2019, Kik reported multiple accounts using similar “anonjohnny” usernames that uploaded at least one image of child pornography or child-sensitive material.
- The reported Kik usernames included “anonjohnny2”, “anonjohnny654321”, “anonjohnny80”, “anonjohnny72”, and “anonjohnny9800”.
- Kik shut down each of the referenced accounts after detecting child exploitive material.
- IP addresses used to access the referenced Kik accounts were serviced by Verizon and traced to an account registered to Defendant A.
- Some of the IP addresses used to access the Kik accounts resolved to State of Maryland networks, indicating access from state government internet services.
- On August 11, 2020, investigators executed a search warrant at Defendant A’s residence and seized several electronic devices including a laptop and a personal iPhone 6S.
- During the search, investigators advised Defendant A of his Miranda rights; after waiving them, he acknowledged owning two cell phones and a laptop.
- During the search, after invoking counsel, Defendant A spontaneously stated that no one else was involved or knew about the conduct.
- The seized iPhone was named in a manner linking it to Defendant A and contained personal identifiers such as social media associations and photos tying the device to him.
- The iPhone was logged into a Kik account using the username “anonjohn1112”.
- In a Kik conversation with a user identified as “Brian,” Defendant A said, “Hi I have naughty thoughts about my teen daughters,” and claimed they were 13 and 17 years old.
- Defendant A sent images of two real minor girls he knew through personal acquaintance while falsely representing that they were his daughters.
- The two minors depicted in the images were not in fact Defendant A’s daughters.
- In another conversation, Defendant A stated a preference for children aged eight and higher.
- The iPhone’s camera roll contained at least 936 images constituting child pornography and 368 videos constituting child pornography.
- Two additional videos constituting child pornography were located within the Wickr application on the iPhone.
- The child pornography on the device included oral, vaginal, and anal penetration of minors, including prepubescent minors.
- Some child pornography involved toddlers and depictions of bondage and other sadistic acts.
- Numerous age-difficult or child exploitive images not categorized as child pornography were also located on the phone.
- One recovered image depicted a prepubescent female under five wearing a “Spongebob” tank top being orally penetrated by an adult male while holding his penis.
- One recovered image depicted a prepubescent female under five lying on her back while an adult male vaginally penetrated her.
- One recovered video showed a prepubescent female spreading her genitals and then being penetrated by an adult male.
- One recovered video showed multiple adult males orally and anally penetrating a prepubescent female.
- One Wickr video depicted a prepubescent female with her legs and hands bound with rope while being orally and digitally penetrated by an adult male.
- Another Wickr video depicted a minor female being orally penetrated by an adult male while he forced her head onto his penis.
- Defendant A knowingly received and distributed child pornography in addition to possessing it.
- Using Wickr with the username “johnanon1123,” Defendant A sent a child pornography video to a Wickr chat group containing 58 participants.
- Using the same Wickr account, Defendant A sent another child pornography video to an individual.
- The iPhone’s Notes application contained numerous links to Dropbox and Mega; most links indicated removal for banned content, and some led to active sites hosting adult and child pornography.
- Defendant A’s possession, distribution, and receipt of the child pornography was to further his own prurient interest.
- The child pornography traveled in and affected interstate and foreign commerce by a computer, including through the internet and the Wickr application.
- The child pornography involved actual minors engaged in sexual intercourse, sadistic and masochistic abuse, masturbation, and lascivious exhibition.
- Defendant A knew that the individuals depicted in the child pornography were minors and knew that the material constituted child pornography.
- Defendant A used Wickr, an encrypted messaging application, to send child pornography to others.
- Defendant A stated in a chat, "I like to trade stuff. Not just give."
- Defendant A sent messages claiming that his purported daughters "have taken my cock."
- Defendant A messaged about his “youngest in my wife's wedding dress.”
- Defendant A asked another user, “Do you ever peek at your daughters panties?”
- Defendant A claimed that his youngest “loves to play with herself,” stating he had caught her watching pornography and masturbating.
- Defendant A claimed that his purported daughters walk around half naked, stating, “Mine do all the time.”
- Investigators traced the IP address linked to each of the reported Kik accounts to Defendant A’s Verizon account.
- Investigators executed a search warrant at Defendant A’s residence on August 11, 2020 and seized his personal iPhone.
- Defendant A’s iPhone contained 936 images and 368 videos of child pornography, excluding ambiguous or age-difficult material and links.
- The iPhone also contained links to active and removed child pornography content hosted on file-sharing services.
- The investigation did not uncover evidence of hands-on sexual abuse by Defendant A.

- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened at the U.S. Capitol to certify the Electoral College vote.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m. on January 6, 2021, members of the House and Senate, including the Vice President, were evacuated and proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- On January 6, 2021, the exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to the public.
- Defendant A traveled from New Jersey to Washington, D.C. on January 5, 2021 to participate in rallies.
- At approximately 2:45 p.m. on January 6, 2021, Defendant A approached the west front of the Capitol while recording on her phone.
- As she approached the Capitol, Defendant A stated, "Let's go! Move forward! They cannot stop us all!"
- As she approached the Capitol, Defendant A said, "We're storming the Capitol right now!" and "This is battle. This is it. This is the battle."
- At approximately 2:47 p.m., Defendant A approached the Lower West Terrace tunnel entrance and waved rioters toward the entrance.
- At approximately 2:48 p.m., Defendant A entered the Lower West Terrace tunnel and moved toward the front to confront Metropolitan Police Department officers.
- Between approximately 2:48 p.m. and 2:51 p.m., while Defendant A was at the front of the crowd in the tunnel, rioters pushed against officers, pulled away two riot shields, and assaulted officers with poles and batons.
- At approximately 2:51 p.m., Defendant A backed away from the front but remained inside the tunnel.
- At approximately 2:52 p.m., Defendant A moved to the front of the crowd again and remained at the front for about four minutes while rioters used strobe lights and batons and poles against officers.
- At approximately 3:02 p.m., Defendant A exited the Lower West Terrace tunnel.
- While outside the tunnel, Defendant A yelled, "We need more men! Keep going! Keep pushing, men! We need men, not women! We need more helmets! More helmets!" while waving others toward the entrance.
- At approximately 3:09 p.m., Defendant A re-entered the Lower West Terrace entrance and exited again at approximately 3:10 p.m.
- At approximately 3:12 p.m., Defendant A joined a large group pushing into the tunnel and then exited again at approximately 3:14 p.m.
- On January 7, 2021, Defendant A texted that she was at the door "the entire time from 1 to 6" and called events a "set up."
- On January 7, 2021, Defendant A texted, "The first shot has been fired of the revolution."
- Defendant A admitted that she committed or attempted to commit an act to obstruct, impede, or interfere with one or more law enforcement officers and aided and abetted other rioters to do the same.
- The riot resulted in substantial damage to the U.S. Capitol requiring more than $1.4 million in repairs.
- Defendant A pleaded guilty to Civil Disorder and Aiding and Abetting in Count Two, in violation of 18 U.S.C. §§ 231(a)(3) and 2.
- The offense of conviction carries a maximum sentence of five years’ imprisonment.
- The offense carries a maximum supervised release term of up to three years.
- The offense carries a maximum fine of up to $250,000.
- A $100 special assessment applies to the felony conviction.
- Defendant A agreed to pay $2,000 in restitution to the Architect of the Capitol.
- Defendant A acknowledged that as of May 17, 2021 the Capitol riot caused approximately $1,495,326.55 in damage.
- Defendant A agreed to allow law enforcement to review her social media accounts and to be interviewed about the events before sentencing.
- The government agreed to move to dismiss the remaining counts of the indictment at sentencing.
- At the time of the plea agreement, the government stated that Defendant A had no prior criminal convictions based on information then available.
- Defendant A signed the plea agreement on October 4, 2022.
- Defendant A signed the Statement of Offense on October 4, 2022, acknowledging it as true and accurate.
- As of October 17, 2022, the government reported approximately $2,881,360.20 in losses arising from the Capitol attack.
- On November 10, 2021, a federal grand jury returned a superseding indictment charging Defendant A with six counts including 18 U.S.C. §§ 231(a)(3) and 2.
- On October 14, 2022, Defendant A was convicted of violating 18 U.S.C. §§ 231(a)(3) and 2 based on a guilty plea.
- Defendant A was present with members of the New Jersey Sons of Liberty on January 6, 2021.
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant A wore a Sons of Liberty hat, a skull-image scarf, and black tactical gloves.
- Several individuals near Defendant A were wearing tactical vests, tactical gloves, and carrying riot shields.
- Inside the tunnel, audio captured taser sounds and rioters shouting, "Grab his shield!" and "Oath breakers!" in videos recorded from Defendant A’s phone.
- In a video she recorded inside the tunnel, Defendant A said, "We're storming the Capitol. Just trying to hold the door," and "I gotta leave this to the men."
- Rioters in the tunnel grabbed shields from officers and shouted, "Push them back!" and "This is our house!"
- The government described that later assaults on two officers in the tunnel area occurred after Defendant A exited the tunnel.
- On November 22, 2020, using the alias "Ayla Wolf," Defendant A forwarded a message claiming the election was fraudulent.
- On November 25, 2020, Defendant A wrote, "Good things will come soon! As soon as Trump is confirmed."
- On December 30, 2020, Defendant A responded to a message about blocking ways into D.C. by saying, "We will park in the neighboring state and walk!!"
- On January 2, 2021, Defendant A messaged that some police in D.C. were not on their side and that "We have to watch our backs."
- On January 7, 2021, Defendant A messaged that it was "quite a battle" and "The first shot has been fired of the revolution!"
- On January 8, 2021, Defendant A messaged, "It was a set up, Im spent, and emotional, but ok!"
- On January 11, 2021, Defendant A messaged, "I just need a couple days of recovery and we will be back at it !!"
- On January 14, 2021, Defendant A messaged about moving photos off her phone to an internal hard drive.
- On January 20, 2021, Defendant A messaged, "Erase anything that lends anything to what is being said... We can do damage control later."
- On January 21, 2021, Defendant A messaged, "No I never went in!" and claimed she never came close to going in or saw violence from the people.
- In a January 7, 2021 YouTube video, Defendant A emerged from the tunnel waving rioters toward the entrance and saying, "More people. Keep going." and "We need more men!"
- In the same video, Defendant A later shouted "More helmets!" and re-entered the tunnel with other rioters.
- Video evidence shows that at 2:32 into one recording, a police shield was passed out of the tunnel back above the crowd.
- Video evidence shows individuals hitting officers with batons inside the tunnel.
- Video evidence shows a rioter spraying an aerosol substance toward officers inside the tunnel.
- Video evidence shows the crowd calling for a "shield wall" and a rioter attempting to remove an officer’s gas mask in the tunnel.
- Video evidence shows an officer with helmet number 4518 screaming with his head between doors in the tunnel.
- Video evidence shows police pushing the crowd out of the tunnel later in the recording.
- Video evidence shows Defendant A outside the tunnel wiping her eyes and coughing, consistent with exposure to a chemical irritant.
- Defendant A repeatedly shouted, "We need more men... keep going, keep pushing... not women," and "We need more helmets" while outside the tunnel.
- Defendant A used an iPhone with a pink case to record video, with the recording indicator visible on the screen.
- Defendant A wore a black baseball cap and pullover with a tree roots image and lettering including parts of the words consistent with "Sons of Liberty" and "Jersey," along with camouflage leggings.
- Law enforcement matched Defendant A’s DMV photograph to the person depicted in the videos exhorting the crowd at the Capitol.
- A New Jersey State Police Major who previously interacted with Defendant A identified her as the person depicted in the video.
- The Statement of Facts reports that a news article described rioters dragging an officer down steps and beating him, with the officer suffering a mild heart attack.
- The initial complaint sought probable cause for 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3) and § 2(a), 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1) and (2), and 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D) and (E).
- A U.S. Magistrate Judge attested the complaint by telephone on January 21, 2021.
- Defendant A did not enter the Capitol building interior, as described in sentencing materials.
- The defense states that while crouched outside the tunnel, Defendant A was struck by a baton, crushing a finger that required reconstructive surgery.
- The government reported it had no evidence of how or when Defendant A’s finger was injured.
- Defendant A is a mother from New Jersey, as referenced in sentencing filings.
- Defense materials state that Defendant A is the primary caregiver for two minor children with special needs.
- Defense materials state that Defendant A co-teaches at a homeschool cooperative attended by her children.

- The defendant pled guilty on December 14, 2021 to conspiracy to commit money laundering under 18 U.S.C. § 1956(h).
- An indictment was returned on February 4, 2021 charging one count of money laundering conspiracy and two substantive counts of concealment money laundering; the co-defendant was also charged with two counts under 18 U.S.C. § 1957.
- The underlying specified unlawful activity was bribery of a public official under Panamanian law.
- Odebrecht and Braskem pled guilty on December 21, 2016 in the Eastern District of New York to conspiracies related to the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act.
- The conspiracy period in this case ran approximately from August 2009 to September 2015.
- Between 2009 and 2012, approximately $28 million in bribe proceeds were received into Swiss shell-company bank accounts controlled by intermediaries for the benefit of a Panamanian official.
- At least approximately $19 million of the bribe proceeds were transferred through U.S. correspondent bank accounts in New York.
- The defendant and a co-defendant opened and managed secret bank accounts held in the names of shell companies in foreign jurisdictions to receive, transfer, conceal, and spend bribe payments.
- The defendant and a co-defendant served as signatories on Swiss shell-company bank accounts that initially received bribes.
- The defendant and a co-defendant authorized wire transfers through a structure of shell-company accounts to conceal and spend bribery proceeds.
- Many of the relevant financial transactions were in U.S. dollars and made through U.S. banks, including some located in New York.
- When questioned by foreign bankers about fund sources, the defendant and a co-defendant worked with an executive to produce fake contracts to assuage concerns.
- After U.S. dollar accounts were closed by foreign banks, bribe payments were routed to Euro‑denominated accounts via new intermediaries, resulting in approximately $30 million in additional bribes not included in the indictment.
- Immediately after the election of the Panamanian official, the defendant and a co-defendant offered lobbying services to an Odebrecht executive.
- The defendant and a co-defendant agreed to receive $6 million in exchange for specific official actions on three major public works tenders.
- They provided Swiss shell-company account information to begin receiving bribe payments.
- They contacted the Odebrecht executive to offer further lobbying services and leveraged close family credentials to obtain favorable official actions.
- They introduced the Odebrecht executive to key ministers appointed by the Panamanian official.
- They negotiated amounts of additional bribe payments and indicated plans to invest some funds in private projects.
- Approximately $9.5 million of bribe funds were invested in a cell phone service company.
- Millions of dollars in bribe proceeds were invested in portfolios of stocks and bonds.
- The co-defendant purchased a yacht for approximately $1.7 million using bribe funds.
- The co-defendant purchased a Miami condominium for approximately $1.3 million using bribe funds.
- Hundreds of thousands of dollars in bribe proceeds were used to pay the defendant’s personal credit card expenses.
- Odebrecht used a secret internal structure known as the Division of Structured Operations to account for and disburse bribe payments.
- As a result of bribes, Odebrecht secured and maintained large public works contracts in Panama, including the Panama Metro project.
- Panamanian authorities have estimated that Odebrecht projects generated hundreds of millions of dollars in overpayments from public funds.
- Government evidence includes foreign bank records for accounts used to receive, conceal, and transfer bribe payments linked to the scheme.
- Government evidence includes U.S. bank records showing use of U.S. correspondent banks and further transfers to conceal the source of funds.
- Government evidence includes U.S. bank records and corporate receipts showing bribe proceeds spent in the United States.
- Government evidence includes email and corporate records from Odebrecht corroborating the scheme.
- Government evidence includes witness statements from former Odebrecht employees and agents involved in the scheme.
- Government evidence includes records from a secret Odebrecht database used to communicate and track slush funds and bribes.
- Beginning in 2018, the defendant and co-defendant met with U.S. law enforcement regarding ongoing investigations related to Odebrecht.
- By June 2020, plea discussions had advanced to exchanging draft plea documents and logistics for pleas related to the conduct.
- In November 2018, the defendant and co-defendant were detained by immigration authorities in Florida and later released on bail while meetings with U.S. officials continued.
- On November 25, 2019, counsel in Panama requested bail for the defendant and co-defendant on pending Panamanian criminal charges related to Odebrecht and a separate “Blue Apple” matter.
- Bail in Panama was set at $2 million (Odebrecht case) on March 6, 2020 and $5 million (Blue Apple case) on May 20, 2020 for the two brothers.
- On June 22, 2020, the co-defendant posted a total of $7 million in bail in Panama in the two cases.
- On June 25, 2020, the defendant and co-defendant left the United States by boat to the Bahamas and boarded a private jet intended for Panama.
- The private jet was turned away from Panama due to COVID-19 restrictions, landed in Costa Rica on emergency approval, then in El Salvador.
- The defendant and co-defendant traveled by ride-hailing service from San Salvador to the Guatemala border and entered using invalid regional parliamentary credentials during border closures.
- They obtained emergency humanitarian authorization from Panama’s Ministry of Health to enter Panama during pandemic restrictions.
- On July 6, 2020, the defendant and co-defendant were arrested at La Aurora International Airport in Guatemala City while attempting to board a private jet to Panama.
- While incarcerated in Guatemala, the defendant and co-defendant were initially held in an apartment rather than a traditional prison facility, which was later changed due to escape concerns in July 2021.
- On November 8, 2021, a Guatemalan criminal court declared the formal extradition admissible by virtue of the defendant’s consent and suspended the procedure pending surrender.
- The Guatemalan court ordered provisional detention until the defendant’s delivery to the requesting state was effected.
- Two mobile phones were seized in Guatemala to be delivered with the defendant upon extradition.
- Cash of €2,425 and Q55, a regional parliamentary identification, and nine payment cards in the defendant’s name were placed at the Public Prosecutor’s disposal by the Guatemalan court.
- An official communication recorded that no diplomatic agent was registered under the defendant’s name in Guatemala.
- An electronic record from Guatemala’s Supreme Court noted no criminal record for the defendant in that jurisdiction at the time.
- The Guatemalan migration authority noted no current detention order but an active immigration alert for the defendant.
- On December 10, 2021, the defendant was extradited from Guatemala to the United States.
- On November 15, 2021, the co-defendant was removed to the United States following the completion of extradition proceedings.
- Guatemalan courts granted and affirmed the co-defendant’s extradition in May and June 2021, with final notification in October 2021.
- After arriving in the United States in December 2021, the defendant was detained at the Metropolitan Detention Center and initially quarantined per facility procedures.
- Movement restrictions at the detention facility related to COVID-19 and a national lockdown were lifted by February 17, 2022.
- During the plea hearing, the defendant admitted agreeing to wire funds that were proceeds of bribes to a Panamanian official and to cause further transactions to conceal the proceeds.
- The defendant admitted establishing offshore bank accounts in the names of shell companies and serving as a signatory on some of those accounts.
- In the allocution, the defendant stated that between 2009 and 2012, approximately $28 million in bribe proceeds were transferred into accounts for the benefit of a senior official in Panama, with more than $19 million transferred to U.S. banks, some in New York.
- The extradition record described that Odebrecht used shell companies and bank accounts, including accounts based in New York, to route bribe payments to foreign officials and their representatives.
- The extradition record noted that an agent in the United States was used to establish and operate foreign shell-company bank accounts for money laundering and bribery.
- The Panamanian official was a public official as defined in Panama’s Criminal Code, according to the extradition record.
- The extradition dossier summarized that the purpose of the Swiss shell-company accounts was to promote, assist, facilitate, and conceal bribe payments to secure an undue business advantage in Panama.
- The extradition request cited charges of conspiracy to commit money laundering under 18 U.S.C. § 1956(h) and substantive money laundering counts under 18 U.S.C. § 1956(a)(1)(B)(i) and § 1957.
- The defendant faced pending criminal charges in Panama related to the same conduct and fund transfers at the center of this case, according to defense submissions.
- Upon his return to the United States in early December 2021, the defendant was remanded to the Metropolitan Detention Center in Brooklyn.
- The defendant’s counsel reported that the defendant spent more than 17 months in Guatemalan custody before extradition.
- Defense submissions stated that the defendant agreed to forfeit nearly $19 million in funds related to the scheme and to assist in repatriating additional Swiss funds.
- The defendant’s guilty plea was accompanied by a written factual proffer attached to the plea agreement, per defense filings.
- The extradition proceedings in Guatemala referenced application of the 1903 U.S.-Guatemala Extradition Treaty and the United Nations Convention Against Corruption.
- The defendant admitted during his allocution that he caused funds to be wired into an account in the United States to facilitate bribery of a Panamanian government official.
- The government learned the defendant and co-defendant obtained invalid credentials from the Central American Parliament as part of their attempted flight.

- A one-count federal Indictment was returned on February 3, 2021, charging threatening to murder a United States official on or about January 21–22, 2021.
- A criminal complaint was filed on January 27, 2021, charging threatening to murder a United States official on or about January 21, 2021.
- On March 7, 2022, the defendant pled guilty to the Indictment pursuant to a written plea agreement.
- The offense of conviction is a violation of 18 U.S.C. § 115(a)(1), threatening to murder a United States official.
- The statutory maximum penalty for the offense is 10 years’ imprisonment, a $250,000 fine, up to 3 years of supervised release, and a $100 special assessment.
- The defendant agreed to waive the right to challenge venue in the Middle District of Pennsylvania.
- The government agreed not to bring other criminal charges directly arising out of the offense, but reserved the ability to bring criminal tax charges.
- The defendant has been in custody since his arrest on January 22, 2021.
- On December 7–8, 2021, the court held hearings on pretrial motions in this case.
- On January 10, 2022, the court ruled on the defendant’s motions.
- Sentencing was scheduled for June 10, 2022.
- The plea agreement requires payment of a $100 special assessment by the date of sentencing.
- The plea agreement requires the defendant to fully disclose assets, submit a financial statement, and consent to credit reports to facilitate collection of any fine or restitution.
- If imprisoned and fined or owing restitution, the defendant agreed to participate in the Bureau of Prisons’ Inmate Financial Responsibility Program.
- As a condition of supervised release or probation, the court must order DNA collection if authorized by law.
- The defendant agreed to forfeit three firearms seized in the investigation: a Ruger AR-556 rifle, a Smith & Wesson Bodyguard .380 pistol, and a Smith & Wesson Airweight 442 .38 Special.
- The firearms subject to forfeiture were in the custody and control of the Pennsylvania State Police.
- The defendant consented to the destruction of items seized during the investigation at the government’s discretion.
- The defendant waived the right to appeal the conviction and sentence, with the government retaining its own right to appeal.
- The defendant waived collateral attacks on the conviction and sentence, except claims of ineffective assistance of counsel.
- On January 21, 2021, Ex‑Wife A contacted Cornwall Township Police reporting the defendant said he was driving to Virginia to drop off birthday presents for his granddaughter and then threatened to kill Democratic U.S. Senators.
- Ex‑Wife A observed the defendant place an AR rifle and a box of ammunition into his SUV before driving away.
- Ex‑Wife A petitioned for a 302 mental health warrant through the Lebanon County Crisis Center, which was granted.
- Police issued a be-on-the-lookout (BOLO) for the defendant and his vehicle.
- Later that evening, Pennsylvania State Police took the defendant into custody without incident on the 302 warrant at a local Sheetz gas station and transported him to Chambersburg Hospital for evaluation.
- A search of the defendant's vehicle yielded two handguns, an AR rifle, ammunition, and magazines.
- Around 2:00 a.m. on January 22, 2021, a nurse at Chambersburg Hospital contacted the FBI tip line to report statements the defendant made while hospitalized.
- An FBI agent interviewed the defendant at the hospital at approximately 4:18 a.m. on January 22, 2021.
- Following two psychiatric consults, clinicians determined there was not enough evidence to uphold an involuntary commitment and the defendant was discharged.
- Upon discharge from the hospital, the defendant was arrested by Pennsylvania State Police and charged with terroristic threats under state law.
- According to the government’s account, on January 21, 2021, the defendant loaded his car with several firearms, scores of magazines, and hundreds of rounds of ammunition.
- According to a hospital nurse account cited by the government, around 11:00 p.m. the defendant said he wanted to arrive early enough to kill Democratic Senators on their lawns when they were leaving for work.
- According to the same account, the defendant stated he had compiled a list of names and addresses of his targets.
- Hospital staff observed that the defendant was not under the influence of alcohol or drugs, was alert and oriented, and his speech was not slurred.
- The defendant denied to doctors and to the FBI that he made threats to kill Democratic Senators and denied homicidal or suicidal ideations.
- Defense materials state the defendant repeated threats about public officials to Nurse A at approximately 11:00 p.m.
- At approximately 11:51 p.m., during an interview with Nurse B, the defendant said, “Yea I would kill my wife but right now maybe not,” which the defense characterizes as sarcasm.
- The defense asserts that no list of senators’ addresses was found and that no such list existed.
- Items seized from the defendant’s briefcase included an index card listing “guns, ammo, dope, tools, and meds,” local newspapers, other notes, a phone charger, and adult diapers; the card’s other side listed shopping items including a birthday card for Ex‑Wife A.
- The defense states that two of the three weapons and most ammunition were stored in bins in the back of the car along with targets and protective gear consistent with target shooting.
- The defense states the defendant routinely carried two of the three weapons found in his possession.
- The defense objected to PSR language alleging the defendant intended to commit “suicide by cop,” asserting he never used that phrase.
- According to the defense, police interpreted the defendant’s statement that he would not be coming home to mean he would commit “suicide by cop.”
- A government filing asserts medical records reflect the defendant previously disclosed he tried to hurt himself.
- A government filing reports the nurse challenged that arriving in Alexandria at that hour would be very early and the defendant responded he wanted to “get there on time.”
- A government filing states the defendant was mindful of Washington, D.C. area traffic when discussing his plans.
- The defense asserts Ex‑Wife A never reported that the defendant threatened to kill her at any point during initial reporting.
- The defense states the defendant was repeatedly asked about suicidal thoughts at the hospital and he repeatedly denied them.
- The defense characterizes the defendant’s hospital remark about killing his wife as sarcasm made during a medical interview and not an actual threat.
- The defense contends the defendant planned to visit his son and granddaughter in Alexandria, Virginia and to go target shooting with his son.
- The plea agreement reflects that sentencing facts and guideline-related issues would be determined by the court after briefing and hearings, with a preponderance standard and relaxed evidence rules at sentencing.
- The defendant waived the right to withdraw his guilty plea if the court imposed a sentence with which he was dissatisfied.
- The defendant acknowledged that the status of any professional license he held was not protected by the plea agreement.
- On May 12, 2021, the defendant submitted a verified statement of resignation to the Supreme Court of Pennsylvania and was disbarred on consent.
- On April 27, 2021, a psychological evaluation concluded the defendant’s risk to the public is low and the events were an isolated incident exacerbated by alcohol.
- The defendant reported being “out of it” for seven to eight days surrounding the time of the offense.
- The defendant reported drinking two glasses of wine daily after work and being prescribed medical marijuana in 2020.
- Approximately 30 years earlier, the defendant contemplated suicide after an argument with his then‑wife.
- In 2019, the defendant was diagnosed with prostate cancer and underwent surgery; he became cancer‑free in 2021.
- While detained pretrial, the defendant was hospitalized seven times for either a stroke or a heart condition.
- On October 7, 2021, a scalp lesion was removed and diagnosed as squamous cell carcinoma in‑situ.
- On October 21 and 24, 2021, the defendant was treated for rapid heart rate and dizziness; he was admitted on October 25, underwent cardiac catheterization, and discharged on October 27, 2021.
- From November 19 to November 29, 2021, the defendant was admitted for shortness of breath, irregular heartbeat, and acute bronchitis due to COVID‑19 infection.
- The defendant has multiple ongoing medical conditions including sleep apnea, osteoarthritis, anemia, atrial fibrillation, hypertension, and others.
- The defendant is prescribed various medications for his health conditions, including cardiac, respiratory, and gastrointestinal medications.
- Defense materials state the defendant had no prior criminal history and this incident was his first arrest.
- Defense materials describe the defendant as 72 years old at the time of sentencing proceedings.
- The defendant earned a Bachelor of Arts in History in 1971 and a Juris Doctor in 1974.
- The defendant served as a borough council Solicitor for over 28 years.
- The defendant was a Pennsylvania Licensed Title Insurance Agent from 1980 to 2012 and a Notary Public since 1993.
- The defense submitted multiple character reference letters asserting the offense conduct was out of character for the defendant.

- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened at the U.S. Capitol to certify the Electoral College vote.
- By approximately 1:30 p.m. on January 6, the House and Senate adjourned to separate chambers to resolve an objection.
- The Vice President was present and presiding during the January 6 proceedings before evacuation.
- U.S. Capitol Police maintained permanent and temporary security barriers around the Capitol, and only authorized persons with identification were permitted inside.
- On January 6, 2021, the exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to the public.
- U.S. Capitol Police attempted to keep the crowd away from the Capitol building and proceedings on January 6.
- Shortly after 2:00 p.m. on January 6, individuals in the crowd forced entry into the Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting police.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m. on January 6, members of Congress were instructed to evacuate and the joint session was suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- The Vice President remained in the U.S. Capitol from evacuation until the sessions resumed on January 6.
- As of July 7, 2023, approximate losses from the January 6 events were reported as $2,923,080.05.
- The Metropolitan Police Department also reported losses of approximately $629,056 related to January 6.
- On January 5, 2021, Defendant A traveled from Florida to Washington, D.C. to attend a political rally.
- On January 6, Defendant A attended the Rally at the Ellipse and then walked to the U.S. Capitol.
- On January 6, there was a restricted security perimeter around the Capitol grounds and building, with signs and barriers such as bicycle racks and snow fencing.
- Defendant A walked on the West Front of the Capitol and observed chaos as rioters chanted and fought police.
- Defendant A climbed onto the Inaugural stage and climbed the risers facing the police line on the Upper West Terrace shortly before 2:50 p.m.
- At approximately 2:50 p.m., Defendant A scaled down the wall of the Inaugural Stage onto the Upper West Terrace and approached a line of MPD officers.
- Defendant A stood on a bike rack, spat at officers, and threw what appeared to be a water bottle at officers on the Upper West Terrace.
- Defendant A joined other rioters on the Upper West Terrace steps in close proximity to the MPD line.
- Defendant A spit at an MPD officer at approximately 2:51 p.m. on the Upper West Terrace.
- After spitting at an officer, Defendant A pointed and shouted at officers, including saying, “You know it was fucking rigged … and you still defend it. You guys are fucked.”
- Defendant A moved away from the MPD line only after an officer extended a can of OC spray in his direction around 14:51:46.
- Defendant A continued walking near the MPD line after the initial confrontation and was instructed by an officer to move back at approximately 14:53:00.
- At approximately 3:25 p.m., Defendant A filmed himself grabbing the North Door of the Capitol while officers attempted to secure the building.
- Defendant A posted a video of himself at the North Door to his Instagram account and posted a photo of a metal piece of the lock mechanism from that door with the caption, “got some memorobioia, did it myself.”
- At approximately 3:49 p.m., Defendant A approached the East Rotunda Doors, where windows were shattered and alarms were ringing, and filmed while officers were behind the doors.
- While filming at the East Rotunda Doors, Defendant A verbally insulted police and told them to retreat.
- Defendant A gave a podcast interview after January 6 in which he admitted seeing rioters fighting officers on the stairway to the Inaugural Stage and seeing rioters get pepper sprayed.
- In the same podcast interview, Defendant A admitted he climbed scaffolding and that officers threatened him with OC spray.
- In the podcast interview, Defendant A acknowledged entering the Capitol grounds and said, “now it’s criminal.”
- On January 6, 2021, Witness 1 alerted the FBI that Defendant A’s social media accounts displayed participation in the Capitol events.
- Witness 1 provided a screengrab showing a metal piece from the Capitol with a caption about obtaining “memorabilia.”
- On January 7, 2021, a mutual acquaintance (Witness 2) captured and emailed the FBI a copy of an Instagram Story video posted by Defendant A.
- One clip from the Instagram Story showed Defendant A at the bottom level of the west side steps within a large crowd near the Inauguration scaffolding on Capitol grounds.
- The last clip of the Story showed Defendant A at a doorway to the Capitol, filming as he struggled with Capitol Police over opening a door, with his left hand visible on the door.
- By January 7, 2021, many of Defendant A’s rally and Capitol-related posts had been removed from the Instagram account reviewed by the FBI.
- On January 7, 2021, Defendant A posted a public apology on Facebook for his actions at the Capitol and stated he would get off social media and cooperate with investigations.
- On January 7, 2021, during a phone call with the FBI, Defendant A admitted he attended the protests in Washington, D.C. on January 6 and had returned to Broward County, Florida.
- During that phone interview, Defendant A soon became uncooperative and said he had no information to provide.
- Shortly after the FBI interview, Defendant A posted on Facebook that he had spoken to an FBI agent and believed he had been cleared.
- On January 8, 2021, the FBI used open-source searches and Florida DMV records to identify Defendant A and obtain a driver’s license photo.
- On January 14, 2021, Witness 1 confirmed Defendant A’s identity from the driver’s license photo and provided additional images tying him to the Capitol door incident and a metal item he called “memorabilia.”
- A criminal complaint was sworn by telephone on January 15, 2021, in support of charges related to Defendant A’s conduct at the Capitol.
- On August 23, 2023, an indictment charged Defendant A with multiple offenses including 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3), 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1) (Count Two), 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1) (Count Three), 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(2) (Count Four), 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(4) (Count Five), 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D) (Count Six), and 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(F) (Count Seven).
- Before trial, the government moved to dismiss Count Five (18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(4)) and Count Seven (40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(F)).
- At trial, Defendant A was convicted of 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1) (Count Two) and 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1) (Count Three).
- Counts Two and Three are Class A misdemeanors.
- On each of Counts Two and Three, Defendant A faces up to one year of imprisonment, one year of supervised release, one year of probation, a fine up to $100,000, and a $25 special assessment.
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant A recorded and posted videos and photos from the Capitol to his social media accounts.
- Defendant A possessed and displayed on social media a piece of the locking mechanism from a Capitol door on January 6.
- The Capitol and its grounds were restricted on January 6 because U.S. Secret Service protectees were visiting.
- Defendant A used the Instagram handle associated with the username “Skilils” to post content from January 6.
- An Instagram Story later captured by Witness 2 showed Defendant A filming himself at a Capitol doorway while struggling with police over opening the door.
- In a Facebook post on January 7, Defendant A announced he would leave social media for the foreseeable future and cooperate with investigations.
- In the same January 7 Facebook post, Defendant A apologized to friends, family, and the public for his actions at the Capitol.
- Later on January 7, Defendant A posted that he believed he had been cleared after speaking to an FBI agent.
- The FBI observed that many of Defendant A’s Instagram posts and videos from January 6 had been removed by January 7.
- The Instagram Story and other social media content connected Defendant A to the West Front steps and to a Capitol doorway on January 6.
- The government’s sentencing memorandum describes Defendant A spitting at officers and throwing an item at them on the Upper West Terrace.
- Defendant A was present at multiple breach points of the Capitol on January 6, including the North Door and East Rotunda Doors.
- The U.S. Capitol building and grounds were locked or otherwise secured while proceedings were underway on January 6.
- National news coverage on January 6 depicted evidence of numerous individuals inside the Capitol without authority.
- Defendant A’s Instagram post showed a piece of metal from the Capitol labeled as “memorabilia.”
- Defendant A’s actions on January 6 included climbing scaffolding near the Inaugural Stage, according to his later statements.
- According to the PSR summary in the sentencing memorandum, Defendant A had no criminal history and had complied with pretrial release since March 2022.
- The PSR indicated Defendant A regularly sees a psychiatrist, has a bipolar disorder diagnosis, and has a history of mental health hospitalization, and was found competent in 2023.
- The sentencing memorandum notes an understanding that Defendant A’s Instacart employment account was deactivated.
- During a confrontation on the Upper West Terrace, an officer used an OC spray can in Defendant A’s direction, prompting him to move away.
- Defendant A referred to police as “fucking pussies” and told them to “retreat” while filming at the East Rotunda Doors.
- Defendant A used his phone to film at multiple locations around the Capitol on January 6, including the North Door and East Rotunda Doors.
- The government described Defendant A as continuing to antagonize and distract officers by moving near their line on the Upper West Terrace after initial contact.
- The government’s memorandum indicates that Congress and its proceedings were disrupted by the events at the Capitol on January 6.

- Defendant A traveled from Colorado to the Washington, D.C., area on January 5, 2021.
- On the morning of January 6, 2021, Defendant A attended a rally at the Ellipse before going to the U.S. Capitol.
- At approximately 2:30 p.m. on January 6, 2021, Defendant A unlawfully entered the restricted grounds of the U.S. Capitol.
- Defendant A wore a red, white, and blue jacket with the letters "USA" on the back while on Capitol grounds.
- At about 2:39 p.m., Defendant A unlawfully entered the U.S. Capitol building through a hallway on the West side.
- When entering the Capitol, Defendant A wore a light-blue neck gaiter and sunglasses along with the USA jacket.
- Defendant A knew he had no permission to enter the Capitol when he did so on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant A believed he and others were trying to obstruct, influence, and impede Congress’s certification of the Electoral College vote.
- Defendant A acted to stop or delay the Congressional proceeding and admits that he did so.
- Defendant A states he accomplished the interference by intimidating or coercing government personnel supporting the proceeding.
- At 2:39 p.m., Defendant A entered the Rotunda and began taking photographs and videos of his surroundings.
- At 2:41 p.m., Defendant A entered the Ohio Clock Room hallway and stood with others in front of a line of law enforcement officers preventing advancement.
- While in the Ohio Clock Room hallway, Defendant A filmed officers and did not leave despite their presence.
- Defendant A yelled profanities about congressional leaders while in the Ohio Clock Room hallway.
- At 2:51 p.m., Defendant A reentered the Rotunda, continued taking photos and videos, and interacted with other unlawful occupants.
- At 3:02 p.m., officers began herding individuals out of the Rotunda, but Defendant A did not exit and moved toward the center while recording.
- At 3:08 p.m., Defendant A jerked his elbow, shaking officers off as they attempted to remove him from the Rotunda.
- At 3:13 p.m., Defendant A moved to the East Rotunda Doors area and remained inside while chemical irritants were deployed.
- Defendant A exited the Capitol building at approximately 3:30 p.m. through the East Rotunda Doors.
- On January 6 and 7, 2021, Defendant A destroyed his phone and memory card that contained recordings from inside the Capitol.
- On January 6–7, 2021, Defendant A discarded his red, white, and blue “USA” jacket worn during the offense.
- Later in January 2021, after learning law enforcement was seeking his arrest, Defendant A arranged to and did surrender.
- Defendant A self-surrendered on January 14, 2021.
- Open-source video showed an individual in the Capitol Rotunda wearing a dark jacket with “USA” on the back; law enforcement identified the person as Defendant A.
- Defendant A’s identity was corroborated by comparing a DMV photo to images from the Capitol; his listed height is approximately 6 feet, 6 inches.
- Images showed an Olympic patch on the front of Defendant A’s jacket while inside the Capitol.
- On February 10, 2021, a grand jury indicted Defendant A on seven counts, including 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2) and 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3).
- On September 29, 2021, Defendant A pled guilty to obstruction of an official proceeding, 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2) (Count Two).
- The statutory maximum penalty for 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2) as stated in the plea agreement is 20 years’ imprisonment.
- The plea agreement states that a fine of up to $250,000 and up to 3 years of supervised release may be imposed for 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2).
- Defendant A agreed to pay a $100 special assessment for the felony conviction.
- Defendant A agreed to pay $2,000 in restitution to the Department of the Treasury as part of the plea.
- The plea agreement notes estimated damages to the U.S. Capitol of approximately $1,495,326.55 as of May 17, 2021.
- As of July 7, 2023, the government reported approximately $2,923,080.05 in losses from the Capitol attack.
- The plea agreement includes a cooperation provision requiring Defendant A to meet with law enforcement, provide information, and testify when requested.
- The plea agreement states that sentencing may be delayed until cooperation is completed so the Court has all relevant information.
- Defendant A consented to proceedings by video or teleconference under the CARES Act as stated in the plea agreement.
- Defendant A had no criminal convictions at the time referenced in the plea agreement’s criminal history discussion.
- On January 6, 2021, the U.S. Capitol was restricted to authorized persons, with barriers and security in place; the exterior plaza was closed to the public.
- A joint session of Congress convened at approximately 1:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, to certify the Electoral College vote.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m. on January 6, 2021, members of the House and Senate, including the Vice President, were evacuated from their chambers.
- Congressional proceedings were effectively suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, due to the unlawful breach.
- On January 6, 2021, individuals in the crowd forced their way through barricades and into the Capitol, including by breaking windows and assaulting police.
- The Vice President remained in the Capitol from evacuation until the session resumed around 8:00 p.m.
- The government described that more than a hundred law enforcement officers were injured during the January 6 attack.
- Body-worn camera and open-source video depicted Defendant A inside the Capitol for nearly an hour on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant A at times wore a beanie with political text, pulled a bandana over his mouth, and wore sunglasses while inside the Capitol.
- Capitol CCTV shows Defendant A entering through the Upper West Terrace Door at approximately 2:39 p.m.
- In the Senate hallway, Defendant A led chants using profanities about congressional leaders while near a police line protecting the chamber.
- Defendant A remained in the Rotunda after officers began clearing it, used his phone to record, and moved toward the center rather than exit.
- Defendant A sang the national anthem with other rioters in the Rotunda while officers attempted to clear the area.
- When officers tried to remove him, Defendant A ripped his elbow away and yelled statements like “Take it easy!” and “Why do we have to leave?”
- In the East Rotunda Lobby, Defendant A clapped and encouraged other rioters and remained inside despite alarms and chemical irritants.
- While in the East Rotunda Lobby, Defendant A took a selfie with another rioter and told him, “you’re the man, dude!”
- Officers used chemical irritants and a surge of personnel to finally clear Defendant A and others from the East Rotunda Lobby.
- After leaving the Capitol, Defendant A threw his “USA” jacket in a trash can on the way back to his hotel.
- Defendant A destroyed his phone with a hammer soon after January 6, and the government did not recover his recordings.
- By summer 2021, Defendant A met and proffered with the government, describing his conduct, and pled guilty in September 2021.
- Defendant A has cooperated with the investigation for nearly three years, meeting repeatedly to describe events and persons around him on January 6.
- Defendant A admitted during debriefing that he destroyed his phone and discarded his jacket, providing information the government did not otherwise possess.
- The government reported that Defendant A remained on release with conditions since his self-surrender and agreed to continue sentencing to facilitate cooperation.
- On January 6, 2021, the restricted Capitol grounds included areas cordoned off due to a visit by a Secret Service–protected person (the Vice President).
- The indictment included counts for entering and remaining in a restricted building or grounds (18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1)), disorderly or disruptive conduct in a restricted building or grounds (18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(2)), and several 40 U.S.C. § 5104 offenses.
- A Capitol Police officer described Defendant A during the clearing of the Rotunda as not listening to orders to leave and as passive resistant and obstructing the clearing effort.
- Selected open-source video and Capitol CCTV show Defendant A’s jacket bearing “USA” and an Olympic patch while inside the Rotunda.
- The joint session proceedings could not resume until every unauthorized occupant left and the building was secured.
- Officers deployed chemical irritants in the Rotunda and East Rotunda areas during the clearing operations while Defendant A remained inside.
- Defendant A is approximately 6'6" tall and over 250 pounds, as referenced in the record describing his physical presence.
- While near the Senate Chamber hallway, Defendant A stood close to a police line that was between the mob and the chamber doors.
- Defendant A asked aloud why he and others had to leave while officers were attempting to clear the Rotunda.
- Defendant A agreed in his plea to fully disclose assets and provide a completed financial disclosure form related to restitution.
- Defendant A agreed that restitution and any fines imposed would be due and payable immediately and subject to enforcement.
- Defendant A remained in the East Rotunda Lobby approximately 17 minutes before finally exiting the Capitol.
- Officers ultimately sealed the East Rotunda doors after clearing rioters, using benches and stanchions to reinforce them.
- The sentencing hearing was scheduled for December 1, 2023, at 4:00 PM before a U.S. District Judge.
- Defendant A’s plea agreement included waivers of certain trial and appeal rights as part of the resolution.
