- The government reported that Defendant A continued to film and post during his movement toward and into the Capitol, including posts titled "made it in" and a self-facing clip.
- The government stated that while in Senator A’s office, other rioters were present and smoking also occurred there that day.
- The defense memorandum states that Defendant A entered the Capitol at 3:09 p.m., walked around for 17 minutes, and left by 3:26 p.m.
- The defense memorandum acknowledges Defendant A lit a marijuana cigarette and posted a video of himself on social media while inside the Capitol.
- The defense memorandum states that Defendant A traveled to Washington, D.C. alone and was not affiliated with any domestic terrorist group.
- The defense memorandum asserts Defendant A deeply regrets his decision to enter the Capitol and to smoke a marijuana cigarette inside.
- The government reported that after pleading guilty, Defendant A provided a full account of his conduct in a debrief interview.
- The plea agreement notes at least one prior conviction in 2014 for marijuana possession resulting in a three-year probation sentence.
- The government reported that another prior conviction for marijuana possession in 2013 resulted in a criminal history point.
- The Statement of Offense records that the crowd entering the Capitol had not submitted to security screenings or weapons checks before entering.
- The Statement of Offense records that the riot resulted in the suspension of Congressional proceedings until the building was cleared and secured.
- The Statement of Facts includes a video where Defendant A says, "Capitol building smoking with all my people!" while filming himself in a selfie style.
- The Statement of Facts shows Defendant A posted from near the Capitol scaffolding prior to entering, with the caption asserting ownership of the building.
- The Statement of Facts shows Defendant A posted a video walking toward the Capitol with a political flag visible ahead of him.
- The defense memorandum states Defendant A no longer uses marijuana and has undertaken educational and fitness activities since his arrest.

- A joint session of Congress convened at the U.S. Capitol on January 6, 2021, to certify the Electoral College vote, and the Vice President was present and presiding.
- On January 6, 2021, the Capitol’s exterior plaza was closed to the public and only authorized persons with identification were allowed inside.
- Shortly after 2:00 p.m., individuals in the crowd forced entry into the Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m., members of the House and Senate were instructed to evacuate, and proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- The riot resulted in substantial damage to the Capitol requiring more than $1.4 million in repairs as reflected in the Statement of Offense.
- As of October 17, 2022, the approximate losses from the Capitol attack were reported as $2,881,360.20.
- Defendant A resides in Tarrant County, Texas, and accepted an invitation from Codefendant B to fly to Washington, D.C., to attend the January 6 rally.
- On January 5, 2021, Defendant A and others flew from Texas to Manassas, Virginia, and checked into a District of Columbia hotel.
- On the morning of January 6, 2021, the group walked to the rally on the Ellipse and later returned to their hotel due to cold weather and sore feet.
- From the hotel, the group watched news coverage showing rioters confronting police and breaching the Capitol.
- Defendant A sent messages from the hotel stating “Patriots just stormed the capital [sic] building” and “Broke through the baracades [sic].”
- At approximately 2:27 p.m., Defendant A received a forwarded tweet describing rioters breaching the Capitol and fighting police.
- Defendant A returned to the Capitol, approaching the east front and the East Rotunda doors.
- Defendant A approached the East Rotunda door at approximately 3:21 p.m., before the two companions with her.
- When Defendant A entered at the East Rotunda door, building alarms were sounding and windows in the door were visibly broken.
- Approximately 26 seconds after Defendant A’s entry, two companions entered together through the same doors and then exited around 3:23 p.m.
- By 3:26 p.m., Defendant A had entered the Rotunda and shortly thereafter followed officers and exited, crossing the Statuary Hall connector at about 3:27 p.m.
- After exiting the Capitol, Defendant A stated on a recording, “I went into the f***ing Capitol... I made my movement. I stood my ground.”
- Video recovered from devices captured Defendant A calling police officers “traitors,” “sheep,” and “pathetic,” and saying, “You want a f***ing revolution, it’ll happen.”
- Defendant A urged others near the Capitol to resist dispersal, shouting “Stand your ground!” and admonishing the crowd to remain.
- At a press enclosure, Defendant A shouted at media and encouraged destruction, including yelling “burn it!” and “light it up!”
- Defendant A kicked media equipment and threw a piece of equipment on the ground at the press enclosure.
- Later on January 6, Defendant A stated on a livestream, “I stormed the Capitol,” and agreed that what happened at the press enclosure “was not a pretty sight.”
- On January 6, Defendant A also claimed on the broadcast, “There was no vandalism. All we did was push the doors open,” and complained about the Vice President’s actions.
- On January 7, 2021, Defendant A and her group flew back to Texas.
- On January 15, 2021, FBI agents interviewed Defendant A at her residence in Texas, and the interview was recorded.
- During the FBI interview, Defendant A said she approached the Capitol from the Supreme Court side and was pushed by crowds through doors into a lobby.
- Defendant A told the FBI that due to being crushed by the crowd, she requested a Capitol Police Officer’s assistance to exit the building and then left.
- Defendant A told the FBI she did not condone violence, claimed she was told to exit by a Capitol Police officer, and said she believed she was willingly allowed into the building.
- Defendant A declined to identify a companion to agents and falsely claimed she could not recognize a photograph shown to her of that person.
- In the days before January 6, Defendant A anticipated potential violence and sent a January 4 message: “we have to stop the steal” and “most likely s*** will go down.”
- On January 4, Defendant A asked her group about concealed carry for protection and later relayed advice to leave guns at home.
- The group accepted an offer of “bulletproof backpacks” for additional protection before traveling.
- Open-source video and surveillance showed Defendant A inside the Capitol near the Rotunda doors and walking through crowds.
- On January 6, Defendant A responded on Facebook that she went “a little further than” the steps and described doors closed behind them and guns drawn.
- In the same exchange, Defendant A wrote that the National Guard “didn’t move” and referenced a woman being shot before the crowd “raised hell.”
- On January 4, 2021, Defendant A messaged that she, a companion, and another would be “in the center” and anticipated possible violence.
- Defendant A agreed in a plea to pay $500 in restitution to the Architect of the Capitol and to pay restitution to the Associated Foreign Press if identifiable damages existed.
- Defendant A agreed in the plea to allow law enforcement to interview her about January 6 events prior to sentencing if requested.
- Defendant A pleaded guilty on August 18, 2022, to engaging in disorderly or disruptive conduct in a restricted building or grounds, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(2).
- In her plea, Defendant A admitted the elements that she engaged in disorderly or disruptive conduct in a restricted area with intent to impede or disrupt official functions.
- The offense of conviction carries a maximum sentence of one year imprisonment, up to a $100,000 fine, and up to one year of supervised release, with a $25 special assessment.
- The government agreed to dismiss the remaining counts of the Information at sentencing as part of the plea.
- Based on information available at the time of the plea, Defendant A had no prior criminal convictions.
- On January 28, 2021, a complaint affidavit alleged probable cause that Defendant A violated 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1) and (2) and 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D) and (G).
- Defendant A self-surrendered to United States Marshals in the Northern District of Texas on February 1, 2021.
- On June 7, 2021, a second Superseding Information charged Defendant A with 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1) and (2) and 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D), (F), and (G).
- On August 15, 2022, Defendant A signed and adopted the Statement of Offense as true and accurate.
- On August 15, 2022, Defendant A signed the plea agreement acceptance page.
- During a post-exit recording on January 6, a companion stated Defendant A was first among them to enter the Capitol and that chemical spray was used.
- Surveillance and open-source recordings showed Defendant A entering as alarms sounded and passing broken glass panes at the East Rotunda doors.
- Defendant A’s January 5 social media activity documented the group’s travel by private aircraft and arrival at Manassas Regional Airport.
- Defendant A acknowledged awareness of a companion’s social media videos and said the words used were “very bad” and made the group look “bad.”
- Defendant A told investigators she observed the Capitol door had been destroyed before her entry, though she said she saw no one destroying anything.
- During planning communications, Defendant A discussed protective measures such as covering faces and remaining with a group.
- The plea agreement set out that Defendant A would pay $500 restitution to the Architect of the Capitol in light of approximately $1,495,326.55 damage as of May 17, 2021.
- The complaint affidavit explained that the restricted area under 18 U.S.C. § 1752 included the Capitol and grounds due to a Secret Service protectee’s presence.
- The government later reported that more than one hundred police officers were injured during the Capitol attack.
- Defendant A’s brokerage firm stated on January 7, 2021, that she was no longer associated with the company following her posts.
- After January 6, Defendant A received hostile and threatening messages, including exhortations that she should harm herself and references to prison.
- Defendant A initially walked toward the Capitol after the Ellipse rally but returned to the hotel before later deciding to go back to the Capitol area.
- Defendant A told law enforcement she saw what she believed were National Guard members inside and people who appeared to be in custody.
- Defendant A claimed in an interview that officers were “very sweet” and that she was directed to an exit by an officer.
- An early Facebook post by a group member on January 5 showed the group traveling by a small private aircraft with Defendant A on board.
- Upon entering the Capitol, Defendant A proceeded toward the Rotunda while her two companions remained behind and later exited without her.
- Defendant A stated on a livestream later on January 6 that chemical agents such as mace or pepper spray had been used inside near the entrance.
- The Statement of Offense identifies that Defendant A’s conduct impeded or disrupted the orderly conduct of government business or official functions.

- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Count One charging conspiracy to possess with intent to distribute and distribute 400 grams or more of fentanyl and 100 grams or more of heroin.
- The charged offense cited 21 U.S.C. §§ 846, 841(a), 841(b)(1)(A), and 841(b)(1)(B).
- For Count One, the statutory penalty includes a term of imprisonment up to life and a mandatory minimum term of 10 years.
- For Count One, the statutory supervised release term is at least 5 years and up to life.
- For Count One, a fine of up to $10,000,000 may be imposed.
- A $100 special assessment applies to the count of conviction.
- The plea agreement was docketed on October 1, 2021 in the Southern District of Florida case.
- The plea agreement reflects signatures dated September 10, 2021 by Defendant A, defense counsel, and an Assistant United States Attorney.
- The parties jointly recommended that the sentence run concurrent to a sentence imposed in a supervised release violation in a prior case.
- The plea included a Stipulation of Facts admitting the government could prove the conspiracy to involve 400 grams or more of fentanyl and 100 grams or more of heroin.
- During the charged period, the conspiracy involved distribution of heroin and fentanyl in the Southern District of Florida.
- Defendant A’s role included relaying customer orders for drugs to Co-Defendant A, providing addresses for drug shipments, retrieving drug parcels, and collecting payments.
- In October 2019, DEA received information from a confidential source that identified Defendant A as selling heroin in Palm Beach County.
- On October 23, 2019, an undercover agent and a confidential source purchased approximately one ounce of heroin from Defendant A for $2,000.
- On November 15, 2019, an undercover agent and a confidential source received a free sample of approximately one gram of heroin from Defendant A.
- On November 21, 2019, an undercover agent and a confidential source purchased approximately one ounce of heroin from Defendant A.
- On December 4, 2019, an undercover agent and a confidential source purchased approximately one ounce of heroin from Defendant A.
- On December 18, 2019, an undercover agent purchased approximately one ounce of heroin from Defendant A.
- On January 8, 2020, an undercover agent purchased approximately one ounce of heroin from Defendant A.
- On March 18, 2020, surveillance observed Defendant A at a barbershop in Greenacres, Florida around 10:00 a.m.
- Around 11:00 a.m. on March 18, 2020, Defendant A and Person A left the barbershop and traveled toward the Lake Osborne neighborhood in Lake Worth, Florida.
- Agents knew an address in the Lake Osborne neighborhood associated with Person A’s family and checked for any suspicious inbound parcel to that address.
- A U.S. Postal Inspection Service inspector confirmed an approximately six-pound parcel was inbound for the address associated with Person A’s family.
- The parcel’s mailing label had characteristics similar to prior parcels mailed by Co-Defendant B and Co-Defendant C.
- At approximately 1:20 p.m. on March 18, 2020, surveillance observed delivery of the subject parcel to the address in Lake Worth, Florida.
- Agents seized the box from the front porch of the Lake Worth address and brought it to the DEA West Palm Beach office.
- At approximately 1:34 p.m., Defendant A and Person A drove to the area of the Lake Worth address and searched for the parcel at the porch and neighboring locations.
- A federal search warrant to open the parcel was obtained and executed on March 18, 2020.
- Inside the parcel, agents found approximately one-half kilogram of fentanyl.
- Security camera footage indicated a person matching Co-Defendant C’s appearance mailed the parcel from San Diego County, California.
- Defendant A entered a guilty plea on October 1, 2021 to the conspiracy count involving 400 grams or more of fentanyl and 100 grams or more of heroin.
- A Presentence Investigation Report was filed on November 5, 2021 by U.S. Probation, and the defense filed no objections.
- Defendant A’s sentencing was scheduled for March 21, 2022.
- Defendant A had four juvenile offenses between 2005 and 2006 as reported in the PSR.
- Defendant A had eight adult offenses between approximately 2006 and 2014 as reported in the PSR.
- The PSR reported that Defendant A is not deemed a career offender.
- The conspiracy offense carried a statutory minimum of 10 years and a maximum of life under 21 U.S.C. § 841(b)(1)(A).
- Defendant A’s cooperation preceded or coincided with Co-Defendant A pleading guilty to the fentanyl conspiracy count on August 6, 2021.
- Defendant A’s cooperation preceded or coincided with Co-Defendant B pleading guilty to the fentanyl conspiracy count on August 6, 2021.
- Defendant A’s cooperation preceded or coincided with Co-Defendant C pleading guilty to the fentanyl conspiracy count on August 6, 2021.
- Defendant A’s cooperation preceded or coincided with Co-Defendant D pleading guilty to the fentanyl conspiracy count.
- Defendant A’s cooperation preceded or coincided with Co-Defendant E pleading guilty to the fentanyl conspiracy count on October 6, 2021.
- Following related prosecutions, Co-Defendant A received a judgment of 240 months’ imprisonment and 5 years’ supervised release.
- Following related prosecutions, Co-Defendant D received a judgment of 210 months’ imprisonment and 5 years’ supervised release.
- Following related prosecutions, Co-Defendant C received a judgment of 110 months’ imprisonment and 3 years’ supervised release.
- Following related prosecutions, Co-Defendant B received a judgment of 120 months’ imprisonment and 2 years’ supervised release.
- Following related prosecutions, Co-Defendant E received a judgment of 120 months’ imprisonment and 5 years’ supervised release.

- Defendant A agreed to proceed by information and waived prosecution by indictment.
- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to a sole count of securities fraud under 15 U.S.C. §§ 78j(b), 78ff and 17 C.F.R. § 240.10b-5.
- The securities fraud charge alleges use of manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase and sale of a security.
- The count of conviction carries a statutory maximum term of imprisonment of up to 20 years.
- The offense carries a potential term of supervised release of up to 3 years.
- The offense carries a potential fine of up to $5,000,000.
- Restitution is mandatory and the Court must order restitution for the full loss caused by the offense conduct.
- Defendant A admitted that his conduct resulted in an actual or intended loss of $1,305,000.
- Defendant A admitted he was an investment adviser or associated with one at the time of the offense, which involved a violation of securities law.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit proceeds traceable to the offense, including a forfeiture money judgment of $1,305,000.
- Company A is a Florida limited liability company formed on November 4, 2014 and served as the manager and investment adviser of the Fund.
- Defendant A has been the sole officer of Company A since late 2016.
- Company B is a Delaware limited partnership formed on January 2, 2015 and operated as a pooled investment vehicle managed by Company A and Defendant A.
- Defendant A was the General Partner of Company B and sold limited partnership interests to investors.
- Company C is a Delaware limited partnership formed on November 19, 2019, and Defendant A was its General Partner.
- Defendant A sometimes used Company B and Company C interchangeably when selling limited partnership interests.
- Company B and Company C are collectively referred to as the Fund in the case materials.
- Defendant A founded Company A and the Fund with Co-Founder A and Co-Founder B around 2014 and marketed the Fund as an algorithmic trading strategy.
- Co-Founder A and Co-Founder B resigned from Company A and the Fund in late 2016, after which Defendant A operated the Fund alone.
- Defendant A solicited investors through personal networks and provided Fund performance information via Company A's public website, an instrumentality of interstate commerce.
- Defendant A offered and sold limited partnership interests in the Fund, which were securities as defined by 15 U.S.C. § 78c(a)(10).
- Investor materials were sent via email or uploaded through an online document-sharing application.
- The Fund’s fee disclosures stated management fees of up to 2% per year and performance fees up to 20%, with most investors told 1% management and 10% performance.
- From 2015 through February 2020, Defendant A obtained approximately $1,600,000 through the sale of limited partnership interests in the Fund.
- Investor A contacted the Fund by email in late December 2019 seeking information.
- On December 31, 2019, Defendant A responded as “Investor Relations” via a Company A email account and was the only person using Company A email addresses at that time.
- The December 31, 2019 email included a performance sheet falsely showing 2019 year-to-date earnings of 21.9% and positive annual earnings for 2016–2019.
- On January 1, 2020, Defendant A told Investor A that AUM was “a little over $100,” intending to mean $100 million.
- On January 8, 2020, Defendant A sent Investor A an email claiming the Fund received a top award from a hedge fund data service based on performance he had fabricated.
- On January 16, 2020, Defendant A sent Investor A a subscription agreement and limited partnership agreement stating fees of 1% management and 10% performance for the share class discussed.
- The Fund’s limited partnership agreement stated its purpose was to invest and trade in securities and did not authorize paying returns to other investors or personal compensation beyond agreed fees.
- On January 22, 2020, Defendant A knowingly led Investor A to believe the Fund had over $100 million in assets under management when AUM had never exceeded approximately $2 million.
- On February 2, 2020, Defendant A emailed a fake 2018 audit report and opinion letter on letterhead of a non-existent audit firm.
- The fake audit report falsely listed $52,904,796 in total assets for the Fund as of December 31, 2018, when the primary brokerage account held less than approximately $1.4 million.
- Defendant A’s statements to Investor A about the Fund’s performance, existence of an independent audit report, and assets under management were false.
- On February 3, 2020, Investor A sent signed subscription documents for a $200,000 investment in the Fund, including a Founders Share Class Addendum.
- On February 7, 2020, Investor A wired $200,000 to a bank account in the name of the Fund with the expectation of receiving a limited partnership interest.
- On February 12, 2020, $199,500 of Investor A’s money was transferred from the Fund’s bank account to the Fund’s brokerage account, which had a beginning balance of $17.32.
- Between February 12 and December 31, 2020, the brokerage account’s value dwindled to $49.74, with only one other deposit of $13,700 from Company A’s bank account during that period.
- By June 2020, Investor A requested return of the investment, but no funds were returned and Defendant A stopped responding.
- On August 12, 2020, Defendant A transferred most remaining funds from the brokerage account to Company A’s bank account, which he accessed for business and personal expenses.
- Investor A’s $200,000 investment was lost due to poor performance, fees, and transfers to Company A’s bank account far exceeding amounts permitted by the agreement.
- From September 2016 through December 2020, Defendant A misappropriated investor funds for personal purposes and concealed performance losses and the misappropriation.
- Defendant A did not disclose to investors that the Fund’s net asset value was nearly zero in 2020.
- The total loss to investors who were unable to recover their investments was $1,305,000.
- The offense conduct occurred within the Southern District of Florida and in other districts.
- The Fund began operations in 2015 and the two co-founders departed in late 2016.
- Trading losses while the two co-founders oversaw the portfolio in 2015–2016 totaled over $375,000, according to the defense submission.
- After the co-founders left, Defendant A lacked access to the prior algorithmic strategies and had no trading experience, and the Fund’s trading and market losses deepened over the next four years per the defense submission.
- The defense states that of approximately $1,305,000 in investor losses, about $450,000 was misappropriated for improper business and personal expenses, with remaining losses from trading over $725,000.
- Defendant A placed $50,000 in a trust account prior to arrest for the purpose of partially providing restitution to Investor A, per the defense submission referencing the PSI.
- Defendant A pleaded guilty approximately two months after the criminal complaint was filed, per the defense submission.
- In a parallel SEC civil enforcement case, Defendant A did not oppose a preliminary injunction, asset freeze, or the SEC’s motion for judgment, per the defense submission.
- Defendant A’s spouse had multiple arrests between 2014 and 2016 and underwent residential inpatient treatment; the spouse was placed on community control followed by probation, per the defense submission.
- Defendant A and Spouse A separated in May 2018 and their divorce was finalized in August 2019, per the defense submission.
- A temporary restraining order was obtained in October 2018 after a domestic incident, per the defense submission.
- Defendant A was diagnosed with skin cancer in 2019 and underwent two rounds of Mohs surgery, per the defense submission.
- A head and neck surgeon recommended further surgery after a CT scan in 2019, per the defense submission.
- Defendant A sought mental health treatment with a psychologist and had a history of anxiety and depression predating arrest, per the defense submission and provider letters referenced.
- Defendant A has hypertension diagnosed in 2005 and was evaluated in February 2020 for chest tightness; he takes amlodipine and metoprolol, per the defense submission.
- In April 2021, Defendant A was diagnosed with degenerative disc disease with cervical spine findings on MRI and was taking prednisone for pain, per the defense submission.
- The defense submission states Defendant A previously held commission-based jobs where his annual income regularly exceeded $200,000.

- On January 6, 2021, the exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to the public.
- Only authorized people with appropriate identification were allowed inside the U.S. Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- A joint session of Congress convened at approximately 1:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021 to certify the Electoral College vote.
- Around 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals in the crowd forced entry into the U.S. Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m. on January 6, 2021, Members of Congress and the Vice President were evacuated and the proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- The Vice President remained in the U.S. Capitol from evacuation until the joint session resumed on the evening of January 6, 2021.
- The January 6, 2021 riot caused more than $1.4 million in damage to the U.S. Capitol.
- As of May 17, 2021, the damage to the U.S. Capitol from January 6 was approximately $1,495,326.55.
- The defendant traveled from home in Florida to Washington, D.C. to participate in the January 6, 2021 rally and then marched to the U.S. Capitol.
- The defendant unlawfully entered the U.S. Capitol building on January 6, 2021.
- The defendant knew at the time of entry that the U.S. Capitol was a restricted building and that she had no authority to enter.
- While inside the Capitol, the defendant wore a blue flag draped around her shoulders and was photographed next to a co-defendant.
- After exiting the building, the defendant discussed being inside the Capitol and referenced efforts to stop the vote count in a contemporaneous recorded conversation.
- The defendant remained on the Capitol steps among a large crowd after exiting the building on January 6, 2021.
- The defendant and a co-defendant were among a large group that was tear-gassed as law enforcement sought to clear the building.
- The defendant was inside multiple areas of the Capitol, including the Rotunda and Statuary Hall.
- The defendant remained inside the Capitol for roughly thirty-five minutes.
- The defendant was captured on video inside Statuary Hall and on the first floor on the east side of the building with large groups of rioters.
- When confronted by law enforcement inside the building, the defendant persisted in remaining.
- On January 19, 2021, the defendant voluntarily agreed to be interviewed by the FBI and admitted entering the Capitol.
- During the FBI interview, the defendant identified herself in an MPD flyer that contained photographs from inside the Capitol.
- While present at the U.S. Capitol on January 6, 2021, the defendant later stated that no one instructed them that they could not enter the building.
- An anonymous tip to local police on January 10, 2021 identified two individuals from an MPD flyer depicting unauthorized persons inside the Capitol on January 6.
- FBI reviewed open-source photographs taken during the January 6 events to compare with DMV photographs of suspects.
- FBI reviewed social media profiles associated with the individuals and noted that the accounts indicated they were in a relationship.
- Video reviewed by the FBI showed travel toward Washington, D.C. by car on January 5, 2021, with roadside scenery consistent with the I-10/I-95 area in Jacksonville, Florida.
- On January 19, 2021, law enforcement executed search warrants at residences associated with the case and seized a flagpole with an upside down American flag attached.
- On January 25, 2021, a criminal complaint was filed charging two counts related to entering restricted grounds and parading in the Capitol.
- On January 26, 2021, the defendant was arrested in Florida.
- On February 19, 2021, a five-count indictment charged offenses including obstructing an official proceeding, entering and remaining in a restricted building, and two misdemeanors related to Capitol conduct.
- On October 4, 2021, the defendant pleaded guilty to one count of entering and remaining in a restricted building or grounds (18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1)).
- As part of the plea, the defendant agreed to pay $500 in restitution to the U.S. Department of the Treasury.
- The plea agreement provided that the government would move to dismiss the remaining counts at sentencing.
- The statutory maximum penalty for the offense of conviction (18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1)) includes up to one year of imprisonment and a fine up to $100,000.
- The defendant agreed to a $25 special assessment for the Class A misdemeanor offense.
- The defendant admitted in the Statement of Offense that she knowingly entered a restricted building without lawful authority on January 6, 2021.
- Both defendants are military veterans of the U.S. Navy, according to sentencing materials.
- The defendant reported enlisting in the U.S. Navy after high school in 1999 and being discharged in 2000.
- The defendant has prior misdemeanor convictions in Florida for disorderly conduct/battery on a law enforcement officer/resisting, driving while license suspended or revoked, and battery.
- The plea agreement’s criminal history summary listed a 2016 battery conviction and a 2018 driving with a suspended license disposition, each assessed zero criminal history points.
- The defendant agreed in the plea to pay $500 in restitution and to provide financial disclosure information to assist in collection.
- The defendant acknowledged and signed a Statement of Offense confirming the factual basis for the plea in late August 2021.
- The defendant’s sentencing memorandum states that she immediately cooperated when contacted by law enforcement, including providing a phone and answering questions.
- The defendant voluntarily surrendered on January 26, 2021 and was released on bond with conditions, which she followed without violations.
- The defendant’s sentencing memorandum asserts she was not particularly politically active, did not post on social media about the events, and did not plan to breach the Capitol.
- The defendant’s memorandum states she did not engage in violence, took nothing, damaged nothing, and was inside the building for no more than thirty minutes.
- The defendant’s memorandum asserts she and a co-defendant arrived after the initial breach and did not witness earlier violence.
- The defendant’s sentencing memorandum states she was employed at a convenience store at the time, lost that job after her arrest, and later obtained full-time work in construction.
- The defendant’s sentencing memorandum indicates she is a single parent of multiple children and active in their activities.
- The defendant’s conduct and admissions were also documented in a PSR referenced by the sentencing materials, noting compliance with pretrial supervision.
- The crowd that entered the Capitol on January 6 did not undergo security screening or weapons checks before entering.
- The defendant and co-defendant entered the Capitol shortly after the initial breach of the building.
- Both defendants remained on the Capitol grounds after exiting the building and spoke with others about being inside and being tear-gassed.
- The defendant agreed that the government could seek dismissal of the remaining indictment counts at sentencing as part of the plea.
- The defendant’s plea and factual basis were filed in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia in October 2021.
- The defendant signed the Statement of Offense on August 31, 2021 acknowledging its truth and accuracy.
- The defendant and co-defendant traveled together from Florida to Washington, D.C. to attend the rally on January 6, 2021.
- According to investigative materials, the defendant was part of a recorded conversation immediately after exiting the Capitol discussing stopping the certification.

- The U.S. Capitol is secured 24 hours a day by U.S. Capitol Police with permanent and temporary barriers and only authorized individuals permitted inside.
- On January 6, 2021, the exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to the public.
- A joint session of Congress convened at the Capitol on January 6, 2021, at approximately 1:00 PM to certify the Electoral College vote count.
- Around 1:30 PM on January 6, 2021, the House and Senate adjourned to separate chambers during the certification proceedings.
- The Vice President was present and presiding over the joint session and then over the Senate on January 6, 2021.
- By approximately 2:00 PM on January 6, 2021, individuals in the crowd forced their way through barricades and advanced to the Capitol’s exterior facade.
- Shortly after 2:00 PM on January 6, 2021, individuals forced entry into the Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement officers.
- The January 6, 2021 riot resulted in more than $1.4 million in damage to the Capitol requiring repairs.
- At approximately 2:20 PM on January 6, 2021, members of the House, Senate, and the Vice President were evacuated, and congressional proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 PM.
- Congressional proceedings resumed at approximately 8:00 PM on January 6, 2021, after the Capitol was secured.
- The Vice President remained in the Capitol from evacuation until the session resumed on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant resided in New York prior to January 6, 2021.
- On January 5, 2021, Defendant traveled by automobile from New York to Washington, D.C.
- Defendant traveled to Washington, D.C. to protest Congress’s certification of the Electoral College.
- On December 3, 2020, Defendant posted messages stating, among other things, “We must stand up to these people and take our world back” and “It's time to bring the pain upon them.”
- On December 30, 2020, Defendant messaged that they were headed to Washington, D.C. on January 5.
- On December 31, 2020, Defendant posted statements anticipating large numbers of supporters in Washington, D.C., including “I think there gonna be over a million patriots in DC” and indicated intent to go on January 6.
- On December 31, 2020, Defendant urged others to march on D.C. and referenced firearms, stating “just march on DC on the 6th … bring ur guns … I'd exercise my 2A legal rights there.”
- On January 3, 2021, Defendant told another person via message they were going to D.C. on January 6 and had booked a room.
- On January 5–6, 2021, Defendant exchanged messages confirming they were in the D.C. area and discussed the possibility of people showing up with guns and feeling sleep-deprived.
- On the evening of January 5, 2021, Defendant posted a public article on their website predicting possible outcomes for January 6 and writing that “Trump just needs to fire the bat signal ... deputize patriots ... and then the pain comes.”
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant attended the rally near the Ellipse and then marched with others to the Capitol.
- At approximately 2:30 PM on January 6, 2021, Defendant was on restricted Capitol grounds on the east side of the building.
- At approximately 2:37 PM on January 6, 2021, Defendant entered the Capitol through the Memorial Door and went to the Rotunda.
- At approximately 2:55 PM on January 6, 2021, Defendant exited the Capitol on the east side through the Rotunda doors.
- On January 7, 2021, Defendant described the events as dangerous and violent in a Facebook exchange and said people died.
- Defendant admitted the elements of 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1), including knowingly entering and remaining in a restricted building and grounds without lawful authority.
- Defendant signed the Statement of Offense acknowledging its truth and accuracy on April 4, 2022.
- Defense counsel acknowledged concurrence with Defendant’s adoption of the Statement of Offense on April 15, 2022.
- Defendant agreed to plead guilty to entering and remaining in a restricted building or grounds, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1), as Count 1 of the Information.
- The statutory maximum penalty for 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1) is one year of imprisonment and a fine of up to $100,000, with up to one year of supervised release.
- Defendant agreed to allow law enforcement to review any social media accounts for statements and postings in and around January 6, 2021 and to interview Defendant about the events prior to sentencing.
- The government agreed to request dismissal of the remaining count of the Information at sentencing as part of the plea agreement.
- Defendant agreed to pay $500 in restitution to the Architect of the Capitol.
- As of May 17, 2021, the estimated damage to the U.S. Capitol from the January 6 riot was approximately $1,495,326.55.
- On July 6, 2022, Defendant pleaded guilty to one count of entering and remaining in a restricted building or grounds.
- Defendant operated a personal website where they blogged and posted videos, including content showcasing weapons and accessories.
- In late December 2020, Defendant used Facebook to share plans to travel to Washington, D.C. for January 6, including stating “im headed to dc on the 5th.”
- In late December 2020, Defendant told another user they expected over a million people in D.C. and that they were going on January 6.
- On January 3, 2021, Defendant responded affirmatively to a request to take video on January 6 and stated they had booked a room in the D.C. area.
- On December 30, 2020, Defendant invited a Facebook friend to join the D.C. trip and suggested bringing firearms.
- Defendant had acquired multiple firearms by late 2020, including a semiautomatic AR-15 rifle and a shotgun purchased a few months earlier.
- On or about January 4, 2021, Defendant drove from New York to the Washington, D.C. area and checked into an Airbnb in Hyattsville, Maryland on January 5, planning to stay two nights.
- On the evening of January 5, 2021, Defendant acknowledged that Washington, D.C. is not an open carry jurisdiction in a Facebook exchange.
- On January 5, 2021, Defendant shared a photograph of firearms taken in the Hyattsville Airbnb and discussed leaving weapons in a parking garage the next day, possibly keeping a pistol on them.
- In the early hours of January 6, 2021, Defendant posted online that if millions showed up, "the pain comes" and suggested the likelihood of an armed response.
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant entered the Capitol at approximately 2:37 PM through the Memorial Door and walked in the Rotunda area before exiting around 2:55 PM.
- Capitol Police surveillance cameras captured Defendant inside the Capitol on multiple occasions between 2:37 PM and 2:55 PM on January 6, 2021.
- Following January 6, Defendant posted that the events were “awesome,” “dangerous and violent,” and that “people died,” also stating they were tear gassed and pepper sprayed.
- In a video recorded on or about January 7, 2021, Defendant said they taunted officers and claimed the goal was to get into Congress to question officials.
- In the same video, Defendant claimed, “We shoved our way in there, and we started breaking down doors,” describing the situation as very dangerous.
- The government noted it was unable to independently verify Defendant’s statements claiming participation in acts of violence.
- On January 20, 2021, Defendant was charged by criminal complaint with 18 U.S.C. §§ 1752(a)(1) and (2) and 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D), and arrested in New York City the same day.
- On January 20, 2021, agents executed search warrants at the New York City apartment where Defendant was staying and on Defendant’s car.
- From the apartment, agents recovered an AR-15 semi-automatic rifle, a pistol, a machete-style knife, several pre-loaded magazines, and ammunition.
- From the vehicle, agents recovered ammunition, a shotgun, shotgun shells, a tactical vest, an AR-style lower receiver, a rifle foregrip light, and multiple machetes and a knife.
- After receiving Miranda warnings, Defendant admitted traveling to D.C. to attend the rally and to protest the 2020 Presidential Election outcome.
- In the interview, Defendant said they followed the crowd toward the Capitol after leaving the rally and described flash bangs and other disturbances upon arrival.
- When asked by agents, Defendant claimed they went only as far as “the top part… right where the marble starts” and denied participating in violent acts at the Capitol.
- Defendant claimed they did not bring firearms into Washington, D.C. on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant falsely claimed they did not bring firearms into the Hyattsville, Maryland Airbnb where they stayed on January 5, 2021, despite having sent a photograph of firearms in that Airbnb.
- Following arrest, a magistrate judge in the Southern District of New York ordered Defendant detained pending proceedings, finding no conditions would assure appearance or community safety.
- On February 19, 2021, Defendant was charged by Information in D.C. with two counts: 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1) and 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(2).
- On March 12, 2021, Magistrate Judge Zia M. Faruqui ordered Defendant released pending trial in the D.C. case.
- With the D.C. charges pending, Defendant was charged in New York state court with multiple firearms offenses, including second-degree criminal possession of a weapon.
- On February 28, 2022, Defendant pleaded guilty in New York state court to second-degree criminal possession of a weapon.
- On April 4, 2022, a New York state court sentenced Defendant to three years and six months of incarceration, followed by three years of supervised release.
- According to New York Department of Corrections and Community Supervision, Defendant’s earliest release date from the New York sentence is March 29, 2025.
- On July 6, 2022, Defendant pleaded guilty in the D.C. case to Count One, entering and remaining in a restricted building or grounds, and agreed to pay $500 restitution.
- In a January 7, 2021 Facebook message, Defendant stated, “seeing cops literally run ... was the coolest thing ive ever seen in my life.”
- Defendant acknowledged on video that they were tear gassed and pepper sprayed on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant acknowledged on video telling officers, “Who pays your ... salary... The American taxpayers. Why are you defending a fraudulent election?”
- At sentencing, the government represented that Defendant brought at least two firearms—an AR-15 rifle and a pistol—on the trip to the Washington, D.C. area, though not into the District or to the Capitol.
- On January 5, 2021, Defendant said they felt anxious, had not slept 2–3 days, and might need to stay longer than one day in the D.C. area.
- Defendant acknowledged in messages that they were staying at an Airbnb in a neighborhood they described negatively and felt sleep-deprived on January 5–6, 2021.
- Defendant wrote on January 5, 2021, that if millions of supporters did not show up with guns, the cause could be lost.
- Defendant’s early-January 2021 website post predicted three possible outcomes for January 6, including mass arrests of officials or armed actions by supporters if the election was certified.
- In the plea agreement, the government referenced Defendant’s existing conviction for criminal possession of a weapon in the second degree under New York Penal Law 265.03(3).
- Defendant agreed in the plea agreement to disclose financial assets and complete a financial disclosure form to facilitate restitution collection.
- After arrest, Defendant participated in a voluntary FBI interview and admitted confronting officers verbally about the election.
- The government represented that total losses from the January 6 attack are currently estimated at $2.7 million, reflecting damage to the Capitol and costs borne by Capitol Police.
- Defendant’s Facebook and website activity in late 2020 included content glorifying or promoting political violence in the lead-up to January 6.
- Defendant’s clothing on January 6 included blue jeans, a blue or gray shirt, a dark hooded jacket with a front zipper, a backpack, and sunglasses.
- In messages on January 5, 2021, Defendant wrote “Gotta make a stand man ... Not gonna be intimidated.”
- Defendant’s January 7, 2021 messages included anti-police comments and exhortations to prepare for future conflict, urging physical fitness and finding someone with medical or veteran combat experience.
- Defense counsel stated that Defendant was incarcerated from January 20, 2021 until March 10, 2021 before being released at their D.C. arraignment.
- Defense counsel represented that after release in March 2021, Defendant attended classes and individual sessions addressing substance abuse and has remained compliant with release conditions.
- Defense counsel represented that Defendant has longstanding mental health issues dating to early teens and is an admitted alcoholic who previously consumed significant quantities of liquor.
- Defense counsel stated that video evidence shows Defendant inside the Capitol for less than 20 minutes and that he exited as directed without assaulting anyone or destroying property.
- Defense counsel stated that at D.C. arraignment on March 10, 2021, the government did not oppose Defendant’s release on the misdemeanor charges.

- On January 6, 2021, the U.S. Capitol was secured by U.S. Capitol Police with permanent and temporary barriers and was closed to the public.
- A joint session of Congress convened at approximately 1:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021 to certify the Electoral College vote, with the Vice President present.
- Around 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals in the crowd forced entry into the Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting police.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m. on January 6, 2021, members of the House and Senate were evacuated and the joint session was suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- The Vice President remained inside the Capitol from evacuation until proceedings resumed on the evening of January 6, 2021.
- The riot resulted in more than $2.8 million in damage to the Capitol, according to the Architect of the Capitol.
- Defendant traveled from San Francisco, California to Washington, D.C. on or about January 1, 2021.
- One purpose of Defendant’s trip was to support an objection to the certification of the Electoral College and to protest Congress’s certification.
- Defendant attended a rally on January 6, 2021 and then marched with others to the Capitol.
- Between 2 p.m. and 4 p.m. on January 6, 2021, Defendant was on restricted Capitol grounds on the northern Lower West Terrace.
- Outside the Capitol, Defendant used a bullhorn to encourage others to enter the building.
- Defendant stated through the bullhorn: “Behind me, the door is open.”
- Defendant stated through the bullhorn: “we need you to push forward, forward.”
- Defendant stated through the bullhorn: “we need critical mass for this to work.”
- Defendant stated through the bullhorn: “Go behind me and go in.”
- Defendant entered the Capitol through the open Senate Wing door at approximately 3:32 p.m. on January 6, 2021.
- As Defendant entered, an officer reached out and touched him; Defendant turned toward the officer and continued inside.
- While exiting, Defendant paused to identify himself on a livestream after being told to leave by a law enforcement officer.
- As he left, Defendant called a police officer an “Oathbreaker” and told others to get the officer’s badge number.
- Defendant exited the Capitol at approximately 3:33 p.m., about 36 seconds after entry.
- Defendant texted a family member at 3:39 p.m. on January 6, 2021: “Capitol.”
- Defendant texted a family member at 3:56 p.m.: “yes I’m safe” followed by “I went inside.”
- Defendant clarified via text that he went “inside” the “capitol building.”
- When asked why he went inside, Defendant texted: “That’s what we were doing.”
- At 4:03 p.m. on January 6, 2021, Defendant messaged another person: “Patriots rushed the Capitol.”
- On the night of January 6, 2021, Defendant posted on Instagram: “I didn’t break or take anything.”
- Defendant acknowledged and admitted the elements of entering or remaining in a restricted building or grounds in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1).
- On November 7, 2020, Defendant tweeted the Proud Boys logo and wrote: “Stand back and stand by! Show up at your state Capitol at noon today... #StopTheSteal! StopTheSteal.US.”
- On December 28, 2020, Defendant tweeted “#FightForTrump” and “#StopTheSteal” and linked a GiveSendGo account soliciting donations to fund travel to Washington, D.C. on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant pleaded guilty to Count Two of the Superseding Indictment, charging a violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1).
- The statutory maximum sentence for 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1) is one year of imprisonment and a fine of up to $100,000, with up to one year of supervised release.
- As part of the plea, the government agreed to move to dismiss remaining counts at sentencing.
- Defendant agreed to participate in a law enforcement interview regarding the events in and around January 6, 2021 prior to sentencing.
- Defendant agreed to pay $500 in restitution to the Architect of the Capitol.
- The parties recognized estimated damage to the Capitol of approximately $2,881,360.20 as of October 14, 2022.
- Defendant signed the plea agreement on December 20, 2022.
- Defendant signed the Statement of Offense on December 20, 2022, acknowledging it was true and accurate.
- On January 15, 2021, an FBI special agent submitted a Statement of Facts in support of a complaint alleging violations of 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a) and 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2).
- Defendant was identified through a livestream video and confirmation by an associate, and the image matched Defendant’s driver’s license photo.
- While at the Capitol on January 6, 2021, Defendant messaged an associate on Instagram: “Tell your dad if he doesn’t want his guns I can find some folks who will.”
- After January 6, Defendant posted on Instagram that he went inside the Capitol “for a couple of minutes.”
- An FBI affiant stated that Defendant was a self-proclaimed member of the Proud Boys, citing social media posts.
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant identified himself by name on a livestream while inside the Capitol and told others to get a police officer’s badge number, calling the officer an “oathbreaker.”
- Defendant’s bullhorn statements encouraging entry included: “we need critical mass for this to work,” as recorded in government exhibits.
- As Defendant entered at approximately 3:32:24 p.m., a police officer attempted to make contact and shouted “hey!” more than once.
- Inside, officers pointed Defendant toward the exit; an officer briefly grabbed Defendant’s sweatshirt before Defendant dodged around and paused to speak with a livestreamer.
- Defendant called a police officer an “oathbreaker” inside the Capitol before exiting at approximately 3:33 p.m.
- Between 2 p.m. and 4 p.m., the government memorandum places Defendant near the Senate Wing Door on the Upper West Terrace before entry.
- On March 14, 2023, Defendant appeared in a televised interview and identified a website hosting fundraising pages for January 6 defendants, including his own.
- By May 18, 2023, Defendant’s online fundraiser had received $25,676.25 in contributions, according to the government memorandum.
- Defendant was charged by criminal complaint on January 15, 2021 with violations including 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1)-(2) and 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D), (G).
- Defendant was arrested on January 29, 2021 in eastern Texas.
- On November 10, 2021, a five-count Superseding Indictment was filed, including 18 U.S.C. §§ 1512 and 1752(a)(1)-(2) and 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D), (G).
- On January 31, 2023, Defendant pleaded guilty to Count Two charging 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1).
- Defendant agreed in the plea to pay $500 restitution to the Architect of the Capitol.
- The offense of conviction, 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1), is a Class A misdemeanor.
- The presentence report noted Defendant has autism, affecting responses under stress and understanding of non-written rules.
- On January 2, 2021, Defendant was arrested in Wyoming after refusing to leave a store and refusing to provide identification to a police sergeant, according to government exhibits described in the memorandum.
- In the Wyoming matter, Defendant entered a nolo contendere plea, was placed on one year of probation under Wyo. Stat. § 7-13-301, and the case was later dismissed without a conviction after probation completion.
- Defendant’s November 7, 2020 tweet urged people to show up at their state Capitol and included the phrase “Stand back and stand by!”
- Defendant entered the Capitol wearing dark sunglasses, a MAGA hat, and a tan and black jacket, as seen on video referenced in the FBI affidavit.
- While inside, a livestreamer referred to Defendant by an online moniker, and Defendant approached and told the livestreamer to stop “doxing” him before stating his name.
- Defendant sent a link to a news live update article about the certification to a small group on the evening of January 6, 2021.
- On January 6, 2021 at 9:41 p.m., Defendant tweeted commentary stating, “They WANT a revolution... They don’t represent us. They hate us.” in response to another user.
- The plea agreement memorialized that Defendant’s plea would result in dismissal of other counts and included waivers of venue and certain appeal rights.
- Defendant consented in the plea agreement to proceedings by video or teleconference under the CARES Act if necessary.
- Defendant authorized the U.S. Attorney’s Office to obtain a credit report to evaluate ability to satisfy financial obligations tied to the case.

- Defendant’s defense memorandum states he has Autism Spectrum Disorder and asks the Court to consider that diagnosis in understanding his conduct.
- The defense memorandum asserts that Defendant did not hear an order to leave immediately upon entry and that audio analysis did not reveal an audible order at that moment.
- The defense memorandum states Defendant began therapy for Autism Spectrum Disorder in 2018 and continues therapy.
- The defense memorandum denies that Defendant is or claimed to be a member of the Proud Boys, disputing the FBI affidavit’s characterization.

- The defense memorandum states the Wyoming matter ended in a deferred disposition with charges later dropped after a year without offenses.
- Defendant acknowledged in the Statement of Offense that he knowingly entered or remained in a restricted building without lawful authority on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant used social media to publicize and fund his trip to Washington, D.C., including linking a fundraising account in late December 2020.
- Defendant’s conduct included using a bullhorn to urge others toward entry and then personally entering and briefly remaining inside the building.

- The offense of conviction is attempting to provide material support to a foreign terrorist organization in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 2339B.
- The memorandum states that 18 U.S.C. § 2339B is listed in 18 U.S.C. § 2332b(g)(5)(B).
- Defendant A stated they would offer translation services to ISIS's media wing.
- Defendant A told investigators they sought to live under Sharia law in a Caliphate.
- In a communication with family close to the departure for Syria, Defendant A referenced "Jana."
- The memorandum asserts that Defendant A previously tried to enter Somalia under Sheikh Sharif.
- Defendant A attempted to join ISIS in June 2014.
- The memorandum states that the Secretary of State designated ISIL as a foreign terrorist organization one month before Defendant A attempted to join ISIS.
- According to the memorandum, Defendant A asserted that when they traveled to Syria, they believed news accounts of ISIS atrocities were false.
- Defendant A said they had a desire to go into dangerous war zones.
- Defendant A attributed their desire to enter dangerous war zones to being in a manic phase of Bipolar 1.
- In a 2018 statement, Defendant A said they were experiencing only the beginning symptoms of a manic episode at the time of the offense.
- Before traveling, Defendant A created a false story about the purpose of their one-way flights.
- Defendant A changed flights in Turkey during the travel.
- Defendant A disposed of their cell phone in Turkey.
- Defendant A located a hotel in Gaziantep before proceeding toward the Syrian border.
- Defendant A found a driver to take them toward the border.
- Defendant A left for the Syrian border under cover of darkness.
- Defendant A traveled in the company of others seeking to enter ISIS territory.
- Before leaving for Turkey, Defendant A emailed DHS falsely claiming they purchased a one-way ticket to Egypt as a cost-saving measure and were going to visit a sick father and stepsiblings.
- In statements, Defendant A referred to ISIS as ISIL, an older name for the group.
- Defendant A sought to enter an active war zone in Syria where ISIS was committing acts of terrorism.
- The memorandum states that ISIS's media wing disseminated videos of punishments, assassinations, and bombings to recruit individuals.
- The memorandum states that translated texts in ISIS media helped recruit English-speaking individuals who traveled to Syria.
- According to the memorandum, between 2011 and 2016 over 42,000 foreign fighters traveled to join IS from over 120 countries.
- The memorandum states that in January 2014 ISIS captured Raqqa and that al-Qaeda officially renounced connection with ISIS in February 2014.
- The memorandum states that ISIS seized natural resources such as Iraq's oil wells and stole money from populations to fund operations.
- The memorandum states that at its height ISIS acquired over $2 million per day from stolen assets.
- The memorandum states that ISIS destroyed cultural sites including the Mosul Museum, the Mosul Public Library, temples, mosques, and churches.
- The memorandum states that in 2014 ISIS took homes from civilians and enslaved individuals from minority groups.

- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened at the U.S. Capitol to certify the Electoral College vote.
- Vice President was present and presiding over the Senate during the certification proceedings on January 6, 2021.
- On January 6, 2021, the exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to the public and restricted by barriers and police posts.
- At approximately 2:00 PM on January 6, 2021, crowds forced their way through barricades and advanced to the Capitol’s exterior facade.
- Around 2:20 PM on January 6, 2021, members of the House and Senate were evacuated and proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 PM.
- The January 6 riot resulted in substantial damage to the Capitol requiring more than $1.4 million for repairs, per the Statement of Offense.
- As of April 5, 2022, the riot caused approximately $2,734,783.14 in damage to the U.S. Capitol, referenced for restitution purposes.
- As of October 17, 2022, the approximate losses from the Capitol breach were $2,881,360.20, including damage to the Capitol building and grounds.
- Defendant A traveled by car from the Harrisburg, Pennsylvania area with friends to Washington, D.C. to attend the January 6, 2021 rally.
- After the rally, Defendant A walked to the restricted Capitol grounds.
- Defendant A unlawfully entered the Capitol grounds after other rioters broke down barricades.
- On the Capitol steps, Defendant A grabbed Officer B’s jacket and yelled at the officer.
- Defendant A admitted he was not acting in self-defense when he grabbed Officer B’s jacket.
- Defendant A struck Officer A’s hand, head, and shoulder areas during the confrontation.
- Defendant A admitted he was not acting in self-defense when he struck Officer A’s hand, head, and shoulder areas.
- Defendant A admitted he intended to cause bodily injury to Officers A and B when he struck and punched them.
- Defendant A acknowledged that his belief about the election being fraudulent did not legally justify entering the Capitol grounds or using intimidation.
- Defendant A walked down a line of officers after reaching the steps and engaged in physical struggles, striking or attempting to strike officers and pushing the police line.
- Defendant A punched an officer on the officer’s riot helmet during a separate encounter.
- Defendant A attempted to kick a large mace can out of an officer’s hand during another incident.
- During the mace-can incident, Defendant A also made physical contact with another officer with his hand.
- Defendant A was sprayed by officers with an eye irritant during the confrontations and retreated afterward.
- Defendant A engaged in physical and assaultive contact with multiple Metropolitan Police Department officers on the Capitol grounds.
- Defendant A swung his left arm and hit one MPD officer’s left arm.
- Defendant A kicked another MPD officer with his boot, striking the officer’s left arm.
- Defendant A spent approximately 40 minutes on the Capitol grounds during the assaults, from about 12:50 PM to around 1:30 PM.
- Defendant A gave a contemporaneous on-scene interview to a CNN reporter after the assaults.
- In the CNN interview, Defendant A stated, “We broke down the barriers, and we rushed them… We tried to push them back… They started pepper spraying.”
- In the CNN interview, Defendant A stated, “Only we can do it… That’s what we’re doing… fighting back.”
- Defendant A later acknowledged to the FBI that the officers were overwhelmed and scared by the mob’s actions.
- On January 14, 2021, Defendant A contacted local law enforcement to report he was at the Capitol on January 6 and sought to self-surrender to the FBI.
- On January 14, 2021, Defendant A voluntarily interviewed with FBI agents in Harrisburg, Pennsylvania.
- During the FBI interview, Defendant A admitted he became angry when pushed back by officers and grabbed an officer by the jacket while yelling.
- Defendant A admitted to the FBI he had deleted images and videos from January 6, 2021.
- An FBI ‘Be On the Lookout’ Bulletin (Photograph #55-AFO) was published on or about January 11, 2021 depicting Defendant A among sought individuals.
- An individual identified Defendant A from the BOLO photo, and his driver’s license photo appeared to match the image.
- The FBI confirmed Defendant A served in the United States Marines from about 1985 to 1992 with an honorable discharge.
- Video evidence shows Defendant A shoving a U.S. Capitol Police officer on the Capitol steps on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant A confirmed his identity when shown a screenshot derived from social media videos of the events.
- Defendant A pleaded guilty on September 21, 2022 to two counts of assaulting, resisting, or impeding certain officers in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1).
- The two assault counts of conviction each involved physical contact.
- Each assault count carried a statutory maximum penalty of up to 8 years of imprisonment, up to a $250,000 fine, and up to 3 years of supervised release.
- Defendant A agreed to pay a $100 special assessment per felony count.
- Defendant A agreed to pay $2,000 in restitution to the Architect of the Capitol.
- Restitution payments were to be made to the Clerk of Court and were due immediately, with participation in the BOP’s IFRP if imprisoned.
- Defendant A agreed to disclose all assets and submit a financial disclosure to the U.S. Attorney’s Office Financial Litigation Unit 30 days prior to sentencing.
- The government agreed to move to dismiss the remaining counts at sentencing in consideration of the guilty plea to two counts.

- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened at the U.S. Capitol to certify the Electoral College vote and was suspended around 2:20 p.m. after evacuations.
- On January 6, 2021, the Capitol and its exterior plaza were closed to the public and restricted to authorized individuals.
- Defendant A traveled to the U.S. Capitol grounds on January 6, 2021 and entered the restricted perimeter.
- Before January 6, Defendant A publicly posted that he would travel across the country for political events and anticipated unrest.
- On January 3, 2021, Defendant A stated he would bring a grappling hook, rope, and protective gear to Washington, D.C.
- On November 14, 2020, Defendant A attended a protest in Washington, D.C., and later said many participants had firearms.
- On November 7, 2020, Defendant A sent threatening direct messages to a U.S. Senator on Instagram.
- In early January 2021, Defendant A discussed doing reconnaissance in Washington, D.C., and predicted potential violence and unrest.
- Defendant A anticipated confronting law enforcement and expressed he looked forward to fighting them.
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant A filmed and posted a video from inside the Capitol Rotunda captioned “From inside congress.”
- Defendant A’s social media accounts were linked to a phone number registered to him via subpoena returns.
- On January 11, 2021, Defendant A posted a photo of himself inside the Capitol Rotunda.
- Defendant A acknowledged entering the Capitol, telling a commenter he got in and joking he wanted to incriminate himself.
- Capitol surveillance placed Defendant A in the Rotunda at approximately 2:46 p.m. on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant A was part of a crowd that pushed past U.S. Capitol Police officers at the Rotunda doors.
- Before entering, Defendant A crossed the plaza and ran toward the police line on the East Front steps, broke through, and was briefly handcuffed.
- After release from brief detention, Defendant A joined others and passed officers at the Rotunda Doors to enter the Capitol around 2:43 p.m.
- While inside, Defendant A impeded others from exiting and impeded passage through the Capitol, intending to disrupt the certification.
- At about 3:06 p.m., police formed a line in the Rotunda to remove rioters and Defendant A refused to leave.
- Defendant A interfered with officers’ efforts to push him back through the entrance and banged a flagpole on the floor.
- While in the Rotunda, Defendant A paraded while carrying a flag supporting a political figure that had been dropped by another person.
- Defendant A later moved to the West Front and remained on Capitol grounds filming until after 5:00 p.m.
- On January 6, 2021 at approximately 7:26–7:28 p.m., Defendant A replied to a Congresswoman’s post with the message “Assassinate AOC.”
- Defendant A intended his tweet at the Congresswoman to be perceived as a serious intent to commit violence.
- Defendant A acknowledged that his tweet came hours after lawmakers had been forced to evacuate during the attack.
- Defendant A tweeted that rioters would bring guns next time.
- Defendant A publicly claimed that rioters were gentle with police and did not injure them.
- Defendant A wrote that the group charged and decided to go in before a political speech.
- On Instagram, Defendant A admitted he had a rope in his bag on January 6.
- On January 10, 2021, Defendant A wrote that he would get hold of a specific officer and “hug his neck with a nice rope.”
- On January 16, 2021, Defendant A said the officer was not going to survive long and that it was hunting season.
- On January 15, 2021, Defendant A wrote he was happy to make death threats after a social media ban was lifted.
- On January 15, 2021, the FBI obtained a warrant for Defendant A’s phone location; data showed the phone at his residence.
- On January 20, 2021, the FBI arrested Defendant A at his home and recovered gear he had brought to the Capitol, including a grappling hook, rope, and mouth guard.
- At arrest, Defendant A was wearing a shirt referencing being in Washington, D.C. on January 6, 2021.
- Agents found an AR-style rifle with an approximately 8.5 inch barrel in Defendant A’s closet during the arrest search.
- The rifle recovered from Defendant A was later test-fired by the FBI and found to function as a machinegun.
- On February 7, 2023, Defendant A was indicted in the Northern District of Texas for possession of an unregistered firearm under 26 U.S.C. § 5861(d).
- Defendant A told an officer in the Rotunda, “I will not back up,” when ordered to move back.
- Defendant A grabbed at an officer’s baton in the Rotunda and forcibly resisted another officer by putting his hand on her hand and forearm.
- Defendant A stated he intended to further obstruct, interfere with, or impede officers by remaining on the front lines in the Rotunda.
- Defendant A admitted his participation in the attack on the Capitol in contemporaneous public tweets.
- Defendant A texted that they had terrified Congress on the evening of January 7, 2021.
- Defendant A expressed intent to begin assassinations the day after the riot in a social media message.
- Defendant A circulated images and articles about the officer who shot a rioter and advocated execution of that officer in messages.
- Defendant A told an associate on January 15, 2021, that his Twitter ban had lifted and he was happy to make death threats before being suspended again.
- Defendant A pleaded guilty to Counts 1, 2, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, and 13 on the morning of trial in December 2022.

- Prior to trial, Count 3 (18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2)) was dismissed by the Court and remained on interlocutory appeal; Count 6 (18 U.S.C. § 875(c)) was also dismissed.
- Defendant A admitted that the January 6 events constituted a civil disorder involving acts of violence and that commerce and a federally protected function were affected.
- Defendant A acknowledged he knowingly entered and remained in a restricted building and grounds without authority on January 6.
- Defendant A interfered with and impeded officers outside the Rotunda doors as they attempted to prevent entry into the Capitol.
- Defendant A admitted he intended to impede or disrupt the counting of the Electoral College vote.
- Inside the Rotunda, Defendant A engaged in disruptive conduct that interrupted the normal course of government process.
- Defendant A’s conduct in passing officers at the Rotunda Doors interfered with officers engaged in lawful duties.
- Defendant A admitted tweeting at the Congresswoman while knowing the attack was perceived as a threat of violence toward members of Congress.

- The government identified statutory maximum imprisonment terms per count: Counts 1, 2, 13 up to 5 years each; Count 4 up to 8 years; Count 5 up to 5 years; Counts 7–9 up to 1 year each; Counts 10–12 up to 6 months each.
