- The defendant agreed to plead guilty to Count 3 of the First Superseding Indictment charging obstruction of law enforcement during a civil disorder and aiding and abetting, in violation of 18 U.S.C. §§ 231(a)(3) and 2.
- The charged offense carries a statutory maximum of 5 years of imprisonment.
- The charged offense carries a statutory maximum fine of $250,000.
- The charged offense allows a maximum supervised release term of up to 3 years.
- The defendant must pay a $100 special assessment for the felony conviction.
- The government will request dismissal of the remaining counts of the First Superseding Indictment at the time of sentencing.
- The defendant agreed that the Statement of Offense fairly and accurately describes the defendant’s actions and involvement in the offense.
- The defendant agreed to pay restitution pursuant to 18 U.S.C. § 3663(a)(3).
- The defendant acknowledged that the January 6, 2021 riot caused approximately $1,495,326.55 in damage to the U.S. Capitol as of May 17, 2021.
- The defendant agreed to pay $2,000 in restitution to the Architect of the Capitol.
- The defendant agreed to disclose all assets and complete a financial statement for restitution and fine assessment.
- Restitution and fines imposed by the Court will be due and payable immediately, and the defendant agreed to participate in the Inmate Financial Responsibility Program if incarcerated.
- As of the plea, the government understood that the defendant had no prior criminal convictions.
- The defendant was a first-degree member of the Proud Boys who lived in Missouri.
- Beginning in or around December 2020, the defendant and others discussed traveling to Washington, D.C., on January 6, 2021 to show support for a political figure and protest the certification of the Electoral College.
- Trip planning communications included discussions about engaging in violence with perceived opponents, obtaining two-way radios, medical supplies, weapons, and concealing identities.
- On January 4, 2021, the defendant traveled from the Kansas City area to the Washington, D.C. metropolitan area with others.
- The defendant brought a handgun on the trip but did not bring it into Washington, D.C.
- On January 5, 2021, the defendant purchased and modified an axe handle to be used as a walking stick and improvised weapon.
- Orange fluorescent tape was obtained at the defendant’s suggestion so group members could identify each other in a crowd.
- At a January 5, 2021 meeting, another individual advocated using force; the defendant was shocked and understood this to mean force against the government.
- On the morning of January 6, 2021, the defendant and others met a larger group at the Washington Monument and marched toward the U.S. Capitol.
- While marching, the group shouted, “Whose streets? Our streets.”
- The defendant and the group moved to the west side of the Capitol grounds outside a restricted perimeter with barricades staffed by U.S. Capitol Police.
- The defendant wore a backpack, pocket knife, tactical vest, tactical gloves, boots, and a helmet marked with orange tape.
- The defendant and others proceeded past police barricades toward the Capitol’s West Plaza.
- The defendant climbed a wall to reach a higher exterior level of the Capitol grounds.
- Around 2:00 PM on January 6, 2021, the defendant unlawfully entered the U.S. Capitol building.
- Inside the Capitol near retractable doors being lowered by police, the defendant used his hands to stop a door and placed a chair in its path to prevent closure.
- In the same area, other rioters placed a podium to block another door.
- Around 2:50 PM, the defendant exited the Capitol building and later left the restricted grounds.
- The defendant knowingly obstructed, impeded, and interfered with law enforcement officers who were engaged in their official duties during a civil disorder inside the Capitol.
- On January 6, 2021, the exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to the public, with security barriers and access restrictions in place.
- A joint session of Congress convened around 1:00 PM on January 6, 2021 to certify the Electoral College vote, with the Vice President present and presiding.
- Around 2:00 PM on January 6, 2021, individuals forced their way through barricades on the Capitol grounds, causing officers to retreat and the crowd to advance.
- Shortly after 2:00 PM, individuals forced entry into the Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement.
- Around 2:20 PM on January 6, 2021, members of the House and Senate, including the Vice President, evacuated; proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 PM and then resumed.
- The January 6 riot resulted in substantial damage to the Capitol, requiring expenditures of more than $1.4 million for repairs.
- Pre-planning communications included the defendant asking about bringing "flags and flagstaffs" in a discussion of what weapons were permitted in the District of Columbia.
- The defendant expressed a willingness to “fight for freedom,” clarifying that he meant showing up and being present without being violent.
- Members of the group used orange tape as an identifier while marching toward the Capitol on January 6.
- The group arrived at the First Street gate near Peace Circle as Congress was preparing to begin certification proceedings on January 6, 2021.
- At Peace Circle there were multiple lines of barricades, including bike racks and temporary fencing, manned by U.S. Capitol Police; signs read “Area Closed.”
- After barricades were toppled and police retreated at Peace Circle, the defendant stepped over a downed fence and moved toward the Capitol.
- The crowd briefly encountered another police line at a black metal fence before progressing onto the lower west terrace.
- The defendant remained on the west front for more than an hour as the crowd fought with police, who deployed projectiles and chemical irritants.
- The building was first breached by rioters breaking windows; the defendant entered shortly after the initial breach.
- A group including the defendant chased police out of the Crypt into a passageway toward the Capitol Visitor Center as a large metal door began lowering from the ceiling.
- Rioters threw a trash can at officers and attempted to slide furniture into the path of the lowering door.
- Members of the group dragged a movable podium to block the door; the defendant gestured in support.
- The defendant placed a chair into the path of another door in the same area.
- The defendant and others moved into the Capitol Visitor Center and remained briefly before leaving the building.
- On January 12, 2022, a superseding indictment charged the defendant with conspiracy (18 U.S.C. § 371), obstruction of an official proceeding (18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2)), civil disorder (18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3)), entering and remaining in a restricted building (18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1)), and disorderly conduct in a restricted building (18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(2)).
- The defendant entered a guilty plea to the civil disorder charge on April 27, 2022.
- As of July 7, 2023, the government reported approximately $2,923,080.05 in losses resulting from the January 6 attack.
- The Metropolitan Police Department also suffered losses from January 6 and submitted approximately $629,056 in restitution amounts.
- The defendant agreed to extensive cooperation obligations, including answering questions, providing sworn statements, submitting to polygraph examinations, and testifying when requested.
- The defendant agreed to turn over evidence of crimes, contraband, proceeds, and assets traceable to proceeds upon request.
- The defendant agreed to provide a full accounting of all financial assets, including those held by third parties, and to be truthful in financial disclosures.
- The defendant agreed to abide by all release conditions imposed by the Court pending sentencing.
- The government agreed it may seek a motion for a downward departure for substantial assistance at its sole discretion if the defendant provides such assistance.
- The defendant signed the plea agreement and the Statement of Offense on April 22, 2022, and the documents were filed on April 27, 2022.
- The defendant disassociated from the Proud Boys after January 6, 2021, according to the government’s account.
- The defendant’s compliance with pretrial release conditions was described as excellent, with no noted infractions during supervision.
- During pretrial supervision, the defendant received permission on multiple occasions to travel to areas affected by hurricanes to restore electricity and complied with conditions.
- The defendant is approximately 47 years old and was born in Puerto Rico.
- The defendant is the oldest of four siblings and described his upbringing as positive with a supportive family.
- The defendant has three adult children from a prior marriage and is remarried with a step-daughter; a step-son passed away in January 2021.
- The defendant earned a high school diploma and an associate’s degree, and holds a journeyman license.
- The defendant is a member in good standing of IBEW Local 1464.
- At the time of sentencing filings, the defendant was unemployed due to lack of work and recovery from a recent knee surgery.
- The defense represented that the defendant has no history of drug or alcohol misuse.
- Letters submitted on the defendant’s behalf describe early and ongoing remorse for the conduct on January 6, 2021.

- The offense carries a maximum fine of $250,000 under 18 U.S.C. § 3571(b)(3).
- The offense carries a maximum supervised release term of up to 3 years under 18 U.S.C. § 3583(b)(2).

- Defendant A agreed that the attached Statement of Offense fairly and accurately describes the conduct and signed it as a written proffer of evidence.
- The government agreed to move to dismiss the remaining counts at sentencing in consideration for the guilty plea to Count Three.
- Defendant A acknowledged that the charges to be dismissed were based in fact.
- After the plea, the government agreed not to prosecute Defendant A for additional non-violent offenses within the District of Columbia committed before the agreement and disclosed before execution of the agreement, while reserving the right to prosecute crimes of violence.
- Defendant A agreed to allow agents to review any social media accounts operated by Defendant A for January 6, 2021 statements and postings and to participate in an interview before sentencing.
- Defendant A acknowledged that as of October 14, 2022, the U.S. Capitol sustained approximately $2,881,360.20 in damage from the January 6 riot.

- Defendant A authorized the U.S. Attorney’s Office to obtain a credit report to evaluate ability to satisfy financial obligations.

- Defendant A signed the Statement of Offense acknowledging it is true and accurate on 09/14/2023.
- Defendant A signed the plea agreement acceptance on 09/18/2023.
- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened at approximately 1:00 PM to certify the Electoral College vote, with the Vice President present.
- On January 6, 2021, the exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to the public and secured by barriers and U.S. Capitol Police.
- By shortly after 1:00 PM on January 6, a civil disorder occurred at the Capitol that obstructed the U.S. Secret Service’s protection of the Vice President.
- Around 2:00 PM on January 6, individuals forced entry into the Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement.
- The riot resulted in more than $2.8 million in damage to the Capitol requiring repairs.
- At approximately 2:20 PM on January 6, members of the House and Senate, including the Vice President, evacuated their chambers.
- Congressional proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 PM on January 6 and resumed once the building was secured.
- On the morning of January 6, Defendant A and Co-Defendant A met a group including several individuals from the Kansas City area who were members of the Proud Boys.
- Defendant A joined a larger Proud Boys group on the National Mall and marched while chanting slogans including “Whose streets? Our streets!”
- Defendant A and the group moved to the west side of the Capitol outside the restricted perimeter guarded by U.S. Capitol Police and chanted “Whose Capitol? Our Capitol!”
- Shortly before 1:00 PM, members of the crowd breached the first line of barriers, causing officers to run for safety.
- Defendant A, with others, was among the first to trample over toppled barricades at the initial police line.
- Defendant A proceeded past multiple subsequent lines of barricades and entered the Lower West Plaza inside the restricted area.
- Defendant A remained on the west front of the Capitol for more than one hour despite police efforts including orders to disperse and deployment of less-lethal munitions.
- Defendant A stayed close to a co-defendant who was instigating the crowd, including shouting “Don’t let them take him!” when officers attempted to arrest a rioter.
- With assistance from Co-Defendant A, Defendant A put on a helmet to help withstand police crowd-control measures.
- Near the inaugural scaffolding, Defendant A and others pushed with their bodies against barricades and officers at another police line.
- Defendant A reached the base of the Capitol and went onto the Upper West Terrace.
- The Senate Wing Door was opened from the inside after others broke windows and climbed in, and Defendant A entered through the doorway at approximately 2:25 PM.
- Inside the Capitol, Defendant A moved with others to the Crypt area.
- At the Crypt area, officers attempted to lower a large sliding metal barrier, which rioters blocked with chairs and a trash can within view of Defendant A.
- Rioters threw objects and sprayed chemical irritants at police near the barrier before officers retreated further into the building.
- After entering the Capitol Visitor Center, Defendant A returned to the Senate Wing Door and exited the building shortly thereafter.
- While inside the building, Defendant A took possession of a U.S. Capitol Police riot helmet and later brought it home.
- A family member later voluntarily turned the riot helmet over to law enforcement at Defendant A’s direction.
- Defendant A admitted the elements of the offense, including that the act was intended to obstruct, impede, or interfere with law enforcement during a civil disorder affecting a federally protected function.
- Defendant A is 28 years old.
- Defendant A was born in May 1995 and raised in Tucson, Arizona.
- Defendant A’s parents separated when Defendant A was approximately three years old.
- Defendant A was diagnosed with dyslexia and enrolled in special education classes.
- After high school, Defendant A attended one year of community college and withdrew.
- Defendant A is a carpenter by training and works in commercial and residential remodeling when available.
- At the time of arrest, Defendant A was unemployed.
- During the pandemic layoff period, Defendant A suffered from depression but did not receive treatment.
- Defendant A looks up to Co-Defendant A (sibling) and tends to be reserved in interactions with the sibling.
- Defendant A is not politically engaged and does not closely follow current events, according to the defense submission.
- Defendant A traveled to Washington, D.C., on January 6 at the request of Co-Defendant A (sibling).
- Defendant A followed a group including Proud Boys members toward the Capitol on January 6.
- Defendant A trampled overturned police barricades near the first police line around 1:00 PM and moved to the west front of the Capitol, where he remained for over an hour.
- Defendant A and Co-Defendant A pushed through another police line prior to entering the Capitol building.
- Inside the Capitol, officers attempted to lower a metal barrier at the Crypt; Co-Defendant A at one point used a hand to push up on the barrier.
- Defendant A entered the Capitol and then exited after a short time via the Senate Wing Door.
- Defendant A took a police riot helmet during the incident and later had a family member turn it over to law enforcement at Defendant A’s direction.
- According to the defense submission, Defendant A attempted to return the riot helmet to multiple officers at the scene, but they would not take it.
- Defendant A stated he is not a member of the Proud Boys, despite being with a group that included Proud Boys members on January 6.
- Defendant A participated in an hours-long interview and proffer session with prosecutors and federal agents early in the case, per the defense filing.
- At the time of the Probation Office’s recommendation described in the defense filing, Defendant A resided with a parent and was employed full-time.
- The Statement of Offense describes that officers from the Metropolitan Police Department were called to assist U.S. Capitol Police during the events.
- Individuals who entered the Capitol on January 6 did so without security screenings or weapons checks.
- The Vice President remained in the Capitol from the time of evacuation until the session resumed after 8:00 PM on January 6.
- The plea offer letter was dated September 12, 2023, and stated that the offer would expire on September 13, 2023.

- The government agreed to request dismissal of the remaining counts in the superseding indictment at sentencing.

- The statutory maximum imprisonment for threatening a federal officer under 18 U.S.C. § 115(a)(1)(B) is 6 years.
- Each count carries a maximum supervised release term of up to three years.
- Each count carries a maximum fine of up to $250,000.
- Defendant A must pay a special assessment of $100 per felony conviction.
- As of July 7, 2023, the January 6 riot caused approximately $2,923,080.05 in damage to the U.S. Capitol, plus additional MPD losses.
- Defendant A agreed to pay $2,000 in restitution to the Architect of the Capitol.

- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened to certify the Electoral College vote, beginning around 1:00 PM.
- Around 2:20 PM on January 6, members of the House and Senate, including the Vice President, were evacuated from their chambers.
- Congressional proceedings were suspended until approximately 8:00 PM on January 6 due to the unlawful entry and security concerns.
- The riot resulted in more than $2.8 million in damage requiring repairs to the U.S. Capitol.
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant A was a second-degree member of the Proud Boys in the Kansas City chapter.
- Beginning in December 2020, Defendant A and associates planned to travel to Washington, D.C. to protest Congress’s certification.
- Pre-trip communications discussed engaging in violence, bringing radios, medical supplies, weapons, and concealing identities.
- Defendant A asked about firearms and other weapons and suggested using wooden flag staffs in place of firearms.
- Between January 4 and 5, Defendant A drove from the Kansas City area to the Washington, D.C. metro area.
- On January 5, Defendant A arrived at a rented house in Arlington, Virginia referred to as a “safe house,” where a meeting was held that evening.
- Early on January 6, Defendant A and associates traveled to Washington, D.C. and met a large group at the Washington Monument.
- On January 6, Defendant A wore a tactical vest and protective gloves and carried a gas mask and a wooden axe handle with a flag attached.
- Defendant A marched near the U.S. Capitol chanting slogans and moved to the west side outside the restricted perimeter.
- Defendant A later said he understood the group went to the Capitol because the Vice President would not "do the right thing" with the votes.
- Shortly before 1:00 PM, the crowd breached barriers and Defendant A moved forward with them.
- At the front of the crowd, Defendant A waved others forward and shouted "Go! Go! Go!"
- Defendant A yelled, "Don't let them take him!" when seeing officers attempting to arrest a rioter.
- Defendant A remained on the west front for more than an hour while officers attempted to disperse the crowd.
- Defendant A recorded videos bragging about having "stormed the Capitol" and said, "The cops know we have them."
- From the front of the crowd, Defendant A led chants such as "Whose country is this!? Our country!" and encouraged others to "Take it!"
- While near a USCP police line, Defendant A pointed toward officers and shouted, "Hey, if you shoot I'll... take your ass out."
- Defendant A reached the Capitol’s Upper West Terrace and entered through the Senate Wing Door at approximately 2:25 PM.
- Inside the Capitol, Defendant A led a chant of "Whose house!? Our house!"
- In the Crypt area, a sliding metal barrier was being lowered as officers retreated; rioters stopped the door from closing by placing objects in its path.
- Defendant A used his axe handle to press up on the metal door and moved a smaller chair to make room for a lectern to block the door.
- Defendant A later described blocking the emergency doors, saying they were "throwing... chairs under there to block it."
- In the Capitol Visitor Center, Defendant A grabbed an individual whom officers were attempting to arrest, and that person escaped.
- Defendant A exited the Capitol through the Senate Wing Door approximately 26 minutes after entering, around 2:51 PM.
- Defendant A later stated on a recorded call, "We stormed the Capitol Building... We made the House... leave."
- Recordings of Defendant A’s post-riot telephone calls were found on his phone by law enforcement.
- On January 6, Defendant A purchased and carried an approximately two-foot-long wooden axe handle, disguised with an American flag.
- Defendant A wore a helmet with a strip of orange duct tape used for group identification.
- Defendant A helped push down a barricade at the Peace Circle during the initial breach of the restricted perimeter.
- Defendant A turned toward the crowd and waved more rioters forward on the West Front after officers ordered the crowd to turn around.
- Defendant A told nearby rioters, "Don't let them take him," when officers detained a rioter on the West Front.
- After police deployed less-lethal munitions on the West Front, Defendant A pointed his axe handle at officers and issued a violent threat.
- Defendant A rallied the crowd with call-and-response chants encouraging taking "your house" while officers tried to hold their line.
- Defendant A wore a gas mask during confrontations on the West Front as he gestured with the axe handle toward officers.
- While ascending toward the Capitol, Defendant A proclaimed leadership in the initial breach, saying, "We were the ones in front."
- Defendant A thanked bystanders for helping his group as they moved toward the Capitol.
- Inside the Capitol, Defendant A helped hold open sliding metal barriers to allow rioters to pass into the Capitol Visitor Center.
- When officers attempted to detain a rioter in the Capitol Visitor Center, Defendant A moved into the area while still holding the axe handle.
- Defendant A later said he and others made officers "legitimately scared for their... lives" during the events inside.
- On January 12, 2022, a superseding indictment charged Defendant A with six counts including conspiracy, obstruction, civil disorder, threatening a federal officer, and two restricted-area-with-weapon offenses.
- On October 16, 2023, Defendant A pleaded guilty to obstruction of an official proceeding and threatening a federal officer pursuant to a plea agreement.
- The January 6 riot injured more than one hundred police officers.
- As of July 7, 2023, the losses from the January 6 attack were approximately $2,923,080.05, based on government agency estimates.
- Defendant A previously served in the U.S. Army as a medic from 1992 to 1996 and was stationed in Hawaii.
- Defendant A was wearing gloves with knuckle guards on January 6.
- Defendant A used orange duct tape for group cohesion and identification among his associates on January 6.
- Defendant A’s post-plea Statement of Offense was signed and acknowledged by him on October 16, 2023.

- On January 17, 2018, Defendant A traveled from New Jersey to Florida and discharged a Glock 19 9mm pistol during firearms training at a Fort Lauderdale range.
- The Glock 19 pistol used by Defendant A was manufactured outside Florida and had traveled in interstate commerce before January 17, 2018.
- About a week after the training, Defendant A applied to the Florida Department of Agriculture and Consumer Services, Division of Licensing, for a concealed weapon license.
- In the concealed weapon license application, Defendant A affirmed that he had completed firearms training and provided the certificate of completion.
- In the concealed weapon license application, Defendant A affirmed that he had never been convicted of a felony.
- Certified 2007 and 2008 court judgments and transcripts showed that Defendant A had been found guilty of three felony offenses in New Jersey state court before 2018.
- Defendant A knew about his New Jersey felony convictions before 2018 because he had been advised of the convictions during court proceedings.
- On February 12, 2018, the Florida licensing authority denied Defendant A’s concealed weapon license application, citing his three New Jersey felony convictions.
- On March 2, 2021, Defendant A was arrested at his residence in Fort Lauderdale, Florida.
- Defendant A was the only person home during the March 2, 2021 arrest, and his landlord later confirmed he was the sole resident at that address.
- When law enforcement arrived and demanded that he exit, Defendant A briefly emerged, retreated back into the house, then reemerged and surrendered.
- Immediately after Defendant A surrendered on March 2, 2021, authorities executed a federal search warrant at his residence.
- Law enforcement found 848 rounds of ammunition throughout the residence on March 2, 2021.
- The 848 rounds included 666 rounds of 5.56x45mm, 121 rounds of .223, and 61 rounds of 9mm ammunition.
- All of the ammunition found on March 2, 2021 was manufactured outside Florida and had traveled in interstate commerce before that date.
- In the laundry dryer, agents found a disassembled short-barreled rifle consisting of a lower receiver with collapsible stock and a separate upper receiver with a 10.5-inch barrel, next to a loaded magazine.
- None of the short-barreled rifle parts recovered from the dryer had serial numbers.
- Agents determined the parts could be assembled with minimal effort to form a rifle with a barrel less than 16 inches in length.
- The 10.5-inch barrel recovered was perceptibly shorter than 16 inches.
- A search of the National Firearms Registration and Transfer Record showed Defendant A had not registered any short-barreled rifle as required by the National Firearms Act.
- After waiving his Miranda rights, Defendant A gave a video-recorded statement following his March 2, 2021 arrest.
- Defendant A admitted he had purchased the ammunition found inside his residence.
- Defendant A admitted he built a rifle by purchasing parts and watching YouTube tutorials.
- Defendant A took the homemade rifle to a range in Florida and fired a round before it jammed.
- Defendant A acknowledged he took the January 17, 2018 firearms training, submitted the Florida concealed weapon license application soon afterward, and received a denial letter due to his felony convictions.
- Defendant A acknowledged he knew about his 2007 and 2008 New Jersey felony convictions but believed they did not matter to his recent possession because the convictions were old and from a different state.
- The parties stipulated that the factual proffered facts would prove Counts 1, 2, and 3 of the superseding indictment beyond a reasonable doubt.
- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Counts 1, 2, and 3 of the Superseding Indictment.
- Count 1 charges possession of a firearm by a convicted felon in violation of 18 U.S.C. §§ 922(g)(1) and 924(a)(2).
- Count 2 charges possession of ammunition by a convicted felon in violation of 18 U.S.C. §§ 922(g)(1) and 924(a)(2).
- Count 3 charges knowingly possessing an unregistered firearm in violation of 26 U.S.C. §§ 5841, 5861(d), and 5871.
- For each of Counts 1 and 2, the statutory maximum term of imprisonment is up to ten years.
- For each of Counts 1 and 2, the statutory maximum term of supervised release is up to three years.
- For each of Counts 1 and 2, the statutory maximum fine is up to $250,000.
- For Count 3, the statutory maximum term of imprisonment is up to ten years.
- For Count 3, the statutory maximum term of supervised release is up to three years.
- For Count 3, the statutory maximum fine is up to $10,000.
- A special assessment of $100 per count applies, totaling $300 if convicted on all three counts.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit a short-barreled rifle bearing no serial number.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit an upper receiver bearing no serial number.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit a pistol bearing no serial number.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit 863 rounds of ammunition in various calibers.
- The case began with an indictment charging Defendant A with unlawfully possessing a firearm in 2018.
- Before the February 2021 indictment, the FBI monitored Defendant A’s internet presence after receiving civilian complaints that he had threatened them with violence online.
- Defendant A regularly livestreamed to thousands of social media followers wearing outfits such as Nazi regalia and a bulletproof vest, while brandishing what appeared to be knives or firearms.
- During livestreams, Defendant A entered video chats with strangers he deemed undesirable and made graphic threats of violence and other invective.
- Defendant A appeared in videos posted to his social media where he directed confrontational verbal abuse toward individuals in public places.
- Defendant A encouraged his social media followers to harass perceived ideological enemies online.
- Central themes of Defendant A’s online presence included white supremacist ideology and advocacy for a second American civil war.
- Soon after the Capitol Riot, Defendant A posted a video musing that arrest would "accelerate" things and "radicalize" people, stating he was "all right" with that possibility.
- On March 2, 2021, an FBI SWAT team went to Defendant A’s home to execute a federal arrest warrant; he emerged and then retreated inside before later surrendering.
- Agents used stun grenades to clear Defendant A’s home before he surrendered on March 2, 2021.
- Defendant A was the only person inside his home when agents executed the arrest warrant on March 2, 2021.
- Inside Defendant A’s home, authorities found an unregistered short-barreled rifle, a handgun, a combat knife, and a tactical vest with plated body armor inserts.
- Inside the home, authorities found 787 rounds of rifle ammunition and 61 rounds of 9mm ammunition as described in the presentence materials.
- After arrest, Defendant A admitted he sought the Florida concealed weapon license despite earlier felony convictions, which he downplayed as old and from another state.
- Defendant A admitted he bought ammunition and firearms parts at gun shows and assembled firearms using YouTube tutorials.
- Defendant A said he was interested in building firearms because he considered it an important skill given a perceived impending collapse of American society.
- Defendant A admitted to his internet presence but characterized it as comedy intended for monetization rather than action.
- Defendant A told agents that when his approximately 45,000 followers heard about his case, it was going to be "really wild."
- Defendant A questioned the seriousness of his offenses and expressed disbelief at being prosecuted.
- In March 2021, a grand jury returned a superseding indictment charging Defendant A’s 2021 possession of ammunition and a short-barreled rifle in addition to the 2018 offense.
- Defendant A pled guilty to all three counts of the superseding indictment during a virtual hearing.
- The change-of-plea hearing was repeatedly interrupted by individuals making racially charged statements similar to those Defendant A had made online.
- On the day of the change-of-plea hearing, video clips of the proceeding appeared on a public Twitter account.
- The same day, a two-minute video of an apparent prison call by Defendant A appeared online, including his statement that "if everything goes well, I'll serve around 18 months."
- A longer video recording included Defendant A characterizing himself as a "political prisoner."
- In the longer video, Defendant A lamented that his life had been ruined and stated he was trying to help a cause and people.
- In the longer video, Defendant A instructed another person about communicating to his followers on a reconstituted social media chat.
- Defendant A admitted he came to South Florida months before his arrest in part because he believed Florida was more permissive than New Jersey.

- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened at the U.S. Capitol to certify the Electoral College vote and began at approximately 1:00 p.m.
- Around 1:30 p.m. on January 6, 2021, the House and Senate adjourned to separate chambers to resolve an objection while the Vice President presided.
- The U.S. Capitol is secured 24 hours a day by U.S. Capitol Police and only authorized people with identification are allowed inside.
- On January 6, 2021, the exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to members of the public.
- Temporary and permanent barricades were in place and U.S. Capitol Police attempted to keep the crowd away from the Capitol building.
- Shortly after 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals in the crowd forced entry into the Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement, as others encouraged and assisted.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m., members of the House and Senate, including the presiding officer, evacuated their chambers and proceedings were suspended until around 8:00 p.m.
- Congressional proceedings could not resume until the building was cleared of unauthorized occupants and secured.
- The January 6 breach resulted in substantial damage to the Capitol and required more than $1.4 million for repairs.
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant A entered the U.S. Capitol Building at approximately 2:50 p.m.
- Defendant A traveled to Washington, D.C. by bus from Tampa, Florida prior to entering the Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant A entered the Senate chamber at approximately 3:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant A exited the Senate chamber and the U.S. Capitol Building at approximately 3:15 p.m. on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant A carried a red flag with the words "Trump 2020" in white letters while inside the Capitol.
- Defendant A wore a dark T‑shirt with the word "Trump" in white letters while inside the Capitol.
- Defendant A carried a backpack containing protective eye goggles and white latex gloves on January 6, 2021.
- While in the Senate chamber, Defendant A put on and removed protective eye goggles.
- While in the Senate chamber, Defendant A took selfie‑style photographs with a cell phone.
- While in the Senate chamber, Defendant A put on and then removed white latex gloves.
- Defendant A walked to the well of the Senate and stood adjacent to the elevated desk and platform.
- Defendant A stood with a group in the Senate well where one individual used a bullhorn to shout, cheer, and pray.
- Defendant A raised his flag in salute while in the Senate chamber.
- Defendant A knew he did not have permission to enter the U.S. Capitol Building on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant A acted with the intent to corruptly obstruct, influence, and impede Congress’s certification of the Electoral College vote.
- Defendant A signed a Statement of Offense acknowledging it was true and accurate.
- A government filing states that Defendant A’s backpack contained rope in addition to protective goggles and latex gloves.
- A government filing notes that Defendant A did not personally engage in or espouse violence or property destruction on January 6, 2021.
- On January 26, 2021, Defendant A told agents he traveled alone by bus from Florida to Washington, D.C., and admitted he was the person in photographs taken in the Senate chamber.
- Defendant A told agents he saw others breaking windows, a knife fight, and injured individuals while inside the Capitol, but said he did not participate in that conduct.
- Capitol CCTV footage shows Defendant A in the Senate well holding a "TRUMP 2020" flag and interacting with others there.
- Capitol CCTV shows Defendant A putting on white latex gloves near a desk with papers while in the Senate chamber.
- A video published by a media outlet shows Defendant A in the well of the Senate chamber on January 6, 2021.
- An FBI tipster provided a selfie‑style image of Defendant A that was circulated on a social networking application and used for identification.
- An FBI agent compared the received image to a driver’s license photograph and believed it to be the same person.
- On February 9, 2021, a magistrate judge attested to the affidavit by telephone pursuant to Fed. R. Crim. P. 4.1.
- The affidavit asserted probable cause that Defendant A violated 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c) and 2, 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1) and (2), and 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D) and (G).
- Defendant A agreed in a plea agreement to plead guilty to obstruction of an official proceeding under 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2).
- The offense of conviction carries a statutory maximum of 20 years of imprisonment.
- The offense of conviction permits a fine up to $250,000 or twice the pecuniary gain or loss.
- The offense of conviction permits a term of supervised release of up to 3 years.
- Defendant A agreed to pay a $100 special assessment for the felony conviction.
- The government agreed to move to dismiss the remaining counts at sentencing in consideration of the guilty plea to Count One.
- Defendant A acknowledged that, as of May 17, 2021, the riot caused approximately $1,495,326.55 in damage to the U.S. Capitol.
- Defendant A agreed to pay $2,000 in restitution to the Department of the Treasury.
- Defendant A agreed to disclose all assets and submit a completed financial disclosure form to the Financial Litigation Unit 30 days prior to sentencing.

- On March 29, 2022, Defendant A pled guilty to interfering with a law enforcement officer during a civil disorder, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3).
- The maximum statutory penalty for the § 231(a)(3) offense includes up to 5 years of imprisonment, a fine up to $250,000, and up to 3 years of supervised release, plus a $100 special assessment.
- As part of the plea, the government agreed to move to dismiss the remaining counts at sentencing.
- Defendant A agreed to pay $2,000 in restitution to the Department of the Treasury related to the Capitol riot damages.
- On January 6, 2021, around 5:47 p.m., Defendant A positioned himself between MPD officers and a crowd on the West Lawn of the U.S. Capitol while waving a Confederate battle flag attached to a lacrosse stick.
- Defendant A yelled words to the effect of urging others not to move back as officers advanced, including statements like "quit backing up" and "don’t be scared."
- An MPD officer used a baton to push Defendant A back toward the crowd, after which Defendant A squared up and challenged the officer verbally.
- Defendant A thrust the lacrosse-stick-mounted flag into Officer K.P.’s chest area, making contact.
- Immediately after striking the officer, Defendant A was restrained on the ground by multiple officers and removed from the West Lawn.
- While handcuffed and unprompted, Defendant A made a spontaneous statement acknowledging what he did and apologizing.
- During a search incident to arrest, officers recovered a black backpack with 'TEMPO' lettering from Defendant A.
- A silver knife was recovered from Defendant A’s backpack during the search incident to arrest.
- Defendant A told officers he had the knife because he was worried about being attacked by Antifa or other people.
- A roll of tape was also found in Defendant A’s backpack, which he acknowledged he used to tape the flag to the lacrosse stick.
- Defendant A was transported to George Washington University Hospital for head lacerations sustained after officers responded with batons.
- On January 21, 2021, a federal search warrant was issued for Defendant A’s residence in the District of Maryland.
- On January 22, 2021, the warrant was executed at Defendant A’s residence; Defendant A and his mother were present.
- Clothing consistent with what Defendant A wore on January 6 (including a hoodie, jacket, gloves) and a 'Tempo' bag were seized from his bedroom.
- A notebook with 'Save USA' at the top and entries such as 'RIP Covid victims, Officer Brian Sicknick, David Dorn' was found in Defendant A’s bedroom.
- An AR-15 style rifle and a loaded rifle magazine were observed on Defendant A’s bed; paperwork showed they were registered to Defendant A and were not seized.
- A fully assembled lacrosse stick was observed in Defendant A’s bedroom during the search.
- Brass knuckles with a confederate flag print were located in a kitchen drawer at the residence.
- Rifle cartridges and a rifle stock were seen on a desk in the basement of the residence.
- At the time the federal complaint was submitted, Defendant A had a pending D.C. Superior Court misdemeanor assault-on-officer case related to the January 6 arrest, which the government intended to dismiss if the federal complaint issued.
- The affiant articulated probable cause that Defendant A violated 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1), (2), and (4).
- The affiant articulated probable cause that Defendant A violated 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D) and (F).
- The affiant articulated probable cause that Defendant A violated 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3) (civil disorder).
- The affiant articulated probable cause that Defendant A violated 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1) (assaulting, resisting, or impeding an officer).
- On November 10, 2021, a superseding indictment charged Defendant A with multiple counts, including 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1) and (b) (assault on an officer with a dangerous weapon), 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3), 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2), and 18 U.S.C. § 1752 related offenses with a deadly or dangerous weapon.
- The superseding indictment also charged violations of 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(1)(A)(i), § 5104(e)(2)(D), and § 5104(e)(2)(F).
- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened around 1:00 p.m. to certify the Electoral College vote.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m. on January 6, 2021, members of the House and Senate, including the Vice President, were evacuated, and proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- The Statement of Offense reports the riot caused more than $1.4 million in damage to the U.S. Capitol.
- The government later reported approximate losses from the Capitol siege at $2,734,783.15 as of April 5, 2022.
- The civil disturbance on January 6 affected interstate commerce, including drastically reducing same-day sales at Safeway stores in Washington, D.C.
- On February 15, 2021, an arrest warrant was executed for Defendant A; he surrendered through counsel, waived Miranda rights, and was interviewed.
- In his post-arrest interview, Defendant A stated he left his home in Clarksburg around 3 or 3:30 p.m., parked near L Street in Washington, D.C., and walked about an hour to the Capitol grounds.
- In the interview, Defendant A claimed an officer pushed him from behind and that he then "cross check[ed]" the officer with the lacrosse stick.
- Defendant A told agents he traveled to D.C. on January 6 to "fight Antifa" and not to enter the Capitol.
- Defendant A reported prior online discussions involving anti-Semitic remarks and beliefs about "bankers" running the country, which contributed to his view that he needed to "stand up" to Communism.
- The government noted Defendant A expressed remorse immediately after being detained on January 6, 2021.
- The government reported that Defendant A cooperated by assisting during the residence search and later reviewing his social media with an FBI agent.
- On January 6, 2021, the U.S. Capitol grounds and exterior plaza were closed to the public, and access to the building required authorization and screening.
- Rioters breached barricades and forced entry into the Capitol building after 2:00 p.m., with assaults on law enforcement occurring as others encouraged those acts.
- On the West Lawn at about 5:44 p.m., MPD Civil Disobedience Unit officers advanced in a line instructing rioters to "move back" while holding batons laterally.
- When Officer K.P. advanced again, Defendant A pushed his lacrosse stick into the officer’s upper chest area before being detained and arrested.
- The knife recovered from Defendant A’s backpack had a serrated blade approximately four inches long.
- In the notebook found at the residence, Defendant A wrote about not being there for violence or overthrow and about polarization and normalization of political violence.
- According to the government, Officer K.P. did not suffer bodily injury from Defendant A’s act of physical interference.
- Defendant A did not enter the Capitol building on January 6, 2021, with his conduct occurring on the West Lawn.
- Defendant A admitted post-incident that he used duct tape to attach the flag to the lacrosse stick carried on January 6.
- During the residence search, Defendant A volunteered that he had arranged items on his bed because he anticipated agents would be looking for them.
- Defendant A acknowledged in the Statement of Offense that he obstructed, impeded, or interfered with law enforcement during a civil disorder affecting commerce and a federally protected function.
- The plea agreement noted that, based on information then available, Defendant A had no prior criminal convictions.
- The exterior plaza of the Capitol and its grounds were secured by U.S. Capitol Police and closed to the public on January 6, 2021.
- After being detained on January 6, Defendant A apologized to officers within minutes.
- Defendant A sustained scalp lacerations on January 6 that required staples and sutures and was diagnosed with a concussion at the hospital that evening.
- Following January 6, Defendant A reported post-concussion symptoms including headaches and underwent a neurology consultation in January 2021.

- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened at the U.S. Capitol to certify the 2020 Electoral College vote.
- Vice President was present and presiding during the joint session and then in the Senate chamber on January 6, 2021.
- The Capitol building and exterior plaza were restricted to authorized persons on January 6, 2021, with permanent and temporary security barriers in place.
- Shortly after 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals forced entry into the U.S. Capitol, including by breaking windows and assaulting police.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m. on January 6, 2021, members of the House and Senate evacuated and the joint session was suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- On January 16, 2021, the FBI posted a BOLO seeking public assistance identifying individuals who unlawfully entered the Capitol and assaulted law enforcement.
- On January 17, 2021, Defendant A, through counsel, identified himself to the FBI as the person depicted in BOLO #105.
- BOLO #105 image came from a Metropolitan Police Department body-worn camera showing individuals pushing a barricade into MPD officers.
- Body-worn camera footage shows a red-hat individual telling the crowd to push officers and counting down '1, 2, 3 go' immediately before the push.
- Defendant A placed a gloved hand on a metal bike-rack barricade and, with others, pushed it toward police.
- The crowd lifted the bike rack and attempted to duck under it to break the police line.
- Police used chemical irritants to stop the group from breaking through the police line at the bike racks.
- After the confrontation at the bike racks, Defendant A remained in the area and at one point turned to the crowd and waved a flag while the crowd chanted anti-police slogans.
- Investigators matched Defendant A’s DMV photograph to images from MPD body-worn camera and his LinkedIn profile.
- Defendant A voluntarily surrendered to the FBI on January 18, 2021.
- A complaint alleged that Defendant A violated 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3) (civil disorder) and he was released on bond with conditions.
- On November 10, 2021, a second superseding indictment charged Defendant A with eight counts including obstruction of an official proceeding, assault on officers, civil disorder, restricted-grounds offenses, and Capitol-grounds misdemeanors.
- At a bench trial, the court convicted Defendant A on Counts 1 through 6 and Count 8; Count 7 was dismissed before trial.
- A sentencing hearing for Defendant A was scheduled for September 26, 2023.
- As of July 7, 2023, approximate losses from January 6 were reported at $2,923,080.05, reflecting damage to the Capitol and costs borne by the U.S. Capitol Police.
- Defendant A traveled from Pennsylvania to the Washington, D.C. area on January 5, 2021 and attended the rally near the Washington Monument on January 6.
- After the rally, Defendant A marched with others to the Capitol grounds and moved to the police line on the West Plaza.
- At approximately 2:35 p.m., rioters overwhelmed the police line on the West Plaza; police retreated up the southwest stairs and Defendant A followed moments later.
- Defendant A climbed through inaugural stage scaffolding and reached the top of the southwest stairs where a police barricade of bike racks had been established.
- The barricade at the top of the southwest stairs consisted of two rows of bike racks, including a rack with an 'AREA CLOSED' sign, with police officers behind them.
- While at the barricade, Defendant A filmed the police line, chanted 'let us in,' and cheered as another rioter sprayed the police line with a fire extinguisher.
- At about 2:45 p.m., Co-Defendant C used a bullhorn to urge rioters to 'pack it in' and then counted 'one, two, three, GO' immediately before the push at the bike racks.
- At approximately 2:45 p.m., Defendants A and B placed their hands on the bike rack and pushed it forcibly into the police line for at least 15 seconds, requiring additional officers to reinforce the line.
- During the push, the bike rack struck Officer A’s leg, causing him to stumble away from the line and fall to the ground.
- Officer A testified he experienced pain in his leg from being struck and had to sit down briefly before returning to duty.
- Officer B described the assault at the bike racks as frightening and believed officers might die.
- After the assault at the bike racks, Defendant A remained on the southwest stairs for at least 90 minutes and was sprayed multiple times with OC spray during another assault nearby around 3:19 p.m.
- Around 4:20 p.m., Defendant A moved to a ledge overlooking the Lower West Terrace 'tunnel' and watched rioters drag an officer into the crowd.
- At approximately 5:10 p.m., police removed Defendant A from the Upper West Terrace; he remained on restricted grounds until after dark and was removed from the West Lawn by officers.
- Law enforcement obtained consent to search Defendant A’s phone and found screenshots of social media posts about alleged election fraud and text updates about the certification proceeding on January 6.
- Defendant A received a text message at approximately 2:33 p.m. stating 'US Capitol is on lockdown.'
- Although video evidence showed Defendant A filming at multiple points on January 6, no corresponding videos from those times were recovered from his phone.
- Defendant A admitted at trial that he was filming on his phone during times depicted in images introduced as trial exhibits.
- Metadata showed Defendant A took a screenshot of the FBI BOLO poster including his picture on January 17, 2021, one day before counsel contacted the FBI about his self-surrender.
- The Court found portions of Defendant A’s trial testimony not credible on several topics, including his claimed intent in pushing the bike rack and claimed ignorance of the certification proceeding.
- The Court found that Defendants A and B pushed into the police line with the purpose of obstructing or impeding the congressional proceeding.
- The Court found the defendants’ actions contributed to forcing evacuation of Congress and halting the certification session, which later resumed.
- Defendant A set up an online fundraising page titled 'January 6th Legal Fund' that displayed a balance of $1,865 and included images from January 6.
- Defendant A posted in response to a donation, 'No problem I will do whatever it takes to defend my country.'
- Statutory maximum penalties for Defendant A’s counts of conviction include: up to 20 years for obstruction of an official proceeding (18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2)); up to 8 years for assault on a federal officer (18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1)); up to 5 years for civil disorder (18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3)).
- Statutory maximum penalties for the restricted-grounds misdemeanors include up to one year each for 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1), § 1752(a)(2), and § 1752(a)(4).
- The Class B misdemeanor under 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(F) carries up to six months’ imprisonment.
- Neither Defendant A nor Defendant B had prior criminal convictions reported in the presentence materials.
- Defendant A reported having a bachelor’s degree in criminal justice and employment with a landscaping company at the time of sentencing.
- Defendant A told the court he had a 'great' childhood and denied experiencing abuse or neglect.
- At trial, Defendant A admitted he knew the rally was about alleged election fraud and that there was work occurring inside the Capitol on January 6.
- Defendant A admitted he moved up from several steps below to the top stair immediately before placing his hands on the bike rack and keeping contact for at least 10 seconds during the push.
- Defendant A claimed at trial he did not see the 'AREA CLOSED' sign affixed to the barricade despite videos showing him standing directly in front of and touching the sign.
- Co-Defendant B followed the police retreat up the southwest stairs at approximately 2:35 p.m., filming on his phone as he climbed through the scaffolding.
- At the top of the southwest stairs, Co-Defendant B stood in front of the barricade and pushed with Co-Defendant C’s '1, 2, 3, GO' count, then attempted to duck underneath the raised rack.
- Co-Defendant B recorded multiple videos near the 'tunnel' area and posted some on social media, including narration that referred to a 'fucking revolution.'
- Around 5:00 p.m., police attempting to clear the Upper West Terrace ordered Co-Defendant B to get down from a ledge; he refused until physically pulled down and then joked about taking a selfie.
- Law enforcement did not recover from Co-Defendant B’s phone any videos taken at the police line at the top of the stairs where he pushed the bike rack, despite BWC showing him filming there.
- On January 26, Co-Defendant B texted a friend in law enforcement asking if there was a warrant for him; the friend confirmed he was wanted by the FBI.
- Co-Defendant B was arrested on March 6, 2021 after deplaning, rather than self-surrendering.
- On January 6, Co-Defendant B texted acquaintances updates such as 'They're storming the capital' and that he had been sprayed in D.C.
- In early January 2021, Co-Defendant B sent messages advocating violence, including 'Crimes have to be committed to start a revolution' and 'blood shed is the only way now.'
- Co-Defendant B posted on social media after January 6 that 'A revolution never started peacefully' and described the day as 'Definitely wild and a little bloody. Needed to happen.'
- The government’s memorandum states more than one hundred police officers were injured during the January 6 attack.
- Defendant A’s counsel stated Defendant A remained in compliance with release conditions from January 18, 2021 through sentencing.
- Defendant A created an online fundraiser that, according to defense counsel, was used for travel and accommodation expenses for attending the May 2023 trial.
- The police line at the top of the southwest stairs included uniformed Metropolitan Police Department officers behind the bike racks.
- The Court severed the trial of Co-Defendant C; his trial was to be scheduled later following a mental competency evaluation.
- During trial, Defendant A claimed he held the rack to protect himself and an elderly woman, and that his 'woo hoo' noises were a nervous tic; the court found those claims not credible.
- The government’s memorandum states Defendants A and B remained on the Upper West Terrace for extended periods after the initial push and had to be removed by officers.
- The defense memorandum notes Defendant A had seven minor traffic citations over a ten-year period and no significant criminal history.
- The charging statutes and maximum terms included: 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2) (20 years), 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1) (8 years), 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3) (5 years), 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1), (a)(2), (a)(4) (each up to 1 year), and 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(F) (6 months).
- The FBI understood that the joint session of Congress counting the Electoral College votes was a federally protected function implicated in 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3).
- Members of the Metropolitan Police Department responded to assist U.S. Capitol Police on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant A wore a camouflage-style jacket, a hoodie, and a blue skull cap with the word 'America' visible during the confrontation at the bike racks.
- Defendant A carried an American flag while standing against the police barricade just before the push.
- Video evidence depicted Defendant A placing his hand on the bike rack at the red-hat individual's '1, 2, 3 go' count, and then the crowd collectively shoving the barrier into officers.
- The government’s memorandum describes Defendant A cheering while filming a rioter spraying police with a fire extinguisher on the southwest stairs before the push.
- The Court found that by joining the mob and pushing the barricade into officers, Defendants A and B helped ensure Congress faced a sufficient security threat requiring adjournment.

- Defendant A waived indictment and agreed to plead guilty to an Information.
- Defendant A pleaded guilty to one count of wire fraud under 18 U.S.C. § 1343 and one count of money laundering under 18 U.S.C. § 1957.
- The statutory maximum penalty for the wire fraud count is 20 years' imprisonment.
- The statutory maximum penalty for the money laundering count is 10 years' imprisonment.
- Each count carries a potential fine of up to $250,000 and a $100 special assessment.
- Each count provides for up to three years of supervised release following imprisonment.
- Sentencing was scheduled for 9:00 a.m. on July 20, 2021.
- The Paycheck Protection Program (PPP) loans were processed by participating lenders, guaranteed by the SBA, and forgivable if used on permissible expenses such as payroll, mortgage, rent, and utilities.
- PPP applications required certifications including the number of employees and average monthly payroll, supported by documentation.
- Between April 26, 2020, and June 2, 2020, Defendant A submitted fraudulent PPP applications for eight loans for five different businesses.
- For Zuput, Inc., Defendant A applied for $150,000 at Bank of America and the loan was approved.
- For Zuput, Inc., Defendant A also applied for $223,727 at Endeavor Bank and that application was canceled.
- For Zigantic, LLC, Defendant A applied for $304,830 at Endeavor Bank and the loan was approved.
- For GitGrow, Inc., Defendant A applied for $506,277 at Endeavor Bank and the loan was approved.
- For Vangal, Inc., Defendant A applied for $1,728,398 at Endeavor Bank and later withdrew the application.
- For Expect Success, Inc., Defendant A applied for $1,794,700 at Neighborhood National Bank and the application was canceled.
- For Expect Success, Inc., Defendant A applied for $394,000 at Live Oak Banking and the loan was approved.
- For Mahenjo, Inc., Defendant A applied for $431,250 at Peoples Bank and the loan was approved.
- Defendant A falsely represented that each company was operating on February 15, 2020, and had employees or contractors.
- None of the companies had employees or contractors as of February 15, 2020, other than possibly Defendant A or immediate family.
- Defendant A submitted forged IRS Form 940s that falsely showed the businesses paid large amounts in 2019 wages and they were not filed with the IRS.
- Defendant A submitted fabricated payroll reports listing dozens of purported employees who were not actual employees.
- On May 22, 2020, Defendant A purchased Mahenjo, Inc. as a shelf corporation from a company that sells corporations.
- On June 3, 2020, Defendant A applied to Peoples Bank for a $431,250 PPP loan for Mahenjo.
- In the Mahenjo application, Defendant A falsely certified the company had employees and that he did not own or manage any other business.
- For Mahenjo, Defendant A submitted a fabricated 2019 IRS Form 940 claiming $2,376,500 in wages and two fake February 2020 payroll reports listing 24 purported employees.
- Defendant A submitted an altered Mahenjo incorporation document falsely indicating he was made initial director in 2017.
- The disbursement of the Mahenjo PPP loan caused an interstate wire transfer via Fedwire from Washington to California.
- Across the eight loan applications, Defendant A sought a total of $5,533,182.
- Defendant A received PPP loan proceeds totaling $1,786,357 from five approved loans.
- From the Zuput loan funds at Bank of America, $5,500 was transferred to Vangal’s account at Azlo.
- From the Zigantic loan proceeds at Azlo, $231,471 was transferred to a Robinhood account held by Defendant A in five transactions, including a $50,000 wire on May 26, 2020.
- Loan proceeds were deposited into business accounts at JPMorgan Chase for GitGrow ($506,277), Expect Success ($394,000), and Mahenjo ($431,250).
- Only $16,301.16 of the PPP loan proceeds were used by Defendant A before government seizure.
- Defendant A paid $16,301.16 to the Clerk of Court to cover the portion of proceeds that had been used.
- In July 2020, seizure warrants were executed and approximately $1,770,055.84 was seized from accounts holding PPP proceeds.
- The seized funds included $4,500 from Vangal’s Azlo account ending 0798 and $73,262.38 from Zigantic’s Azlo account ending 3419.
- The seized funds included $129,295.46 from Zuput’s Bank of America account ending 6319.
- The seized funds included $394,000 from Expect Success’s JPMorgan Chase account ending 8877.
- The seized funds included $431,250 from Mahenjo’s JPMorgan Chase account ending 9159.
- The seized funds included $506,277 from GitGrow’s JPMorgan Chase account ending 9773.
- The seized funds included $231,471 from Defendant A’s Robinhood account ending 4609.
- Defendant A agreed to pay restitution totaling $1,786,351 to identified lenders or the SBA if it paid those amounts to the lenders.
- The agreed restitution allocations were: Bank of America $150,000; Endeavor Bank $811,107; Live Oak Banking $394,000; Peoples Bank $431,250.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit the seized funds as property constituting or traceable to proceeds of wire fraud and property involved in money laundering.
- The Government indicated MLARS would consider restoration of forfeited funds to satisfy restitution if the Court imposed a $100,000 fine.
- Defendant A used the alias 'Mumo Patel' and the email account mumopatel@gmail.com in PPP lender communications.
- Defendant A used the alias 'Sam Perara' and the email account sam@vangal.com in PPP-related communications.
- Defendant A also used the aliases to obtain small consulting work and billed a referral company for that work.
- Defendant A received approximately $2,000 for consulting projects performed under fictitious names.
- Defendant A was employed as a senior executive at a Canadian e-commerce company and earned more than $200,000 annually prior to the case.
- Defendant A previously worked at technology companies including Amazon and Microsoft before the offense period.
- For Mahenjo, the PPP disbursement went to a JPMorgan Chase account ending in 9159.
- For Zuput, the $150,000 PPP loan proceeds were deposited into a Bank of America account ending in 6319.
- For Zigantic, $304,830 PPP proceeds were deposited into an Azlo account ending in 3419.
- For GitGrow, $506,277 PPP proceeds were deposited into a JPMorgan Chase account ending in 9773.
- For Expect Success, $394,000 PPP proceeds were deposited into a JPMorgan Chase account ending in 8877.
- The total PPP loan amount sought across all eight applications included duplicates for two companies and one withdrawn application.
- Defendant A caused interstate wire transmissions in connection with loan disbursements, including the Mahenjo Fedwire transfer between states.
- When agents arrived at Defendant A’s residence with warrants in July 2020, he fainted and later self-surrendered.
- Defendant A agreed not to dissipate assets except for routine expenses until 30 days after MLARS' decision on restoration.
- Defendant A agreed to fully assist in forfeiture, including executing documents and not contesting forfeiture proceedings.
- The PPP program exhausted its initial appropriation by April 16, 2020, and a second round of funding followed.
- Defendant A incorporated Vangal, Inc. in April 2020 before submitting PPP loan applications.
- Defendant A previously incorporated Zuput, Inc. in 2018 prior to the PPP applications.
- Defendant A agreed that restitution is due and payable immediately on entry of judgment, subject to a payment schedule if found unable to pay immediately.
- Defendant A agreed to disclose all assets and cooperate in financial disclosure and enforcement related to restitution.
- Defendant A acknowledged that supervised release violations could result in a return to prison for all or part of the term.
- Defendant A acknowledged that restitution may be ordered as part of the sentence.
- Defendant A admitted guilt to the offense conduct in the plea agreement’s Statement of Facts.
- The PPP lending resulted in five loans being approved and funded for Defendant A’s entities prior to seizure.
- Defendant A’s $231,471 transfer of PPP proceeds to a personal Robinhood account was a monetary transaction over $10,000 involving criminally derived property.

- Defendant A traveled from Tennessee to Washington, D.C. to attend a rally on January 6, 2021.
- On December 8, 2020, Defendant A posted on Facebook that a "Republican Rebellion is coming soon."
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant A attended the Stop the Steal rally and then walked to the U.S. Capitol.
- Defendant A unlawfully entered restricted U.S. Capitol grounds near the northwest scaffolding around 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021.
- Around 2:09 p.m. on January 6, 2021, Defendant A observed members of the crowd attacking law enforcement.
- Defendant A recorded attacks on law enforcement with his phone on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant A moved up to the northwest terrace by the Senate wing of the Capitol before entry.
- Defendant A entered the Capitol through a door that had been broken open by other rioters.
- Defendant A entered the Capitol moments after three co-defendants.
- Defendant A wore ski goggles and a respirator mask when he entered the Capitol.
- After entering, Defendant A moved through Statuary Hall.
- Defendant A proceeded to the Statuary Hall Connector and joined a group of rioters outside the House Chamber.
- Defendant A took a selfie photo with a co-defendant inside the Capitol.
- Defendant A remained inside the Capitol for approximately 24 minutes.
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant A posted a compilation of pictures on Facebook depicting the events of the day.
- Defendant A’s Facebook post from January 6, 2021 included the statement "We The People took the Capitol!"
- When Defendant A entered the U.S. Capitol on January 6, 2021, it was a restricted building.
- Defendant A knew he lacked lawful authority to enter the U.S. Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- On January 8, 2021, Defendant A texted that he had taken down his Facebook, Instagram, and Twitter accounts.
- On January 9, 2021, Defendant A texted a co-defendant claiming they were "PRESS documenting the event."
- On January 12, 2021, Defendant A asked a co-defendant to remove pictures and videos related to January 6 posts.
- On January 16, 2021, FBI agents executed an arrest warrant for Defendant A and a search warrant at his residence.
- When agents arrived to execute the warrants on January 16, 2021, they saw a handwritten note asking not to break down the door and referencing dogs and a garage code.
- During the search, agents seized one cell phone from Defendant A’s residence.
- Agents noticed a charger for a different phone that they did not find, identified as the phone used by Defendant A on January 6, 2021.
- The phone seized on January 16, 2021 was not Defendant A’s usual phone.
- On January 27, 2021, Defendant A messaged a friend that agents broke his front door and that agents only obtained a "$60 go phone from Walmart."
- After indictment, Defendant A turned over the phone he used on January 6, 2021 to the FBI.
- The phone turned over after indictment had no memory card or SIM card installed.
- Defendant A signed a Statement of Offense acknowledging that it is true and accurate.
- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Count Two of the Indictment: Entering and Remaining in a Restricted Building or Grounds, 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1).
- The offense of conviction carries a maximum penalty of one year of imprisonment.
- The offense of conviction carries a maximum fine of up to $100,000.
- The offense of conviction allows a term of supervised release of not more than one year.
- Defendant A agreed to pay a $25 special assessment for the Class A misdemeanor conviction.
- The government agreed to move to dismiss the remaining counts of the Indictment at sentencing.
- Defendant A agreed to pay $500 in restitution to the Architect of the Capitol.

- Law enforcement used tip information and social media posts to identify Defendant A as a participant in the Capitol breach.
- A screenshot from Defendant A’s Facebook account included the statement "We The People took the Capitol!"
- An open records search found a Pinterest account with a profile image matching Defendant A.
- LinkedIn profiles showed Defendant A and a co-defendant attended the same high school in the early 2000s.
- Investigators confirmed Defendant A’s identity by comparing a state driver’s license photo to a photo taken inside the Capitol.
- As Defendant A moved toward the Capitol, there were signs indicating the area was closed or restricted.
- Shortly before 2:00 p.m., Defendant A was near the Northwest scaffolding and stairs north of the inauguration stage.
- People near Defendant A chanted "Hang 'em high!" repeatedly near the Northwest scaffolding.
- Defendant A donned ski goggles and a respirator mask on the Capitol grounds to protect from chemical agents.
- Defendant A witnessed tear gas being deployed into the crowd near the Capitol.
- A tear gas canister landed near Defendant A and someone yelled for a rioter to throw it back toward the Capitol.
- Defendant A witnessed rioters attacking Metropolitan Police Department officers as they moved to assist Capitol Police.
- Defendant A used purloined bike rack fencing to climb onto the north balustrade of the North West Stairs.
- From the balustrade, Defendant A stood and filmed the scene before proceeding up to the northwest terrace.
- While on the northwest terrace, Defendant A exclaimed on video, "Where's your 80 million now, Biden!"
- Defendant A entered through the Senate Wing door soon after it was breached, with damage visible and an audible alarm.
- Inside the Capitol, Defendant A went through the Crypt on the ground floor.
- Defendant A went up the stairs near the Memorial Door by the Speaker of the House’s office suite as some chanted "Nancy."
- Defendant A entered the Rotunda and moved through Statuary Hall toward the Statuary Hall Connector near the House Chamber.
- Near the House Chamber, people around Defendant A chanted "Stop the steal!" and "Break it down!"
- Defendant A moved around hallways outside the House Chamber and later exited through the Upper House Door.
- After exiting, Defendant A lingered on the balcony outside the Upper House Door and took additional photos.
- Defendant A returned toward the Mall more than an hour and a half after arriving on the Capitol grounds.
- On January 6, 2021, later in the day, Defendant A messaged a co-defendant "Good times man."
- Defendant A reported being banned on social media for titling a video "we are coming for you" while marching toward the Capitol.
- The Statement of Offense notes that the riot resulted in substantial damage to the Capitol requiring expenditures exceeding $1.4 million.
- Defendant A acknowledged in the plea agreement that as of May 17, 2021, damage to the Capitol was approximately $1,495,326.55 and agreed to pay $500 restitution.
- On January 4, 2021, Defendant A posted that he was leaving to head to Washington, D.C. from Nashville.
- On December 27, 2020, Defendant A discussed contacting a local chapter of the Proud Boys about joining.
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant A posted rally photos with hashtags including #MarchforTrump and #StopTheSteal.
- The U.S. Capitol was secured by U.S. Capitol Police and the exterior plaza was closed to the public on January 6, 2021.
