- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Count Seven charging Entering and Remaining in a Restricted Building or Grounds in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1).
- The statutory maximum imprisonment for 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1) is one year.
- The maximum fine for the offense is $100,000 pursuant to 18 U.S.C. § 3571(b)(5).
- The maximum term of supervised release for the offense is one year pursuant to 18 U.S.C. § 3583(b)(3).
- The mandatory special assessment for the Class A misdemeanor is $25.
- The government agreed to request dismissal of the remaining counts of the Indictment at the time of sentencing.
- Defendant A agreed to allow law enforcement to review social media accounts for statements and postings in and around January 6, 2021, prior to sentencing.
- Defendant A agreed to pay $500 in restitution to the Architect of the Capitol.
- As of October 2022, the riot on January 6, 2021 caused approximately $2,881,360.20 in damage to the U.S. Capitol.
- Defendant A acknowledged having no prior criminal convictions based on information available at the time of the plea agreement.
- Defendant A signed the plea agreement acceptance on December 13, 2022.
- The plea offer letter was issued on December 12, 2022 and stated an expiration date of December 13, 2022.
- The case is captioned in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia as Case No. 21‑cr‑195‑CKK.
- A superseding indictment filed on December 17, 2021 charged Defendant A with four counts related to January 6, 2021.
- The charged counts included 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1) (Count Seven), 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(2) (Count Eight), 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D) (Count Ten), and 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(G) (Count Eleven).
- On December 14, 2022, Defendant A pleaded guilty to Count Seven pursuant to a plea agreement.
- Leading up to January 6, 2021, Defendant A sent private messages to encourage others to join in Washington, D.C. on January 6.
- On December 21, 2020, Defendant A messaged that she was returning to Washington on January 6 and referenced a call for patriots and possible civil war if electors did not elect.
- On December 21, 2020, Defendant A messaged that she and others had guns and were getting together with neighbors.
- On December 27, 2020, Defendant A wrote that her fiancé had a permit to carry and that protection might be needed because January 6 could be bad depending on the electoral vote.
- On January 5, 2021, Defendant A messaged about having mace for a family member and planned to meet at 5 a.m.
- On January 5, 2021, Defendant A warned that opponents might wear disguising apparel and stated she would wear black.
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant A attended a rally and walked to the Capitol, arriving at the Peace Circle at about 2:10 p.m.
- At 2:37 p.m. EST on January 6, 2021, Defendant A texted that doors were being tested and that she was at the Capitol, followed by "Tear gas."
- At 2:49:47 p.m. EST on January 6, 2021, Defendant A entered the Capitol through the Senate Wing Doors.
- Upon entry, Defendant A held her cellphone and appeared to record while walking through the Senate Wing Door lobby.
- Defendant A recorded broken furniture in the lobby area; screenshots of this video were found on her cellphone.
- Shortly after entering, Defendant A shouted for the Speaker of the House to be brought out to the crowd.
- Defendant A moved toward the Crypt and continued recording on her cellphone.
- Defendant A remained in the Crypt for several minutes, taking photos and videos.
- Around 3:10 p.m. on January 6, 2021, Defendant A approached Senator Merkley’s office and took photographs of the interior.
- Defendant A exited the Capitol through the Senate Wing Doors at approximately 3:13 p.m. on January 6, 2021.
- After exiting, at approximately 3:19 p.m., Defendant A texted a family member asking their location; the reply indicated being inside the back at about 3:22 p.m.
- Defendant A remained on restricted Capitol grounds and moved from the west side to the east side to meet a family member.
- A photo including Defendant A and a family member on restricted grounds was taken at 3:45:49 p.m. on January 6, 2021, according to cellphone metadata.
- On the evening of January 6, 2021, Defendant A messaged that they had stormed the Capitol.
- Defendant A posted a video taken inside the Capitol to a private Facebook group with a claim of no violence and blaming another group for disguising themselves.
- On January 10, 2021, Defendant A posted that media accounts were false and characterized January 6 as a peaceful rally while blaming another group for terrorism.
- On January 14, 2021, Defendant A publicly stated having no regrets about her choice and would stand for beliefs.
- On January 16, 2021, Defendant A shared photos from inside the Capitol to a private message group; metadata showed one photo taken at 3:10 p.m. on January 6, 2021.
- On January 17, 2021, after arrests of other January 6 participants, Defendant A wrote that it was time to shut Facebook down and to check out another platform.
- On February 9, 2021, Defendant A messaged that there were no pictures or messages and stated that she erased all.
- A review of Defendant A’s cellphone showed some videos had been deleted, evidenced by screenshots remaining without the videos.
- On January 31, 2021, Defendant A’s web history showed searches on how to delete iPhone apps and how to delete applications through settings.
- On February 2, 2021, Defendant A searched for information about arresting a person who took a selfie at the Capitol and viewed a report about arrests using selfies and social posts.
- Defendant A was arrested on February 19, 2021 and later admitted entering the Capitol and taking videos and selfies during a post‑arrest interview.
- During the post‑arrest interview, Defendant A minimized her conduct, claimed she did not know it was wrong, and said she entered to help people.
- Defendant A’s conduct occurred within the context of the January 6, 2021 attack on the U.S. Capitol that caused injuries to more than one hundred officers and significant losses.
- Defendant A pleaded guilty to violating 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1), a Class A misdemeanor.
- Defendant A’s codefendant son was found guilty on December 15, 2022 of Counts 1–12 in the same case, with sentencing scheduled for August 7, 2023.
- Defendant A holds both an associate degree and a bachelor’s degree, with the associate degree in the field of legal assistant.
- Defendant A encouraged her two adult sons to join her in Washington, D.C. on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant A told others she was bringing weapons, including firearms and mace, in messages before January 6, 2021.
- Defendant A encountered chemical agents during the breach, as reflected by her text stating "Tear gas" on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant A witnessed property destruction inside the Capitol, including in the area near the Senate Wing Doors and in a senator’s office suite.
- Defendant A did not attempt to stop property destruction but instead photographed and recorded it.
- Defendant A minimized the violence of January 6 in social media posts, falsely characterizing the events as peaceful.
- Defendant A deleted evidence from her cellphone and social media after learning others were being prosecuted for January 6 conduct.
- Defendant A had not provided consent forms or financial history forms to the Probation Office by the time the PSR was written.
- Defendant A and a family member operated an online fundraising page and had raised approximately $350 toward a $50,000 goal by early January 2023.
- Defendant A responded to at least one donor on the fundraising page with a message of thanks in early January 2023.
- Defendant A agreed to complete a financial disclosure form and cooperate with the Financial Litigation Unit in advance of sentencing.
- Defendant A authorized the U.S. Attorney’s Office to obtain a credit report to evaluate ability to satisfy financial obligations.
- Defendant A understood that restitution or fines imposed would be due immediately and agreed to participate in the BOP’s Inmate Financial Responsibility Program if imprisoned.

- Defendant A pleaded guilty to Count One of a superseding indictment charging a violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1958 (Use of Interstate Commerce Facilities in the Commission of Murder-For-Hire).
- Defendant A signed the plea agreement on October 22, 2021.
- Defendant A entered her guilty plea on February 7, 2022.
- Sentencing was scheduled for May 26, 2022.
- The offense carries a maximum penalty that includes life imprisonment or the death penalty.
- The offense carries a maximum fine of up to $250,000.
- Up to five years of supervised release may follow any term of imprisonment for this offense.
- A $100 mandatory special assessment applies to the conviction in Count One.
- Restitution is mandatory and may include restitution arising from all relevant conduct, not limited to the offense of conviction alone.
- The plea agreement was conditioned upon the approval of the Attorney General.
- The government agreed to dismiss the original indictment and Count Two of the superseding indictment at sentencing.
- Defendant A waived her rights to a jury trial and to have her guilt proven beyond a reasonable doubt.
- Defendant A agreed to pay the $100 special assessment to the U.S. District Clerk.
- Defendant A agreed to provide complete and truthful information about her participation in the offense and to submit a personal financial statement under oath upon demand.
- Defendant A admitted that she reconnected online with a former high school boyfriend (Co‑conspirator A) on or about March 17, 2020.
- Between March 17, 2020 and October 8, 2020, Defendant A and Co‑conspirator A engaged in numerous communications including emails, texts, voice calls, and letters.
- In April 2020, Defendant A told another person she was having a full-blown emotional affair with Co‑conspirator A.
- Defendant A admitted that starting around April 2020 she falsely told Co‑conspirator A that Victim A was physically and sexually abusing her.
- On April 9, 2020, Defendant A created a fake email account in Victim A’s name and used it to send emails to Co‑conspirator A falsely claiming abuse.
- Defendant A attached images to some of the fake emails that depicted extreme physical and sexual abuse, some of which she downloaded from the internet and were not of her.
- On May 13, 2020, Defendant A created a second fake email account in the name of a close friend (Friend A) to send Co‑conspirator A more false claims of abuse.
- On May 20, 2020, Co‑conspirator A wrote he would not feel better until Defendant A was away from Victim A or she let him put a bullet in Victim A’s head; Defendant A replied from the Friend A account encouraging the idea and offering an alibi.
- On October 9, 2020, approximately one minute into a morning dog walk, Co‑conspirator A approached Victim A from behind and shot him seven times, causing death.
- Co‑conspirator A traveled in interstate or foreign commerce prior to the murder.
- Murder is illegal in the state where the homicide occurred.
- On November 11, 2020, Defendant A initiated a claim for approximately $629,000 in life insurance benefits on behalf of a family trust.
- Before and after the murder, Defendant A provided Co‑conspirator A and his family with money and gifts.
- Defendant A used her Venmo account, credit cards, and other means to send items of value including money, a gaming console, airline tickets, and a large-screen television to Co‑conspirator A and his family.
- Defendant A used specific credit cards to make purchases for Co‑conspirator A and ship items to him in another state.
- Defendant A used funds from a GoFundMe account titled Support for Defendant A to pay monthly balances on those credit cards.
- On November 25, 2020, Defendant A sent Co‑conspirator A images of her credit cards and encouraged him to use them, stating one card had no limit.
- Defendant A learned from witness accounts that the killer fled in an older model black pickup with a distinctive white 'T' sticker in the back window.
- On December 2, 2020, Defendant A participated in a televised interview and publicly emphasized the importance of locating the black truck with the 'T' sticker.
- At the time of the December 2, 2020 interview, Defendant A knew the truck being sought belonged to Co‑conspirator A.
- Two days after the interview, Defendant A texted Co‑conspirator A urging him to remove the 'T' sticker from his truck immediately.
- On December 5, 2020, Co‑conspirator A confirmed to Defendant A that the sticker was removed; Defendant A replied that life insurance was waiting on a detective.
- On December 29, 2020, Defendant A texted Co‑conspirator A that the life insurance claim was delayed because a detective told the insurer she couldn’t be ruled out as a suspect.
- On January 7, 2021, Co‑conspirator A sent Defendant A images of a residence in another state and Defendant A offered to provide her personal identifiers for a lease application.
- On January 10, 2021, after being contacted by a detective, Defendant A texted Co‑conspirator A a coordinated explanation to give investigators about their relationship and finances.
- On or about February 2, 2021, Defendant A asked an intermediary to pass a message of loyalty to Co‑conspirator A while he was in custody.
- A Ring security camera recorded the last seconds before Defendant A and Victim A left their residence on the morning of the homicide.
- Co‑conspirator A shot Victim A three times in the head, three times in the torso, and once in the groin.
- A neighbor’s Ring camera recorded audio of the ambush and Defendant A’s reaction immediately after it occurred; Victim A was pronounced deceased at the scene.
- A neighbor photographed the shooter’s vehicle, a truck with a distinctive 'T' decal on the driver’s side back window, as it fled.
- Cell phone records reflected over 122,000 contacts between Defendant A’s and Co‑conspirator A’s phones between May 2020 and January 11, 2021.
- Co‑conspirator A reported that Defendant A told him Victim A was inflicting extreme physical and sexual abuse, including through messages purporting to be from Victim A.
- Defendant A created a fake Gmail account in Victim A’s name and took steps to make it appear genuine, including forwarding a diet email to herself and then to Co‑conspirator A.
- Photographs of injuries sent to Co‑conspirator A were found on Defendant A’s desktop hard drive, and metadata showed they were taken in 2012 from a car wreck, not current abuse.
- A photograph of a bloody lip sent to Co‑conspirator A was a stock image downloaded from a commercial website, not Defendant A.
- Friend A confirmed he had never communicated with Co‑conspirator A by email and that the account in his name was created without his knowledge or consent.
- Defendant A allowed Co‑conspirator A to electronically track her and Victim A’s movements, including dog-walk routes, via an app during September 2020.
- Defendant A provided Co‑conspirator A with the flight itinerary for a September 2020 trip to another state.
- Co‑conspirator A said the defendant asked him in August 2020 to help kill Victim A, claiming she could not take the abuse any longer.
- On October 8, 2020, Co‑conspirator A departed his home to travel to the city of the homicide in a black pickup with a 'T' decal, carrying a .45 caliber handgun.
- Defendant A told Co‑conspirator A that a neighboring house was vacant and he could hide in its backyard before the shooting.
- Defendant A and Co‑conspirator A exchanged texts to create a fake alibi involving a hunting trip.
- In the early hours of October 9, 2020, a Ring system recorded Co‑conspirator A in the backyard of the vacant house near Defendant A’s residence.
- After shooting Victim A, Co‑conspirator A removed Victim A’s wedding ring and later threw it from his vehicle while driving back to his home state.
- Blood from Victim A was transferred to the slide of the .45 caliber handgun used in the shooting.
- Four days after the murder, Defendant A created a group text for friends and neighbors to share daily case updates, and she included Co‑conspirator A in that group.
- Defendant A and Co‑conspirator A continued frequent direct communications after the murder, including intimacy-related exchanges and plans to rent a house together in another state.
- Defendant A also sought to obtain nearly $400,000 from Victim A’s retirement account at his employer after the murder.
- A GoFundMe account titled Support for Defendant A was created the day after the murder, and between October 13 and December 3, 2020, Defendant A withdrew approximately $58,000 from it.
- Defendant A used donated funds to pay credit card balances and to buy gifts for Co‑conspirator A, including a large-screen television and airline tickets.
- Defendant A did not use the GoFundMe donations to pay all funeral expenses, with a balance of $6,500 outstanding.
- When first interviewed by detectives on the day of the murder, SMS text messages between Defendant A and Co‑conspirator A had been deleted from Defendant A’s phone.
- When asked about possible suspects, Defendant A did not mention Co‑conspirator A and instead provided other names she knew were uninvolved.
- In a televised interview on December 2, 2020, Defendant A publicly described the suspect vehicle as a black extended cab pickup with a 'T' sticker and urged the public to help find it.
- On January 10, 2021, Defendant A told Co‑conspirator A she planned to factory reset her phone after deleting texts before a police interview and instructed him on consistent explanations.
- On January 6, 2021, a search warrant for Co‑conspirator A’s residence was issued by a U.S. Magistrate Judge, and an arrest was scheduled for January 11, 2021.
- On January 11, 2021, Co‑conspirator A was arrested; agents seized a cell phone, credit cards in Defendant A’s name, a mask, and recovered the .45 caliber handgun from his home.
- While Co‑conspirator A was being taken into custody, his phone received a text from Defendant A asking, "everything ok?"
- Shortly after learning of Co‑conspirator A’s arrest, Defendant A transferred $45,000, $8,000, and then $65,000 from her account to Child A’s account on specific January 2021 dates.
- Defendant A used Co‑conspirator A’s relative to relay messages to him in custody and urged that he not speak without a lawyer.
- Defendant A asked the intermediary to pass a code (“3-8-1”) to Co‑conspirator A that she said would have meaning to him.
- Defendant A continued attempting to contact Co‑conspirator A’s relative from jail as late as mid‑March 2022.
- Defendant A wrote jail letters to Victim A’s parents professing innocence even after signing a factual stipulation admitting her role.
- In recorded jail calls, Defendant A told Child A she did nothing wrong and characterized the government’s case as false.
- On October 25, 2021, Defendant A instructed Child A to set aside $200,000 for her prison commissary and phone account needs after sentencing.
- During her plea hearing, Defendant A admitted under oath to creating two fake email accounts and to causing Co‑conspirator A to travel across state lines to kill Victim A for consideration of things of value.
- Defendant A acknowledged that the factual resume is not a complete accounting of all events, but establishes a factual basis for her plea.
- Defendant A and Co‑conspirator A exchanged affectionate messages, with each expressing love and plans for a future together during 2020.
- Defendant A told a friend in April 2020 she was in constant contact with Co‑conspirator A all day every day and described it as feeling like a teenager.
- When asked by Co‑conspirator A about a TV interview where she was shown with a memory book of Victim A, Defendant A disparaged the segment in a private text.
- Co‑conspirator A reported that fake messages purportedly from Victim A threatened worse abuse if he reported the situation to police or failed to respond.
- Co‑conspirator A turned off his cell phone and left an iPad on to track Defendant A’s movements during the trip to commit the homicide.
- Defendant A admitted that the conduct resulted in the death of another person.
- Defendant A acknowledged providing Co‑conspirator A with her credit card billing address and told him he need not worry about declines because she paid the balances monthly.
- Defendant A stated to Co‑conspirator A that she would provide consistent explanations about their relationship and finances if questioned by police.
- Defendant A referred to the life insurance process as awaiting input from a detective in early December 2020.
- Defendant A admitted she knew the truck law enforcement sought belonged to Co‑conspirator A when she publicly discussed it on television.
- Defendant A and her counsel stipulated that Friend A’s email account was misused by Defendant A to send messages to Co‑conspirator A from May to August 2020.
- Defendant A admitted sending some images to Co‑conspirator A that were not of herself but were downloaded from the internet.
- In May 2020 exchanges, Co‑conspirator A expressed willingness to kill Victim A and Defendant A, posing as Friend A, indicated she would provide an alibi.
- Defendant A acknowledged that she committed all essential elements of the offense in Count One of the superseding indictment.
- Defendant A confirmed that a photograph of Co‑conspirator A’s truck with a 'T' decal was released publicly by investigators seeking leads.
- Co‑conspirator A said he removed Victim A’s wedding ring after the shooting because he believed the act was part of ensuring the plan succeeded.
- Co‑conspirator A described the homicide to investigators as a 'rescue mission' and stated he and Defendant A never discussed the murder afterward.
- Defendant A told Co‑conspirator A in January 2021 to tell detectives that her relationship with Victim A was good if questioned.
- Defendant A used an intermediary to tell Co‑conspirator A she would always be with him despite what had happened, acknowledging communications were monitored.
- After the murder, Defendant A gave Co‑conspirator A two credit cards in her name and told him to use them freely, saying she would pay them off monthly.
- Defendant A publicly appealed for information about the suspect vehicle while privately telling Co‑conspirator A to remove a distinctive sticker from his truck.
- Defendant A coordinated with Co‑conspirator A to align explanations for frequent communications and financial assistance if questioned by law enforcement.
- Defendant A acknowledged that the murder-for-hire conduct involved causing another person to travel in interstate commerce for consideration of things of pecuniary value.

- A joint session of Congress convened at the U.S. Capitol at approximately 1:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021 to certify the 2020 Electoral College vote.
- By approximately 1:30 p.m. on January 6, 2021, the House and Senate adjourned to separate chambers to resolve an objection.
- The Vice President was present and presiding over the Senate on January 6, 2021 before evacuation.
- On January 6, 2021, the exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to members of the public.
- The U.S. Capitol is secured 24 hours a day by U.S. Capitol Police and only authorized persons with identification are allowed inside.
- At approximately 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals in the crowd forced their way over barricades and advanced to the Capitol’s exterior.
- The exterior doors and windows of the U.S. Capitol were locked or otherwise secured when rioters forced entry shortly after 2:00 p.m.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m., members of Congress and the Vice President evacuated the chambers and proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- Congressional proceedings resumed at approximately 8:00 p.m. after the Capitol was secured.
- The January 6 attack resulted in substantial damage to the U.S. Capitol requiring expenditure of more than $1.4 million for repairs.
- The attack constituted a civil disturbance that affected interstate commerce by reducing sales at Safeway stores in Washington, D.C. on January 6, 2021.
- As of October 17, 2022, approximate losses from the January 6 attack totaled $2,881,360.20.
- More than one hundred police officers were injured during the January 6 attack.
- On January 17, 2021, the FBI identified social media videos depicting a person unlawfully inside and around the Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- In a video recorded on January 6, 2021, the subject verbally identified himself and stated he was from Tennessee.
- The subject’s face was clearly visible in several social media postings from January 6, 2021.
- Investigators identified a Facebook account with a profile photo that matched the subject seen in the videos.
- Tennessee driver’s license records produced an image that matched the subject seen unlawfully at the Capitol and the Facebook profile image.
- Two associates of the subject confirmed to law enforcement that the individual seen in the videos was the same person.
- In one recorded post on January 6, the subject said he was on the top step of the Capitol.
- Near scaffolding outside the Capitol, the subject responded to another person’s statement about going to the stands by saying, “Do it!”
- In another recorded post, the subject said, “I'm the guy who got you in this building.”
- A still image shows the subject grabbing the top portion of a U.S. Capitol Police officer’s riot shield in front of a Capitol doorway with a broken window.
- The defendant unlawfully entered the Capitol through the Upper West Terrace doorway at approximately 2:35 p.m. on January 6, 2021.
- As he headed toward the Rotunda, the defendant shouted, “Fight for this!”
- At approximately 3:00 p.m., multiple officers entered the Rotunda and began to clear the room of rioters including the defendant.
- The defendant ignored verbal commands to leave the Rotunda.
- At approximately 3:08 p.m., the defendant physically confronted an officer by leaning into the officer’s baton as the officer tried to push him toward the exit.
- Body-worn camera footage shows an officer had to push the defendant away three times in roughly 25 seconds around 3:06:30 p.m.
- Around 3:07:59 p.m., another officer used a baton to push against the defendant’s torso as he moved toward the officer.
- An officer deployed a chemical irritant to move the defendant out of the Rotunda at approximately 3:14 p.m.
- The defendant exited the Capitol through the East Rotunda Doors at approximately 3:18 p.m.
- After exiting, the defendant remained directly outside the East Rotunda doorway rather than leaving the area.
- At approximately 3:39 p.m., the defendant was still near the East Rotunda doorway gesturing toward people inside.
- Around 3:43 p.m., officers inside the East Rotunda Doors deployed riot shields and sprayed irritant at rioters outside.
- At approximately 3:45 p.m., the defendant grabbed and pulled on an officer’s riot shield with his hand.
- A social media image from the opposite side of the doorway shows the defendant pulling down on a riot shield.
- Outside the building, the defendant stated on video, “I’m the guy that got you in this building… Watch the videos.”
- The defendant’s offense conduct occurred while he was on probation for an assault conviction.
- A superseding indictment filed on November 10, 2021 charged the defendant with civil disorder and other offenses including obstruction of an official proceeding and multiple restricted-area and Capitol building offenses.
- On July 28, 2022, the defendant pleaded guilty pursuant to a plea agreement to civil disorder in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3).
- Under the plea agreement, the defendant agreed to plead guilty to Count One charging interference with law enforcement during a civil disorder.
- The plea agreement provides that the remaining counts would be dismissed at sentencing.
- The defendant stipulated that the Statement of Offense fairly and accurately describes his actions and involvement.
- The defendant signed an acknowledgment that the Statement of Offense is true and accurate.
- The defendant agreed to allow law enforcement agents to review any social media accounts he operated for statements and postings around January 6, 2021.
- The offense of conviction carries a statutory maximum of 5 years’ imprisonment.
- The offense of conviction carries a maximum fine of $250,000 or twice the pecuniary gain or loss.
- The offense of conviction carries a term of supervised release of not more than 3 years.
- The defendant agreed to pay a mandatory $100 special assessment.
- The defendant agreed to pay $2,000 in restitution to the Architect of the Capitol.
- The plea agreement acknowledges that as of May 17, 2021, the riot caused approximately $1,495,326.55 in damage to the U.S. Capitol.
- The defendant agreed to complete and submit a financial disclosure form to facilitate restitution collection.
- The defendant agreed that restitution or fines imposed would be due and payable immediately and subject to enforcement by the United States.
- The defendant agreed to participate in the Bureau of Prisons’ Inmate Financial Responsibility Program if sentenced to imprisonment.
- The plea agreement records prior Tennessee convictions including two counts of burglary not involving a habitation in 1997.
- The plea agreement records misdemeanor convictions for driving while impaired in 2000 and reckless endangerment in 2001.
- The plea agreement records a misdemeanor domestic assault conviction in 2019.
- The plea agreement states the defendant was on probation for the domestic assault conviction when he committed the January 6 offense.
- The sentencing memorandum notes the defendant’s 1997 burglary conviction included a two-year sentence with repeated probation revocations.
- The sentencing memorandum references a 2001 DUI conviction and a 2005 domestic assault conviction not counted for scoring in the PSR.
- The sentencing memorandum reports a 2020 domestic assault conviction that resulted in a suspended prison sentence.
- A complaint-supported Statement of Facts was attested by telephone on January 29, 2021 under Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 4.1.
- The Statement of Facts asserted probable cause for violations of 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1), (2), and (4) related to restricted building or grounds.
- The Statement of Facts asserted probable cause for violations of 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D), (F), and (G) relating to conduct on Capitol grounds and buildings.
- The Statement of Facts asserted probable cause for violating 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3) relating to interference with law enforcement during a civil disorder.
- The Statement of Facts asserted probable cause for violating 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2) relating to obstruction of an official proceeding.
- A video shows the defendant on the Upper West Terrace shortly after 2:30 p.m. proclaiming he was on the top steps of the Capitol.
- The defendant entered the Capitol approximately 15 minutes after the initial breach and roughly two minutes after the Upper West Terrace door was breached.
- As he proceeded toward an interior doorway and stairs, the defendant looked into another rioter’s camera and yelled, “Fight for this!”
- Body-worn camera footage shows the defendant repeatedly moved toward officers and had to be pushed back multiple times while officers were clearing the Rotunda.
- The defendant was among the last group of rioters to leave the Rotunda as shown on security camera imagery.
- After exiting the building, the defendant remained on Capitol grounds near the East Rotunda doorway for more than 20 minutes.
- The defendant physically interfered with law enforcement by pulling on an officer’s riot shield outside the East Rotunda Doors.
- The defendant told another rioter to watch videos and claimed responsibility for getting people into the Capitol building.
- The plea agreement includes a waiver permitting proceedings such as plea and sentencing to be held by video teleconference or teleconference under the CARES Act.
- The defendant agreed that if sentenced to imprisonment he would participate in the Inmate Financial Responsibility Program regardless of a court payment schedule.
- Following arrest, the defendant was detained on March 8, 2021.
- After pleading guilty, the defendant was released on his own recognizance on August 4, 2022.
- The defendant served approximately 17 months and 7 days in pre-sentencing confinement in this case.
- The defendant acknowledged in the plea agreement that the plea was voluntary and that he was pleading guilty because he was in fact guilty.
- The defendant admitted he entered the Capitol approximately 15 minutes after the initial breach and two minutes after the Upper West Terrace door was breached.
- Rioters advancing to the Capitol did not submit to security screenings or weapons checks by authorized security officials before entering.
- Individuals in the crowd forced entry into the Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement.
- While outside near scaffolding, the defendant encouraged movement toward the inauguration stands, responding, “Let’s do it!”

- Defendant pleaded guilty to Counts One through Four of the Indictment under Rule 11(c)(1)(A).
- Counts One through Four charge passing counterfeit obligations or securities in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 472.

- On January 6, 2021, the U.S. Capitol’s exterior plaza was closed to the public and access inside required authorization.

- Around 1:30 p.m. on January 6, 2021, the House and Senate separated to resolve an objection, with the Vice President presiding in the Senate.
- Around 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals forced entry into the Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting officers.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m. on January 6, 2021, Congress evacuated and proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- The Vice President remained in the Capitol from evacuation until the joint session resumed the evening of January 6, 2021.
- The Capitol riot resulted in more than $2.8 million in damage costs.
- Defendant A planned in late December 2020 to travel to Washington, D.C. to attend the January 6 rally.
- Defendant A purchased a Faraday bag in preparation for travel in an attempt to prevent government location tracking.

- As Defendant A approached the Capitol, he saw downed metal barricades and broken fencing around the building.
- On the West Front, Defendant A saw rioters climbing scaffolding, scaling walls, fighting police, spraying chemical irritants, and throwing objects.
- Defendant A saw police officers retreating from rioters on the West Front.
- Defendant A and Co-Defendant B each picked up a metal pole broken from a bike rack barricade and carried the poles throughout their time on restricted grounds and inside the Capitol.
- An MPD Civil Disturbance Unit (CDU 42) attempted to move through the crowd toward the front to protect the Capitol building.
- A mob initiated violent confrontations with the officers of MPD CDU 42 on the West Front.
- While other rioters pushed and wrestled with officers, Defendant A stepped toward Officer A and swung at the officer with a metal pole.
- Officer A attempted to regain footing and adjusted equipment after contact with rioters during the confrontation.
- Government evidence describes Defendant A violently swinging and striking an officer with a metal pole during the West Front clashes.
- The officer attacked on the West Front suffered several blows to the head and body that day and had headaches for weeks; he does not remember a specific strike from Defendant A.
- Defendant A entered the Capitol through the Senate Wing Door approximately eight minutes after the initial breach, still carrying the metal pole.
- Defendant A walked through the Crypt and Rotunda inside the Capitol.

- Defendant A exited the Capitol approximately 34 minutes after entry.
- After January 6, Defendant A deleted videos from his cell phone that he had recorded at the Capitol.

- Defendant A took a video of the Senate Wing Door breach area while officers guarded a nearby door.
- Defendant A observed rioters forcing doors open and fighting with officers inside the Capitol and did not leave immediately.
- On exiting, Defendant A heard that a woman had been shot and saw blood on the stairs.
- After leaving the building, Defendant A climbed on a government vehicle and remained on Capitol grounds until evening.
- Co-Defendant statements indicate Defendant A bragged on the drive home about hitting an officer with the metal pole, specifically mentioning a strike to a helmet.
- Defendant A retained possession of the metal pole after January 6.

- A Confidential Source acting at the direction of law enforcement met in Colombia with co-conspirators beginning in January 2021 to discuss drug trafficking.
- Co-conspirators told the Confidential Source they were involved in distributing crystal methamphetamine and heroin from Michoacán, Mexico to Houston, Texas and Atlanta, Georgia.
- A co-conspirator said approximately 300 kilograms of crystal methamphetamine were in Atlanta, Georgia, and that he had access to heroin.
- The Confidential Source initially sought to purchase approximately 500 kilograms of crystal methamphetamine for import from Mexico to South Florida, with an initial agreement for 250 kilograms at $14,000 per kilogram later increased to 500 kilograms total.
- The trafficking plan included shipping crystal methamphetamine dissolved in liquid house paint to avoid detection.
- On February 5, 2021, the Confidential Source conducted a virtual money flash of approximately $6 million in bulk U.S. currency using an encrypted video app to demonstrate funding for the transaction.
- During the money flash, the Confidential Source also displayed several wrapped appliance boxes said to contain money and asked for a list of items needed to extract methamphetamine from paint once the shipment arrived in Miami.
- A co-conspirator stated he had a business partner who could receive wire transfers to help move money and indicated the partner should not know the money’s source.
- Before meeting the Confidential Source, Defendant A did not know the money would come from drugs but had reason to suspect it came from something illegal.
- On February 10, 2021, Defendant A contacted the Confidential Source upon arriving at Miami International Airport and was picked up and taken to a hotel where they met in the hotel's restaurant.
- At the February 10, 2021 meeting, Defendant A and the Confidential Source discussed moving money to Mexico, and the Confidential Source initially proposed moving $8,150,000 in various ways.
- Defendant A told the Confidential Source he had accounts that could be used to move the money and provided bank account information for three different companies.
- The Confidential Source indicated to Defendant A that the CS’s clients had 'drug issues' but said those issues were the clients’ problem, not the CS’s or Defendant A’s.
- Defendant A said a residence in Aventura, Florida, belonging to a family member could be used to hold cash and offered to show it to the Confidential Source.
- Defendant A proposed moving money through his shoe company and investment company and showed product samples such as shoes and face masks as cover for funding flows.
- On March 11, 2021, a co-conspirator arrived at Miami International Airport to receive the first shipment of methamphetamine, and the Confidential Source picked him up and discussed the materials needed to extract methamphetamine from paint.
- On March 12, 2021, the Confidential Source and the co-conspirator purchased materials including an aluminum pot, coolers, knives, plastic containers, a propane burner, a propane tank, measuring cups, and a thermometer for the extraction process.
- The co-conspirator explained to an undercover agent the process of extracting methamphetamine from paint and said he knew the entire process except for one separation step.
- On March 19, 2021, the plan changed: the initial methamphetamine shipment would arrive in large concrete tiles rather than in paint, transported by two truckers from Texas to Hialeah, Florida.
- The tiles containing methamphetamine originated in Mexico, were stashed in Texas, and were transported to the Miami area where they were taken to an undercover warehouse on March 20, 2021.
- At the undercover warehouse on March 20, 2021, four crates of concrete tile were unloaded by an undercover agent using a forklift; after the drivers left, tiles were broken open and approximately 200 kilograms of wrapped methamphetamine were recovered and field tested positive.
- The undercover warehouse was wired to record audio and video during the unloading and opening of the tiles on March 20, 2021.
- On March 21, 2021, the Confidential Source told Defendant A that money was not yet available because only part of the methamphetamine had been received and the remainder was pending; Defendant A agreed to stay in Miami until the money was delivered and mentioned that an additional payment had been discussed but he ultimately did not receive it.
- At some point, Defendant A asked a co-conspirator about drugs involved, and the co-conspirator told him the Confidential Source was 'crazy' and not to listen to him.
- On March 23, 2021, a co-conspirator informed the Confidential Source that approximately 350 kilograms of methamphetamine would arrive in paint as the final portion of the original 500-kilogram deal.
- On March 26, 2021, a truck delivered approximately 245 five-gallon paint buckets to the undercover warehouse; the same driver and trailer as the earlier delivery were used.
- On March 27, 2021, two chemists arrived at the undercover warehouse and began assisting with the extraction of methamphetamine from paint after demonstrating the process on three buckets.
- Of the approximately 245 paint buckets delivered on March 26, 35 contained methamphetamine mixed with paint and the remaining buckets held only paint.
- On March 27, 2021, the chemists purchased additional materials and continued extraction with the Confidential Source and undercover agents through midnight.
- On March 28, 2021, extraction efforts continued and field tests of both extracted material and unprocessed paint/meth mixtures were positive for methamphetamine.
- On March 28, 2021, the Confidential Source told Defendant A again that they were waiting for a methamphetamine delivery and clarified that Defendant A’s role related only to moving money, not drugs.
- On March 28, 2021, the Confidential Source discussed moving $4,150,000 with Defendant A, with $1,000,000 to go through a company of Defendant A’s.
- After the March 28, 2021 discussion, Defendant A had reason to believe that some of the money involved in the transaction was illegal proceeds based on the nature and structure of the transactions.
- On March 30, 2021, undercover agents and co-conspirators extracted roughly eighteen pounds of methamphetamine from two paint buckets; law enforcement later seized the methamphetamine and field tests were positive.
- On March 30, 2021, the Confidential Source arranged a meeting at a local hotel for a money pickup involving an undercover agent, a co-conspirator, and Defendant A.
- At the hotel on March 30, 2021, an undercover agent and a co-conspirator placed a large suitcase with fake currency into Defendant A’s vehicle, and Defendant A acknowledged it looked good before closing the trunk.
- Defendant A and the co-conspirator at the hotel were arrested immediately after the suitcase with fake currency was placed in Defendant A’s vehicle on March 30, 2021.
- Shortly after the hotel arrests on March 30, 2021, law enforcement arrested the two chemists at the undercover warehouse.
- The parties agreed in a signed factual proffer that the recited facts were sufficient to prove Defendant A’s guilt in the case.
- The factual proffer was executed in late September 2022 by the United States and Defendant A with counsel present.
- Defendant A is a lifelong resident of Mexico who lived with family and worked in a family-owned shoe business for many years before this case.
- Defendant A had no prior trouble with law enforcement before this case according to the sentencing memorandum.
- Defendant A holds a college degree in Business Administration per the sentencing memorandum.
- Defendant A maintained close ties with his mother and two brothers in Mexico per the sentencing memorandum.
- Defendant A had an eight-year relationship with Partner A and they planned to marry in mid-November 2021, but the wedding was postponed due to this case.
- Defendant A was arrested on March 30, 2021 and detained at FDC Miami, per the sentencing memorandum.
- Upon arrest, Defendant A spent approximately 25 consecutive days in solitary confinement with only a single 30-minute break every three days, during which he had to choose between a shower or a phone call.
- After solitary, Defendant A remained in lockdown for an average of 22 hours per day for approximately 11 months at FDC Miami.
- Defendant A reported exposure to violent inmates and threats, causing fear for his safety while detained, as described in the sentencing memorandum.
- Defendant A has a gastric medical condition and reported being deprived of medical care for months despite requests, per the sentencing memorandum.
- During detention, there were only two to four functioning showers for about 120 inmates, limiting access to hygiene according to the sentencing memorandum.
- Defendant A and a cellmate endured a broken toilet for approximately four months, requiring them to fill it with buckets of water for each use, per the memorandum.
- For about three months, commissary access was not permitted, and inmates resorted to searching trash cans for food according to the sentencing memorandum.
- Defendant A reported constant exposure to secondhand smoke despite not being a smoker, with side effects including dizziness and headaches, per the memorandum.
- For approximately nine months during detention, inmates were not permitted family visits, according to the sentencing memorandum.
- Defendant A, a devout Catholic, reported limited ability to see a priest or attend religious services while detained.
- Defendant A’s February 10, 2021 meeting with the Confidential Source was audio and video recorded in the hotel restaurant in Miami.
- The Confidential Source and a co-conspirator communicated regularly with truck drivers transporting the tiles, receiving location updates and photographs during the trip from Texas to Florida.
- On March 28, 2021, Defendant A discussed that $1,000,000 of the funds would be routed through a company he controlled as part of the planned money movement.
- Defendant A understood by March 28, 2021 that the transactions involved illegal proceeds, based on the way the transactions were structured and the discussions with the Confidential Source.
- At the March 21, 2021 dinner, Defendant A stated his focus was on money and commented that 'there are several government things involved,' reflecting awareness of risk and environment.
- Defendant A initially believed the funds were connected to a prior legal investment with a co-conspirator before learning of the illegal source.
- The Confidential Source and a co-conspirator discussed being 'cunning' with Defendant A concerning the exact nature of the funds.
- The Confidential Source told Defendant A on March 28, 2021 that his role related only to moving money and not to the drug handling itself.
- The Confidential Source initially proposed moving $8,150,000 on February 10, 2021, before later discussing $4,150,000 on March 28, 2021 as the amount to be moved.
- According to the sentencing memorandum, Defendant A traveled to the United States after being told he would be repaid for a prior investment with a co-conspirator and was asked to pick up and wire $1 million through his accounts.
- The sentencing memorandum asserts that the remaining cash to be moved was no more than $2,500,000 and was to be taken to a parking lot in Aventura for pickup by associates of the Confidential Source.
- The sentencing memorandum states that a co-conspirator recruited Defendant A because the co-conspirator had a U.S. visa issue and needed someone to travel to pick up the money.
- The sentencing memorandum claims the Confidential Source was introduced to Defendant A as an employee of the U.S. government during the recruitment to travel to Miami.
- The sentencing memorandum acknowledges that by the final meetings Defendant A reasonably should have believed he was being asked to launder illegal proceeds.

- On January 6, 2021, a Joint Session of Congress convened at the U.S. Capitol to certify the Electoral College vote.
- On January 6, 2021, the Capitol’s exterior plaza was closed to the public due to the certification proceeding.
- The Joint Session began at approximately 1:00 p.m. with the Vice President presiding in the Senate chamber.
- Shortly after 2:00 p.m. on January 6, crowd members forced entry into the Capitol by breaking windows, ramming doors, and assaulting officers.
- Members of Congress were evacuated at approximately 2:20 p.m., and the certification proceeding was halted.
- The Joint Session reconvened at approximately 8:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, after law enforcement regained control of the Capitol.
- Defendant A is a founding lifetime member of the Oath Keepers organization.
- On November 14, 2020, Defendant A attended the “Million MAGA March” in Washington, D.C. and participated with other Oath Keepers to protest the 2020 election result.
- During a November 14, 2020 interview at the march, Defendant A made statements predicting confrontation and referring to the election as fraud.
- Defendant A stated on November 14, 2020 that if violence was brought, people were ready to respond, and referenced potential bloodshed.
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant A attended the “Stop the Steal” rally at the Ellipse in Washington, D.C.
- On January 6, Defendant A wore a tactical vest and a hat reading “Oath Keepers Lifetime Member.”
- On January 6, Defendant A carried bear spray on his person.
- Bear spray is not intended for use against humans and qualifies as a dangerous weapon.
- After the rally ended on January 6, Defendant A joined a large crowd marching from the Ellipse to the Capitol.
- Defendant A was aware that the Joint Session to certify the Electoral College vote was underway as he marched to the Capitol.
- During the march, Defendant A learned that the Vice President planned to proceed with the certification.
- After arriving on Capitol grounds, Defendant A walked through a dense crowd past restricted barriers and approached doors on the west side of the Capitol.
- At approximately 2:40 p.m., Defendant A positioned himself at the front of a mob that broke open doors near the Senate Parliamentarian’s office guarded by Capitol Police in riot gear.
- Defendant A was among the first six individuals to push past the damaged doors into the Capitol, forcing officers to retreat.
- Once inside, Defendant A and others advanced toward five to six backpedaling officers attempting to maintain a security line.
- Officers deployed a chemical irritant to disperse the advancing mob inside the Capitol.
- Defendant A was sprayed in the face by the chemical irritant during the confrontation inside the Capitol.
- Defendant A exited the building about nine minutes after entry, leaving through the same doors with his bear spray unholstered in hand.
- When he unlawfully entered, Defendant A knew the certification proceeding had commenced and that the Vice President did not intend to stop the certification.
- Defendant A entered the Capitol wearing a tactical vest and armed with bear spray to stop or delay the Congressional proceeding by intimidation or coercion.
- Defendant A admits that his belief that the Electoral College results were fraudulent is not a legal justification for his actions.
- Defendant A agreed and stipulated to the Statement of Offense as the factual basis for his guilty plea.
- Defendant A pled guilty to an Information charging one count of Obstruction of an Official Proceeding (18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2)) and one count of Entering and Remaining in a Restricted Building or Grounds with a Deadly or Dangerous Weapon (18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1), (b)(1)(A)).
- Defendant A waived his right to be indicted and pled guilty to an Information.
- A violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2) carries a maximum sentence of 20 years, a fine up to $250,000, and up to 3 years of supervised release.
- A violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1), (b)(1)(A) carries a maximum sentence of 10 years, a fine up to $250,000, and up to 3 years of supervised release.
- Defendant A agreed to pay a $100 special assessment per felony conviction.
- The Plea Agreement stated that, based on information then available, Defendant A had no prior criminal convictions.

- The Plea Agreement required Defendant A to promptly turn over evidence of crimes, contraband, proceeds, and assets traceable to such proceeds.
- The Plea Agreement required Defendant A to submit a full accounting of all financial assets, including those held in third-party names.
- Defendant A agreed to testify fully and truthfully before grand juries and at trials or other court proceedings where his testimony may be deemed relevant.
- The Plea Agreement required Defendant A not to commit any federal, state, or local crimes during the cooperation period or before sentencing.

- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened at the U.S. Capitol to certify the Electoral College vote, with the Vice President present and presiding.
- On January 6, 2021, the exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to the public and only authorized people with identification were allowed inside.
- Shortly after 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals in the crowd forced entry into the U.S. Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement.
- By approximately 2:20 p.m., members of the House and Senate evacuated and proceedings were suspended until about 8:00 p.m. that evening.
- The riot resulted in substantial damage to the U.S. Capitol that required more than $1.4 million to repair.
- As of July 7, 2023, the approximate losses from the January 6 incident were $2,923,080.05, reflecting damage to the Capitol and related costs.
- U.S. Capitol Police requested assistance from the Metropolitan Police Department to protect the Capitol and expel the crowd.
- Officer A wore a full MPD uniform, including a marked helmet, tactical vest, badge, and duty belt with official insignia displayed.
- Officer A was pulled from the lower west terrace doorway into the crowd, where he was beaten and tased, and his badge, radio, and a 17-round magazine were taken.
- During the assault, an individual attempted to remove Officer A’s service weapon and threatened to take the gun and kill him.
- Officer A lost consciousness after the assault and was hospitalized for injuries including a likely concussion and taser-related injuries, with cardiac monitoring.
- Officer A’s radio was securely attached to his tactical vest and his badge was pinned to the vest.
- Officer A’s vest had a hole where the badge had been forcibly ripped off, with the tear aligning with the badge pin design.
- Officer A’s body-worn camera recorded an individual reaching toward him and pulling out items consistent with a badge and police radio.
- Defendant A moved to the mouth of the lower west terrace tunnel around 3:08 p.m., entered the tunnel, and advanced toward the police line.
- Defendant A exited the tunnel around 3:11 p.m.
- As Defendant A exited the tunnel, he said, “Let me out. Let me out,” and then said he was from Buffalo and needed to get refreshed.
- Defendant A recorded and posted an Instagram video from near the inauguration stage on the lower west terrace stating, “Just got tear-gassed... we’re pushing forward now!”
- Defendant A’s Instagram post carried the caption, “Wildest experience of my life !!”
- At about 3:19 p.m., while Officer A was being attacked, Defendant A reached toward the officer and forcibly removed the officer’s badge and radio from the tactical vest.
- When Defendant A removed the badge and radio, he knew the officer was performing official duties.
- Around 3:20 p.m., people in the crowd surrounded Officer A to protect him and escorted him back to the police line.
- At approximately 3:36 p.m., Defendant A posed for a photo holding and pointing to a U.S. Capitol Police riot shield.
- On January 7, 2021, Defendant A posted an Instagram video stating he was very worried for America, calling the events a disgrace, and comparing the violence to prior protests.
- On January 27, 2021, during an FBI interview, Defendant A acknowledged being at the Capitol, said he saw an officer attacked, and claimed he tried to pull the officer away but left due to the violence.
- On February 2, 2021, Defendant A told an agent his previous account was accurate and falsely claimed he had not participated in the assault in any way.
- On February 23, 2021, after being shown body-worn camera images, Defendant A admitted grabbing the officer’s badge and radio and claimed he pressed the radio’s emergency button to summon help.
- Records show the emergency button on the officer’s radio was pressed at 3:37 p.m., about 16 minutes after Officer A had been escorted back to safety.
- Defendant A gave inconsistent accounts of what he did with the badge and radio: first claiming he dropped them immediately, then that he threw them in a trash can on Constitution Avenue.
- Defendant A later said he took the items to his hotel and then to his home in Buffalo, planned to turn them in, but instead threw them in a dumpster.
- On February 24, 2021, Defendant A specified the dumpster location as behind the Lenox Hotel at 140 North Street in Buffalo.
- After being told the dumpster’s video would be checked, on February 26, 2021, Defendant A admitted he had buried the badge in his backyard, purchased a metal detector to find it, and returned it to an agent.
- Defendant A stated that he discarded the radio and it was never recovered.
- The stolen police radio had a value of $5,500.79.
- Officer A was tased multiple times during the attack, experienced excruciating pain, and sustained burn marks that resulted in scarring.
- Officer A lost consciousness for more than two minutes following the attack.
- Body-worn camera audio captured Officer A screaming during the tasing at approximately 15:19:15, 15:19:17, and 15:19:21–22.
- Officer A collapsed around 15:21 and was carried back inside the Capitol, where officers called for medical assistance.
- Officer A regained consciousness at approximately 15:23:32 and asked if the door had been retaken.
- Radio system data showed emergency activations for Officer A’s radio at 2:37:56 p.m. and 3:37:13 p.m. on January 6, 2021.
- Officer A’s body-worn camera showed Defendant A taking the radio at approximately 3:19 p.m., and by about 3:21 p.m. other officers had escorted Officer A back to safety.
- Initial investigators noted that once the radio was reported stolen, it was deactivated, erasing records of its use.
- Defendant A posed with a U.S. Capitol Police shield during the events at the Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- During his January 27, 2021 interview, Defendant A said the shield had been passed through the crowd and he asked someone to take his picture with it.
- Defendant A agreed and stipulated to the factual basis in the Statement of Offense as part of his guilty plea.
- On December 1, 2021, a superseding indictment charged Defendant A with multiple offenses including 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2), 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3), 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1), 18 U.S.C. § 2111, 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1)–(4), and 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(E) and (F).
- On March 3, 2023, Defendant A pled guilty to one count of 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1) and two counts of 18 U.S.C. § 661 in connection with taking an officer’s badge and radio.
- The statutory maximum penalty for 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1) in this case is up to 8 years’ imprisonment and up to 3 years of supervised release.
- The statutory maximum penalty for each count of 18 U.S.C. § 661 is up to 5 years’ imprisonment and up to one year of supervised release.
- During the attack on Officer A near the lower west terrace tunnel, a member of the mob threatened to take the officer’s gun and kill him.
- Officer A’s badge was later returned to law enforcement by Defendant A, covered in mud, after being dug up from Defendant A’s backyard.
- In the immediate aftermath on January 6, Defendant A publicly characterized the day as the “wildest experience” of his life on social media.
- Defendant A acknowledged to agents that he heard people in the crowd saying to get the officer’s gun and kill him.
- Defendant A initially claimed to agents that he tried to pull the officer away but was unable to reach him and left because of the violence.
- Body-worn camera stills show the officer’s radio being pulled out by the antenna and the back of the badge in a hand.
- Defendant A admitted to pressing the radio’s emergency button, though records show an activation occurred at 3:37 p.m., after the officer had been returned to safety.
- Officer A’s body-worn camera captured bystanders creating a protective circle around him before escorting him back toward the tunnel.
- Defendant A told agents he feared being arrested if he turned the badge and radio over to an officer at the scene.
- An agent used a ruse email indicating security cameras at the Lenox Hotel would be checked; subsequently Defendant A admitted the badge was buried in his backyard.
- On January 6, 2021, the lower west terrace tunnel was a location of intense fighting where officers formed a police line to prevent entry.
- Defendant A publicly stated on January 7, 2021, that “an innocent life was lost” during the events of January 6.
- During his February 23, 2021 interview, Defendant A said he dropped the badge and radio and left, then later claimed he carried them away and dropped them in a trash can on Constitution Avenue.
- Defendant A later told agents he brought the items back to his hotel and then to his home in Buffalo, intending to turn them in but throwing them in a dumpster instead.
- On March 3, 2023, the court filed the Statement of Offense containing Defendant A’s stipulation to the factual basis for the plea.
- Defendant A’s conduct included removing equipment from a law enforcement officer he knew was engaged in official duties.
- More than one hundred police officers were injured during the January 6 attack as noted in the government’s description of the events.
- Defendant A initially denied involvement in taking the officer’s items before admitting his actions when confronted with body-worn camera evidence.
- Following the assault, Officer A was transported to the Emergency Room.
- A witness provided the FBI with a video of Defendant A at the lower west terrace stating he had been tear-gassed and that they were pushing forward.
- A second witness provided a photograph of Defendant A holding a U.S. Capitol Police shield that had been posted on a social media account.
- Defendant A’s removal of the officer’s badge created a tear in the fabric of the tactical vest consistent with the badge pin design.
- The officer’s radio was never recovered after being taken during the assault.
- Defendant A told agents he was afraid of being arrested if he turned in the stolen items, contributing to his decision to discard or bury them.
- Defendant A’s conduct occurred in the lower west terrace area where officers had formed a line to prevent entry into the Capitol building.
- In a January 27, 2021 interview, Defendant A claimed the attackers could not remove the officer’s gun due to a plastic retention device on the holster.
- At the time of the assault, the officer’s badge and radio were securely affixed to his vest.
- The superseding indictment included offenses for entering or remaining, disorderly conduct, impeding ingress/egress, and engaging in physical violence in restricted grounds under 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1)–(4).
- The superseding indictment included two misdemeanor counts under 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(E) and (F).
- Defense counsel asserts Defendant A was arrested around March 14, 2021 and detained until his release to home confinement on October 26, 2021.
- Defense counsel states Defendant A spent about two months in administrative segregation while detained pretrial.
- Defense counsel states Defendant A has been on 24-hour home confinement for approximately 21 months following his release on October 26, 2021.
- Defense counsel represents that Defendant A was prescribed Adderall before January 6, 2021, and was later determined to have been misdiagnosed, with the medication being contraindicated for his actual condition.
- Defense counsel asserts that Defendant A’s aunt, a physician, spoke with him on the evening of January 6 and perceived pressured, frenzied speech and confusion about the events.
- Defense counsel asserts Defendant A was prescribed a 40 mg daily dose of Adderall, described as a maximum daily dose per CMS, during the relevant period.

- On January 6, 2021, the exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to members of the public.
- The U.S. Capitol is secured 24 hours a day by U.S. Capitol Police, with access limited to authorized persons with identification.
- A joint session of Congress to certify the 2020 Electoral College vote convened at approximately 1:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, with the Vice President present and presiding.
- Around 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals forced their way through barricades and advanced to the exterior façade of the Capitol.
- Members of the crowd entered the Capitol without authorization and without security screening or weapons checks.
- Individuals forced entry into the Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement officers shortly after 2:00 p.m.
- The riot caused more than $1.4 million in damage to the U.S. Capitol requiring repairs.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m., the House and Senate, including the Vice President, evacuated and congressional proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- The Vice President remained in the Capitol from evacuation until the session resumed after the building was secured.
- U.S. Capitol Police requested assistance from MPD and other agencies to protect the Capitol and remove the crowd.
- Officer A, an MPD officer assisting at the Capitol, wore a full MPD uniform with helmet, tactical vest, badge, duty belt, and insignia displayed.
- Around 3:00 p.m., a police line formed at one of two glass doorways on the lower west terrace to prevent entry into the Capitol.
- At about 3:14 p.m., Defendant A joined rioters in the lower west terrace archway and pushed against the police line.
- Defendant A advanced into the tunnel near the police line, took riot shields from other rioters, and used them to strike at police.
- Around 3:15 p.m., Defendant A used a riot shield to push against officers at the front of the police line and struck an officer’s hand.
- At 3:18:52 p.m., Defendant A wrapped his arm around Officer A's neck and pulled the officer into the crowd outside the tunnel, shouting a declaration that he had one.
- At 3:19:01 p.m., Officer A pushed against Defendant A’s chest to try to break the hold.
- Defendant A maintained control of Officer A and pulled him further into the crowd for approximately 25 seconds before separation.
- After being pulled into the crowd, rioters assaulted Officer A, including tasing him and robbing his badge and police radio.
- Rioters held Officer A’s limbs and tried to forcibly remove his service weapon from its fixed holster.
- A member of the crowd threatened to take Officer A’s gun and kill him during the attack.
- Officer A was kicked, punched, pushed, grabbed, and hit with objects by the crowd during the assault.
- Some members of the mob later surrounded Officer A to protect him and escorted him back to the police line.
- Defendant A knew Officer A was a law enforcement officer engaged in the performance of official duties when he pulled the officer into the crowd.
- Another individual repeatedly applied a taser to the back of Officer A’s neck during the assault.
- The tasing caused excruciating pain and rendered Officer A momentarily helpless, as captured on body-worn camera.
- Tasing caused burn marks to Officer A’s neck resulting in scarring.
- Officer A lost consciousness for more than two minutes shortly after being tased.
- Officer A was transported to the emergency room after the assault.
- Defendant A stated he does not have first-hand knowledge of the actions of three identified co-defendants on January 6, 2021, and could not truthfully testify about their activities.
- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Count Four of the indictment charging violation of 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1).
- A violation of 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1) carries a statutory maximum sentence of 8 years of imprisonment.
- The offense carries a potential fine of up to $250,000 or twice the pecuniary gain or loss.
- The offense carries a potential term of supervised release of not more than 3 years.
- Defendant A must pay a $100 special assessment for the felony conviction.
- The government agreed to request dismissal of the remaining counts at sentencing in exchange for the guilty plea.
- Defendant A acknowledged that, as of May 17, 2021, damage to the Capitol from the riot was approximately $1,495,326.55.
- Defendant A agreed to pay $2,000 in restitution to the Department of the Treasury.
- Defendant A agreed not to object to detention pending sentencing.
- The plea agreement was executed on April 26, 2022.
- Defendant A signed the Statement of Offense acknowledging its truth and accuracy on April 26, 2022.

- Defendant A had prior convictions for vandalism and public intoxication in Sullivan County, Tennessee, with sentences imposed on November 3, 2015.
- Defendant A had a prior conviction for theft up to $500 with a sentence imposed on November 3, 2015, including 180 days confinement.

- Defendant A was arrested on April 14, 2021.
- Defendant A made his initial appearance in the Eastern District of Tennessee on April 15, 2021, was ordered detained, and committed to the District of Columbia.
- Defendant A has remained in continuous federal custody since April 15, 2021.
- Defendant A made his initial appearance in the District of Columbia and was arraigned on May 6, 2021.

- Since 2004, ISIS and its predecessors have been designated as a Foreign Terrorist Organization and as a Specially Designated Global Terrorist under federal law.
- On May 15, 2014, the ISIS designation was amended to add aliases such as ISIL, Daesh, and other names.
- On September 21, 2015, additional aliases including Islamic State, ISIL, and ISIS were added to the FTO listing.
- ISIS remains designated as a Foreign Terrorist Organization.
- ISIS disseminates propaganda and recruitment materials via social media platforms such as Twitter, Facebook, Telegram, YouTube, Instagram, and Paltalk.
- Since on or about September 22, 2014, ISIS advocated for the killing of Americans and Europeans, including civilians, and gave approval for indiscriminate killing by supporters in those regions.
- The FBI and a federal grand jury in the District of Columbia were conducting an international terrorism investigation into whether individuals in Washington, DC and elsewhere were committing offenses related to ISIS, including attempted travel and planning terrorist acts in the United States.
- The investigation included potential violations of 18 U.S.C. § 2339B regarding providing, attempting to provide, and conspiring to provide material support to ISIS.
