- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened at the U.S. Capitol to certify the Electoral College vote.
- On January 6, 2021, the exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to members of the public.
- Only authorized people with appropriate identification were allowed access inside the U.S. Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- Around 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals in the crowd forced entry into the Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m., members of the House and Senate were evacuated and Congressional proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- Vice President Pence remained in the Capitol from the time he was evacuated from the Senate Chamber until the session resumed on January 6, 2021.
- The riot at the Capitol required the expenditure of more than $2.7 million for repairs.
- Defendant A traveled to Washington, D.C., to protest Congress’s certification of the Electoral College and what the defendant believed was a fraudulent election.
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant A attended the “Stop the Steal” rally and then walked to the Capitol.
- Defendant A went to the Lower West Terrace area that was being assembled for the upcoming Presidential Inauguration.
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant A wore a Harley Davidson beanie, a gray or black jacket, large-frame prescription glasses, a lanyard with a “media” card, and an earbud.
- Defendant A carried a camera he used to film events at the Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- While at the front of a police line, Defendant A called out the names and badge numbers of specific police officers.
- Defendant A told officers their equipment belonged to him and yelled for them to give him tear gas, a helmet, and a badge.
- Defendant A yelled for the officers to lay down their weapons, go home, and let the crowd into what he called his building.
- At approximately 2:24 p.m. on January 6, 2021, Defendant A shouted at officers, “We’re gonna run over you! Do you think your little pea-shooter guns are gonna stop this crowd?”
- At approximately 2:31 p.m., officers retreated from a police line up onto the inaugural stage, and Defendant A followed them.
- Defendant A was part of the first wave of rioters to rush the Lower West Terrace area of the exterior of the Capitol Building.
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant A yelled, “I want my bullet and goin in my house,” as he made his way up a wide stone railing of the Capitol exterior.
- At approximately 2:56 p.m., Defendant A advanced to the Lower West Terrace Tunnel.
- Defendant A entered the Lower West Terrace Tunnel on two occasions while another active police line was being maintained.
- Defendant A remained in the Lower West Terrace area for several hours, filming and shouting, before leaving the restricted grounds at approximately 5:15 p.m.
- Defendant A admitted the elements of 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1) by acknowledging he knowingly entered or remained in restricted U.S. Capitol grounds without lawful authority.
- Defendant A signed the Statement of Offense in early April 2023 confirming its accuracy.
- A tip received on or about January 19, 2021, referenced a video on Archive.org titled “capitol-eviction-notice-opart-2.”
- The video showed a male claiming the name “Andrew” of “Austin, Texas,” discussing involvement in the Capitol events while filming other protestors.
- In the video, the individual encouraged others to make entry into a U.S. Capitol entrance.
- The video records the individual shouting variations of “we want into our house.”
- The video captures the individual shouting, “I want my bullet and goin in my house,” while climbing a wide stone railing outside the Capitol.
- The affidavit describes a standoff in the West Terrace tunnel where protestors attempted to re-enter and used batons and riot shields against officers.
- During the standoff, the individual encouraged others to take over the building and shouted about wanting the door, bullets, and tear gas while approaching the west tunnel entrance.
- At approximately 8:39 in the video, the individual yelled, “we need more people.”
- Around 12:15 in the video, he yelled “send helmets forward” while rioters were fighting with law enforcement.
- At around 12:50 in the video, he described people pushing in, getting their “bullet,” being cracked on the head, and rotating.
- Near 16:34 in the video, he asked, “fresh people—fresh people, are we in?”
- The individual filmed himself wearing a beanie, jacket, large-frame glasses, a lanyard with a “media” card, and an earbud.
- The reporting party stated the video was originally uploaded by the individual and later made private.
- Investigators identified the video’s subject as Defendant A using records including a motor-vehicle photograph and other identifying information.
- Defendant A is the content creator of a YouTube channel called “Political Trance Tribune.”
- The individual presents himself as a law-enforcement auditor and routinely records police during public duties.
- On March 10, 2021, agents executed a search warrant at Defendant A’s residence in Texas.
- During the search, Defendant A said he went to Washington, D.C., because he expected “something unprecedented” and wanted to cover events as an independent journalist and civil rights auditor.
- Defendant A confirmed photos were of him near the Capitol wearing a press-access lanyard.
- He stated that at one point near an archway, the crowd pushed up against him and shoved him into a wall.
- Defendant A acknowledged the referenced video footage was his and that he can be heard yelling “send helmets forward,” which he claimed he repeated after someone else.
- He said he wore his press card so people would know who he is.
- He noted that at about 17:49 in the video he said, “Are we in?”
- Defendant A described himself as being there as a “chameleon.”
- He said he wishes he had not said the things he did and should have just filmed events.
- He expressed concern that the government had been hijacked and referenced a banned video about election fraud.
- He stated he is like-minded with QAnon and believes people in power keep power through child pedophilia to control the masses.
- A local police officer who had recent contact with Defendant A identified him as the person depicted in the Capitol video.
- Defendant A’s spouse confirmed his identity in a sanitized photograph showing him in the west tunnel of the U.S. Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant A’s spouse said he drove to the Capitol with a group of political YouTube auditors who sometimes refer to themselves as independent patriots.
- The affidavit asserted probable cause that Defendant A violated 18 U.S.C. §§ 1752(a)(1) and (2).
- The affidavit asserted probable cause that Defendant A violated 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2).
- The affidavit asserted probable cause that Defendant A violated 40 U.S.C. §§ 5104(e)(2)(D) and (G).
- The affidavit asserted probable cause that Defendant A aided and abetted the above-referenced offenses under 18 U.S.C. § 2.
- According to a defense filing, Defendant A pleaded guilty to one misdemeanor count of entering or remaining in a restricted building or grounds in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1).
- The defense states that Defendant A never physically entered the Capitol building and did not engage in destruction or violence.
- The defense acknowledges that Defendant A encouraged others to engage in misconduct, shouted at police officers, and videotaped much of his behavior on January 6, 2021.
- The defense represents that Defendant A is remorseful and has ceased the type of citizen journalism and auditing that preceded his conduct.
- The defense filing describes Defendant A as a 62-year-old experienced arborist who owns his own business.
- The defense states that Defendant A often assists with emergency services in the aftermath of hurricanes and other storms.
- The defense reports that Defendant A has four children and two adult stepchildren, and he and his spouse have fourteen grandchildren.
- The defense states that Defendant A and his spouse serve as ministry leaders and work with a non-profit creating a gated homeless community.
- The defense indicates that Defendant A previously considered himself a “First Amendment auditor,” but stopped after January 6, 2021.
- According to the defense, Defendant A has one criminal history point and has complied with pretrial release conditions.
- The defense states that Defendant A has been sober for nearly 20 years, after prior substance abuse that contributed to minor offenses between 1979 and 2000.
- The defense reports that Defendant A’s only recent conviction was for interfering with public duties, for which he received a suspended 15-day sentence.
- The defense asserts that Defendant A cooperated with law enforcement and provided a detailed statement about his conduct.
- The defense indicates that Defendant A did not organize the events of January 6 and did not engage in after-the-fact obstructive behavior or destroy evidence.
- According to the defense, Defendant A recorded much of his activity on January 6 and later expressed remorse.
- The affidavit alleges that Defendant A gained entry inside the U.S. Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- The affidavit states the certification proceedings were still underway at the time individuals forced entry, and the exterior doors and windows were locked or otherwise secured.
- Members of the U.S. Capitol Police attempted to maintain order and keep the crowd from entering the Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- The video shows the individual encouraging others, including shouting about wanting a door and tear gas, near the west tunnel entrance.

- On March 26, 2021, a criminal complaint with arrest warrant was filed in the District of Columbia related to the January 6, 2021 Capitol events.
- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of the U.S. Congress convened to certify the Electoral College vote and began at approximately 1:00 p.m.
- Vice President was present and presiding at the Capitol on January 6, 2021, first in the joint session and then in the Senate chamber.
- By approximately 2:20 p.m. on January 6, 2021, members of the House and Senate, including the Vice President, evacuated their chambers and proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- The U.S. Capitol was secured 24 hours a day by U.S. Capitol Police and the exterior plaza was closed to the public on January 6, 2021.
- Shortly after 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals in the crowd forced entry into the U.S. Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement.
- The January 6, 2021 attack resulted in substantial damage to the U.S. Capitol, requiring expenditures of more than $2.7 million.
- As of October 17, 2022, losses related to the January 6 Capitol breach were approximately $2,881,360.20.
- A tipster (Person A) informed the FBI on January 29, 2021, that the defendant was at the U.S. Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- On February 11, 2021, Person A told the FBI they recognized the defendant’s voice on a video posted to the defendant’s Instagram account depicting the Capitol interior on January 6.
- On March 24, 2021, an FBI agent reviewed publicly available video showing an individual resembling the defendant inside the Capitol; the agent compared frames to a DMV photo and concluded it appeared to be the same person.
- Cell-site records on January 6, 2021 indicated the defendant’s phone utilized a site consistent with servicing the interior of the U.S. Capitol building.
- On January 5, 2021, while traveling to Washington, D.C., the defendant posted on Instagram that they were going to get into trouble the next day.
- On January 6, 2021 at approximately 1:09 p.m., the defendant posted a video of individuals walking toward the Capitol with superimposed text saying “Time to storm the Capitol.”
- At approximately 1:20 p.m. on January 6, 2021, the defendant recorded themselves stating that barricades had been broken and that they were going in.
- Between about 1:00 p.m. and 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, the defendant was near scaffolding on the West Terrace and assisted other rioters to climb a displaced bike rack.
- At approximately 2:09 p.m., the defendant moved under scaffolding, raised a face covering, and climbed steps to join the front line confronting police on the Northwest steps.
- Shortly thereafter, rioters broke through the police line on the Northwest steps; the defendant was among the first through and proceeded toward the Upper West Terrace.
- While on the Upper West Terrace, the defendant recorded a video approaching the Northwest Terrace door and stated, “We’re storming the ... Capitol. Promises kept.”
- The defendant climbed bleachers overlooking the inauguration stage and proclaimed, “We broke through… We did it.” before moving toward the Senate Wing Door.
- At approximately 2:15 p.m. on January 6, 2021, the defendant entered the Capitol Building by climbing through a broken window to the right of the Senate Wing Door.
- After entry, the defendant moved through the Crypt, Rotunda, and Statuary Hall Connector, repeatedly joining the front line of rioters confronting police.
- At about 2:36 p.m., rioters broke through the police line at the Statuary Hall Connector; the defendant then moved toward the Speaker’s Lobby.
- On the way to the Speaker’s Lobby, the defendant signaled to rioters outside the Senate Door, attempted to open the doors from inside, but others opened them first.
- The defendant entered a bathroom next to the Speaker’s Lobby and damaged a television and a water cooler belonging to the United States.
- The estimated replacement value of the damaged television was $443; the water cooler was replaced by the rental company.
- At approximately 2:45 p.m., after a rioter was shot while attempting to enter the Speaker’s Lobby, the defendant exited the bathroom and later left the Capitol.
- On January 7, 2021, the defendant posted on social media that they had been at the very front line when taking the steps and that “we forced our way in, foot by foot.”
- On January 7, 2021, the defendant wrote, “We just wanted to storm Congress and stop the certification.”
- The defendant admitted they knowingly entered the U.S. Capitol without permission and engaged in disorderly or disruptive conduct with intent to impede or disrupt a session of Congress.
- The defendant admitted they willfully and knowingly paraded, demonstrated, or picketed while inside the Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- The defendant admitted they corruptly obstructed, influenced, and impeded an official proceeding, namely Congress’s certification of the Electoral College vote, on January 6, 2021.
- On the morning law enforcement arrived at the defendant’s residence to execute arrest and search warrants, the defendant disabled and hid their cellphone intending to impair its availability for use in an official proceeding.
- On April 1, 2021, at about 6:13 a.m., FBI agents arrived at the defendant’s house to execute an arrest; the defendant did not exit until around 6:30 p.m., when they were arrested.
- Around midnight on March 31, 2021, the defendant posted on Instagram, “F*ck, cops outside ... Destroying phone now,” as part of an announced April Fools “joke” aimed at confusing law enforcement.
- Upon entry to the residence on April 1, 2021, agents found a charger for the defendant’s cellphone but could not locate the phone due to the volume of materials in the home.
- On April 11, 2021, a federal grand jury returned an indictment charging the defendant with offenses arising from January 6, 2021.
- On July 28, 2021, a superseding indictment added charges including obstruction of an official proceeding (18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2), § 2) and destruction of government property (18 U.S.C. § 1361).
- On August 23, 2022, the defendant was convicted on all seven counts at a bench trial on stipulated facts.
- Count One charged obstruction of an official proceeding and aiding and abetting under 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2) and § 2.
- Count Two charged injuring, damaging, or destroying property of the United States where the damage does not exceed $1,000, under 18 U.S.C. § 1361.
- Count Three charged entering or remaining in a restricted building or grounds, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1).
- Count Four charged disorderly or disruptive conduct in a restricted building or grounds, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(2).
- Count Five charged disorderly or disruptive conduct in a Capitol building, in violation of 40 U.S.C. § 5104(c)(2)(D).
- Count Six charged parading, demonstrating, or picketing in a Capitol building, in violation of 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(G).
- Count Seven charged corruptly altering, destroying, mutilating, or concealing a record, document, or other object, under 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(1).
- The statutory maximum term of imprisonment is 20 years on each of Counts One and Seven.
- The statutory maximum term of imprisonment is one year each for Counts Two, Three, and Four.
- The statutory maximum term of imprisonment is six months each for Counts Five and Six.
- The maximum fines are $250,000 for Counts One and Seven; $100,000 for Counts Two, Three, and Four; and $5,000 for Counts Five and Six.
- The maximum terms of supervised release are three years for Counts One and Seven, and one year for Counts Two, Three, and Four.
- The defendant admitted that on January 6, 2021 they intended to obstruct and impede the congressional proceeding certifying the Electoral College vote.
- The defendant publicly posted after January 6, 2021 that their goal had been to stop the certification and that law enforcement did not simply let rioters into the Capitol.
- The defendant used their cellphone on January 6 to record videos while moving through the Capitol, including scenes of police confrontations in the Crypt.
- The defendant moved to open rooms and encouraged others to enter areas inside the Capitol, including the Rayburn Reception Room.
- The defendant later posted voice messages describing damaging a bathroom television and water cooler near the Speaker’s Lobby.
- The defendant’s phone was turned off or out of service shortly after FBI announced presence at the residence on April 1, 2021.
- The defendant initially told agents the cellphone was left at a friend’s house, later saying they did not know where it was.
- On August 23, 2022, the defendant signed a Statement of Facts for a stipulated trial, acknowledging and accepting the factual proffer.
- The defendant admitted violating 40 U.S.C. § 5104 by uttering loud, threatening, or abusive language, or engaging in disorderly or disruptive conduct in the Capitol with intent to impede or disrupt a session of Congress.
- The defendant acknowledged in the Statement of Facts that a restricted building includes areas where the Vice President or other Secret Service protectee is temporarily visiting.
- The defendant’s Instagram account was publicly viewable and linked to a public Discord account observed by investigators in March 2021.
- In March 2021, the user of the defendant’s social media accounts posted statements such as “I rushed the Capitol” and “I’ll ... do it again.”
- The defendant told followers on January 6, 2021 that “we’re pushing all the way through” while at the Capitol steps amid confrontations with police.
- The defendant entered the Crypt and positioned at the front line of rioters facing police before lines were breached inside.
- The defendant attempted to facilitate entry of additional rioters by signaling to those outside and moving to open interior doors at the Senate Door.
- The Statement of Facts identifies that the damaged items in the Speaker’s Lobby bathroom were United States property or of a department or agency thereof.
- The defendant later described remaining in the Capitol until around 3:30 p.m. on January 6, 2021 in social media posts referenced by investigators.
- On March 7, 2021, the defendant posted a video addressing law enforcement stating that “The 6th was a warning.”
- On March 8, 2021, the defendant posted that they planned a sequel to January 6, stating an intention to return “on steroids.”
- On March 11, 2021, the defendant stated on Discord that “The 6th was our Boston Tea Party,” expressing a political analogy to the events.
- At the time of the January 6 events, the defendant was on bond for a state charge of Aggravated Stalking, according to the government’s memorandum referencing the PSR.
- The defendant was arrested in Florida on April 1, 2021 and detained thereafter in connection with this case.
- The defendant agreed to proceed by stipulated bench trial and signed a Statement of Facts acknowledging the evidence would establish each element of the charged offenses if found beyond a reasonable doubt.
- The defendant’s posts after January 6 reflected lack of remorse and statements that they would repeat similar conduct.
- The defendant admitted that on January 6 they entered a restricted building without lawful authority and did so knowingly.
- The defendant’s social media statements included “Time to storm the Capitol” as they walked toward the Capitol on January 6.
- Rioters broke the windows next to the Senate Wing Door around 2:13 p.m., after which rioters began entering; alarms were sounding and broken glass was visible.
- The defendant used social media to document their movements inside the Capitol, including from the Crypt and other areas.
- The defendant concealed their cellphone with the intent to impede its use as evidence in their criminal case and a grand jury investigation related to January 6.
- Counts Five and Six relate to conduct in the Capitol building prohibited by 40 U.S.C. § 5104 and occurred during the defendant’s time inside the building on January 6, 2021.
- The defendant’s conduct included helping other rioters scale obstacles by using bike racks as makeshift ladders on the West Terrace.
- The defendant acknowledged aiding and abetting others in obstructing the official proceeding within the stipulated elements of Count One.
- The defendant’s Statement of Facts was signed and acknowledged after consulting with counsel, indicating voluntary acceptance of its contents.

- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened to certify the Electoral College vote.
- By approximately 1:30 PM on January 6, 2021, the House and Senate adjourned to separate chambers to resolve an objection.
- The exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to the public on January 6, 2021.
- Only authorized people with appropriate identification were allowed inside the U.S. Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- Around 2:00 PM on January 6, 2021, individuals forced their way through barricades and advanced to the exterior façade of the building.
- Shortly after 2:00 PM, individuals forced entry into the Capitol by breaking windows and by assaulting members of law enforcement.
- At approximately 2:20 PM, members of Congress were evacuated and proceedings were effectively suspended until shortly after 8:00 PM on January 6, 2021.
- Congressional proceedings resumed at approximately 8:00 PM after the building had been secured.
- The riot resulted in expenditures of more than $2.7 million for repairs to the U.S. Capitol.
- On the morning of January 6, 2021, the defendant traveled by automobile with two companions from Pennsylvania to Washington, D.C., to protest Congress’s certification.
- The defendant and companions attended a rally and then marched with other protestors to the Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- At about 2:09 PM on January 6, 2021, while on the West Front of the Capitol, the defendant threw an object that hit a Metropolitan Police Department officer near a bike rack barricade.
- A companion saw the defendant throw the object that struck the officer.
- After throwing the object, the defendant and a companion retreated into a crowd of protesters.
- At approximately 2:20 PM on January 6, 2021, the defendant and two companions entered the Capitol through the Senate Wing door.
- While inside the Capitol, the defendant went to the Crypt, the Speaker of the House’s suite, the Rotunda, and the Senate Chamber.
- While in the Rotunda, the defendant moved velvet rope stanchions to help other protesters walk through.
- While on the third floor in the Senate Gallery hallway, the defendant had zip ties and handcuff restraints in his hands.
- The defendant and companions went to the Senate Chamber Press Gallery and then back to the second floor.
- The defendant and companions entered the Senate Chamber at approximately 2:29 PM on January 6, 2021.
- In the Senate Chamber, the defendant and companions looked through Senators’ desks and took pictures of documents.
- The defendant took a letter from a Senator’s desk and destroyed it after leaving the Capitol.
- Before destroying the letter, the defendant sent a picture of it to a friend.
- The defendant and companions exited the Senate Chamber at approximately 2:59 PM.
- They exited the Capitol through the Senate Carriage door at approximately 3:05 PM.
- On March 9, 2021, the defendant went to the FBI’s Washington Field Office and was arrested.
- After being advised of his Miranda rights on March 9, 2021, the defendant waived those rights and was interviewed by the FBI.
- During the March 9, 2021 interview, the defendant falsely claimed he was unaware of any assaults on law enforcement and that he did not participate in any violent acts.
- The defendant admits he forcibly assaulted a person designated in 18 U.S.C. § 1114 engaged in official duties by throwing an object that hit a law enforcement officer on the West Front of the Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- The defendant admits he knew the officer was engaged in the performance of official duties at the time of the assault and that he assaulted the officer on account of those duties.
- The defendant admits he willfully and knowingly entered the U.S. Capitol knowing he did not have permission to do so.
- The defendant admits that while inside the Capitol he corruptly obstructed, influenced, and impeded an official proceeding, namely the certification of the Electoral College vote.
- The defendant signed the Statement of Offense on September 26, 2022, acknowledging it is true and accurate.
- The defendant agreed to plead guilty to one count of 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1).
- The defendant agreed to plead guilty to one count of 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2) and 2.
- For 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1), the defendant acknowledged a statutory maximum penalty of 8 years of imprisonment and a fine of up to $250,000.
- For 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2) and 2, the defendant acknowledged a statutory maximum penalty of 20 years of imprisonment and a fine of up to $250,000.
- The defendant agreed to pay a $100 special assessment per felony conviction.
- The government agreed to request dismissal of the remaining counts of the Fourth Superseding Indictment at sentencing.
- The defendant agreed to allow law enforcement to review any social media accounts for statements and postings in and around January 6, 2021.
- The defendant agreed to an interview by law enforcement regarding the events in and around January 6, 2021, prior to sentencing.
- The defendant acknowledged that the January 6, 2021 riot caused approximately $2,734,783.14 in damage to the U.S. Capitol as of April 5, 2022.
- The defendant agreed to pay $2,000 in restitution to the Architect of the Capitol.
- Based on information available at the time of the plea, including a pre-plea criminal history calculation, the defendant had no criminal convictions and was estimated to have zero criminal history points.
- The criminal complaint was attested telephonically under Fed. R. Crim. P. 4.1 on February 12, 2021.
- Around January 18, 2021, the FBI received multiple tips identifying the defendant in images of unlawful activity on January 6, 2021.
- A person acquainted with the defendant received texts and videos directly from him on January 6, 2021, appearing to be filmed from inside the Capitol, including the Senate floor.
- In two videos sent to an acquaintance, the defendant panned the camera so his face could be seen and was wearing green tactical clothing, a helmet, and gear.
- Publicly posted videos showed a male matching the defendant’s description on the Senate floor holding documents from a Senate desk.
- In publicly posted videos, the individual read from a document indicating an objection to certification of Arizona’s electoral votes and stated a comment expressing belief of betrayal before acknowledging being mixed up.
- In a hallway video, the individual identified as the defendant can be heard yelling an expletive while proceeding through the hallway.
- Another acquaintance identified the defendant in public-source video by appearance and voice while he commented about a Senator after reviewing papers on a Senate desk.
- The Capitol’s exterior doors and windows were locked or otherwise secured during the certification proceedings on January 6, 2021.
- U.S. Capitol Police attempted to maintain order and keep the crowd from entering the Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- Members of Congress could not resume proceedings until after unauthorized occupants had been removed and the building was secured.
- A restricted building includes an area where the Vice President or other Secret Service protectee is or will be temporarily visiting or any building so restricted for a special event of national significance.

- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to a single-count indictment charging felon in possession of a firearm and ammunition in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(1).
- The plea agreement was filed on September 24, 2021.
- The case is in the Central District of California.
- On or about July 1, 2020, Defendant A knowingly possessed a firearm and ammunition in Los Angeles County within the Central District of California.
- The firearm was a Glock, model G23, .40 caliber handgun bearing serial number PGB797.
- Defendant A possessed seven rounds of Independence .40 caliber ammunition.
- Defendant A possessed four rounds of Winchester .40 caliber ammunition.
- Defendant A possessed the firearm and ammunition at the residence where he was residing.
- The firearm and ammunition were manufactured outside the State of California.
- Before July 1, 2020, the firearm and ammunition had traveled in and affected interstate commerce.
- At the time of possession on July 1, 2020, Defendant A had previously been convicted of at least one felony offense punishable by more than one year of imprisonment.
- Defendant A had a prior conviction for Attempted Pandering by Encouraging on or about February 13, 2018.
- Defendant A had a prior conviction for Possession of a Controlled Substance for Sale (Cal. H&S § 11351.5) on or about April 17, 2017.
- Defendant A had a prior conviction for Possession of a Controlled Substance for Sale (Cal. H&S § 11351) on or about September 19, 2013.
- Defendant A had a prior conviction for Felon in Possession of a Firearm (Cal. Penal Code § 12021(a)(1)) on or about July 27, 2010.
- Defendant A had a prior conviction for Robbery (Cal. Penal Code § 211) on or about March 10, 2010.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit the Glock model G23 .40 caliber firearm, serial number PGB797.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit seven rounds of Independence .40 caliber ammunition.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit four rounds of Winchester .40 caliber ammunition.
- Defendant A agreed that the Court may enter an order of forfeiture at or before sentencing for the forfeitable property.
- Defendant A agreed that the preliminary order of forfeiture shall become final as to him upon entry.
- Defendant A agreed to take steps necessary to pass clear title to the forfeitable property to the United States.
- Defendant A agreed not to contest any administrative or civil forfeiture proceedings for the forfeitable property and to withdraw any such claims or petitions.
- Defendant A agreed not to assist any other individual in falsely contesting the forfeiture of the forfeitable property.
- Defendant A agreed that forfeiture would not be counted toward satisfaction of any special assessment, fine, restitution, costs, or other penalty.
- Defendant A acknowledged that the statutory maximum imprisonment for a violation of 18 U.S.C. § 922(g) is 10 years.
- Defendant A acknowledged a maximum supervised release term of 3 years for the offense.
- Defendant A acknowledged a possible fine of up to $250,000 or twice the gross gain or loss, whichever is greater.
- Defendant A acknowledged a mandatory special assessment of $100.
- Defendant A agreed to pay the applicable special assessment at or before sentencing unless he demonstrated inability to pay.
- Law enforcement obtained a state search warrant for a residence in Los Angeles associated with Defendant A on June 29, 2020, based in part on recent social media posts.
- The social media posts depicted Defendant A holding firearms with text referencing that he was a 'one-man army.'
- The search warrant was executed on July 1, 2020.
- During the search, law enforcement recovered the Glock model G23 .40 caliber firearm and ammunition from the bedroom of Defendant A's girlfriend where Defendant A had been staying.
- The firearm recovered during the search bore serial number PGB797.
- The ammunition recovered during the search included seven rounds of Independence .40 caliber and four rounds of Winchester .40 caliber.
- Defendant A pleaded guilty and was convicted of being a felon in possession of a firearm in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(1).
- The Presentence Investigation Report (PSR) was filed on December 17, 2021.
- A sentencing hearing was scheduled for April 22, 2022 at 1:00 p.m.
- The government filed its sentencing position on or about March 15, 2022.
- The government stated that Defendant A committed the instant offense while on probation.
- The government described Defendant A’s criminal history as including multiple drug offenses, a firearm offense, and a robbery conviction.
- Defendant A agreed to appear for all court appearances, surrender as ordered for service of sentence, obey bond conditions, and other court orders.
- Defendant A agreed not to commit any crime while under the agreement and to be truthful with the United States Probation and Pretrial Services Office and the Court.

- Defendant pled guilty to two counts of Threats in Interstate Commerce under 18 U.S.C. § 875(c).
- Each § 875(c) count carries a statutory maximum of up to five years in prison.
- Each § 875(c) count carries up to three years of supervised release.
- Each § 875(c) count carries a fine of up to $250,000.
- Each § 875(c) count carries a $100 special assessment.
- The Court may impose consecutive sentences on the two counts, with a combined maximum of 10 years’ imprisonment.
- The plea agreement is a Rule 11(c)(1)(C) agreement.
- Count 1 covered a period from about December 31, 2020 to about February 24, 2021 and involved an interstate electronic communication threatening to injure Victim 1.
- For Count 1, the communication was transmitted for the purpose of issuing a threat or with knowledge it would be viewed as a threat.
- Count 4 covered a period from about February 15, 2021 to about April 11, 2021 and involved an interstate electronic communication threatening to kidnap Victim 2.
- For Count 4, the communication was transmitted for the purpose of issuing a threat or with knowledge it would be viewed as a threat.
- In early 2021, Defendant attempted to hire hitmen on the dark web to harm Victim 1 and to kidnap Victim 2.
- Defendant used the moniker Scar215 and the password Mufasa$$ on the dark web sites to conceal his identity.
- Defendant transmitted dozens of messages from Eastern Washington, Mexico, and possibly elsewhere to dark web sites operated outside the Eastern District of Washington.
- Approximately $60,000 in Bitcoin was paid to advance the two dark web schemes.
- For Victim 1, Defendant solicited a purported hitman to assault her in early January 2021 through February 2021.
- Defendant specified that Victim 1 should be given a significant beating that injures or breaks both hands.
- Defendant provided Victim 1’s address, a link to her employer, and her picture to the purported hitman.
- Defendant paid approximately $2,000 in Bitcoin for a hitman to assault Victim 1.
- Defendant sought confirmation and evidence that the assault on Victim 1 had occurred and indicated he had another, more complicated job for a different target.
- Beginning in late March 2021, Defendant solicited hitmen on at least two dark web sites to kidnap Victim 2, his estranged wife.
- At the time of the kidnapping solicitation, Defendant was subject to a no-contact order prohibiting direct or indirect contact with Victim 2.
- Defendant paid approximately $60,000 in Bitcoin for hitmen to kidnap Victim 2, extort her, inject her with heroin, and hold her hostage for seven days.
- Defendant structured a bonus scheme tied to specific goals regarding Victim 2 after her release, including withdrawing legal motions, returning to Defendant, keeping silent, self-injecting heroin, and planting drugs and used needles.
- Defendant suggested the hitmen could use any and all means necessary, including threatening Victim 2’s father, slaughtering her dog, and threatening to cause her older son to become addicted to heroin.
- Defendant provided Victim 2’s address, a link to her place of employment, and information about her work schedule to the purported hitmen.
- Defendant indicated to the hitmen that he had other jobs he wished to accomplish in the near future.
- Around the time of the kidnapping plan, Defendant solicited a hacker on the dark web to compromise Victim 2’s devices and accounts and to plant messages indicating she used drugs.
- Defendant paid approximately $3,000 in Bitcoin to a dark web hacking service advertising the ability to hack social media or computers and plant evidence.
- In an FBI interview, Defendant admitted using a moniker like "Scar2 something" and affirmed that his moniker was Scar215.
- Defendant admitted transmitting tens of thousands of dollars in Bitcoin to dark web hitmen sites but falsely told the FBI he was paying the hitmen as a way to commit suicide.
- Dark web messages contained unique identification or verification codes that Defendant had saved on his phone, written in a book in his biometric safe, and sent to an encrypted email account.
- The content of the dark web messages referenced details of Defendant’s divorce proceedings, Victim 2’s living situation, and court-mediated agreements, as well as Bitcoin payment addresses belonging to Defendant.
- The dark web messages indicated knowledge of Victim 2’s work schedule and a temporary visitation schedule for Defendant and Victim 2’s minor child.
- The username Scar215 and the associated password Mufasa$$ were consistent with Defendant’s affinity for lions.
- Defendant used a specific Bitcoin wallet recovered from his biometric safe to conduct nearly all of his dark web transactions.
- Blockchain records showed Defendant funded the wallet from his Coinbase.com account.
- Defendant made deposits to his Bitcoin wallet from ATMs in Spokane, where he was captured on camera making the deposits.
- Funds from the Bitcoin wallet were sent directly to purported hitmen.
- On the same day the FBI questioned him about dark web activity, Defendant attempted suicide, leaving a note apologizing for an "irreparable" mistake next to an FBI business card.
- Defendant survived the suicide attempt after ingesting 40 Xanax pills.
- While in custody, Defendant attempted to tamper with at least one witness by asking her to marry him to control whether she would testify, offering to pay tuition for her children, and directing her to burn evidence.
- At sentencing, the United States agreed to move to dismiss Counts 2 and 5 (cyberstalking), Count 3 (attempted kidnapping), Count 6 (attempted damage to a protected computer), and Count 7 (witness tampering).
- The parties agreed restitution is mandatory for Victim 1 and Victim 2, with amounts to be determined at or before sentencing.
- Defendant agreed to pay at least 10% of his net monthly income toward restitution if restitution is ordered.
- Defendant agreed to a no-contact provision prohibiting direct or indirect contact with Victim 1 and her immediate family, Victim 2 and her immediate family (with specific exception for court-authorized child contact), and Witness 1 and her immediate family.
- On August 10, 2022, Defendant entered his guilty plea and accepted responsibility for his crimes, according to his sentencing memorandum.
- Defendant reported being in custody at the Spokane County Jail since April 16, 2021.
- Defendant’s sentencing memorandum states he has no prior criminal history.
- Defendant began mental health treatment with a clinical psychologist in December 2019 following a referral by the Washington Physician’s Health Program.
- A clinician diagnosed Defendant with Adjustment Disorder with Depressed Mood and Anxiety, per the defense filing.
- Defendant’s defense-cited clinician reported his mental health worsened by late winter and early spring 2021 due to employment and interpersonal stressors.
- A defense-retained forensic psychologist opined Defendant had low risk factors for recidivism on certain actuarial measures and recommended resuming therapy.
- The government’s sentencing memorandum states Defendant told the FBI he hired a hitman to kill himself, which the government characterizes as a false statement.
- The government reports Defendant drafted a document on his phone to explain Bitcoin payments as part of a supposed suicide plan if questioned by police.
- The government reports Defendant ran a shredder application on his phone around April 11, 2021, deleting much of its content.
- In a handwritten letter while in custody, Defendant asked Witness 1 to marry him and wrote that marriage would allow them to decide if she would testify.
- In the same letter, Defendant acknowledged he was taking a risk sending it and that it could keep him in jail until trial.
- A no-contact order was obtained precluding Defendant from contacting Witness 1 or directing others to contact her.
- The government reports that a former cellmate and Defendant’s new fiancé later contacted Witness 1 despite the no-contact order.
- The government states Defendant was subject to a protective order at the time of the dark web messages based on his history of harassing Victim 2.
- The government reports Defendant placed tracking devices on Victim 2’s car multiple times in 2019 and 2020 and installed a child tracker app on her phone.
- According to the government, Defendant sent hundreds of messages to Victim 2 in December 2020, including threats to go to her home despite the no-contact order.
- The government states that in July 2020 Defendant discussed a "kidnapping scenario" involving Victim 2 with a 22-year-old nanny in the context of roleplay.
- The government reports Defendant threatened Victim 2 with a taser in 2021 and tried to control her behavior including food and workouts and by putting Xanax in her drinks.
- The government reports Defendant physically and sexually assaulted Witness 1, forced her into a hole, made her sign a master-slave contract in blood, and burned her with a cattle prod.
- The government asserts video evidence shows Defendant masturbating within arm’s length of a young child who appears to be asleep.
- In a recorded June 11, 2021 interview with a reporter, Defendant claimed he was being prosecuted due to his sexual orientation as a dominant in a BDSM relationship and discussed monetizing his case.
- In the same media call, Defendant denied writing the dark web emails.
- The government reports that on a November 4, 2022 call, after the plea, Defendant said the facts presented in court were not accurate and again discussed a potential book or movie deal.
- The defense memorandum references prior employment stressors, including a human resources investigation and termination in December 2020, as precipitating factors preceding the offense conduct.
- Defendant’s plea agreement requires disclosure of financial information and authorizes a credit report to facilitate restitution and fine collection until paid in full.
- Defendant agreed to notify the Court and the United States of material changes in economic circumstances while restitution remains unpaid.
- The government’s sentencing memorandum states Defendant used approximately $60,000 in Bitcoin in total to advance his schemes on the dark web.
- The defense sentencing memorandum references Defendant’s long medical career as a neonatologist in Spokane and leadership roles in local hospitals and volunteer organizations prior to the offense period.
- Defendant and the United States agreed the Court would consider an independent Presentence Investigation Report and that sentencing is in the Court’s discretion up to statutory maximums.
- Defendant’s defense memorandum incorporates objections previously filed to the draft Presentence Investigation Report.

- The case is in the Eastern District of Missouri, Eastern Division, under case number 4:21-cr-00272-MTS.
- A plea agreement was filed on August 25, 2021.
- Defendant A pleaded guilty to Count One pursuant to Rule 11(c)(1)(A).
- Count One charges a violation of 26 U.S.C. § 7206(1) (making and subscribing a false tax return).
- The government agreed that no further federal prosecution will be brought in the district for known violations arising out of the events set forth in the indictment.
- The elements of 26 U.S.C. § 7206(1) were listed in the plea agreement, including willfully making and subscribing a materially false tax return under penalty of perjury without believing it to be true and correct as to every material matter.
- Defendant A worked as a political campaign manager, consultant, and lobbyist.
- Defendant A was associated with a partnership in which Defendant A owned a 50% interest (Partnership A).
- Defendant A owned 100% of a corporation (Company A).
- During tax years 2012 through 2014, Defendant A had several sources of income and failed to report a substantial portion on individual income tax returns.
- In 2012, Defendant A received $111,500 from a political campaign in Missouri that was not reported as income.
- In 2013, Defendant A received $64,464 from a political campaign that was not reported as income.
- In 2014, Defendant A received $33,535 from a political campaign that was not reported as income.
- In 2013, Defendant A received $30,000 in lobbying fees from a company that was not reported as income.
- In 2012, Defendant A earned $122,580 from an independent media producer that was not reported as income.
- Defendant A deposited the identified funds into a personal bank account rather than business accounts.
- Defendant A did not disclose these funds to a tax return preparer and did not provide personal bank account information and statements to the preparer.
- As a result of the nondisclosure, the funds were not included on Defendant A’s individual Form 1040 tax returns for 2012, 2013, and 2014.
- Defendant A knew the funds were reportable income for tax purposes and knew that not providing this information would result in false tax returns being transmitted to the IRS.
- By failing to provide the earnings information to the tax preparer, Defendant A caused the filing of false personal Form 1040 tax returns and substantial under-reporting of income for 2012–2014.
- Defendant A failed to report a total of $443,633 in income for tax years 2012–2014.
- The resulting tax due from the underreporting for 2012–2014 was $151,843.
- On or about April 15, 2015, in the Eastern District of Missouri, Defendant A willfully made and subscribed the 2014 individual income tax return under penalty of perjury.
- Defendant A did not believe the 2014 individual income tax return to be true and correct as to every material matter when it was signed and filed.
- The offense of conviction carries a statutory maximum term of imprisonment of not more than 3 years.
- The offense of conviction carries a statutory maximum fine of not more than $100,000.
- The offense of conviction carries a maximum supervised release term of not more than 1 year.
- A mandatory special assessment of $100 per count applies and Defendant A agreed to pay it at sentencing.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit all interest in items seized during the investigation, including currency, weapons, property, and assets.
- Defendant A admitted that the seized items constituted proceeds of illegal activity, were commingled with illegal proceeds, or were used to facilitate illegal activity.
- The factual conduct described for tax years 2012–2014 may be considered as relevant conduct for sentencing purposes, as stated in the plea agreement.

- Defendant admitted involvement in a conspiracy to commit wire fraud in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1349.
- The charged conspiracy occurred between approximately 2014 and 2016.
- The conspiracy occurred in the Southern District of Florida and elsewhere.
- The scheme involved marketing medically unnecessary compound medications with high insurance reimbursement rates.
- Beneficiaries of Tricare/ChampsVA and other private commercial insurers were targeted by the scheme.
- Beneficiaries received unsolicited calls from telemarketers and were referred to telemedicine doctors who would sign off on pre-determined, medically unnecessary compound prescriptions.
- Prescriptions were selected for high reimbursement rather than individualized medical needs.
- The conspiracy used interstate wires, including emails, text messages, and electronic submission of claims for reimbursement.
- Complete Healthcare Concierge, Inc. (CHC) connected beneficiaries supplied by marketers with doctors willing to sign off on the prescriptions.
- Signed prescriptions were sent to specific pharmacies that in some instances paid a portion of the reimbursement to a CHC principal and marketers.
- Defendant was a knowing and willful participant in the CHC-centered conspiracy.
- Defendant worked at CHC and performed coordination with complicit doctors, pharmacies, and marketers.
- Defendant tracked doctor consults and payments to and from doctors and marketers, sometimes handled invoicing, and received complaints from doctors and marketers.
- Evidence cited to support Defendant’s participation included CHC corporate records, banking and transaction records, and text and email communications with co-conspirators.
- Defendant acknowledged awareness of the conspiracy’s unlawful purpose and that he knowingly joined it.
- The factual proffer states the information is sufficient to prove Count 2 beyond a reasonable doubt.
- The factual proffer was executed on November 20, 2023.
- Defendant’s sentencing was scheduled for January 31, 2024.
- CHC was formed as a Florida profit corporation on June 18, 2013.
- At formation, CHC was authorized to issue one share of stock.
- CHC’s stated corporate purpose was any and all lawful business.
- CHC listed CEO and COO titles among its initial officers at formation in 2013.
- CHC was administratively dissolved in mid-2014 and later reinstated.
- CHC was reinstated on October 6, 2014, according to the Florida records excerpted in the exhibits.
- The 2014 reinstatement record listed CEO and COO officer positions for CHC.
- CHC filed a 2015 annual report on April 10, 2015 listing a CEO officer position.
- CHC filed a 2016 annual report on April 29, 2016 listing a CEO officer position.
- The defense asserts a CHC bank account was opened in April 2014 and that only one individual was an authorized signer on the account(s).
- From December 2014 through February 2015, multiple individuals left USA Healthcare and began working for CHC.
- An email dated March 6, 2015 from a CHC-affiliated sender identified a different individual as Chief Operating Officer.
- The defense states the Defendant was displaced from any COO role when other personnel joined CHC in early 2015.
- The defense claims other CHC personnel made significantly more money than the Defendant during the same period.
- The defense asserts the Defendant had no decision-making authority or ability to hire or fire at CHC.
- The defense asserts the Defendant had no proprietary interest in CHC and was paid to perform tasks.
- The defense narrative claims a search warrant was executed at CHC in November 2015 and outside counsel was retained thereafter.

- Outside counsel planned to interview CHC’s key current employees and referenced that interviews could begin immediately.
