- Between June 2019 and May 2020, Defendant A executed a scheme to defraud his employer by selling company assets through shell companies.
- The scheme occurred in the Western District of North Carolina and elsewhere.
- Defendant A directed more than $4 million in fraudulent proceeds to bank accounts he controlled.
- Defendant A was employed by Victim Company A from March 2008 until May 26, 2020.
- During the scheme, Defendant A worked as a Fleet Operations Manager primarily out of Fort Mill, South Carolina.
- As Fleet Operations Manager, Defendant A was responsible for disposal of aged equipment (assets).
- Defendant A used an alias, Alias A, to help perpetrate the scheme.
- Victim Company A is a large equipment rental company headquartered in Fort Mill, South Carolina.
- Company B is an online auction house used by Victim Company A for more than a decade to sell used heavy construction equipment.
- Defendant A previously used Company B as one of several auction houses to dispose of Victim Company A’s aged equipment.
- Second Life Equipment LLC was created by Defendant A and used as a purported purchaser and reseller of used equipment in the scheme.
- Defendant A signed the Articles of Registration for Second Life and was listed as its General Manager.
- Second Life’s business address was an address associated with Defendant A.
- All Seasons Sales and Service was another shell company created by Defendant A and used in the fraudulent scheme.
- JB Acquisitions was a shelf company used in summer 2020 to launder fraud proceeds and to purchase assets.
- Between July 2019 and April 2020, approximately 398 of Victim Company A’s assets were auctioned through Company B after being misrepresented as owned by shell companies.
- Most assets sold through Company B were still owned by Victim Company A at the time of sale.
- Defendant A requested access for Second Life to Company B’s online auction sales portal.
- An Application for Credit for Second Life was submitted to Victim Company A.
- Alias A contacted Company B claiming to know Defendant A and to have equipment to sell through Company B.
- Alias A emailed Defendant A introducing himself as Second Life’s General Manager and seeking available machines.
- The Victim Company A employee referenced in Alias A’s email had never heard of Alias A.
- Alias A signed a contract with Company B authorizing Company B to sell used equipment on Second Life’s behalf.
- Company B contacted Defendant A about Alias A, and Defendant A falsely confirmed Alias A and Second Life were in good standing with Victim Company A.
- Alias A sent emails with asset lists to Company B to upload to Second Life’s customer portal account.
- Company B inspected the assets and sold them through eBay auction platforms.
- Sales proceeds were sent to an account controlled by Defendant A and another individual, who kept the proceeds.
- Defendant A manipulated serial numbers in Victim Company A’s system to make it appear assets had previously been sold.
- Defendant A manipulated records to make it appear certain assets had been disposed of or junked when they had not.
- Defendant A manipulated credit remittances spreadsheets by adding or dropping assets while keeping the dollar amount the same to avoid detection.
- Total losses to Victim Company A exceeded $4 million from the sale of approximately 398 assets.
- Fraud proceeds were transferred to a bank account in the name of Second Life controlled by Defendant A and another individual.
- The scheme was discovered during Victim Company A’s end-of-year review in May 2020.
- Victim Company A asked Defendant A to attend a meeting after discovery of the scheme.
- Defendant A declined the meeting and traveled to Florida.
- In Florida, Defendant A visited various company locations, used multiple employee identities, logged into the company system, and sought to review documentation.
- Victim Company A terminated Defendant A’s employment on May 26, 2020.
- On May 27, 2020, Defendant A withdrew or caused the withdrawal of all remaining funds from the Second Life bank account, reducing the balance to zero.
- On June 8, 2020, the phone number associated with Alias A was terminated.
- Between September 2019 and November 2020, Defendant A engaged in multiple financial transactions over $10,000 derived from the fraud proceeds and involving interstate commerce.
- On August 28, 2019, Defendant A purchased a condo in South Carolina for $170,000 using fraud proceeds.
- On September 24, 2019, Defendant A purchased a 2014 boat and 2019 trailer for $90,945 using fraud proceeds.
- On October 15, 2019, Defendant A purchased land in New York for $53,800 using fraud proceeds.
- On April 4, 2020, Defendant A purchased a 2020 Chevrolet Equinox for $30,919.50 using fraud proceeds.
- On September 15, 2020, Defendant A purchased a 2013 Chevrolet Silverado for $17,950 using fraud proceeds.
- On September 18, 2020, Defendant A purchased a 2020 Cadillac XT4 for $38,141 using fraud proceeds.
- On October 24, 2020, Defendant A purchased a 2020 Heartland Bighorn Traveler for $50,015 using fraud proceeds.
- On November 19, 2020, Defendant A purchased a 2019 Chevrolet Silverado for $44,617.46 using fraud proceeds.
- The reasonably foreseeable loss associated with the scheme was between $3,500,000 and $9,500,000.
- On May 10, 2021, Defendant A was charged by a two-count Bill of Information with wire fraud and money laundering.
- On May 20, 2021, Defendant A pled guilty to both counts pursuant to a Rule 11(c)(1)(B) plea agreement.
- Defendant A entered the guilty plea before a Magistrate Judge and was released on a $25,000 unsecured bond with Pretrial Services supervision.
- On June 30, 2021, the Probation Officer filed the Presentence Report, and neither party lodged objections.
- Defendant A is the sole defendant in this case.
- The wire fraud count carries a statutory maximum of 20 years, and the money laundering count carries a statutory maximum of 10 years.
- The statutes of conviction do not impose mandatory minimum sentences in this case.
- Defendant A’s restitution obligation connected to the offense conduct is $4.1 million, as stated in the sentencing memorandum.
- Defendant A acknowledged setting up a shell corporation to siphon money and property from his employer for himself.
- Defendant A reported that after a 2016 promotion with increased hours and responsibility, he experienced increased stress and began drinking heavily again.
- Defendant A was prescribed medication for panic attacks prior to committing the offense conduct, according to the memorandum.
- After receiving a negative review and reduced bonus in 2019, Defendant A decided to set up a shell corporation to siphon money from his employer.
- Defendant A reported meeting with federal agents and taking responsibility for his actions.
- Defendant A reported ceasing alcohol use and attending therapy following discovery of the offense conduct.
- Defendant A reported he lost his job and his house as consequences of his actions.
- Defendant A has no prior criminal history other than one driving-while-intoxicated offense at age 19, according to the memorandum.
- Defendant A stated he cooperated with Pretrial Services and abided by all conditions of release.
- Defendant A contributed volunteer time to a nonprofit organization creating courses for incarcerated learners.
- A nonprofit’s letter states that more than 100,000 people in jails and prisons will have access to a course Defendant A helped create.
- The nonprofit letter expresses appreciation for Defendant A’s commitment to continue contributing to the program, including while serving a sentence.
- A therapist letter states Defendant A has been in weekly therapy since September 2, 2020, for anxiety and alcohol abuse and is remorseful.
- The sentencing memorandum states Defendant A immediately pled guilty to all conduct charged.
- The sentencing memorandum states Defendant A accepts full responsibility and regrets committing the acts against his former employer.
- The sentencing memorandum references a letter from Defendant A to the Court incorporated into the Presentence Report.
- Company B sold the assets via eBay auction platforms rather than internal company channels.
- Defendant A’s scheme included creating a false narrative that the shell companies owned the assets being auctioned.
- Victim Company A had longstanding legitimate business with Company B for auctioning used equipment.
- Defendant A falsely told Company B that Victim Company A had sold equipment to Alias A’s company and that the entity was in good standing.
- The shell company Second Life maintained a bank account into which auction proceeds were deposited.
- Alias A used emails and contracts to facilitate uploading asset lists and conduct sales through Company B’s portal.
- Defendant A attempted to conceal the fraud by altering internal asset records to make it appear assets had been disposed of or previously sold.
- Defendant A adjusted credit remittance spreadsheets to match expected dollar totals despite swapped asset listings.
- Defendant A used a shelf company, JB Acquisitions, to launder proceeds and make purchases in 2020.
- Victim Company A asked Defendant A to meet after discovering irregularities during an end-of-year review.
- Following his termination, Defendant A rapidly emptied the Second Life bank account to a zero balance.
- The phone number linked to Alias A was terminated shortly after the scheme’s discovery.
- The sentencing memorandum asserts that probation or home confinement would still allow Defendant A to make restitution payments.
- The sentencing memorandum notes Defendant A worked with a nonprofit to develop a course titled for incarcerated learners and offered to continue supporting tasks like strategy and content management.
- Defendant A’s therapy letter attributes his relapse and misconduct in part to unresolved issues and stressors, noting ongoing remorse and commitment to sobriety.
- The sentencing memorandum indicates that the Presentence Report incorporated Defendant A’s personal letter to the Court.
- The sentencing memorandum states there are no policy statements identified by the defense that would bear on the sentencing decision in this case.
- The sentencing memorandum states the statutes allow probation of one to five years per count even if guidelines might suggest ineligibility.
- The sentencing memorandum states Defendant A intends never to repeat unlawful conduct.
- The sentencing memorandum attributes the origin of the offense to workplace stress, heavy drinking, a negative performance review, and rationalization that the money could be repaid if discovered.

- The case is pending in the Southern District of Florida under case number 1:21-cr-20306-CMA.
- The document titled Stipulated Factual Proffer was filed as part of the case.
- On or about May 1, 2020, a maritime patrol aircraft detected a go-fast vessel in the Caribbean Sea.
- The go-fast vessel was approximately 69 nautical miles south of Santo Domingo, Dominican Republic when detected.
- The go-fast vessel had two outboard engines.
- A tarp covered the forward portion of the go-fast vessel, an observation consistent with maritime drug smuggling.
- The go-fast vessel had no indicia of nationality.
- The USS Wichita, with an embarked USCG LEDET 402, was patrolling nearby and diverted to intercept the go-fast vessel.
- The USS Wichita launched its aircraft during the interdiction effort.
- The suspected smuggling vessel initially refused to heave to when approached by authorities.
- Authorization was requested and granted to employ Airborne Use of Force, including warning shots and disabling fire, to gain compliance.
- After warning shots were employed, the vessel came to a stop.
- Law enforcement completed a right-of-visit boarding of the go-fast vessel once it was dead in the water.
- There were three individuals on board the go-fast vessel.
- Two crewmembers were Colombian nationals and one crewmember was a Dominican national.
- None of the individuals on board claimed to be the master or person in charge of the vessel when asked by authorities.
- One crewmember made a verbal claim that the vessel was of Colombian nationality.
- The Government of Colombia could neither confirm nor deny the registry of the vessel when contacted by U.S. authorities.
- The vessel was treated as a vessel without nationality and therefore subject to the jurisdiction of the United States.
- The parties stipulated that the facts provided a sufficient basis for a finding that the vessel was without nationality and subject to U.S. jurisdiction under 46 U.S.C. § 70502(c).
- A full law enforcement boarding and search of the go-fast vessel yielded 12 bales of suspected cocaine.
- The at-sea weight of the suspected cocaine was approximately 289 kilograms.
- A Narcotics Identification Test Kit produced positive results indicating the substance was cocaine.
- An IONSCAN test on the vessel and bales yielded positive results for the presence of cocaine.
- Based on law enforcement training and experience, the quantity of cocaine was intended for distribution to others at the destination.
- The stipulated factual proffer was signed by the United States Attorney, an Assistant United States Attorney, defense counsel, and the defendant in late November 2021.
- The stipulated factual proffer was entered on the docket on November 24, 2021 as Document 56.
- A defense sentencing memorandum was filed on February 9, 2022 as Document 83.
- The defense memorandum states the defendant is a 37-year-old fisherman from the Dominican Republic.
- The defense memorandum states the defendant was born in La Barca, San Pedro De Macoris, Dominican Republic.
- According to the defense memorandum, the defendant was born out of wedlock.
- The defense memorandum reports the defendant’s father died in 1993.
- The defense memorandum reports the defendant’s mother is unemployed and unable to work due to diabetes.
- The defense memorandum states the defendant was raised under lower socio-economic conditions.
- The defense memorandum states a stepfather figure taught the defendant to work as a fisherman.
- The defense memorandum states the defendant began fishing at age eight to support his family.
- The defense memorandum states the defendant has two minor children.
- The defense memorandum states the defendant contributed approximately 500 Dominican pesos per month to his children before his arrest.
- The defense memorandum acknowledges the defendant was a crewmember on a boat in the Atlantic Ocean with approximately 289 kilograms of cocaine.
- The defense memorandum states the defendant’s role was transporting drugs from Colombia to the Dominican Republic.
- The defense memorandum states the defendant is extremely remorseful and acknowledges the seriousness of the case.

- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened at the U.S. Capitol to certify the Electoral College vote.
- The exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to the public on January 6, 2021.
- U.S. Capitol Police maintained security with permanent and temporary barriers and allowed access only to authorized people with identification.
- Around 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals in the crowd forced entry into the U.S. Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m. on January 6, 2021, members of Congress, including the Vice President, evacuated the chambers and proceedings were suspended.
- Congressional proceedings resumed at approximately 8:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021 after the building was secured.
- The January 6, 2021 riot resulted in more than $1.4 million in damage to the U.S. Capitol.
- Defendant was at the Lower West Terrace archway area of the U.S. Capitol at approximately 4:50 p.m. on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant threw an orange traffic barrier and two stick-like objects at officers protecting the Lower West Terrace tunnel.
- Based on their size, weight, and the force used, the objects thrown by Defendant were capable of inflicting serious bodily injury.
- At approximately 5:00 p.m., Defendant took possession of a police riot shield, struck it against the ground, and held it in front while confronting officers.
- When he threw objects at officers, Defendant knew the officers were engaged in the performance of their official duties.
- Witness tip on February 25, 2021 informed the FBI that Defendant had posted a picture of himself at the U.S. Capitol during the riots on a social media account.
- In publicly available photos and videos, an individual resembling Defendant was seen near the Lower West Terrace tunnel around 5:00 p.m., throwing objects at law enforcement.
- At approximately 5:02 p.m., the individual resembling Defendant was holding a police shield and hitting it against the ground.
- Probable cause was found for violations including 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1) and (b), 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3) and § 2, 18 U.S.C. § 641 and § 2, 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1), (2), and (4) with (b)(1)(A), and 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D) and (G).
- Defendant agreed to plead guilty to assaulting, resisting, or impeding certain officers using a dangerous weapon, in violation of 18 U.S.C. §§ 111(a) and (b).
- The offense of conviction carries a statutory maximum of 20 years of imprisonment.
- The offense of conviction carries a maximum fine of $250,000 and up to three years of supervised release.
- The special assessment for the felony conviction is $100.
- The government agreed to dismiss remaining counts of the indictment at sentencing in consideration of the guilty plea.
- Defendant agreed to pay $2,000 in restitution to the Architect of the Capitol.
- The total damage to the Capitol from the riot as of May 17, 2021 was approximately $1,495,326.55.
- Defendant agreed to allow law enforcement to interview him regarding the events around January 6, 2021 prior to sentencing.
- Defendant acknowledged and signed the Statement of Offense as true and accurate.
- Defendant posted shortly after the riot that he did not make it inside the Capitol.
- Defendant posted that he attacked police, stating he "got some good shots in" and described being pepper sprayed and gassed.
- Defendant described the confrontation at the Lower West Terrace as an "all out fight" and said he was on the front lines.
- Defendant posted that participants in the violence were not Antifa and wrote, "We did that. And it felt good."
- Defendant posted a statement advocating future violence: "Next time we come back with rifles. It's not a game."
- Defendant posted that in the face of "tyranny" violence may be the only answer.
- Defendant posted that a peaceful protest would not have swayed officials and reiterated that violence is the only option in the face of what he called tyranny.
- Defendant posted a graphic marking where he spent time on the Upper West Terrace and marked the Lower West Terrace tunnel as the site of a huge fight.
- Within the Lower West Terrace area, Defendant threw a white piece of wood toward officers around 4:53 p.m.
- Defendant helped throw a heavy black audio speaker toward officers guarding the tunnel.
- Body-worn camera recorded the speaker impacting officers' riot shields in the tunnel.
- Defendant threw two sticks in rapid succession at officers, which landed inside the tunnel.
- Defendant threw another stick that ricocheted off officers' riot shields.
- Defendant threw a large orange traffic bollard that ricocheted off a shield and then collided with multiple officers inside the archway.
- Defendant threw a pepper spray container toward officers.
- Defendant threw a bottle of liquid toward officers.
- Defendant threw another piece of wood toward officers in the tunnel area.
- Defendant threw another stick at police after the piece of wood.
- After throwing the last item, Defendant slammed a riot shield on the ground several times and thrust it toward police.
- Defendant recorded a video after the riot describing a "straight-up fight" in the tunnel, including chants of "heave ho" and a ladder used as a battering ram.
- Defendant was arrested on April 15, 2021 in South Carolina and detained by a magistrate judge.
- Law enforcement found an AR-15 rifle with a magazine, two shotguns, a tactical vest, a pistol case, ammunition, and brass knuckles in Defendant’s room at arrest.
- Law enforcement found a notebook with references to a Washington rally, emergency supplies, and firearms.
- Defendant’s phone contained a note stating: "If you are ok with fraudulently certifying an election to win, then I'm ok with attacking a government building to stop you."
- Defendant’s phone contained a note addressing the FBI: "if you shoot my dog, we will all die."
- Defendant’s phone contained a note criticizing the FBI for "intimidating and persecuting" those involved in January 6.
- Defendant’s phone contained images referencing extremist groups and symbols, including Proud Boys leaders, a Three Percenters logo, and Nazi iconography.
- With consent, a search of a Vermont trailer where Defendant previously lived found notebook pages with headings about Washington and a "Target list" including "Attack," "Breach," and "Assault."
- Outside the Vermont trailer, agents found a makeshift target and vehicle riddled with bullet holes.
- Defendant claimed on social media on the night of January 6, 2021 that he carried a firearm that day.
- In a social media video posted on January 8, 2021, Defendant stated, "I had a gun on me, too."
- The indictment charged Defendant on May 12, 2021 with 18 U.S.C. §§ 111(a)(1) and (b), among other offenses related to January 6, 2021.
- Defendant pleaded guilty on November 3, 2021 to 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1) and (b).
- Defendant agreed that he would be detained pending sentencing because the offense falls under 18 U.S.C. § 3142(f)(1)(A)-(C).
- Defendant consented to conduct plea and sentencing proceedings by video or teleconference under the CARES Act.
- Defendant stipulated that the officers he targeted were U.S. Capitol Police and Metropolitan Police Department officers protecting the Lower West Terrace entrance.
- Defendant’s admitted conduct occurred near the Lower West Terrace archway leading to the Capitol interior.
- Defendant’s social media activity the night of January 6 described the scene as violent and criticized calls to disperse.
- Defendant compared his actions to revolutionary violence and framed them as justified in posts the day after the riot.
- Defendant acknowledged that the Lower West Terrace tunnel was the site of a lengthy fight where people were injured or killed, according to his social media post.
- Defendant’s plea agreement required him to pay restitution immediately and to participate in the Inmate Financial Responsibility Program if incarcerated.
- Defendant agreed to provide a completed financial disclosure form to the U.S. Attorney’s Office’s Financial Litigation Unit before sentencing.
- Defendant’s social media described being on the Upper West Terrace and later descending to the Lower West Terrace to participate in the confrontations.
- According to a complaint affidavit, the FBI matched a driver's license photograph to images posted on social media by the same person at the Capitol.
- Defendant’s social media posts included a request for followers to send him photographs of him or his flag from the scene.
- Defendant wrote that his goal had been to get inside the Capitol, as indicated in his social media post stating he never made it inside.
- Defendant confirmed knowledge that the officers were engaged in official duties during the confrontation.
- Defendant’s guilty plea covers conduct involving dangerous objects used as weapons against officers.

- Defendant A operated a tax and accounting services business in the Western District of Virginia.
- Defendant A claimed to provide accounting services to small businesses, including filing federal taxes, in exchange for a monthly fee.
- Defendant A devised a scheme to defraud at least eight small businesses and their owners.
- Many of the victim business owners in the scheme spoke limited English.
- Defendant A held herself out as a tax and accounting services professional during the scheme.
- Defendant A advised victims that they owed a tax liability to the IRS.
- Defendant A falsely represented to victims that she had established IRS installment agreements for their tax liabilities.
- Defendant A instructed victims to make installment payments directly to her, claiming she would pay the IRS on their behalf.
- Victims made installment payments to Defendant A, typically by checks made out to her, which she deposited into her personal bank account.
- Defendant A did not file taxes on behalf of any victim.
- Defendant A did not make tax payments to the IRS on behalf of any victim.
- Defendant A did not have a Preparer Tax Identification Number (PTIN).
- Defendant A did not have an Electronic Filing Identification Number (EFIN).
- Defendant A was not registered as an Enrolled Agent with the IRS.
- Defendant A did not have power of attorney or any authorization to communicate with the IRS or file taxes for victims.
- Defendant A provided some victims with fictitious receipts for their installment payments.
- The fictitious receipts were created using the IRS symbol.
- The fraudulent documents purported to show the existence of an installment agreement and provided an electronic payment receipt.
- On November 13, 2017, Defendant A sent a fictitious receipt to Victim A via email over the internet.
- The subject line of the November 13, 2017 email to Victim A read "Receipt."
- The attached document was dated November 10, 2017.
- The document purported to be an electronic payment receipt for tax year 2017 ending December 31, 2017.
- The document showed a payment amount of $2,000.
- The document listed a remaining balance of "UNKNOWN/OTHER."
- The document stated "PAYEE TIN STATUS: INSTALLMENT AGREEMENT."
- Defendant A reviewed and agreed that the Agreed Statement of Facts was true and accurate and that the United States could prove those facts beyond a reasonable doubt.
- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Count 1 of an Information.
- Count 1 charges wire fraud in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1343.
- The maximum statutory penalty for the wire fraud count includes up to twenty years of imprisonment.
- The maximum statutory fine stated for the wire fraud count is $250,000.
- A term of supervised release of up to three years is identified for the wire fraud count.
- Defendant A waived the right to be charged by indictment and to have a grand jury vote on probable guilt.
- Defendant A acknowledged that restitution may be ordered and that assets may be subject to forfeiture.
- Defendant A agreed to pay restitution for the entire scope of her criminal conduct, including matters included as relevant conduct.
- The plea agreement was entered pursuant to Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 11(c)(1)(C).
- The plea agreement was signed by Defendant A on March 22, 2021.
- The plea agreement was executed by counsel and the prosecutor on March 23, 2021.
- The case was filed in the United States District Court for the Western District of Virginia, Roanoke Division.
- The case was docketed as Case No. 7:21-cr-00020.
- Defendant A pled guilty to one count of an information.
- A presentence report was filed on February 9, 2022.
- The presentence report recommended ordering restitution totaling $190,264.01.
- Defendant A provides care for Mother A, including transportation to and from medical appointments.
- Defendant A cares for Grandchild A while her child is working.
- Defendant A has served as a caregiver for other family members since childhood.
- At the time of the sentencing memorandum, Mother A was in an intensive care unit, and Defendant A served as her power of attorney.
- Defendant A planned for Mother A to live with her upon hospital discharge to facilitate caregiving.
- Defendant A's siblings struggle with alcoholism, and the family experienced a nephew's suicide in recent years.
- The presentence report indicated Defendant A suffers from situational anxiety.
- Defense stated that Defendant A does not have a situational anxiety diagnosis and does not have a significant psychiatric diagnosis.
- The sentencing memorandum identifies the case as Case No. 7:21-CR-00020 in the Western District of Virginia, Roanoke Division.

- A two-count federal indictment was returned on May 11, 2021 in the Eastern District of Tennessee.
- Count One charged false use of a Social Security number in violation of 42 U.S.C. § 408(a)(7)(B).
- Count Two charged aggravated identity theft in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1028A(a)(1).
- Defendant A agreed in writing to plead guilty to both counts of the indictment.
- Defendant A pleaded guilty to both counts on October 4, 2021.
- Sentencing was scheduled for March 21, 2022 at 1:30 p.m.
- A Presentence Investigation Report was prepared in the case.
- No objections to the Presentence Investigation Report were filed.
- For Count One, the statutory maximum imprisonment was five years.
- For Count One, the maximum fine was $250,000 and supervised release could be up to three years.
- For Count One, a mandatory special assessment of $100 applied.
- For Count Two, a mandatory term of imprisonment of two years had to run consecutive to any other sentence.
- For Count Two, the maximum fine was $250,000 and supervised release could be up to one year.
- For Count Two, a mandatory special assessment of $100 applied.
- Fraudulent use of a Social Security number under 42 U.S.C. § 408(a)(7)(B) is a Class D felony.
- There were no remaining counts in the indictment beyond Counts One and Two.
- Law enforcement in North Carolina alerted a Tennessee Highway Patrol investigator about Victim A’s identity theft.
- Victim A reported being plagued by identity theft issues for over twenty years.
- In March 2021, Victim A received notice from a pharmacy about a COVID-19 vaccination being administered in his name that he had not received.
- Victim A provided an address in Newport, Tennessee as a potential location used by the imposter.
- On August 20, 2017, Defendant A caused submission of an online application to renew a previously obtained Tennessee driver's license in Victim A’s name.
- The online application used Victim A’s name, date of birth, and Social Security number to impersonate him.
- The 2017 application resulted in issuance of a third Tennessee driver’s license in Victim A’s name with Defendant A’s photograph.
- Surveillance video from a Walgreens in Newport, Tennessee showed Defendant A using Victim A’s identifiers to obtain a COVID-19 vaccination.
- At all times relevant, Defendant A knew Victim A was a real person.
- On May 27, 2021, after receiving Miranda warnings, Defendant A admitted using Victim A’s name and identifiers.
- Defendant A stated he is pleading guilty because he is in fact guilty.
- Defendant A affirmed having read the indictment, discussed the charges and possible defenses with counsel, and understood the crimes charged.
- The plea agreement stated the court may order restitution as appropriate to victims of the charged offenses and related conduct.
- Defendant A agreed to pay the special assessment prior to sentencing.
- The United States agreed not to further prosecute Defendant A in the district for other non-tax offenses related to the indictment and known at the time of the plea.
- The government noted that restitution did not appear at issue, subject to the victim’s statement at sentencing.
- Defendant A enlisted in the United States Navy in 1976.
- Defendant A deserted from the Navy in 1977.
- Defendant A lived under Victim A’s appropriated identity for years in Newport, Tennessee.
- Since 2005, Defendant A fraudulently obtained at least three Tennessee driver’s licenses using Victim A’s identity.
- Victim A is a resident of North Carolina.
- The identity theft came to light after Defendant A used Victim A’s identifiers to obtain a COVID-19 vaccination at a Walgreens in Newport, Tennessee, triggering a notification to Victim A.
- A magistrate judge found at a detention hearing that Defendant A had been dishonest about his name, date of birth, and Social Security number with almost everyone, including a long-term partner.
- According to the PSR, Defendant A married his spouse in the 1980s and remains legally married, though separated.
- Defendant A has three children with Person B, who had been referred to as his girlfriend.
- Defendant A, his lawful spouse, and Person B lived together at the same residence.
- Person B is Defendant A’s daughter by marriage, making their relationship incestuous.
- Person B did not learn Defendant A’s true identity until the date of his arrest in this case.
- Defendant A’s use of Victim A’s identity has been ongoing for decades.
- The government asserted that Defendant A committed financial crimes using Victim A’s identity, though they were too remote in time to prosecute.

- On August 28, 2024, Defendant A pleaded guilty to Entering and Remaining in a Restricted Building or Grounds (18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1)) and Interfering with Law Enforcement During a Civil Disorder (18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3)).
- On March 24, 2021, Defendant A was arrested in the Middle District of Florida and released on bond the same day, and he has remained on bond since then.
- On May 29, 2024, a third superseding indictment charged Defendant A with multiple counts including 18 U.S.C. §§ 1512(c)(2) and 2, 18 U.S.C. § 1361, 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3), 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1), 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(2), 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D), and 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(G).
- According to the sentencing memorandum, the obstruction charge under 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2) was dismissed following the Supreme Court’s decision in Fischer.
- For the civil disorder count (18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3)), Defendant A’s statutory penalties included up to 5 years of imprisonment, up to 3 years of supervised release, up to a $250,000 fine, restitution, and a $100 special assessment.
- For the restricted building count (18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1)), Defendant A’s statutory penalties included up to 1 year of imprisonment, up to a $100,000 fine, up to 1 year of supervised release, restitution, and a $25 special assessment.
- The parties agreed that Defendant A would pay $2,000 in restitution.
- The Statement of Offense was signed by Defendant A on July 24, 2024, acknowledging its truth and accuracy.
- On January 6, 2021, the U.S. Capitol grounds and building were restricted due to the Vice President’s presence, with signs stating the area was closed.
- A joint session of Congress began around 1:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, to certify the Electoral College vote, with the Vice President presiding.
- By shortly after 1:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, the situation at the Capitol had become a civil disorder that obstructed the Secret Service’s protection of the Vice President.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m. on January 6, 2021, Members of Congress, including the Vice President, evacuated the chambers, and proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- The riot resulted in more than $2.9 million in damage to the Capitol requiring repairs.
- The civil disorder adversely affected commerce within the District of Columbia; for example, Safeway grocery stores closed at 4 p.m., resulting in lost sales.
- As of January 6, 2021, Defendant A was 41 years old and a resident of Florida.
- As of January 6, 2021, Defendant A was president of the “Trigger City” chapter of the Proud Boys in the Tampa, Florida area.
- On December 27, 2020, Defendant A was invited to join a hand-selected Proud Boys group referred to as the Ministry of Self Defense (MOSD).
- In early January 2021 MOSD messages discussed the potential for violence at the Capitol, including statements about storming the Capitol and police lines.
- On January 4, 2021, Defendant A communicated in the MOSD chat about carrying a medical emergency card and asked for help programming a BaoFeng radio for use on January 6.
- On January 5, 2021, a MOSD leader messaged a group about planning for the next day and indicated being at Defendant A’s place that evening.
- On the evening of January 5, 2021, Defendant A stayed at a rented home in Washington, D.C., with other Proud Boys leaders and understood Congress would certify the vote the next day and that the Vice President would be at the Capitol.
- Proud Boys messaging instructed members in Washington, D.C., to meet at the Washington Monument at 10 a.m. on January 6 and not to wear Proud Boys colors.
- On the morning of January 6, 2021, shortly after 10 a.m., Defendant A met a group of approximately 100 Proud Boys near the Washington Monument and did not wear Proud Boys colors.
- Around 11:20 a.m. on January 6, 2021, Defendant A was part of a group march near the west side of the Capitol during which leadership used a megaphone to address the crowd.
- At approximately 12:10 p.m. on January 6, 2021, the group stopped near food trucks at 2nd Street and Constitution Avenue NW, and at approximately 12:45 p.m. they marched back toward the Capitol.
- At approximately 12:50 p.m. on January 6, 2021, Defendant A arrived at Peace Circle, at the edge of the restricted portion of Capitol grounds, where signs stated the area was closed.
- At approximately 12:53 p.m., the crowd surged toward a police barricade, overwhelmed officers, and trampled barricades.
- At approximately 12:54 p.m., Defendant A ran up the Pennsylvania Walkway on the restricted grounds of the Capitol.
- Law enforcement formed a line behind a waist-high black metal fence and ordered the crowd to stop advancing and disperse.
- At approximately 12:55 p.m., rioters began shaking the black metal fence; Defendant A then rocked and shook the fence panel directly in front of him.
- By rocking the fence back and forth, Defendant A broke his section of fence loose from its posts, and the crowd tore the fence to the ground.
- After the fence fell, Defendant A advanced into the West Plaza of the Capitol, trampling another section of fence as he moved forward.
- Defendant A defied officers’ commands to disperse and was among the first wave of rioters into the West Plaza.
- While approaching a police line, Defendant A waved others forward and shouted, “Push forward, guys! Push forward!”
- While filming in the West Plaza, Defendant A said, “All I want to do is be violent and have Trump as my president.”
- From near the front of the crowd, Defendant A saw rioters assault law enforcement using chemical spray and hand-to-hand violence.
- At approximately 1:21 p.m., Defendant A moved away from the police line and began to regroup on the west lawn of the Capitol.
- At approximately 1:28 p.m., Defendant A appeared in a selfie-style video and said, “We have war boners!”
- Around 1:44 p.m., Defendant A and others formed a stack formation and moved toward stairs leading to the Upper West Terrace.
- In the area at the base of the concrete stairs, a rioter shoved officers and the crowd surged up the scaffolding; Defendant A initially climbed under scaffolding but retreated when less-than-lethal tactics were deployed.
- At approximately 2:10 p.m., Defendant A climbed a concrete wall to access stairs leading toward the Capitol building.
- By approximately 2:13 p.m., windows by the Senate Wing Door had been shattered and an alarm was sounding.
- Defendant A entered the Capitol through the Senate Wing Door at approximately 2:14 p.m., filming as he went inside.
- Upon entering, Defendant A said words to the effect of “Let’s go find [Legislator A]!” while a loud alarm sounded.
- Inside the building near the Senate Wing Door, Defendant A encouraged others to take drinks from a small canteen and took a bottle of water himself.
- Defendant A advanced further into the building, then exited through the Senate Carriage Door on the east side at approximately 2:17 p.m.
- At approximately 2:40 p.m., Defendant A entered the Capitol a second time through the Rotunda Door on the east side.
- Defendant A remained in the Rotunda until approximately 3:10 p.m., observing rioters berating police officers.
- Defendant A exited the Capitol at approximately 3:12 p.m. through the Rotunda door.
- After exiting, Defendant A posed for a photograph on Capitol grounds with associates, holding an American flag that had been stolen from a corridor near the Senate Gallery.
- Defendant A acknowledged in writing that he knowingly committed an act with the intended purpose of obstructing, impeding, and interfering with law enforcement by pulling down a black metal fence.
- Defendant A acknowledged that at the time of his conduct, law enforcement officers were engaged in the lawful performance of their official duties during a civil disorder.
- Defendant A acknowledged that the civil disorder obstructed, delayed, and adversely affected commerce and a federally protected function.
- Defendant A acknowledged that he entered and remained in the restricted building and grounds of the United States Capitol without lawful authority and that he did so knowingly.
- Proud Boys messaging before January 6 indicated that members were told police were the primary threat and to avoid wearing identifying colors.
- In a January 3, 2021 MOSD chat, participants discussed storming the Capitol and breaching police lines.
- On January 4, 2021, a message in the MOSD chat contemplated a scenario of one million people storming and taking the Capitol building and asserted authorities would do nothing.
- Defendant A wore a dark grey jacket and a black trucker-style hat with cream-colored mesh sides on January 6, 2021.
- Closed circuit camera footage captured Defendant A’s entrance into the Capitol near the Senate Wing Door at approximately 2:14 p.m.
- Defendant A repeatedly exclaimed “they’re arresting people” as he approached a line of officers inside the Capitol during his first entry.
- After his first entry, Defendant A exited the Capitol within minutes, then regrouped with associates on the east side of the building before reentering.
- While in the Rotunda during his second entry, Defendant A stayed with associates for nearly half an hour before exiting around 3:12 p.m.
- The Statement of Offense describes that the crowd’s unlawful entry caused proceedings to recess and required the building to be secured before resumption that evening.
- The attack adversely affected the United States Secret Service’s protection of the Vice President, and commerce in the District of Columbia was affected by early store closures.
- Defendant A is a father and husband and has been employed in aviation and as an operations manager for many years.
- Defendant A has a limited prior record including a 2021 boating-related offense reduced to reckless operation and a 2004 reckless driving offense; a 2009 domestic violence arrest was later dropped.
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant A did not wear Proud Boys colors as instructed in pre-event messaging.
- Pre-January 6 messages to participants emphasized that law enforcement was the primary threat and to avoid being caught by police.
- At the West Plaza, Defendant A filmed the crowd as some called police “traitors” and used derogatory language, while chanting “we want in.”
- Defendant A’s first entry into the Capitol occurred approximately one minute after the initial breach at the Senate Wing Door.
- During events at the base of the stairs to the Upper West Terrace, a small group of officers guarded the entrance to the stairs and were overwhelmed by the crowd’s push.
- In the aftermath on the Capitol grounds, Defendant A posed for a photo with associates while holding an American flag taken from inside the building.
- Defendant A understood before January 6 that Congress would be meeting to certify the electoral vote and that the Vice President would be present at the Capitol.
- Defendant A marched from the Washington Monument toward the Capitol with a group before the initial breach, chanting slogans and taunting officers during the approach.
- The Statement of Offense defines the attack at the U.S. Capitol on January 6, 2021, as a “civil disorder” under federal law.
- Defendant A admitted that he pulled down a black metal fence to interfere with law enforcement officers during the civil disorder.
- The parties agreed that the riot caused approximately $2,923,080.05 in damages as of July 2023 for restitution purposes.
- Pre-January 6 planning included group instructions that participants should not get drunk until off the streets.
- During the West Plaza phase, law enforcement used less-than-lethal tactics to repel members of the crowd.

- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened at the U.S. Capitol to certify the Electoral College vote count.
- Around 1:30 p.m. on January 6, 2021, the House and Senate adjourned to separate chambers to resolve an objection.
- The Vice President was present and presiding during the January 6, 2021 proceedings before evacuation.
- The U.S. Capitol was secured by U.S. Capitol Police with restricted access and barriers, and the exterior plaza was closed to the public on January 6, 2021.
- By around 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals forced entry into the Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting police.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m. on January 6, 2021, members of Congress and the Vice President were evacuated and the joint session was suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- On January 8, 2021, Witness A identified Defendant A as having entered the U.S. Capitol on January 6, 2021, and stated Defendant A called from Washington, D.C., reporting entry into the Capitol.
- Witness A reported Defendant A said police made a gap and allowed the crowd to enter the building.
- Witness A reported Defendant A said he walked around, looked at statues, entered a conference room, and sat to rest while inside the Capitol.
- Witness A reported Defendant A said he attempted to leave the Capitol by climbing out of a broken window to avoid the flow of people entering through doors.
- On February 6, 2021, Defendant A was interviewed by FBI agents with counsel present and admitted entering the U.S. Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant A provided the FBI the cellphone he had at the Capitol and a flash drive containing videos he recorded on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant A showed the FBI where he entered and exited the U.S. Capitol building.
- Defendant A brought to the interview the clothing he wore on January 6, 2021, including a gray hooded sweatshirt, light blue jeans, a black hat with a white American flag with a blue stripe, and a blue and red flag bearing 'TRUMP' and 'Keep America Great.'
- In a video he provided, Defendant A approached the Capitol’s East side entrance while shouting 'Stop the Steal!'
- In another video, Defendant A is seen entering the building through an open doorway and stating, 'We're in the Capitol building.'
- During his February 6, 2021 interview, Defendant A stated he was pushed into a door and into the Capitol building.
- U.S. Capitol surveillance showed Defendant A freely walking into the Capitol while holding his phone up to record, with no individuals immediately behind him pushing.
- Surveillance and Defendant A’s own video show him continuing into the Capitol and waving a lit cellphone rhythmically as others chanted 'Whose house? Our house!'
- Defendant A was present with a large crowd inside the Crypt of the U.S. Capitol where officers were attempting to maintain order and prevent crowds from crossing the center.
- A separate U.S. Capitol surveillance video also captured Defendant A’s presence in the Crypt.
- In another video, Defendant A was in a crowd that appeared to push past a law enforcement officer to move further into the Capitol.
- Defendant A recorded a hallway where some in the crowd shouted for 'Nancy' and one person said, 'We're gonna kill her,' as they approached a door marked 'OFFICE OF THE SPEAKER.'
- Other rioters opened the door to the Speaker’s area, someone shouted an expletive at the Speaker, and Defendant A entered the Speaker’s Conference Room.
- Inside the Speaker’s Conference Room, Defendant A asked, 'You guys want a tour?'
- Defendant A recorded the screen of an open laptop on the conference table with a username partially displayed as 'Spk.ConferenceRoom…' and asked, 'Who's good at hacking?'
- Defendant A sat at the head of the Speaker’s Conference Room table, put his feet on the table, and said, 'I think I like my new dining room. I pay for it.'
- An open-source video also captured Defendant A inside the Speaker’s Conference Room, wearing the same attire and sitting near the laptop he had recorded.
- In other videos, Defendant A stood on a Capitol terrace overlooking the East side and shouted to the crowds below, 'Watch out for the tear gas!'
- The affiant asserted probable cause that Defendant A violated 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2) by obstructing, influencing, or impeding an official proceeding.
- The affiant asserted probable cause that Defendant A violated 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1) and (2) involving entering or remaining in restricted grounds and disorderly or disruptive conduct with intent to impede or disrupt government business.
- The affiant asserted probable cause that Defendant A violated 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(C), (D), and (G) relating to entering or remaining in rooms designated for official use, disorderly conduct, and parading/demonstrating in Capitol Buildings.
- The Statement of Facts was attested to by a U.S. Magistrate Judge on May 7, 2021, pursuant to Fed. R. Crim. P. 4.1.
- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to Count Three of the Indictment charging Disorderly and Disruptive Conduct in a Restricted Building or Grounds in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(2).
- The offense of conviction, 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(2), carries a maximum sentence of one year of imprisonment.
- The offense of conviction carries a maximum fine of $100,000.
- The offense of conviction carries a term of supervised release of not more than one year.
- Defendant A agreed to pay a $25 special assessment for the Class A misdemeanor conviction.
- Defendant A agreed to allow law enforcement to review any social media accounts operated by him for statements and postings in and around January 6, 2021, prior to sentencing.
- The government agreed to request dismissal of the remaining counts of the Indictment at sentencing in consideration of the guilty plea to Count Three.
- Defendant A acknowledged that the charges to be dismissed at sentencing were based in fact.
- After the guilty plea, the government agreed not to further prosecute Defendant A for non-violent offenses in the District of Columbia known to it prior to the agreement, reserving the right to prosecute crimes of violence.

- On January 6, the U.S. Capitol grounds were restricted and closed to the public.
- On January 6, a joint session of Congress convened to certify the Electoral College vote beginning at approximately 1:00 p.m.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m., Congress evacuated and proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.; the Vice President remained in the Capitol until the session resumed.
- The defendant traveled from a rally on the Ellipse to the West Plaza of the Capitol grounds on January 6, 2021.
- At the West Plaza, the defendant cheered as the crowd overwhelmed a police line preventing further advance onto restricted grounds.
- The defendant moved to the front of the crowd near officers, was affected by a chemical agent, and lifted his shirt to wipe his face.
- Outside the Upper West Terrace doors, the defendant filmed and shouted phrases urging others forward, and exclaimed, "we took this thing."
- At 2:45 p.m., the defendant unlawfully entered the Capitol through the Upper West Terrace doorway knowing he lacked permission.
- At approximately 2:58 p.m., the defendant arrived in the Rotunda as officers began moving rioters toward the East Rotunda Doors.
- Around 3:05 p.m., the defendant pushed an unknown officer.
- Around 3:05 p.m., the defendant struck a Metropolitan Police Department officer.
- When an officer told the defendant to leave, he replied, "You leave. You leave. This is our house."
- At approximately 3:07 p.m., when an officer used a baton to push rioters toward the exit, the defendant struck the officer and lunged for the baton.
- At approximately 3:08 p.m., the defendant grabbed another officer’s baton and then returned it seconds later.
- The defendant shouted "lock arms" and linked arms with other rioters in defiance of police orders.
- Officers pushed the defendant out of the Rotunda, and he exited through the East Rotunda Doors at 3:22 p.m.
- About 45 minutes later, the defendant reached the front of the Lower West Terrace tunnel where rioters were attacking officers.
- At approximately 4:13 p.m., another rioter handed the defendant a wooden handrail with metal brackets, which he accepted.
- The defendant lifted the wooden handrail over his head and launched it into the tunnel toward officers.
- The handrail appeared to strike a shield/visor of an officer before officers seized it and passed it back beyond rioters’ reach.
- Shortly after throwing the handrail, the defendant participated with others in pushing into the tunnel against officers until about 4:20 p.m., when officers regained control.
- The defendant admitted he forcibly assaulted, resisted, opposed, impeded, intimidated, or interfered with persons designated under 18 U.S.C. § 1114 while they performed official duties.
- The defendant admitted that the wooden handrail he launched was capable of causing death or serious bodily injury and that he intended to cause bodily injury.
- The defendant admitted he knew the officers were engaged in official duties and that he assaulted them on account of those duties.
- Body-worn camera footage showed the defendant inside the Capitol at approximately 2:52 p.m., lifting his shirt and displaying a stomach tattoo.
- While confronting officers, the defendant yelled statements such as "we're coming in here, whether you like it or not" and "fight with us, not against us."
- The complaint describes the defendant striking an officer, retreating, and then approaching again despite commands to move back.
- The complaint reports that the defendant lunged for an officer’s baton during the Rotunda confrontation.
- A concerned citizen reported the defendant had said before January 6 that he intended to travel to Washington, D.C. to protest and later showed a video of himself inside the Capitol.
- Another tipster reported that the defendant assaulted Capitol Police Officers on January 6 and provided photographs.

- The government reported the defendant flew to Washington, D.C. on January 5, 2021 from California and brought bear mace with him.
- During an August 23, 2022 FBI interview, the defendant admitted he brought bear mace to the rally and left it with security before entering the rally grounds.
- The defendant told the FBI he went to the rally to support the "Stop the Steal" movement.
- The government described the defendant striking a female officer near the stairs immediately after entry, causing her to reposition her helmet and eyeglasses.
- The government described that near the Old Senate Chamber the defendant reached around another rioter to grab underneath an officer’s helmet.
- The government reported that after exiting the Rotunda the defendant remained on Capitol grounds and reached the Lower West Terrace tunnel by approximately 4:07 p.m.
- The government stated the defendant taunted officers near the tunnel, yelling "We're here all night" before launching the wooden handrail.
- The government stated the defendant held a pole at the East Rotunda doorway for approximately six minutes after exiting.
- In an April 14, 2022 radio interview, the defendant claimed he "tossed" a pole and did not know it was in the direction of officers and said there was no victim.

- In his FBI interview, the defendant admitted he knew he probably should not have entered the Capitol even though he followed the crowd.
- In his FBI interview, the defendant admitted he swung his arm toward a female officer "out of frustration."
- In his FBI interview, the defendant admitted he assaulted officers but claimed he felt assaulted too.
- In his FBI interview, the defendant denied being part of the push into the Lower West Terrace tunnel, contrary to other records described by the government.
- In his FBI interview, the defendant acknowledged that his prior public claim about receiving extensive head stitches was untrue and that his records show only a concussion.
- The defendant served in the U.S. Marine Corps from 2003 to 2007.
- A service record entry reported he sustained a level one concussion and left knee pain from an explosion and that a Purple Heart was not recommended.

- Defendant pled guilty to Count One charging conspiracy to commit securities fraud.
- Defendant entered the guilty plea on October 7, 2021.
- At sentencing, the government agreed to move to dismiss the remaining counts of the indictment against Defendant.
- Count One carries a statutory maximum of 5 years in prison.
- Count One carries a supervised release term of up to 3 years.
- Count One carries a maximum fine of $250,000 or twice the gross gain or loss, whichever is greater.
- A mandatory special assessment of $100 applies to the felony count.
- From 2017 through 2019, Defendant participated in a scheme in Minnesota and elsewhere to manipulate stock prices of dormant public shell companies.
- The scheme used the means and instrumentalities of interstate commerce, the mails, and the facilities of national securities exchanges.
- Defendant and co-conspirators identified dormant public shell companies that had stopped making required SEC and secretary of state filings.
- These dormant shell companies traded on the over-the-counter market for fractions of a penny per share.
- Defendant and co-conspirators bought large quantities of shares in the dormant shell companies at very low prices before the scheme’s public steps.
- They created fake resignation letters and board minutes to falsely show prior officers resigned and conspirators were appointed as new officers/directors.
- They used fraudulently obtained corporate documents to reinstate corporate registrations with state authorities.
- They used the fraudulent documents to gain control over the companies’ SEC EDGAR accounts and change EDGAR passwords.
- They made public filings through EDGAR on behalf of the hijacked companies.
- They issued fraudulent press releases and public filings designed to inflate the stock prices of the hijacked companies.
- They promoted the stock online via Twitter and investor message boards to increase interest and price.
- They sold their shares at artificially inflated prices after the promotions.
- The mass selling after the pump caused stock prices to crash, leaving investors with nearly worthless shares.
- Companies targeted in the scheme included Bell Buckle Holdings Inc. (OTC: BLLB), Encompass Holdings Inc. (OTC: ECMH), and Utilicraft Inc. (OTC: UITA).
- Defendant helped identify dormant public shell companies for hijacking.
- Defendant worked to promote the hijacked companies’ stock through false and fraudulent online statements.
- Defendant purchased shares at low prices and later sold at inflated prices.
- The government asserts Defendant made more than $50,000 from the scheme.
- Defendant consented to a forfeiture money judgment of $53,487 as proceeds of the scheme.
- The parties agreed that the Mandatory Victim Restitution Act would not apply because determining losses would complicate or prolong sentencing.
- Defendant agreed to forfeit any property constituting or derived from proceeds of the securities fraud scheme.
- Defendant agreed to forfeit any property used or intended to be used to commit, facilitate, or promote the offense.
- Defendant agreed to disclose assets, complete a financial statement, and permit a credit report for purposes of financial obligations.
- Defendant waived any challenge to venue in the District of Minnesota.
- On June 16, 2021, Defendant was charged with conspiracy to commit securities fraud, securities fraud and aiding and abetting, and wire fraud.
- Defendant has remained in federal custody since his initial arrest in this case.
- The scheme included hijacking Bell Buckle Holdings Inc. and other companies, as admitted by Defendant.
- Defendant was an active trader and promoter of OTC stocks and used social media platforms to promote penny stocks.
- During November 2017, Defendant purchased 29 million shares of Encompass Holdings Inc. at $0.0004 per share for $17,865.
- During November 2017, a co-conspirator purchased 12 million shares of Encompass at $0.0002 per share for $3,100.
- Defendant posted numerous messages about Encompass on an investor message board intending to increase the stock price.
- On November 3, 2017, a co-conspirator issued a press release falsely announcing his appointment as President and CEO of Encompass and a new business focus.
- A subsequent press release falsely stated the acquisition of $6,400,000 of another company's assets and gross revenue.
- On November 13, 2017, Defendant and a co-conspirator sold Encompass shares and profited.
- In February 2018, the conspirators assumed control over Bell Buckle Holdings Inc. using false board minutes that claimed the CEO resigned and appointed another conspirator.
- For Bell Buckle, a co-conspirator purchased 8 million shares for $2,399; Defendant purchased 30 million shares for $29,310; another co-conspirator purchased 20.5 million shares for $43,262.
- Documents announcing the Bell Buckle leadership changes were filed via EDGAR.
- Bell Buckle’s stock was promoted on investorhub.com by an individual involved in the scheme.
- Between February and March 2018, the conspirators sold Bell Buckle shares and profited.
- Defense states the conduct created a loss between $150,000 and $250,000.
- Defense claims Defendant actually lost more than $50,000 due to a co-conspirator’s influence.
- Defendant and co-conspirators changed SEC EDGAR account access to enable fraudulent public filings.
- The scheme involved false announcements such as potential mergers or new business opportunities to move stock prices.
- Defendant agreed to pay the $100 special assessment prior to sentencing.
- Defendant waived pretrial motions in exchange for the concessions in the plea agreement.
- Defendant and co-conspirators used fraudulent secretary of state documents listing conspirators as corporate officers and directors.
- Defendant’s offense conduct included making untrue statements of material fact and omitting material facts in connection with securities transactions.
- The charged conduct implicated SEC Rule 10b-5 (17 C.F.R. § 240.10b-5).
- Defendant’s change-of-plea and sentencing posture contemplated dismissal of remaining counts upon sentencing.

- Defense reports Defendant contracted COVID-19 while detained and experienced respiratory issues.
- Defense states Defendant’s conduct occurred while he was on probation for a misdemeanor offense.

- Defendant A lives in Kentucky and drove to the Washington, D.C. area on January 5, 2021 with three other people.
- Defendant A and companions knew Congress would be meeting on January 6, 2021 to certify the election results.
- On the morning of January 6, Defendant A attended a speech near the White House and then walked toward the U.S. Capitol.
- Defendant A became separated from the three companions while walking toward the U.S. Capitol on January 6.
- Defendant A entered Capitol grounds and stood near a line of law enforcement officers outside the U.S. Capitol Building.
- While on Capitol grounds, Defendant A was sprayed by law enforcement with chemical spray used for riot control.
- Defendant A approached the U.S. Capitol Building carrying a pointed wooden stick.
- From outside the building, Defendant A used the pointed wooden stick to shatter the left pane of an exterior Capitol window and further broke the glass with his fist.
- The right pane of the same exterior Capitol window had already been broken before Defendant A struck the left pane.
- While smashing the window, Defendant A shouted, "This is our house! Let us in! Our house!"
- Defendant A unlawfully entered the U.S. Capitol Building at approximately 2:42 p.m. on January 6, 2021.
- Inside the Capitol, Defendant A walked to the area known as the Speaker's Lobby.
- Defendant A was present near the Speaker's Lobby while others shouted and banged on the door.
- Defendant A was present when an individual attempted to climb through the Speaker’s Lobby door and was shot by law enforcement.
- Defendant A remained in the area until Metropolitan Police Department officers arrived and forced the crowd, including him, out of the building.
- Defendant A left the Capitol Building after being cleared from the Speaker’s Lobby area by responding officers at approximately 2:56 p.m.

- On January 6, 2021, the U.S. Capitol exterior plaza was closed to the public.
- Only authorized individuals with identification were allowed inside the U.S. Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- A joint session of Congress convened at approximately 1:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021 to certify the Electoral College vote, with the Vice President present and presiding.
- By approximately 1:30 p.m. on January 6, 2021, the House and Senate adjourned to separate chambers.
- At approximately 2:00 p.m., individuals in the crowd forced their way past barricades and officers toward the U.S. Capitol building.
- Shortly after 2:00 p.m., individuals in the crowd forced entry into the U.S. Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement officers.
- The riot on January 6, 2021 resulted in substantial damage to the U.S. Capitol requiring expenditures of more than $1.4 million for repairs, as reflected in the Statement of Offense.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m., members of the House and Senate were instructed to evacuate, and Congressional proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- The Vice President remained in the Capitol from evacuation until the session resumed after the building was secured.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m. on January 6, 2021, Defendant A and his son (Person A) climbed through a broken window near the Senate Wing Door on the west side to enter the Capitol.
- Defendant A and Person A remained inside the Capitol for approximately 26 minutes and stayed together the entire time.
- While inside, Defendant A walked near the Memorial Door, climbed to the second floor, and entered the Rotunda.
- At approximately 2:37 p.m., Defendant A approached the East Rotunda doors where officers were positioned to prevent entry from outside.
- Along with others, Defendant A rushed the line of officers at the East Rotunda doors and helped push open the doors, allowing rioters outside to enter.
- Defendant A was at or near the front of the interior group when the East Rotunda doors were opened.
- Defendant A and Person A then climbed to the third floor and exited on the first floor at approximately 2:46 p.m.
- On January 7, 2021, Defendant A posted on Facebook referring to a 'beginning of the revolution' and 'hangings on the front lawn of the capitol.'
- On January 13, 2021, Defendant A posted that events would 'get very ugly and probably result in some version of a civil war.'
- On February 7, 2021, Defendant A posted, 'Bring it on Biden! I have no problem dying in a pool of empty shell casings.'
- Defendant A knew he did not have permission to enter the Capitol when he did so.
- Defendant A obstructed, impeded, or interfered with law enforcement officers performing official duties during a civil disorder affecting commerce and a federally protected function.
- Defendant A signed the Statement of Offense on December 15, 2021, acknowledging that it is true and accurate and that he signed voluntarily.
- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3) (Civil Disorder).
- A violation of 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3) carries a maximum sentence of five years’ imprisonment.
- The statutory maximum fine for 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3) is $250,000 or twice the pecuniary gain or loss.
- A term of supervised release of up to three years may be imposed for 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3).
- Defendant A must pay a $100 special assessment for the felony conviction.
- The government agreed to dismiss the remaining counts of the information at sentencing.
- The government agreed not to further prosecute Defendant A for conduct set forth in the Statement of Offense after the guilty plea.
- The government agreed not to charge non-violent offenses committed within the District of Columbia before the agreement and disclosed by Defendant A before execution of the agreement.
- Defendant A agreed to allow law enforcement to interview him about the events in and around January 6, 2021 prior to sentencing.
- Defendant A agreed to pay $2,000 in restitution to the Architect of the Capitol.
- As of May 17, 2021, the plea agreement states that the Capitol riot caused approximately $1,495,326.55 in damage.
- Defendant A agreed to complete and submit a financial disclosure form to the U.S. Attorney’s Office Financial Litigation Unit 30 days prior to sentencing.
- Defendant A signed the plea agreement on December 15, 2021.
- As of April 5, 2022, the government reported approximate losses from the Capitol attack of $2,734,783.15.
- Defendant A traveled with his adult son from Iowa to Washington, D.C. on January 5, 2021.
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant A and his son attended the 'Stop the Steal' rally and then walked to the U.S. Capitol.
- Defendant A entered the Capitol about eight minutes after the initial breach, through a smashed-out window near the Senate Wing Door at approximately 2:20 p.m.
- Between approximately 2:28 p.m. and 2:32 p.m., Defendant A was near the Memorial Door and poured water onto another rioter’s eyes.
- At approximately 2:33 p.m., Defendant A and his son made their way into the Rotunda.
- At approximately 2:37 p.m., Defendant A and his son encountered three officers guarding the East Rotunda doors, which had broken windows, and could see rioters outside attempting entry.
- For roughly thirty seconds, Defendant A and his son watched the officers; the son appeared to chant or yell toward the officers.
- Defendant A and his son pushed against the line of officers at the East Rotunda doors from 2:38:22 to 2:38:49 p.m.
- When the East Rotunda doors opened, Defendant A and his son were at the front of the group on the interior.
- As a result of this breach, additional rioters and trespassers flooded into the Capitol.
- A major altercation between rioters and law enforcement occurred near the breached East Rotunda doors about 25 minutes later.
- While on the third floor, Defendant A’s son attempted to open a door to a private room but found it locked.
- The United States obtained a search warrant for Defendant A’s Facebook account in the investigation.
- On December 20, 2021, a superseding information charged Defendant A with Civil Disorder and additional offenses including 18 U.S.C. §§ 1752(a)(1), 1752(a)(2), and 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D), (G).

- Defendant A was compliant with conditions of pre-trial release.
- Defense counsel asserts that Defendant A did not break property, take anything, or injure any person during the events.

- Defense memo states there is no allegation that Defendant A engaged in an altercation with a law enforcement officer.
- Defense memo reports that the probation officer described Defendant A’s actions as those of 'an average participant.'
- Defense memo states Defendant A operates multiple small businesses including laundromats, a tanning salon, and a manual car wash through several corporations.
- Defense memo states many of Defendant A’s businesses are located in Minnesota, particularly in Minneapolis/St. Paul.
- Defense memo states Defendant A has served on the board of a national industry association and its charitable foundation and became association chairman in 2021.
- Defense memo describes Defendant A’s involvement in organizing 'Free Laundry Day' community events and literacy initiatives in laundromats.
- Defense memo states Defendant A has volunteered as a marriage counselor and officiated at weddings.
- Defense memo states Defendant A supports veterans’ activities through local organizations and places wreaths and flags at headstones during holidays.
- Defense memo states Defendant A has Crohn’s disease diagnosed in 1995 and receives ongoing medication.
- Defense memo states Defendant A stepped back from foundation activities after the offense due to concern about adverse impact on the organization.
- Defense memo states two of Defendant A’s laundries in Minneapolis were severely damaged during unrest in 2020–2021.
- Defense memo states Defendant A traveled to Washington to attend a rally and did not intend to enter the Capitol.
- Defendant A provided a written statement expressing remorse for entering the Capitol and accepting responsibility.
