- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened at the U.S. Capitol to certify the Electoral College vote for the 2020 Presidential Election.
- Vice President was present and presiding during the joint session on January 6, 2021.
- On January 6, 2021, the exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to members of the public and only authorized persons were allowed inside.
- At approximately 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals in the crowd forced entry into restricted areas at the U.S. Capitol, breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m. on January 6, 2021, members of the House and Senate were evacuated and the proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- The January 6 riot caused substantial damage to the U.S. Capitol requiring expenditures of more than $1.4 million for repairs as of early 2021.
- As of May 17, 2021, the damage to the U.S. Capitol from January 6 was approximately $1,495,326.55.
- As of July 2023, damages associated with the Capitol breach were approximately $2,923,080.05 according to figures cited in the presentence report.
- Defendant agreed and stipulated to a Statement of Offense describing the factual basis for the guilty plea.
- Defendant signed the Statement of Offense on June 29, 2021, acknowledging it as true and accurate.
- Defendant pleaded guilty to two counts: Conspiracy (18 U.S.C. § 371) and Obstruction of an Official Proceeding (18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2)).
- The conspiracy count (18 U.S.C. § 371) carries a statutory maximum of 5 years of imprisonment and up to 3 years of supervised release.
- The obstruction count (18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2)) carries a statutory maximum of 20 years of imprisonment and up to 3 years of supervised release.
- Each felony count carries a $100 mandatory special assessment, totaling $200 for two counts.
- Defendant agreed to pay $2,000 in restitution to the Department of the Treasury.
- In advance of January 6, 2021, Defendant coordinated with individuals affiliated with the Oath Keepers to make plans for events in Washington, D.C.
- Defendant and at least some co-conspirators discussed bringing firearms to the Washington, D.C. area for January 6.
- On January 4, 2021, Defendant drove from Alabama to Atlanta, Georgia.
- On January 5, 2021, Defendant traveled with at least one co-conspirator from Atlanta to the Washington, D.C. metropolitan area.
- Defendant brought a shotgun and a semi-automatic handgun, plus ammunition, to the D.C. area and provided them to a co-conspirator to store in a Virginia hotel.
- On the morning of January 6, 2021, Defendant met with co-conspirators at an event near the White House.
- Around 2:30 p.m. on January 6, Defendant and others boarded golf carts and drove around barricades of marked law enforcement vehicles toward the U.S. Capitol.
- Defendant unlawfully entered the restricted grounds of the U.S. Capitol wearing a backpack, tactical gloves, boots, an Oath Keepers hat, and carrying a large stick.
- Defendant joined with co-conspirators in walking up the stairs on the east side of the U.S. Capitol.
- At around 3:21 p.m. on January 6, Defendant unlawfully entered the U.S. Capitol building carrying a large stick.
- At the time of entry, Defendant believed he and co-conspirators were trying to obstruct, influence, and impede Congress’s certification of the Electoral College vote.
- Defendant acted to stop or delay the Congressional proceeding by intimidating and coercing personnel participating in or supporting the proceeding.
- At around 3:25 p.m., Defendant exited the U.S. Capitol as law enforcement began deploying chemical irritants in his direction.
- On January 8, 2021, a co-conspirator told Defendant to delete messages about January 6, and Defendant deleted those messages.
- Defendant was a member of the Alabama chapter of the Oath Keepers and was described in messages as the second-in-command for the state.
- Defendant was added to an encrypted Signal group chat titled "DC OP: Jan 6 21" used by leadership from multiple states to plan operations.
- Participants in the planning chats coordinated lodging, transportation, and discussed what weapons and gear to bring for January 6.
- Planners arranged for an armed quick reaction force to guard a cache of weapons outside the District of Columbia to ferry weapons into the city if called.
- A group leader’s December 23, 2020 open letter warned of taking up arms if Congress’s certification was not stopped and was forwarded to Defendant before a planning call.
- On January 6, 2021, a group member livestreamed while the group drove golf carts around police barricades, stating they were headed to the Capitol because patriots had stormed it.
- As the group crossed into the restricted area, they moved hands-on-shoulder through the crowd from the west to the east side of the Capitol.
- At about 3:15 p.m. on January 6, three members of the group breached the Capitol through the East Rotunda doors, and Defendant followed a few minutes later.
- Defendant did not proceed further than the foyer just inside the East Rotunda Doors before leaving as riot police began clearing the building.
- After leaving the Capitol, Defendant and others met up with other Oath Keepers members and affiliates just outside the Capitol.
- Defendant told law enforcement he joined the Oath Keepers several years earlier seeking like-minded people who serve and to participate in disaster relief efforts.
- In late 2020, planning communications emphasized opposing the transfer of power, including by force if necessary.
- On an encrypted chat titled "Jan 5/6 DC Op Intel team," a participant posted a link to a video titled "live stream of patriots storming capital," and others discussed patriots taking action.
- On January 6, 2021, at approximately 1:25–1:38 p.m., messages by the group leader indicated that leaders were not going to stop the certification and that patriots were taking matters into their own hands.
- A co-conspirator messaged in the planning chat that they were coming to the Capitol with an ETA of 30 minutes, and Defendant then headed toward the Capitol.
- On January 8, 2021, a co-conspirator told Defendant via Signal to ensure that all Signal communications about the operation were deleted and burned, and Defendant confirmed deletion.
- Defendant contacted the FBI through counsel in March 2021 to arrange an interview admitting responsibility for January 6 conduct.
- Defendant debriefed extensively with the government during the investigation.
- Defendant consented to law enforcement to search his phone, though much relevant evidence had been deleted days after the attack.
- Defendant testified before the grand jury and was prepared to testify at trial, though his trial testimony was ultimately not needed.
- Defendant pled guilty early and publicly pursuant to a cooperation plea agreement, becoming one of the first in the conspiracy to do so.
- Defendant waived indictment and proceeded by information when entering his plea of guilty.
- The plea agreement includes a cooperation section requiring Defendant to answer questions, provide statements, and testify as deemed relevant by the government.
- The plea agreement requires Defendant to testify fully before any grand juries and at trials or proceedings where his testimony is deemed relevant by the government.
- Defendant agreed to disclose assets and complete a financial disclosure form to facilitate restitution and financial obligations.
- Defendant acknowledged that he would pay $2,000 restitution and the special assessment promptly and authorized a credit report for evaluation of ability to pay.
- At sentencing time, the parties prepared a supplemental statement of offense to address a Supreme Court decision clarifying the obstruction statute and intended to have Defendant adopt it under oath.
- Defendant’s personal background includes approximately two years of service in the United States Army as a Combat Engineer and an honorable discharge.
- Defendant had no criminal history at the time of sentencing.
- Employment records referenced in the presentence report show Defendant worked as a service technician from September 2015 to April 2020 and later as an appliance delivery coordinator since March 2021.
- A period of Defendant’s unemployment coincided with the pandemic and issues with his back and knees.
- On January 8, 2021, when told via Signal how to delete messages, Defendant asked whether leaving the group would delete them and was instructed to manually delete messages.
- Defendant was present in encrypted planning communications where a group leader described the situation on January 6 as a call to action after leaders did not intercede.
- Defendant’s group used golf carts to approach the Capitol and parked just outside the entrance to the grounds before proceeding on foot.
- Defendant wore tactical gear and carried a large stick when entering the Capitol, while other group members wore additional tactical equipment like protective vests and goggles.
- Defendant’s early cooperation included self-identifying his role before the government knew the full extent of his conduct or his appearance on January 6.
- Defendant agreed in his plea to cooperate fully with law enforcement and to abide by release conditions imposed by the Court.
- Defendant met co-conspirators on the east side of the Capitol and entered after some had already breached through the East Rotunda doors.
- Defendant later disassociated from the Oath Keepers organization, according to defense representations.
- Defense materials state that Defendant carried first aid supplies on January 6 rather than shields or bear spray.
- Defense materials claim Defendant refused to bring firearms into the District of Columbia, despite storing firearms in Virginia.
- Defense materials indicate Defendant abandoned the firearms he brought to Virginia with a co-defendant in Alabama after January 6.
- Defense materials assert that Defendant abided by all pretrial release conditions and remained available to the government during the case.
- Defense materials report that after the public cooperation plea, vehicles including media appeared around Defendant’s residence and online posts by others targeted him.
- Defense materials and the government sentencing memorandum concur that Defendant entered the Capitol at approximately 3:21 p.m. and exited a few minutes later as police began clearing the building.
- The two felony counts of conviction each carry a potential fine of up to $250,000.
- The plea agreement states the government will not further prosecute Defendant for conduct set forth in the Statement of Offense after the guilty plea to the identified counts.
- The plea agreement includes a non-prosecution provision for non-violent offenses in D.C. disclosed by Defendant prior to execution of the agreement, with an explicit reservation for crimes of violence.
- Defendant agreed to waive any challenge to venue in the District of Columbia.
- Defendant agreed to a waiver of certain trial rights, including the right to a jury trial and the right to appeal the conviction within the limits stated in the agreement.
- Defendant’s plea agreement acknowledges the possibility of delayed sentencing until cooperation is completed so the Court has the benefit of all relevant information.

- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened at the U.S. Capitol to certify the 2020 Electoral College vote count.
- The Vice President was present and presiding during the January 6, 2021 proceedings and remained in the Capitol until the session resumed that evening.
- On January 6, 2021, the exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to members of the public, with security barriers and U.S. Capitol Police restrictions in place.
- Around 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals in the crowd forced their way past barricades toward the Capitol building.
- Shortly after 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals forced entry into the Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m., members of the House and Senate were instructed to evacuate, and congressional proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- The January 6, 2021 riot resulted in more than $1.4 million in damage to the U.S. Capitol according to the Statement of Offense.
- As of May 17, 2021, the Architect of the Capitol estimated approximately $1,495,326.55 in damages from the January 6 riot.
- As of April 5, 2022, the approximate losses from the January 6, 2021 attack were $2,734,783.15.
- Defendant A traveled from California to Washington, D.C., to be present at the Capitol area on January 6, 2021.
- At approximately 2:24 p.m. on January 6, Defendant A stood near the East Columbus doors of the U.S. Capitol.
- Defendant A wore a dark jacket, beanie, goggles, and gloves while near the East Columbus doors.
- Defendant A used a cellphone with a distinctive case reading "I [heart] my proud boy" to record events near the Capitol on January 6.
- Defendant A held a small green canister in his hand while near the East Columbus doors.
- At approximately 2:34 p.m., Defendant A sprayed U.S. Capitol Police officers with a chemical irritant.
- After spraying the officers, Defendant A threw the green canister at the officers.
- Defendant A knew the officers he sprayed and threw the canister at were U.S. Capitol Police officers engaged in official duties.
- Multiple U.S. Capitol Police officers were guarding the East Columbus doors at the time Defendant A sprayed a chemical irritant.
- The assaulted officers at the East Columbus doors did not report any lasting injuries from the spray.
- Around the East Columbus doors, Defendant A celebrated as the doors were forced open and pulled his goggles down over his eyes.
- Defendant A was recorded holding both his phone and the green chemical canister shortly before spraying the officers.
- Defendant A sprayed officers during a sequence in which rioters attempted to seize police shields and shouted to "pepper spray" officers and to "break those windows."
- At approximately 3:02 p.m., Defendant A entered the U.S. Capitol through the East Columbus doors and walked toward the Rotunda.
- Defendant A remained in the Rotunda area for a period and exited the Capitol at approximately 3:20 p.m.
- Defendant A later posted on social media statements including, "I think they got the message from everyone of all ages" and a comment referencing giving a police officer "special prizes."
- A search warrant was executed for Defendant A's phone, but no videos of the January 6 events were located.
- A search of Defendant A's Facebook account revealed that the referenced post was no longer available.
- Defendant A pled guilty to Assaulting, Resisting, or Impeding Certain Officers, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1), as Count Two of the indictment.
- The statutory maximum penalty for the 18 U.S.C. § 111(a)(1) offense is 8 years of imprisonment.
- The offense of conviction carries a maximum fine of $250,000 and up to 3 years of supervised release.
- Defendant A agreed to pay a $100 special assessment for the felony conviction.
- As part of the plea, Defendant A agreed to pay $2,000 in restitution to the Architect of the Capitol.
- As part of the plea, Defendant A agreed to allow law enforcement agents to interview him about January 6 events prior to sentencing.
- The government agreed to seek dismissal of the remaining counts of the indictment at sentencing after Defendant A's guilty plea to Count Two.
- Defendant A acknowledged in a signed Statement of Offense that the factual basis was true and accurate.
- On April 7, 2022, Defendant A pled guilty to the § 111(a)(1) count and sentencing was set for August 5, 2022.
- Defendant A surrendered into custody after entering his plea and has been detained since that date.
- Defendant A has been detained since April 7, 2022, the date of the plea.
- On June 23, 2021, Defendant A was indicted with eight counts related to January 6 conduct.
- On December 1, 2021, a grand jury returned an eight-count Superseding Indictment, including Civil Disorder (18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3)) and § 111(a)(1).
- The superseding indictment also charged offenses under 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1), (a)(2), (a)(4) and 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D), (F), and (G).
- Defendant A initially appeared out-of-district in the Eastern District of California and was released on pretrial conditions.
- On July 22, 2021, Defendant A made an initial appearance in the District of Columbia.
- In the plea agreement, the parties attached a Statement of Offense that Defendant A agreed fairly and accurately described his actions.
- Defendant A agreed in the plea to pay $2,000 restitution to the Architect of the Capitol through the Clerk of Court.
- The government noted that Defendant A used goggles and a chemical irritant at the East Columbus doors and celebrated as the doors were opened.
- Security footage captured Defendant A entering through the East Columbus doors around 3:02 p.m., traversing toward the Rotunda, and exiting around 3:20 p.m.
- The government identified at least six officers present at the East Columbus doors when Defendant A sprayed the chemical irritant.
- The government was unable to identify the specific chemical irritant Defendant A used to spray officers.
- Defendant A later wrote on social media that a police officer had sprayed him and struck him, and he referenced retaliatory "special prizes."
- Defendant A admitted in the Statement of Offense that he posted about the events on Facebook after January 6.
- Defendant A's conduct included entering the Capitol building but he was not among those who destroyed or stole property, according to the defense sentencing memorandum citing the PSR.
- Defense counsel asserted that Defendant A did not engage in hand-to-hand combat and that there were several people between him and the officers he sprayed.
- Defense counsel stated that Defendant A walked away from the East Columbus doors after deploying the chemical irritant and returned later to enter the building.
- Defense counsel stated that Defendant A was present inside the Capitol building for approximately 18 minutes.
- Defense counsel represented that Defendant A was under the influence of a combination of substances during the events of January 6.
- Defense counsel reported that while on pretrial release, Defendant A stalled providing urine samples on seven occasions, failed to test seven times, and admitted to using methamphetamines on three occasions and marijuana once.
- Defense counsel stated that Defendant A attempted to enter a treatment program while the case was pending but was unable to do so due to case-related delay concerns.
- Defense counsel represented that Defendant A was accepted into a thirteen-month, faith-based inpatient drug treatment program in central California.
- The government described Defendant A as a self-professed member of the Proud Boy organization.
- The defense stated that Defendant A was at a booth for a pro-event group near the Ellipse earlier on January 6 and was not part of a separate group that met away from the Ellipse.
- The government reported that more than one hundred law enforcement officers were injured during the January 6 attack.
- Defendant A agreed in his plea that, upon his guilty plea to Count Two, the government would not further prosecute him for the conduct set forth in the Statement of Offense and would dismiss the remaining counts at sentencing.
- The plea agreement advised that restitution or fines imposed would be due and payable immediately and subject to enforcement by the United States.
- The plea agreement noted that Defendant A had one California conviction for aiding and abetting an exhibition of speed, for which a fine was imposed.
- The government stated that Defendant A had three prior arrests resulting in criminal charges for violent conduct that did not result in convictions and a pending charge for felony assault of a spouse with a deadly weapon.
- Defense counsel asserted that Defendant A did not enter offices or attempt to enter the Senate Chamber and spent relatively little time in the Rotunda.
- Defense counsel stated that Defendant A did not chant violent rhetoric such as calls to harm the Vice President or Speaker of the House.
- Defense counsel indicated that Defendant A did not coordinate with others to commit his offense on January 6.
- Defense counsel acknowledged that Defendant A sprayed a chemical irritant and threw the canister but characterized the action as impulsive and isolated.
- The government noted that Defendant A sang the national anthem with other rioters outside the East Columbus doors shortly before spraying the officers.
- According to the government, Defendant A maneuvered closer to the officers before deploying the chemical spray.
- The government stated that it could not identify the exact chemical used by Defendant A to spray officers.
- Defense counsel stated that Defendant A supported law enforcement and later expressed shame for his conduct.
- Defense counsel reported that Defendant A lost his employment during the COVID-19 pandemic and had previously opened his own insurance agency.
- Defense counsel reported that Defendant A experienced the suicide of his father in 2020.
- Defense counsel described long-term substance abuse issues for Defendant A, including methamphetamine and opiate use.
- Defense counsel stated that Defendant A overdosed on methamphetamine in October 2017.
- Defense counsel indicated Defendant A’s child is on the autism spectrum and that Defendant A dedicated substantial time to the child’s therapy.
- The plea agreement stated that Defendant A waived any challenge to venue in the District of Columbia.
- Defendant A consented in the plea to the possibility of holding proceedings by video teleconference and/or teleconference under the CARES Act.
- The government recounted that alarms were blaring inside the Capitol as rioters forced open the East Columbus doors around 2:38 p.m., and police were overwhelmed in that area.
- Defense counsel compared Defendant A’s conduct to other January 6 cases, emphasizing that he did not lead the charge and did not commit multiple assaults.

- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened at approximately 1:00 p.m. to certify the Electoral College vote, with the Vice President presiding.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m. on January 6, 2021, members of the House and Senate were instructed to evacuate and the joint session was suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- The exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to the public on January 6, 2021, with metal bike rack barriers and large “AREA CLOSED” signs.
- At around 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals in the crowd forced entry into the U.S. Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting officers.
- At 2:03 p.m., police broadcast a continuous dispersal order declaring the area restricted and warning of arrest and riot control agents.
- At 12:52 p.m., the crowd breached unmanned barricades near the Peace Circle and engaged U.S. Capitol Police at the first manned barrier.
- By 12:58 p.m., rioters overwhelmed the second manned police barrier and flooded the Lower West Plaza.
- Defendant A was identified in multiple videos encouraging others, pushing a large sign into officers, and spraying a chemical at a police line on the West Terrace area.
- Investigators compared a driver's license photo to riot footage and assessed that it was consistent with Defendant A.
- Body-worn camera captured Defendant A at about 1:31 p.m. using a megaphone near a police barricade, making provocative statements to officers.
- Around 1:40 p.m., Defendant A was seen helping push a large metal sign into officers and yelled “Put it up there! Put it up there!”
- Video showed Defendant A scuffling with an officer in an attempt to defeat a barricade.
- Video captured Defendant A deploying a yellow chemical spray toward officers; the canister was holstered at his right hip.
- Travel records show Defendant A flew to Washington, D.C. on January 5, 2021, and booked a return for January 7, 2021, with two other individuals on the reservation.
- Bank records corroborated airfare purchases associated with Defendant A’s travel on January 5, 2021.
- A video recorded a female voice calling Defendant A by first name and him responding on a bullhorn to gather people.
- Hotel records show Defendant A stayed at a hotel near the Capitol from January 5 to January 7, 2021, with three adults listed in the room.
- Hotel CCTV showed the identified female companion using elevators between approximately 12:30 p.m. and 12:50 p.m. on January 6, 2021.
- Investigators were still working to definitively identify the female companion, though her characteristics matched the female traveler on Defendant A’s reservation.
- Defendant A was on probation on January 6, 2021, from a 2018 DUI-related case for which he received five years of probation and 180 days in jail.
- The supervising probation deputy initially could not identify the person in photos but later stated the photos resembled Defendant A.
- In October 2020, Defendant A sent a selfie to his probation officer; investigators compared that photo to January 6 images and noted distinctive features matched.
- Investigators surveilled an Auburn, California residence associated with Defendant A and observed him entering and exiting on April 5, 2021.
- State records reflected that Defendant A owned a construction and electrical business associated with the Auburn address.
- Law enforcement received approximately ten tips regarding the identity of the subject later determined to be Defendant A; one tip pointed to a video where a female called him by first name.
- In late December 2020, Defendant A posted on social media that he was going to Washington, D.C. on January 5–7 to “fight” and referenced “storm[ing] Congress.”
- In the early hours of January 6, 2021, Defendant A posted that “1776 will commence again January 6, 2021.”
- Defendant A carried a canister of bear spray in a holster on his belt to the Capitol.
- Defendant A agreed that his use of bear spray on officers constituted use of a deadly or dangerous weapon.
- The spray used by Defendant A was SABRE Frontiersman Bear Attack Deterrent with 2.0% capsaicinoids, the strongest EPA-allowed formula for bear spray.
- The bear spray used had an effective range of approximately 35 feet and the canister emptied in about five seconds.
- The bear spray formula used by Defendant A was more than 50 percent stronger than police pepper spray and hazardous for human use, with risk of irreversible eye damage.
- Two additional 9.2-ounce cans of the same brand of bear spray were seized from Defendant A’s residence during a May 27, 2021 search.
- Before the rally concluded, Defendant A used a megaphone to urge people to “march on Congress directly after Trump’s speech.”
- While approaching the Capitol, Defendant A recorded a video stating “we’re storming the Capitol” and that they would tell Congress not to accept Electoral College votes.
- At about 12:53 p.m., Defendant A was at the Peace Monument breach and moved up the Pennsylvania Walkway past a second defensive line after it was breached.
- Defendant A led chants of “Whose house? Our house!” near a temporary black fence on the north side of the Lower West Plaza.
- At approximately 12:58:54 p.m., after being directed by an officer not to enter, Defendant A ran through a gap and was one of the first to breach into the West Plaza.
- At about 1:00 p.m., Defendant A and another rioter pulled on a bike rack barricade manned by a USCP officer, creating an opening before officers regained control.
- Between approximately 1:00 p.m. and 1:11 p.m., Defendant A remained near the front line at the south end of the West Plaza, verbally confronting officers.
- At around 1:11 p.m., Defendant A fired two long, continuous bursts of bear spray from a distance of a little more than 10 feet, sweeping a wide area of officers.
- Multiple officers were hit by Defendant A’s spray and some had to leave the line to decontaminate.
- A U.S. Capitol Police officer identified as Officer A attributed temporary blindness and inability to perform duties to being sprayed by Defendant A and later returned to the line.
- From about 1:11 p.m. to 1:40 p.m., Defendant A stayed at the front of the crowd, using a bullhorn to taunt officers and urge rioters forward.
- Defendant A shouted threats and statements such as “You are protecting communists!” and “The Second Amendment is behind us!” at officers.
- Defendant A grabbed a barricade, tugged on it, and said “hell yeah” while telling the crowd “come on you guys.”
- Around 1:40 p.m., Defendant A twice helped push a large wheeled metal billboard sign into officers while shouting through his megaphone “Put it up there!”
- The large metal sign used against officers had sharp edges and protruding wheels and required 15–20 officers to move due to its weight and size.
- After the riot, Defendant A posted on Facebook that he “unloaded a whole can of bear spray on a line of cops” and claimed “we stormed them and we took Congress.”
- Defendant A posted that he was at “the front” and that he had been sprayed in the face multiple times, claiming his contacts melted to his eyes.
- On November 10, 2021, a superseding indictment charged Defendant A with ten counts including assault on officers with a dangerous weapon and obstruction of an official proceeding.
- On April 17, 2023, Defendant A was convicted of assaulting officers with a dangerous weapon and obstruction of an official proceeding after a stipulated trial.
- Each count of conviction carried statutory exposure of up to 20 years’ imprisonment, up to three years’ supervised release, and up to a $250,000 fine, plus a $100 special assessment per count.
- The government attributed more than $2.9 million in losses to the January 6, 2021 events and reported that more than one hundred police officers were injured.
- Defendant A did not enter the Capitol building, according to assertions in the defense sentencing memorandum.
- The defense asserted that Defendant A had been awake for nearly 48 hours by about 1:00 p.m. on January 6 and claimed impaired judgment.
- The defense claimed Defendant A carried the spray for self-defense against anticipated counter-protesters and reacted after seeing his mother struck by police projectiles.
- The defense claimed wind conditions, rather than aiming, caused an officer to be affected by the spray.
- The defense stated that Defendant A accepted responsibility for his actions on January 6.
- The government stated Defendant A participated in four separate attempts to breach police perimeters: initial breach of West Plaza, wrestling for a barricade, spraying officers, and pushing a large metal sign.
- During the West Plaza confrontations, Defendant A used a megaphone to make statements including “You are gonna hear the lion roar!” and “Nazis followed orders!”
- A government filing states that Defendant A has not filed personal income taxes with the IRS since 2016.
- The government reported that while detained on July 10, 2023, Defendant A was identified by D.C. Department of Corrections staff as part of a group that assaulted another inmate.
- Defendant A raised more than $70,000 via a GiveSendGo crowdfunding page as of late August 2023.
- An August 31, 2023 post on Defendant A’s fundraising page stated, “I did not hurt anyone and I did not enter the Capital [sic] yet they want to give me 11.5 years.”
- Defendant A’s prior convictions include misdemeanor possession of a stolen vehicle (2006), receiving stolen property and petty theft (2007), and obstructing or resisting a public officer (2007).
- Defendant A’s record includes driving with knowledge of suspension (2009) and a 2010 statutory rape conviction reduced to a misdemeanor with a probation violation.
- Defendant A was convicted in 2010 of driving under the influence (DUI) and separately of battery/domestic violence, with failures to comply with a domestic violence program requirement.
- He was convicted in 2010 of driving on a suspended license with multiple probation violations, and in 2011 of vandalism with a 2014 probation violation.
- Defendant A was convicted in March 2014 of entering a non-commercial dwelling and separately of DUI, followed by a December 2018 DUI with two priors and driving while license suspended/revoked.
- Defendant A used a megaphone on January 6 to warn officers that the crowd would “exercise our Second Amendment rights,” saying officers “ain’t gonna hold the line.”
- Defendant A exhorted the crowd to advance toward the police line, including saying “Come on! Let’s go!” and “Bring it in.”
- Defendant A was present at the initial breach of the restricted Capitol grounds near the Peace Circle and became part of the breach of the West Plaza.
- Some officers at the West Plaza turned away or backed away in reaction to the spray deployed by Defendant A, and some left the line to decontaminate.
- The large billboard sign used by rioters had the potential to cause serious injury to an officer if rammed into the head due to weight and sharp edges.
- Defendant A used social media on January 6 to boast that there were “easily over a million people” and that he sprayed officers.
- During January 6 confrontations, Defendant A told officers, “We’re coming, you can’t stop us forever.”
- The government reported that Defendant A later gave an interview minimizing his culpability and characterizing his bullhorn use as political dissent.

- A joint session of Congress convened at approximately 1:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, to certify the Electoral College vote.
- By approximately 1:30 p.m. on January 6, 2021, the House and Senate adjourned to separate chambers during the certification proceedings, with the Vice President present and presiding.
- The U.S. Capitol exterior plaza was closed to the public on January 6, 2021, with security barriers and U.S. Capitol Police present.
- At approximately 2:00 p.m., individuals forced their way through barricades and advanced to the Capitol’s exterior, without security screenings or weapons checks.
- Shortly after 2:00 p.m., individuals forced entry into the Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement officers.
- The January 6 riot caused substantial damage to the U.S. Capitol requiring expenditures of more than $1.4 million for repairs, as reflected in the offense stipulation.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m., members of the House and Senate evacuated, and Congressional proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- The Vice President remained in the Capitol from evacuation until proceedings resumed at approximately 8:00 p.m.
- At approximately 2:35 p.m., Defendant A was in a crowd at the East Rotunda Doors while officers guarded the doors inside and outside.
- Members of the crowd near the East Rotunda Doors chanted phrases such as “our house!” and “stop the steal!” and at least one person sprayed a chemical irritant at officers.
- At approximately 2:38 p.m., individuals inside the Capitol pushed the East Rotunda Doors open from the inside.
- Some in the crowd outside the East Rotunda Doors yelled “keep the door open!” and “hold the line!” when the doors opened.
- Individuals fought police in the doorway at the East Rotunda Doors as others tried to force officers out of the way to clear a path for entry.
- A group at the East Rotunda Doors shouted “Heave! Ho!” and pushed toward the entrance in unison.
- Defendant A joined the group shouting “Heave! Ho!”, pushed forward, slipped through a narrow passage between police and the doorframe, and entered the Capitol at approximately 2:40 p.m.
- Once inside, Defendant A climbed on a bench and appeared to take a photograph or video with a mobile phone.
- Defendant A proceeded upstairs, hugged another individual, and raised his fists in the air before entering the Senate gallery.
- After exiting, Defendant A gave an on-camera interview on the steps near the East Rotunda Doors that was posted to Twitter, where he said, among other things, “We took the building… we will … do it again.”
- Defendant A knew he did not have permission to enter the Capitol at the time he entered.
- Defendant A admitted the elements of civil disorder: he committed acts to obstruct, impede, or interfere with law enforcement officers performing official duties during a civil disorder which affected commerce or a federally protected function.
- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to a criminal information charging civil disorder, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3).
- The civil disorder offense carries a statutory maximum of five years’ imprisonment.
- The civil disorder offense carries a potential fine of up to $250,000.
- The civil disorder offense carries a potential term of supervised release of up to three years.
- Defendant A agreed to pay a $100 special assessment for the felony conviction.
- As part of the plea, Defendant A agreed to allow law enforcement to review any social media accounts for statements and postings in and around January 6, 2021, and to be interviewed prior to sentencing.
- The government agreed to request dismissal of the Indictment at sentencing in consideration of the guilty plea to the information.
- Defendant A acknowledged prior convictions including a 2017 DUI and a 2018 probation violation.
- Defendant A agreed to pay $2,000 in restitution to the Architect of the Capitol.
- The plea agreement acknowledged approximately $1,495,326.55 in damage to the U.S. Capitol as of May 17, 2021.
- As of April 5, 2022, the government cited approximately $2,734,783.15 in losses from the Capitol breach.
- More than one hundred law enforcement officers were injured during the January 6 events, as described in the government’s filing.
- Defendant A traveled by car from northern Florida to Washington, D.C. with family and a friend to attend a rally on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant A believed the election had been stolen and viewed the trip as a last chance to show support for a then-sitting president, according to the government’s account of his statements.
- Before heading to the Capitol, Defendant A left a flag at the hotel out of concern it could be mistaken for a weapon and put on a camouflage jacket.
- Defendant A moved to the east side of the Capitol after hearing flash bangs and smelling smoke.
- At the East Rotunda Doors, rioters repeatedly sprayed a chemical irritant toward police guarding the door, as captured in sources cited by the government.
- Defendant A appeared to activate his phone’s record function outside the East Rotunda Doors shortly before entry, as observed in video referenced by the government.
- Defendant A entered the Capitol at approximately 2:39:55 p.m., according to timestamped video cited by the government.
- Inside the Capitol, Defendant A raised his arm triumphantly and bounced on a bench while holding his phone, per video referenced by the government.
- As he moved upstairs, Defendant A pounded on his chest and spoke excitedly, as described in the government’s memorandum and supported by images.
- Defendant A shouted statements inside the Capitol such as “I knew this was going to happen,” “we’re back here for our votes,” and “1776 all over again,” according to body-camera video described by the government.
- In the Senate gallery, Defendant A yelled, “this is our house now!” according to video described by the government.
- Defendant A left the Capitol through the East Rotunda Doors at approximately 2:52 p.m., per the government’s account.
- A media interview with Defendant A on the Capitol steps was posted on social media at approximately 3:34 p.m., containing statements including “We took the building… we will… do it again.”
- On January 22, 2021, after the FBI attempted contact, defense counsel informed the FBI that Defendant A would decline to speak at that time.
- On March 1, 2021, a criminal complaint charged Defendant A with obstructing an official proceeding and other offenses; he surrendered to the FBI on March 5, 2021.
- FBI agents warned Defendant A not to erase any data from his two cell phones during the arrest process, and he said he understood.
- The FBI seized two cell phones: one used on January 6 and another purchased afterward; the post-January 6 phone contained no relevant information per the government’s review.
- On the phone used on January 6, the photo/video application contained zero photographs or videos dated between January 4 and 13, 2021, although some images were recovered from text messages.
- Agents recovered a 45‑second video recorded in the Senate gallery that Defendant A had sent to his mother via text message.
- Text and call records show Defendant A’s mother texted “Andrew call me” at 2:12 p.m., called 11 times in the next ten minutes, and texted “the cops are coming” at 2:36 p.m. on January 6, 2021.

- On January 6, 2021, the U.S. Capitol grounds and building were restricted, with access limited to authorized people.
- The Capitol was secured by U.S. Capitol Police with permanent and temporary barriers and posts.
- The Vice President was present at the Capitol on January 6, 2021, presiding over proceedings.
- A joint session of Congress to certify the 2020 Electoral College vote began at approximately 1:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021.
- By shortly after 1:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, a civil disorder had developed at the Capitol.
- The civil disorder obstructed the Secret Service’s protective function for the Vice President on January 6, 2021.
- Around 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals forced their way through barricades and advanced to the exterior façade of the Capitol building.
- Prior to entry by rioters, the exterior doors and windows of the Capitol were locked or otherwise secured.
- Shortly after 2:00 p.m., individuals forced entry into the Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement officers.
- The riot caused more than $2.9 million in damage to the Capitol requiring repairs.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m., members of the House and Senate were evacuated, and proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- Proceedings resumed around 8:00 p.m. after the building had been secured.
- The Vice President remained in the Capitol from evacuation until proceedings resumed the evening of January 6, 2021.
- The attack at the Capitol affected commerce in the District of Columbia, including early closures of grocery stores and lost sales.
- A 6 p.m. curfew was imposed in the District of Columbia on January 6, 2021, in response to the riot.
- The attack at the Capitol on January 6, 2021, was a “civil disorder” as defined in 18 U.S.C. § 232(1).
- Defendant A rejoined the Proud Boys in December 2020 and was a member of the “Space Coast” chapter in central Florida.
- The Proud Boys have local chapters led by chapter presidents and an initiation process; members often wear black and yellow apparel with Proud Boys logos.
- A new Proud Boys chapter called the Ministry of Self Defense (MOSD) was created for hand-selected members as a national rally planning chapter in late 2020.
- Defendant A was invited to join the MOSD on December 27, 2020.
- MOSD chats in which Defendant A participated discussed potential violence at the Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- An MOSD chat on January 3, 2021, included calls to “stack those bodies in front of Capitol Hill” and to push through police lines and storm the Capitol buildings.
- On January 4, 2021, MOSD messages discussed attacking the Capitol and asserted that authorities would “do nothing.”
- Tarrio was ordered to stay away from Washington, D.C., after his arrest on January 4, 2021, and would not be present on January 6, 2021.
- Participants were told to meet at the Washington Monument at 10 a.m. on January 6, 2021, and not to wear Proud Boys colors but to “come out as patriot.”

- At approximately 2:16 p.m., Defendant A was at the front of a group attempting to breach a line of officers on the east side of the Capitol.
- The crowd breached the police line near the east side, and Defendant A advanced toward the Capitol building with associates.
- At approximately 2:18 p.m., Defendant A attempted to enter the building near the Senate Carriage Door but was repelled by officers issuing commands and physically deterring entry.
- On the east terrace near the Columbus Doors, the crowd sang the National Anthem shortly before 2:40 p.m., then began to violently attack officers at the doors.

- When entering the building, Defendant A understood he was not permitted to enter and that the building was restricted to allow certification of the election results.
- Defendant A climbed to the second floor in a stack formation while the crowd around him chanted “treason!”

- Defendant A exited the Capitol at approximately 2:53 p.m. through the Senate Carriage Door.

- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to a count charging Entering and Remaining in a Restricted Building or Grounds, in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1).

- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened at the U.S. Capitol to certify the Electoral College vote.

- The exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to the public on January 6, 2021.
- Around 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals forced entry into the U.S. Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m. on January 6, 2021, members of the House and Senate were evacuated from their chambers.
- Congressional proceedings were effectively suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, and later resumed around 8:00 p.m.
- Vice President was present and presiding during the proceedings on January 6, 2021, and remained in the Capitol until proceedings resumed.
- The riot caused substantial damage to the U.S. Capitol requiring more than $1.4 million in repairs, as reflected in the stipulated offense facts.
- As of July 7, 2023, the approximate losses from the January 6 events were reported as $2,923,080.05.

- Following the fall, other officers assisted the injured officer out of the building.
- After the stairwell confrontation, Defendant A continued to push against officers until additional police arrived and regained control.
- Defendant A was directed out of the east side of the Capitol and exited the building at approximately 3:12:17 p.m.
- Defendant A knew at the time he entered the U.S. Capitol that he did not have permission to enter.
