- While falling, the injured officer felt arms attempting to grab her and was extremely frightened, unsure if she was being helped or dragged.
- After regaining her senses, the injured officer asked whether she still had her duty firearm.
- Defendant A later recorded a selfie-style video from an airport on the day of the riot describing his conduct, which he streamed to his social media account.
- Defendant A told officers in the Capitol, according to his video, to stand down because there were too many rioters.
- Defendant A later messaged that he pushed police from the front to the back of the Capitol.
- Defendant A’s social media postings before January 6 expressed eagerness to prevent the President-elect from taking office.
- A law enforcement affiant reviewed multiple MPD body-worn camera videos that recorded Defendant A inside the Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- At approximately 2:57:16 p.m., Defendant A was seen directing unknown individuals to come toward him, after which a larger group approached the west Rotunda entrance.
- At approximately 3:01:47 p.m., Defendant A’s contact with the officer’s baton preceded the officer losing balance and falling down the stairs.
- Defendant A continued pushing against officers and broke through the police line into an atrium leading to an office area before being pushed out.
- Defendant A’s pre-January 6 social media activity included asking others whether they would be in D.C. on January 6.
- Defendant A described January 6 afterward as the coolest thing he had ever done in his life.
- Defendant A later downplayed his conduct on social media and stated he had his hands down the entire time.
- Defendant A publicly stated after January 6 that he had no qualms about what he did and that he welcomed potential consequences.
- Defendant A’s prior convictions include a 2009 marijuana possession (less than one ounce) and a 2004 trespassing offense.
- Defendant A admitted and stipulated in the Statement of Offense that he obstructed and impeded an official proceeding and acted corruptly.
- In the Senate wing corridor at approximately 2:49 p.m., Defendant A pressed against an officer blocking a stairway and maneuvered past him.
- During the Western Rotunda confrontation, the injured officer was part of a police line pushed back by rioters and was wearing a bicycle helmet.
- Defendant A’s conduct included beckoning others and physically shoving an officer after being told to back up.
- Defendant A’s social media message on January 6 expressed that they pushed police out the back door of the Capitol.
- Defendant A recorded that he was immediately detained when he returned to the Capitol on January 7, according to his own posted video.
- Defendant A was an MMA instructor and also worked as a bartender prior to sentencing.
- Defendant A cooperated with execution of a search warrant by providing access to his cellular telephone, according to defense filings.
- According to defense filings, Defendant A maintained a respectful demeanor with agents and the Court and appeared for all court hearings.
- Defense filings state that Defendant A had minimal issues on pretrial supervision, with reference to an early missed reporting call.
- Defense filings indicate that Defendant A has been employed as a bartender at a hotel and convention center prior to sentencing.
- Defense materials report that Defendant A struggled with substance use prior to January 6 and sought to achieve sobriety afterward.
- A magistrate judge attested to the complaint on May 17, 2021, initiating the case via a complaint with arrest warrant.

- Defendant A agreed to plead guilty to one count of possession of firearms as an unlawful user of a controlled substance under 18 U.S.C. §§ 922(g)(3) and 924(a)(2).
- Defendant A waived indictment and consented to proceed by criminal information.
- The offense of conviction carries a statutory maximum of 10 years imprisonment.
- The offense of conviction authorizes up to 3 years of supervised release.
- The offense of conviction authorizes a fine of up to $250,000.
- A mandatory $100 special assessment applies and Defendant A agreed to pay it at sentencing.
- On August 15, 2020, approximately 50 individuals dressed in black converged on the Minneapolis Police Department’s 5th Precinct and the event became violent and destructive.
- During the August 15, 2020 incident, objects were thrown at the 5th Precinct building, windows were broken, exterior surfaces were spray painted, and paint was used to obscure surveillance cameras.
- Mortar-type fireworks were fired at the 5th Precinct building and at officers stationed on the roof on August 15, 2020.
- An MPD investigator identified Defendant A on surveillance as one of the individuals spray painting the front windows of the 5th Precinct on August 15, 2020.
- On October 14, 2020, an MPD investigator issued a probable-cause pickup for Defendant A based on felony damage to property related to the August 15 incident.
- A 'PC pickup' is an alert for officers to arrest a person based on probable cause they committed a crime.
- On October 14, 2020, investigators obtained a search warrant for Defendant A’s residence in Blaine, Minnesota to seek evidence of the August 15 incident, including a T-shirt seen in surveillance.
- On October 15, 2020, Hennepin County Sheriff's Deputies arrested Defendant A inside the Hennepin County Government Center.
- The arrest on October 15, 2020 occurred during a protest at the Government Center tied to a court appearance of four officers charged in connection with George Floyd’s death.
- At the time of arrest on October 15, 2020, Defendant A had a loaded Glock 26 Gen5 9mm semi-automatic pistol tucked into his waistband.
- After the arrest, MPD executed the residential search warrant and observed firearms, suspected illegal controlled substances, and drug paraphernalia, then obtained a follow-up warrant to seize them.
- At Defendant A's residence, officers seized a Remington Arms Model 770 rifle with scope, side saddle, live ammunition, and sling in a case.
- At the residence, officers seized a Kel-Tec P-11 9mm semi-automatic pistol with a laser attachment and magazines in a holster.
- Over 1,400 rounds of live ammunition of various types were found at the residence.
- Five empty gun boxes related to firearms not found in the residence, holsters, and a gun cleaning kit were present at the residence.
- Several bottles and vials containing suspected marijuana oil/wax and three suspected marijuana vape pens were at the residence.
- Approximately 96 grams of marijuana and marijuana joints were found at the residence in jars and bottles.
- A glass smoking pipe was seized from the residence.
- Defendant A’s vehicle, a blue 2005 Toyota Tacoma with Michigan plates, was located near the Government Center, towed to an impound lot, and searched pursuant to a warrant the next day.
- In a backpack in the vehicle, officers found a Kel-Tec 9mm rifle, magazines, marijuana, a ballistic panel, a GoPro, and books.
- In the vehicle’s console, officers found a Taurus .38 caliber revolver, marijuana packages, and approximately 2.5 grams of psychedelic mushrooms.
- A Ruger AR-556 rifle with five magazines was recovered from the vehicle.
- A Savage Springfield Arms 12-gauge shotgun with shells was recovered from the vehicle.
- A Smith & Wesson SD9VE 9mm pistol with ammunition and speed loaders was recovered from the vehicle.
- In a storage cubby in the vehicle, officers found two empty jars, a glass pipe, a jar with approximately 0.4 grams of cocaine, and marijuana joints.
- Additional items in the vehicle included a hatchet, crowbar, gas masks, and a bullhorn/megaphone.
- Defendant A admitted that the eight firearms and ammunition seized from his person, residence, and vehicle on October 15–16, 2020 belonged to him.
- Defendant A admitted that during the time he possessed the firearms, including October 2020, he was an unlawful user of controlled substances including marijuana and cocaine.
- Defendant A acknowledged knowing that marijuana and cocaine are controlled substances and that purchasing and using them was illegal.
- Defendant A did not dispute that some or all of the firearms and ammunition were manufactured outside Minnesota and traveled in interstate commerce before his possession in Minnesota in October 2020.
- Defendant A agreed to forfeit all firearms, ammunition, and associated accessories seized on or about October 15–16, 2020.
- The eight firearms identified for forfeiture included a Glock 26 Gen5 9mm pistol and a Remington 770 rifle.
- The eight firearms identified for forfeiture also included a Kel-Tec P-11 9mm pistol and a Kel-Tec 9mm rifle.
- The eight firearms identified for forfeiture also included a Taurus .38 caliber revolver and a Ruger AR-556 rifle.
- The eight firearms identified for forfeiture also included a Savage/Springfield Arms 12-gauge shotgun and a Smith & Wesson SD9VE 9mm pistol.
- Defendant A agreed that the United States could forfeit the firearms and ammunition through civil, criminal, or administrative proceedings and consented to destruction of the firearms and ammunition.
- At the time of his move to Minnesota in July 2020, Defendant A was 29 years old and had no criminal history.
- Defendant A moved to Minneapolis in July 2020, reportedly to be closer to the Black Lives Matter movement and expand activism goals.
- Within a month of arriving in Minnesota, Defendant A vandalized a Minneapolis police station by spray painting surveillance cameras and windows.
- On October 15, 2020, Defendant A brought a loaded Glock to a protest at the Hennepin County Government Center on a day when four police officers charged in connection with George Floyd’s death had a court appearance.
- After the October 15 arrest, officers searched Defendant A’s truck parked nearby and his apartment in Blaine, Minnesota pursuant to warrants.
- Officers searching the truck recovered additional guns and ammunition, mushrooms, cocaine, marijuana, a hatchet, a crowbar, a bullhorn, and a go-bag with a gun, ammunition, survival book, marijuana, a Go-Pro, and a ballistic plate.
- Across his person, residence, and truck, Defendant A possessed hundreds of rounds of ammunition, eight guns, and several types and quantities of illegal drugs.
- Defendant A was arrested on state charges and bonded out on October 19, 2020.
- On December 31, 2020, Defendant A participated in a downtown Minneapolis protest that authorities deemed a riot and possessed a pocket knife when encountered by authorities.
- On January 7 and 12, 2021, Defendant A made two attempts to acquire another firearm.
- Defendant A’s spouse reported that he owned some of his firearms prior to moving to Minnesota in July 2020.
- Of the eight guns seized on October 15, 2020, at least three were purchased in Minnesota in August and September 2020, and two were purchased in Michigan in May and June 2020; purchase dates for the remaining three were unclear.
- During the period surrounding the offense conduct, Defendant A reported a personal marital crisis and admitted regular use of marijuana, cocaine, and psychedelic mushrooms.
- Defendant A was facing Minnesota state felony charges in Hennepin County related to some of the conduct described and the parties believed those charges would be dismissed following his federal plea.
- As a result of the federal plea agreement, the pending state cases have been or will be dismissed.
- Defendant A’s first contacts with the criminal justice system occurred in 2020, stemming from protest-related activity connected to the current case.
- Defendant A acknowledges participating in an August 2020 protest where property damage occurred.
- Defendant A acknowledges having a gun at a protest at the Hennepin County Government Center, which led to the federal charge.
- Defendant A asserts he has never engaged in violence and did not use or plan to use the firearms for violence or other criminal activity.
- Defendant A asserts he acquired the firearms legally, obtained a permit to carry, and took safety training and precautions.
- Defendant A states he kept multiple firearms in his vehicle at the time of arrest to avoid proximity to a friend’s teenage child where he was staying after separating from his spouse.
- Defendant A claims he typically carried a pistol, went to the October 15 protest at a friend’s request, and did not consider prohibitions on possessing a gun at a courthouse.
- Defendant A asserts he did not enter or attempt to enter secure areas of the courthouse with a gun during the protest.
- Since his arrest and release in this case, Defendant A asserts he has not used drugs and has remained law-abiding on pretrial release.
- During pretrial release, Defendant A obtained employment at a gas station and worked overnight at a group home for elderly and disabled people.
- Defendant A plans to finish a bachelor's degree and pursue law school.
- Defendant A reports receiving mental health treatment since 2019, including therapy for anxiety and depression diagnoses.
- Defendant A claims his unlawful drug use during the offense period was a brief response to emotional trauma from marital infidelity, and that he stopped using drugs after his arrest.

- On January 6, 2021, the U.S. Capitol was secured by U.S. Capitol Police with the exterior plaza closed to the public and access limited to authorized persons.
- A joint session of Congress to certify the 2020 Electoral College vote began around 1:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, with the Vice President present.
- Around 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals in the crowd forced entry into the U.S. Capitol, including by breaking windows and assaulting police.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m., Congress members, including the Vice President, were evacuated and the joint session was suspended until after 8:00 p.m.
- The defendant traveled by car on January 5, 2021, from Bloomington, Indiana to Washington, D.C., with Family Member A to attend the rally at the Ellipse.
- After the rally, the defendant walked down Pennsylvania Avenue toward the Capitol with a crowd and entered the restricted west side of the Capitol grounds despite fences, signs, and barricades.
- As early as 2:15 p.m., the defendant used a cellphone to photograph rioters swarming the Capitol building and continued taking photos and videos as he moved closer.
- The defendant proceeded through scaffolding and up stairs toward the Capitol while other rioters scaled the scaffolding nearby.
- While ascending, a nearby rioter yelled at the defendant, "They're gonna eat you. They're gonna cheat you. Do not stop."
- The defendant passed clouds of pepper spray and sounds of conflict on the Lower West Terrace as he moved toward the building.
- Upon reaching the Upper West Terrace, the defendant took a video of a rioter climbing the exterior of the Capitol building while the crowd cheered.
- Before the defendant entered, officers had formed a line outside the Upper West Terrace Door but fell back as the crowd grew.
- Rioters exiting the Upper West Terrace Door triggered a piercing alarm that continued to sound while the door remained open.
- Rioters outside held the Upper West Terrace Door open while others streamed in as the crowd chanted "Let us in!"
- The defendant photographed rioters streaming in through the Upper West Terrace Door and people positioned on a banister and balcony above.
- An improvised barricade of bike racks and scaffolding poles was between the crowd and a police line near the Upper West Terrace Door as the defendant approached.
- The defendant entered the Capitol at 2:38 p.m. through the Upper West Terrace Door, which bore an "Emergency Exit Only" sign describing an alarm and delayed unlock.
- As he entered, the defendant held one of the double doors open and walked past multiple law enforcement officers inside.
- The defendant proceeded upstairs to the Rotunda and Statuary Hall and remained in those public areas for roughly 20 minutes.
- While inside the Rotunda, the defendant raised his fist and yelled "Freedom!"
- The defendant posed with an American flag held overhead in the center of the Rotunda after handing his phone to another person to take a photo.
- Police reinforcements began directing people out of the Rotunda around 3:02 p.m., and the defendant exited the building at 3:05 p.m. through the East Rotunda Doors.
- After exiting, the defendant remained in the restricted East Plaza area and continued taking cellphone pictures.
- Verizon cell-site records placed the defendant’s phone inside the Capitol area from 2:38 p.m. to 3:05 p.m. on January 6, 2021.
- The defendant texted at 3:18 p.m. on January 6, 2021 that he "stormed" the Capitol with Family Member A.
- The defendant sent a Rotunda photo of himself and Family Member A to at least five different people via social media messages.
- The defendant messaged that he and Family Member A had "stormed" the Capitol and that they "helped stop the vote count for a bit."
- The defendant claimed on social media that police "stood down and retreated after we clearly outnumbered them" and suggested officers "let us in."
- The defendant wrote that it would be "so easy to storm the Capitol with arms" and that officers "had it easy today."
- FBI received tips that the defendant posted images and videos depicting his presence in Washington, D.C., and inside the Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- Witnesses identified the defendant from social media posts; one screenshot showed him inside the Capitol Rotunda with a woman believed to be his mother.
- The defendant’s Instagram account used the handle "vodevivre," and posts showed the Pentagon, Washington Monument crowds, and the Capitol exterior with crowds.
- FBI obtained Facebook and Instagram records via search warrants showing the defendant acknowledged being inside the Capitol on January 6, 2021.
- The defendant stated in messages that he and Family Member A "stormed" the Capitol.
- An associated phone number ending in -4677 was linked to the defendant through records and his own acknowledgment to an associate on May 6, 2021.
- On the evening of January 5, 2021, a friend messaged the defendant on Instagram to "Rush the main entrance, security is week [weak]," and he replied "too late."
- The defendant entered the Capitol through a west-side door accessible from the Upper West Terrace and reached the Rotunda around 2:40 p.m., later leaving around 3:05 p.m.
- The defendant and Family Member A posed for a photo in the Rotunda with the defendant holding an American flag handed to him by another person.
- After the riot, the defendant messaged that "my [family member] and I stormed it [the Capitol] lol" and wrote to others that they had stormed the Capitol.
- The defendant wrote that he and Family Member A "helped stopped the vote count for a bit" and described police as having "stood down and retreated" after being outnumbered.
- The defendant was convicted by a jury on September 22, 2023 of four counts: 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1), 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(2), 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D), and 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(G).
- Counts under 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1) and (a)(2) are Class A misdemeanors, while counts under 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D) and (G) are Class B misdemeanors.
- During his trial, the defendant violated pretrial release conditions by visiting the 'Freedom Corner' near the D.C. jail and was placed on a curfew from 8:00 p.m. to 7:00 a.m. while in Washington, D.C.
- After conviction, the defendant’s social media bio described him as a "J6 wrongful convict," and he posted that there was "zero jury of peers and 100% a kangaroo court."
- On October 23, 2023, the defendant appeared on an online talk show and stated, "I thought that I was allowed to go in" and claimed that signs had been removed before he arrived.
- On March 12, 2024, the defendant stated in a podcast interview that rioters were at the Capitol for a "scheduled event," that there "was no violence," and that officers stood aside to "uphold" rights.
- The defendant used the Facebook account 'antony.d.vo' and Instagram account 'vodevivre,' which contained messages and images corroborating his presence in and around the Capitol.
- The defendant and Family Member A were present together in the Capitol Rotunda on January 6, 2021, based on a photo circulated among acquaintances.
- On February 15, 2022, the government offered the defendant a plea to Count Four, 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(G), with the defendant signing acceptance on March 11, 2022.
- As part of the signed plea materials, the defendant agreed to pay $500 in restitution to the Architect of the Capitol.
- In the signed Statement of Offense, the defendant admitted he knowingly entered the U.S. Capitol without permission and paraded, demonstrated, or picketed inside.
- The statutory maximum penalty for 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(G) is 6 months’ imprisonment, up to 5 years’ probation, and a fine up to $5,000.
- On January 6, 2021, the defendant took photographs and videos of crowd activity, including a torn tarp and rioters using a barricade as a makeshift ladder.
- The Upper West Terrace Door is a double fire door that serves as an emergency exit and not a public entrance, and an alarm sounded when opened.
- While inside the Capitol, the defendant spoke with other participants and took photographs before police reinforcements arrived.
- The defendant sent and received social media messages about his presence and actions on January 6 within hours of leaving the building.
- Witnesses told the FBI they knew the defendant to support former President Trump and engage with conspiracy theories, and one knew him to subscribe to libertarian ideologies.
- The defendant claimed to have met a person who stole the Speaker of the House’s gavel, according to his post-riot messages.
- On or about September 22, 2023, during trial, the court found the defendant violated release conditions by visiting an area near the D.C. jail not related to court business.
- The government cited an estimated loss figure of approximately $2,923,080.05 from the January 6 attack as of July 7, 2023, excluding certain MPD losses.
- The Statement of Offense recites that the riot resulted in more than $1.4 million in damage to the Capitol.
- The defendant and Family Member A separated briefly before rejoining inside the Capitol and left together around 3:05 p.m.
- FBI affiant Agent A concluded from imagery and timing that the defendant's Facebook photo showed him inside the Rotunda after the breach.
- The defendant wrote that police opened gates, sharing a tweet titled "the police opened the f***ing gates," and told a friend officers "stood down."
- The defendant walked past a posted sign on the interior of the Upper West Terrace Door reading 'Emergency Exit Only' with alarm instructions.
- The defendant yelled and gestured among the crowd in the Rotunda, as depicted in trial exhibits referenced by the government.
- On January 6 and 7, 2021, the defendant repeatedly described on social media that he and Family Member A had stormed the Capitol.
- The defendant asserted after conviction that he believed he was allowed to enter the Capitol and that signs were removed before he arrived.
- The upper west terrace crowd chanted as outmanned officers were overwhelmed near the entry door shortly before the defendant entered.
- The defendant's criminal history includes a May 15, 2017 arrest in Texas resulting in a guilty plea to Possession of Marijuana and six months of community supervision.
- On August 21, 2019, the defendant was charged with Possession of Marijuana in Virginia, later amended to Unauthorized Possession of Paraphernalia, with a $300 fine.
- On June 24, 2020, the defendant was charged with Possession of a Schedule I/II Drug in Virginia, amended at sentencing to Accessory After the Fact, with 12 months jail suspended and a $500 fine.
- The defendant sent and received messages indicating awareness that the certification proceedings were affected, stating "we stopped the vote count for a bit."
- The Statement of Facts explains that for 18 U.S.C. § 1752, a "restricted building" includes areas where the Vice President is or will be temporarily visiting.
- The defendant’s Instagram and Facebook communications occurred soon after the events of January 6, according to witnesses and records.

- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened at the U.S. Capitol to certify the Electoral College vote.
- As the joint session continued, a large crowd gathered outside the Capitol and U.S. Capitol Police attempted to keep the crowd away.
- At about 2:00 PM on January 6, 2021, individuals in the crowd forced their way through barricades and advanced to the Capitol’s exterior facade.
- Individuals forced entry into the Capitol by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement shortly after 2:00 PM on January 6, 2021.
- The riot caused more than $2.7 million in damage requiring repairs to the Capitol.
- At approximately 2:20 PM on January 6, 2021, members of the House and Senate were evacuated, and proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 PM.
- The Vice President remained in the Capitol from the time he was evacuated from the Senate chamber until the session resumed around 8:00 PM on January 6, 2021.
- On January 5, 2021, Defendant A traveled by car from Auburn, Alabama to Washington, D.C., to protest Congress’s certification of the Electoral College.
- Defendant A stated he traveled because he believed the election was fraudulent and to support a protest at the Capitol.
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant A followed a schedule promoted publicly to meet at the Ellipse at 9:00 a.m. and then move to the Capitol at 1:00 p.m.
- Defendant A was wearing dark pants and a yellow hooded sweatshirt and had a long beard on January 6, 2021.
- A crowd near the Capitol chanted phrases at officers such as “Freedom,” “Put down your weapons,” and references to oaths to protect the public.
- Defendant A had a pocketknife on his waistband while on Capitol grounds and inside the Capitol building on January 6, 2021.
- The knife had a blade about three to four inches in length.
- Defendant A used his knife to help tear down cloth around inauguration scaffolding so the crowd could move further up the steps toward the Capitol building.
- Just after 2:00 p.m., the police line on the west side was overrun and Defendant A, along with others, moved up the Capitol steps across the Upper West Terrace toward the Senate Wing Doors.
- At approximately 2:13 p.m., Defendant A entered the Capitol by completing the breaking of a partially broken windowpane at the Senate Wing Door area and jumping through the frame.
- At the time of entry, Defendant A had a pocketknife and a can of OC spray that he had found; both items were dangerous weapons.
- After entering, Defendant A went to the Ohio Clock Corridor near the Senate chambers and encountered law enforcement officers there.
- In the Ohio Clock Corridor, Defendant A was relatively quiet and helped an officer try to calm the crowd before being escorted out.
- Defendant A posted a Snapchat photo of himself in the Ohio Clock Corridor with a caption asserting he was pleading for peaceful discourse and rejecting being called Antifa.
- Defendant A knew while on Capitol grounds and inside the building that he did not have lawful authority to be there.
- The approximate losses from the January 6 Capitol breach were about $2,881,360.20 as of October 17, 2022.
- Defendant A brought a stun gun and a knife with him to Washington, D.C., and carried the knife into the Capitol building.
- Defendant A helped overrun officers defending the Northwest Stairs and cut through inauguration scaffolding to facilitate movement toward the Capitol building.
- While on Capitol grounds, Defendant A obtained a Sabre Red OC spray canister.
- On the Upper West Terrace, Defendant A approached officers with the OC spray canister outstretched as if about to deploy it.
- Defendant A smashed remaining glass in a Senate Wing Door window to make it easier for himself and others to climb through and enter the Capitol.
- The Senate Wing Door entry point at 2:13 p.m. was the first breach of the Capitol building on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant A was among the first 40 rioters to breach the Capitol building.
- After entering, Defendant A turned toward the Senate Grand Stairs and joined rioters pursuing a U.S. Capitol Police officer toward the Ohio Clock Corridor.
- Defendant A and others entered the Ohio Clock Corridor between 2:14 and 2:15 p.m., coming near an entrance to the Senate Chamber before full evacuation.
- At approximately 2:17 p.m., Defendant A addressed the crowd using a microphone, saying police were willing to work with them and proposing to gather more people to discuss what needed to be done.
- Over the next 30 minutes in the Ohio Clock Corridor, Defendant A remained, observed a rioter deploy a fire extinguisher toward officers, and pushed past officers at 2:48 p.m.
- Defendant A was waved toward the exit by officers around 2:51 p.m. and escorted out of the Capitol at approximately 2:55 p.m. through the Senate Carriage Door.
- On January 11, 2021, after waiving Miranda rights, Defendant A admitted to the FBI that he used a pocketknife to cut inauguration scaffolding and entered the Capitol through a broken window.
- In a January 11, 2021 interview, Defendant A stated he was not aware of individuals causing violence and attributed violence to law enforcement’s use of tear gas and mace.
- On January 15, 2021, Defendant A admitted obtaining an OC spray canister, gave conflicting accounts of how he acquired it, and described pointing it as a ‘don’t … do anything to me’ signal and readiness to defend himself.
- Defendant A said he did not disclose possession of the OC spray in his prior interview because he feared more severe punishment if he admitted it.
- An estimate from the Architect of the Capitol placed the cost of repairing each window in the Senate Wing Door area at over $10,000.
- On August 6, 2021, a federal grand jury returned an indictment charging eight counts, including obstruction of an official proceeding (18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2)) and entering and remaining in a restricted building or grounds with a deadly or dangerous weapon (18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1), (b)(1)(A)).
- On November 18, 2022, Defendant A was convicted of obstruction of an official proceeding and entering and remaining in a restricted building or grounds with a deadly or dangerous weapon as the result of a stipulated trial.
- For Count One (18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2)), the statutory maximum is up to 20 years of imprisonment, up to three years of supervised release, up to a $250,000 fine, and a $100 special assessment.
- For Count Three (18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1) and (b)(1)(A)), the statutory maximum is up to 10 years of imprisonment, up to three years of supervised release, up to a $250,000 fine, and a $100 special assessment.
- On December 31, 2020, during a livestream, Defendant A stated he would kill the President-elect rather than serve a 15-year prison sentence.
- The livestream platform provided law enforcement with the quoted statement and a screenshot attributed to an account operated by Defendant A, and investigators recognized him as the person livestreaming.
- On July 2, 2020, law enforcement executed a search warrant at Defendant A’s residence in Alabama, locating controlled substances, paraphernalia, cash, and multiple firearms.
- On August 12, 2020, a grand jury indicted Defendant A on two counts of drug trafficking and two counts of possession of a controlled substance in Alabama.
- On January 5, 2021, Defendant A traveled out of state to Washington, D.C., while on pretrial release in Alabama under conditions that forbade him from leaving the state.
- On January 11, 2021, after Defendant A returned to Alabama, a writ of arrest issued for violating his bond conditions by traveling out of state, his bond was revoked, and he was detained pretrial.
- On April 28, 2021, Defendant A’s state bond was reinstated with added conditions, including wearing an ankle monitor and biweekly drug tests.
- In the Ohio Clock Corridor, an officer asked if anyone had weapons, and Defendant A showed a pepper spray canister; when asked to surrender it, he put it back in his pocket.
- Defendant A used a microphone in the Ohio Clock Corridor to address the crowd about cooperating with police.
- A U.S. Capitol Police officer reported Defendant A was quiet, reserved, receptive to the officer, and eager to help calm the crowd in the Ohio Clock Corridor.
- The same officer was aware that Defendant A had a pepper spray canister during the encounter in the Ohio Clock Corridor.
- Defendant A entered the Capitol through a window that had already been broken by another person and further broke the windowpane as he climbed through.
- Defendant A spent approximately four months in custody at the Lee County Jail after his bond was revoked in the Alabama case related to his out-of-state travel.
- Defendant A was on location monitoring and home detention for about a year and a half, incurring fees and being restricted to home and work with officer permission for other travel.
- Since his arrest, Defendant A worked in restaurants in the Auburn and Montgomery areas and focused on culinary work.
- Defendant A has a child who was born prematurely in late December, weighing approximately 1 pound and 10 ounces, and the child has been progressing well.

- Defendant pleaded guilty to Interstate Threats in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 875(c) as charged in Count Four of the Indictment.
- The offense of conviction carries a statutory maximum term of imprisonment of up to five years.
- The offense of conviction carries a statutory maximum fine of up to $250,000.
- The offense of conviction authorizes a term of supervised release of up to three years.
- In September 2019, Defendant was employed as a pharmacist with the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs.
- Defendant was arrested twice for driving under the influence in September 2019.
- Defendant was placed on administrative leave in January 2020 due to the DUI arrests.
- Beginning in April 2020, Defendant made a series of disturbing posts on Facebook.
- In April 2020, Defendant posted pictures of the Virginia Tech University mass shooter on Facebook.
- Defendant complained on Facebook that coworkers informed his supervisor about his DUI arrests, leading to his suspension from work.
- On May 13, 2020, Defendant messaged a friend that he wanted coworkers to think he was going to “shoot the place up.”
- On May 13, 2020, Defendant told a friend he had recently bought an AR-15 and suggested he might target people at the Seattle VA.
- Around May 2020, Defendant relocated to New Mexico to stay with his mother.
- On May 19, 2020, Defendant posted a message implying that Victims 1 and 2 would die in June.
- Defendant intended his May 2020 posts to be taken as true threats.
- On May 21, 2020, Albuquerque police went to Defendant’s residence but did not recover any firearm.
- On May 22, 2020, Defendant posted a message addressing police and taunting them about a newly purchased AR-15.
- Officers later learned Defendant had purchased parts for an AR-15.
- On August 20, 2020, Defendant was indicted in a related case with one count of threats against a federal official (18 U.S.C. § 115(a)(2)) and one count of interstate threats (18 U.S.C. § 875(c)).
- Defendant pleaded guilty in the related case on March 9, 2021.
- On June 15, 2021, Defendant was sentenced in the related case to time served for approximately 12 months and three years of supervised release.
- On June 17, 2021, two days after sentencing in the related case, Defendant began posting disturbing images on Facebook targeting Victims 1–3.
- Following the June 2021 posts, the Court added conditions to Defendant’s supervision in the related case, including that Probation would monitor his computer and other electronic devices.
- On July 30, 2021, Defendant was arrested after making specific threats to kill Victims 1–3 in text messages sent to his mother.
- Defendant sent numerous threatening text messages over a 29-hour period from July 28 to July 29, 2021.
- In one message, Defendant wrote he was going to kill Victims 1–3 and their families.
- In one message, Defendant threatened to mutilate pets and send them to a victim’s child.
- In one message, Defendant threatened to kill personnel at the Puget Sound VA.
- In one message, Defendant stated he would massacre Victims 1–3 and referenced an AR-15 as a weapon for hunting humans.
- At the time of the July 2021 text threats, Defendant had consumed alcohol in violation of his conditions of release in the prior case.
- Defendant was aware that U.S. Probation was monitoring his phone and would become aware of his communications.
- Defendant’s mother notified Probation and law enforcement of the July 2021 messages, and Defendant was arrested on July 30, 2021.
- At 8:08 pm on July 29, 2021, Defendant texted his mother “just kidding” after previously sending the threatening messages.
- Defendant has been incarcerated at the Federal Detention Center since his arrest on July 30, 2021.
- During his pretrial detention, Defendant has experienced extended lockdown conditions due to COVID mitigation measures at the FDC.
- In May 2020, Defendant was involuntarily hospitalized at a behavioral health facility from May 22 to May 29 before transfer to Washington for the first case.
- Defendant scheduled an appointment for August 4, 2021 to re-establish care with a surgeon for his neck issues but could not attend due to his late July 2021 arrest.
- Defendant scheduled an appointment with an addictions psychiatrist for August 31, 2021 but was re-arrested on July 30, 2021 before he could attend.
- On November 10, 2021, defense counsel moved in the prior case for Defendant’s release so he could present to an emergency room for psychiatric assessment; the motion was denied in connection with the supervised release violations.
- Defendant’s father died in June 2019 after a prolonged illness.
- Defendant’s chronic neck and jaw pain led him to use devices such as an inversion table and a cervical traction device.
- Defendant received approximately six weeks of physical therapy for neck pain.
- Defendant consulted multiple neurosurgeons and underwent diagnostic procedures including MRI, neck flexion-extension X-ray, EMG, discogram, and a corticosteroid injection.
- Defendant had two surgical procedures related to his pain prior to the July 2021 arrest.
- Defendant took medications for pain and was in treatment with a pain management clinic for about two years.
- Defendant has documented mental health conditions including anxiety, obsessive-compulsive disorder, depression, and TMJ diagnosed earlier in life.
- Defendant reported self-medicating with alcohol around the time of his father’s illness and death.
- After the 2019 DUI arrests, Defendant experienced performance issues at the VA and was reassigned to a logistics position in January 2020.
- In February 2020, Defendant took leave under the Family Medical Leave Act for mental health issues shortly after the reassignment.
- Defendant’s mother traveled to assist him in early 2020 and reported he experienced severe depression and a delay in accessing psychiatric care.
- Defendant moved from Seattle to stay with his mother in Albuquerque in early 2020.
- The COVID-19 pandemic in March 2020 disrupted access to non-emergency medical care relevant to Defendant’s planned surgery.
- Between May 22, 2020 and July 30, 2021, Defendant was largely in custody except for the period from June 15, 2021 to July 30, 2021 when he was on supervision.
- Victim impact statements were filed in this case.
- Defendant submitted a written personal statement to the Court acknowledging responsibility and stating he did not intend to actually harm his victims.
- The parties agreed that the United States would move to dismiss the remaining counts in the Indictment at the time of sentencing in this case.
- The plea agreement was executed on January 4, 2022.
- The Court accepted Defendant’s guilty plea on January 4, 2022.
- A sentencing hearing was set for April 8, 2022 at 1:00 pm.
- Defendant’s offense conduct in this case involved text messages sent to his mother while he was intoxicated and under federal supervision from a prior case.

- The defendant was charged by Information with one count of mail fraud in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1341.
- The defendant agreed to plead guilty to Count One of the Information.
- Count One carries a statutory maximum of 20 years of imprisonment.
- Count One carries a maximum fine of $250,000 or twice the gross gain or loss, whichever is greater.
- Count One carries a maximum term of supervised release of not more than three years.
- The defendant agreed to pay a $100 special assessment for the felony count.
- The defendant agreed to waive indictment by a grand jury and proceed by Information.
- The defendant agreed that restitution is mandatory to the University of South Florida Medical Services Association (UMSA).
- The defendant admitted obtaining at least $12,800,000 as a result of the offense and agreed restitution should be ordered for at least that amount.
- The defendant agreed to liquidate specified assets to pay restitution, including bank and brokerage accounts, real property in Palm Harbor, Florida, a parcel in St. John, U.S. Virgin Islands, a vehicle, and jewelry/memorabilia.
- The defendant agreed to forfeit $12,800,000 in criminal proceeds obtained from the offense.
- The defendant agreed that the criminal proceeds were transferred to third parties and could not be located upon due diligence, permitting forfeiture of substitute assets under 21 U.S.C. § 853(p).
- The defendant consented to forfeiture-related discovery to identify, locate, and forfeit substitute assets under Fed. R. Crim. P. 32.2(b)(3).
- The defendant agreed to waive constitutional, statutory, and procedural challenges to forfeiture carried out in accordance with the plea agreement.
- The defendant agreed to cooperate fully with the United States, including truthful testimony and disclosure of relevant information and documents.
- The defendant was employed as an accounting manager for UMSA and oversaw UMSA credit cards.
- From approximately June 2014 through November 2020, the defendant used multiple UMSA credit cards to make unauthorized charges for personal benefit.
- Unauthorized charges included rent payments, home renovations, travel, chartered yachts, and contributions to women affiliated with an interactive adult website.
- The defendant charged at least $12,800,000 to UMSA credit cards for his benefit.
- The defendant made false journal entries to make the unauthorized charges appear related to UMSA business.
- Around August 2018, the defendant recruited a woman to join an adult website and used UMSA credit cards to make contributions to her through the site.
- Under the scheme, the woman kept 40 percent of the contributions and mailed the remainder (less taxes) back to the defendant via personal checks.
- The defendant laundered more than $1.3 million through this arrangement.
- On or about November 6, 2019, a check for $18,953.28 was delivered to the defendant in the Middle District of Florida via the United States Postal Service.
- On August 11, 2021, a one-count Information charging mail fraud was filed in the Middle District of Florida.
- On August 26, 2021, the defendant entered a guilty plea to the one-count Information pursuant to a plea agreement.
- After his guilty plea, the defendant was placed on home confinement with monitoring and complied without violations for approximately 13 months.
- UMSA is a nonprofit corporation that assists USF Health in billing, collection, and disbursement of funds generated by the USF medical faculty.
- UMSA’s financial results are consolidated with those of the University of South Florida despite independent operations.
- The defendant began employment with UMSA in 2006 in the accounting and finance department and was promoted to Accounting Manager.
- As Accounting Manager, the defendant was given possession and authorization to use UMSA credit cards for business-related expenditures with little oversight.
- Beginning in 2014, the defendant used corporate credit cards issued by SunTrust Bank in UMSA’s name outside the scope of UMSA business.
- Unauthorized purchases included airline tickets, travel and lodging, gifts, home renovations, rental payments, weddings, and contributions to women on an adult website.
- The defendant used UMSA cards for his personal benefit and the benefit of his family.
- In 2018, Person C formed a company called Tropical Familia Investment that focused on real estate in the Caribbean.
- Bank accounts were set up and land was purchased for Tropical Familia Investment, with salaries and expenses paid using UMSA credit cards.
- The adult website used by the defendant was MyGirlFund.com, a platform where members tip performers who receive a share of the tips and the site retains a percentage.
- Most of the defendant’s transactions were to MyGirlFund.com, which accumulated millions of dollars in fees from the tips.
- Performer A, a Canadian citizen, engaged in a relationship with the defendant via MyGirlFund.com and direct messaging.
- Approximately $6,000,000 was paid to Performer A, who also used the UMSA credit card for gifts, vacations, travel, and medical treatment for a family member.
- The defendant told Performer A that the funds she received were paid with UMSA credit cards.
- Performer A refused to return funds after learning of the investigation and civil litigation.
- In 2018, Person B agreed with the defendant that she would sign up as a webcam performer on the platform to receive tips funded by UMSA credit cards.
- Under the arrangement with Person B, the performer kept 40% of the funds and mailed back the remaining 60% (after platform fees) via checks.
- Person B personally received in excess of $1,300,000 through this arrangement.
- The defendant’s employment with UMSA was terminated in December 2020.
- After learning of the investigation, the defendant admitted guilt and agreed to cooperate with the government.
- The defendant met with agents in a series of proffers and provided information about the offense, location of money, and identities of others involved.
- The defendant cooperated with attorneys for the University in related civil litigation, including agreeing to a civil default and serving as a witness and information source.
- On June 29, 2021, the defendant signed a stipulation regarding restitution to liquidate assets.
- On August 30, 2021, a Motion and Order to Deposit Pre-Judgment Funds was granted and entered in the case.
- On August 31, 2021, the defendant paid $1,212,626.29 to the Clerk of Court toward restitution.
- On September 9, 2022, the defendant signed a stipulation authorizing the U.S. Marshal to liquidate the Virgin Islands property for restitution.
- On September 19, 2022, the defendant made an additional $20,000 payment toward restitution.
- According to a University of South Florida investigative report referenced in the record, payments to MyGirlFund.com totaled $11,487,433 during the scheme.
- The defense memorandum reports that MyGirlFund.com retained approximately 20% of payments and performers received approximately 80%, based on an internal audit reference.
- The defendant’s employment with UMSA ended before he sought other employment and he later worked in hourly positions at retail stores.
- The defendant set aside earnings from employment to make monthly restitution payments prior to sentencing.
- The defendant attempted suicide after learning of the investigation and then engaged in treatment, including counseling and medication, following a Baker Act hospitalization.
- The defendant has a history of a traumatic brain injury from a 1990s automobile accident, which the memorandum states affected cognition and behavior.
- As of September 2021, a clinic recommended the defendant complete twenty additional hours of hyperbaric therapy for the brain injury, according to the memorandum.
- The defendant has multiple physical conditions including osteoarthritis and a degenerative spine and has undergone several knee surgeries and two hip surgeries, per the memorandum.
- The defendant has foot conditions requiring regular podiatry care approximately every six weeks, according to the memorandum.
- The defendant has documented mental health diagnoses including anxiety, depression, major depressive disorder, panic attacks, mood instability, ADHD, PTSD, neurocognitive disorder, and bipolar disorder, per the memorandum.
- The defendant is prescribed multiple medications and receives therapy for mental health treatment, according to the memorandum.
- The defendant moved in with and became the primary caregiver for his elderly mother prior to sentencing, according to the memorandum.
- The defense filed a sentencing memorandum and motion for downward variance on September 20, 2022.

- On January 6, 2021, a joint session of Congress convened at the U.S. Capitol to certify the Electoral College vote count.
- Vice President was present and presiding over the joint session on January 6, 2021.
- On January 6, 2021, the exterior plaza of the U.S. Capitol was closed to members of the public and access inside required authorization and screening.
- Shortly after 2:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021, individuals in the crowd forced entry into the U.S. Capitol, including by breaking windows and assaulting law enforcement.
- At approximately 2:20 p.m. on January 6, 2021, Members of the House and Senate were evacuated, and congressional proceedings were suspended until shortly after 8:00 p.m.
- The January 6 riot resulted in substantial damage to the Capitol requiring more than $2.7 million in repairs, as reflected in one official summary.
- An updated loss figure cited by the government reflects approximately $2.88 million in losses as of October 17, 2022.
- Defendant A lived in Colorado in December 2020.
- As early as December 20, 2020, Defendant A discussed plans to attend a January 6, 2021 rally in Washington, D.C.
- On December 20, 2020, Defendant A texted that the rally might be the start of something.
- On December 23, 2020, Defendant A texted that people were planning a revolt on January 6.
- On December 23, 2020, Defendant A texted that people were bringing guns to D.C. and would not let the declared winner take office.
- On December 23, 2020, Defendant A texted about tar and feathers for politicians.
- On or around January 5, 2021, Defendant A drove from Colorado to Washington, D.C.
- Defendant A traveled to Washington, D.C. with the purpose to stop or prevent Congress from certifying the Electoral College results.
- On the morning of January 6, 2021, Defendant A attended a rally at the Ellipse.
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant A texted a family contact that he hoped something would actually happen.
- Around 11:34 a.m. on January 6, 2021, Defendant A texted that he was there for riots if the incumbent was not declared the winner and referenced civil war talk.
- After the rally, Defendant A went to the West Front of the Capitol grounds and joined the gathering crowd.
- At 1:25 p.m. on January 6, 2021, Defendant A texted that it was happening and that they were going to storm the Capitol.
- At 1:42 p.m. on January 6, 2021, Defendant A texted that they were trying to storm the Capitol.
- Shortly before 2:00 p.m., the crowd forced through barricades on the West Front and advanced to the Capitol exterior; Defendant A was among them.
- At 1:52:32 p.m. on January 6, 2021, Defendant A texted, "We just got in part."
- At 2:14:46 p.m. on January 6, 2021, Defendant A entered the U.S. Capitol through the Senate Wing Doors.
- Soon after entry, Defendant A texted that he was inside the Capitol building and claimed the door had been busted down.
- Near the Crypt around 2:19 p.m., Defendant A gestured at officers and used language indicating they needed to move, including telling them to stand down.
- By approximately 2:25 p.m., officers near the Crypt retreated and Defendant A and others proceeded deeper into the Capitol.
- Around 2:32 p.m., Defendant A went to a hallway outside the Senate Gallery on the Capitol's third floor and was one of the first rioters to reach that location.
- Three plain-clothes Capitol Police officers attempted to close and lock the Senate Gallery doors as rioters gathered.
- A group of rioters, including Defendant A, confronted the officers at the Senate Gallery doors; members of the group pushed and hit the officers, forcing them to retreat before a door could be locked.
- Defendant A did not personally hit or push an officer at the Senate Gallery doors according to the agreed facts.
- As officers retreated from the Senate Gallery area, Defendant A approached one officer and squared up into a fighter's stance; no fight ensued.
- Defendant A gained access to the Senate Gallery and was among the first individuals to enter it.
- While inside, Defendant A texted that he was the first one in the chamber and that they stormed the Capitol and broke down the door.
- Defendant A texted a contact that they took the whole thing and Congress had to evacuate, then stated they would do it every day votes were attempted.
- Defendant A exited the Capitol at approximately 3:53 p.m. via the Senate Carriage Door.
- Capitol CCTV footage documented Defendant A’s movements, including picking up a book or binder, engaging with plain-clothes officers, and joining a group trying to access doors near the Senate Gallery.
- CCTV shows Defendant A squaring up with a plain-clothes officer outside the Senate Gallery as officers retreated.
- Body-worn camera captured Defendant A telling officers, “Next time we’re coming with [unintelligible],” and yelling “So were the Nazis!” and “Stand down!”
- CCTV footage shows Defendant A using a cell phone and joining a crowd pushing at an interior door near the Rotunda.
- A witness who knew Defendant A for his entire adolescent and adult life identified him in multiple CCTV screenshots from January 6, 2021.
- A witness provided the FBI a recent photograph from Defendant A’s social media for comparison with CCTV images.
- Subscriber records showed a phone number associated with Defendant A, and time-zone data indicated travel from Mountain to Eastern Time zones around January 5–6, 2021.
- Google location data associated with accounts linked to Defendant A placed a device within the U.S. Capitol from approximately 2:15 p.m. to 3:25 p.m. on January 6, 2021.
- On January 6, 2021, Defendant A texted contemporaneously that he was inside the Capitol and sent multiple messages describing storming and breaking down a door.
- Defendant A texted that the location he entered was the voting chamber.
- Defendant A was convicted after a stipulated bench trial of six counts: 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2); 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1); 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(2); 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(4); 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D); and 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(G).
- Count One carries a statutory maximum penalty of up to 20 years’ imprisonment.
- Counts Two, Three, and Four each carry up to one year of imprisonment and a special assessment.
- Counts Five and Six each carry up to six months’ imprisonment and a special assessment.
- The Court proceeding was a stipulated trial as reflected by the jointly submitted Statement of Offense.
- Defendant A acknowledged the Statement of Offense as true and accurate by signing on January 20, 2023.
- Defense counsel acknowledged concurrence with Defendant A’s adoption of the Statement of Offense on January 23, 2023.
- The government asserted that at approximately 2:17 p.m. in the Crypt area, Defendant A threw an object toward a line of officers.
- The defense disputed that Defendant A threatened physical injury and argued he mirrored another man’s pose briefly before walking away.
- The defense argued there was no evidence that any object allegedly thrown by Defendant A was capable of injuring officers or that officers felt threatened by it.
- Defendant A told officers to “Stand down!” during interior confrontations captured on video.
- A law enforcement officer reported that Defendant A used intimidating language indicating the crowd could take the officers during a corridor confrontation.
- Witness accounts and device data showed Defendant A used a mobile phone to communicate and record events while inside the Capitol.
- The FBI used a driver’s license photograph and witness identifications to match Defendant A to individuals depicted on Capitol CCTV from January 6, 2021.
- The Statement of Offense records that Defendant A admitted facts establishing each element of the charged offenses if the Court so found beyond a reasonable doubt.
- On August 25, 2021, a federal grand jury returned a superseding indictment charging six counts later proven at the stipulated trial.
- During a corridor confrontation, CCTV shows Defendant A gesturing at officers and then moving to the front line facing officers as they retreated.
- In one recorded exchange, Defendant A placed his hands on an officer during the January 6 interior confrontation.
- The government described that Defendant A signaled other rioters to move forward at an interior door on the second floor near the east side, contributing to a breach.
- The U.S. Capitol Police maintained temporary and permanent barricades around the Capitol on January 6, 2021, and attempted to keep the crowd away from the building.
- Proceedings could not resume on January 6, 2021, until unauthorized occupants had left and the building was secured.
- Defendant A sent messages from inside the Capitol asserting he helped break down a door and was first in the chamber, and expressed intent to repeat disruptions.
- The Statement of Facts alleged probable cause that Defendant A violated 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1), (2), and (4); 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D), (E), and (G); 18 U.S.C. § 231(a)(3); and 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2).
- The Vice President remained at the Capitol from evacuation until proceedings resumed around 8:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021.
- Defendant A acknowledged planning and intent messages before January 6, such as wanting to be part of history and expecting a revolt.
- During the Senate Gallery door confrontation, officers were plain-clothes Capitol Police attempting to secure the chamber doors.
- Defendant A used a mobile device associated with him to communicate during the events, including messages sent at 1:25 p.m., 1:42 p.m., 2:18 p.m., and after 3:00 p.m.
- Defendant A had prior misdemeanor-level interactions with the justice system, including a 2006 controlled substance case resulting in probation, a 2010 DUI and criminal impersonation, and a 2015 open fire offense resulting in a fine, per PSR summary in the record.
- The government described that Defendant A went onto the second and third floors and into the Rotunda while inside the Capitol.
- The government asserted that Defendant A’s actions, with others, caused officers to retreat at an east-side second-floor door, enabling additional rioters to enter.
- A law enforcement officer reported that Defendant A indicated to officers that the mob could overrun them during interior confrontations.
- Defendant A’s presence inside the U.S. Capitol on January 6, 2021, is corroborated by CCTV imagery, witness identification, and device location records.
- Defendant A was convicted on Count One of obstructing an official proceeding and aiding and abetting in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1512(c)(2).
- Defendant A was convicted on Count Two of entering or remaining in a restricted building or grounds in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(1).
- Defendant A was convicted on Count Three of disorderly or disruptive conduct in a restricted building or grounds in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(2).
- Defendant A was convicted on Count Four of engaging in physical violence in a restricted building or grounds in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1752(a)(4).
- Defendant A was convicted on Count Five of disorderly or disruptive conduct in a Capitol building in violation of 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(D).
- Defendant A was convicted on Count Six of parading, demonstrating, or picketing in a Capitol building in violation of 40 U.S.C. § 5104(e)(2)(G).
- CCTV shows Defendant A entering the Senate Wing Door at 2:14:46 p.m. and later exiting via the Senate Carriage Door at approximately 3:53 p.m.
- The government reported that more than one hundred police officers were injured during the January 6 attack.
- Defendant A told a contact after entering that they took the whole thing and Congress had to evacuate.
- The Statement of Offense records that Defendant A did not personally strike officers during the Senate Gallery door incident, although others in the group did.
